Even after the machine has stopped, a finger or hand may already be inside the guard opening by an additional distance $C_r$ (the reach value based on opening geometry, per ISO 13857 Table 1).

$$
d_{\text{total intrusion}} = K \times (T_1 + T_2) + C_r
$$

**Step 3 — Equate total intrusion to safety distance.**

The minimum safety distance $S$ from the outer surface of the guard to the hazard must be at least equal to this total intrusion, otherwise the hand will reach the hazard:

$$
S = K \times (T_1 + T_2) + C_r
$$

This is the canonical **ISO 13857 / EN 349 safety distance formula**.

$$
\boxed{S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C_r}
$$

### 3.2 Reach Value $C_r$ Table (ISO 13857, Annex A, Table 1)

| Opening Size $e$ (mm) | Reach Value $C_r$ (mm) | Body Part Allowed |
|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 4 – 6 | 0 | Finger tip only |
| 6 – 8 | 10 | Finger to first knuckle |
| 8 – 10 | 20 | Finger |
| 10 – 12 | 35 | Finger to base |
| 12 – 15 | 80 | Finger / partial hand |
| 15 – 20 | 110 | Hand |
| 20 – 25 | 135 | Hand |
| 25 – 35 | 195 | Hand / partial arm |
| 35 – 50 | 285 | Arm to shoulder |
| 50 – 65 | 320 | Arm |
| 65 – 75 | 380 | Arm to shoulder |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model QP Style):**
> A fixed guard with circular perforations of **12 mm diameter** is to be installed on a hydraulic press. The sensing system response time is **0.05 s**, and the mechanical stopping time is **0.20 s**. Calculate the minimum safety distance $S$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Given Data:**

- Opening diameter $e = 12$ mm → from Table 3.2, $C_r = 35$ mm (interpolating between 10–12 mm)
- $T_1 = 0.05$ s
- $T_2 = 0.20$ s
- $K = 1600$ mm/s (standard hand-arm constant)

**Step A — Total stop time:**

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.25 \text{ s}
$$

**Step B — Substitute into Equation:**

$$
S = K \times T + C_r
$$

**Step C — Insert numerical values:**

$$
S = 1600 \, \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{s}} \times 0.25 \, \text{s} + 35 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step D — Compute the multiplication:**

$$
1600 \times 0.25 = 400 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step E — Final addition:**

$$
S = 400 + 35 = 435 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The fixed guard must be installed such that its inner face is at least **435 mm (0.435 m)** away from the nearest hazardous moving part.

$$
\boxed{S_{\min} = 435 \text{ mm}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fixed Guard Classification Architecture

The following block-level functional architecture maps the position of Fixed Guards within the broader machine-guard taxonomy as taught in KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Machine Guarding Hierarchy]
    nodeB[Fixed Guards]
    nodeC[Movable Guards]
    nodeD[Interlocked Guards]
    nodeE[Adjustable Guards]
    nodeF[Self Adjusting Guards]
    nodeG[Solid Sheet Metal]
    nodeH[Perforated Mesh]
    nodeI[Bar Grid]
    nodeJ[Enclosure Hood]
    nodeK[Tool Only Removal]
    nodeL[No Tools Required]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeI
    nodeB --> nodeJ
    nodeB --> nodeK
    nodeC --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM[Requires Screws Nuts Bolts Rivets]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Welded Construction Permitted]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Removal Forces Maintenance Mode]
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

### 4.2 Schematic Reach-Distance Block Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP[Operator Hand Position]
    nodeQ[Guard Outer Face]
    nodeR[Safety Distance S]
    nodeS[Hazard Zone]
    nodeT[Cr Reach Buffer]

    nodeP --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeR --> nodeT
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `nodeP` represents the operator's hand resting at the start of a reflex reach.
- `nodeQ` is the **outer face** of the fixed guard.
- `nodeR` ($S$) is the calculated safety distance — the engineered gap.
- `nodeS` is the **hazard zone** (e.g., rotating gear).
- `nodeT` ($C_r$) is the additional buffer contributed by opening geometry.

This block flow visually enforces the KTU learning outcome: **"Calculate and justify the placement distance of a fixed guard."**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a fixed guard. List any two distinguishing features.**
*(CO2, Remembering Level)*

**Model Answer:**
A fixed guard is a permanent barrier that forms an integral part of a machine and can only be removed with tools. **Two distinguishing features:**
1. It requires tools (screwdriver, wrench) for removal.
2. It provides **passive, fail-safe protection** — it does not depend on operator action or electrical signals.

---

**Q2. State the standard formula for calculating the safety distance of a fixed guard.**
*(CO2, Understanding Level)*

**Model Answer:**
The standard ISO 13857 formula is:

$$
S = K \times (T_1 + T_2) + C_r
$$

where $K = 1600$ mm/s, $T_1$ = response time, $T_2$ = stopping time, $C_r$ = reach value from opening size table.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Explain the classification of fixed guards with neat sketches. Discuss the design principles for each type. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer Outline:**
- **Solid Sheet Metal Guard:** Constructed from a single plate of MS/SS — used where visibility is not required (e.g., flywheels). Design principle: must withstand impact energy without deformation.
- **Perforated Mesh Guard:** Allows visual inspection of running machinery — mesh size must be ≤ 6 mm to prevent finger entry.
- **Bar Grid Guard:** Vertical or horizontal bars spaced such that the gap between bars follows ISO 13857 reach table.
- **Enclosure Hood:** Fully sealed cabinet — used for CNC, laser, dust-emitting operations.
- **Design Principles Common to All:** Tool-only removal, corrosion-resistant material, no sharp edges, properly anchored, minimum height 1.8 m, regular integrity inspection.

**(b)** A fixed guard has an opening of 20 mm and is to be installed on a power press. The press stopping time is 0.30 s and the sensing system response is 0.08 s. Calculate the minimum safety distance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

$$
S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C_r
$$

For opening $e = 20$ mm, from reach table $C_r = 110$ mm.

$$
S = 1600 \times (0.08 + 0.30) + 110
$$

$$
S = 1600 \times 0.38 + 110
$$

$$
S = 608 + 110 = 718 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\min} = 718 \text{ mm} \approx 0.72 \text{ m}}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)**

**(a)** Compare fixed guards, movable guards, and interlocked guards. State two advantages and two disadvantages of fixed guards specifically. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer Outline:**

| Feature | Fixed | Movable | Interlocked |
|---------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Tool required for removal | Yes | No | No |
| Operator intervention | None | Manual | Manual + sensor trip |
| Failure mode | Mechanical only | Mechanical | Electrical + mechanical |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

**Advantages of Fixed Guards:**
1. Highest reliability — purely passive, no electrical failure modes.
2. Zero operator action required — cannot be "forgotten."
3. Lowest cost and longest service life.

**Disadvantages of Fixed Guards:**
1. Requires tools for every maintenance access → can encourage "toolless bypass" shortcuts.
2. Fixed position may interfere with frequent tool changes or loading operations.

**(b)** With the help of the ISO 13857 safety distance equation, derive the relationship $S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C_r$. Identify each variable and explain its physical meaning. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
(See full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.)

- $S$ — minimum safety distance from guard to hazard (mm).
- $K$ — hand-arm approach speed constant, **1600 mm/s** per ISO 13857.
- $T_1$ — response time of the safeguarding device (s).
- $T_2$ — mechanical / electrical / hydraulic stopping time of the machine (s).
- $C_r$ — additional reach value determined by opening geometry, from ISO 13857 Table 1.

The derivation follows from equating uncontrolled reach distance during total stop time plus reach-buffer to the engineered safety gap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $K$ must be in **mm/s**, times in **seconds**, and $C_r$ in **mm**. Mixing units is the #1 mark-losing error.
> 2. **Wrong $C_r$ lookup:** Students often choose $C_r$ based on opening width rather than the correct row of ISO 13857 Table 1.
> 3. **Confusing fixed guard with interlocked guard:** A fixed guard is *not* interlocked — it has no sensors. Examiners deduct full marks if the definition includes "interlock."
> 4. **Skipping the derivation:** In 7-mark sub-questions asking for derivation, merely writing the final formula without kinematic reasoning scores ≤ 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring fastener specification:** A guard fastened with wing nuts is **not** a fixed guard as per IS 9474 / ISO 12100.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fixed Guard (PESFT864 – Module 2):**
>
> - Fixed guard = **permanent**, **tool-removable only**, **passive** barrier.
> - **Cannot be defeated** without deliberate, supervised maintenance.
> - First choice in the **hierarchy of controls** (engineering control above PPE).
> - **Safety distance equation:** $S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C_r$, with $K = 1600$ mm/s.
> - **Reach value $C_r$** is read from **ISO 13857 Table 1**, based on opening size.
> - **Opening ≤ 6 mm** prevents finger insertion.
> - **Minimum guard height:** **1.8 m (6 ft)** for general floor-level hazards.
> - **Material must withstand** expected impact energy without failure.
> - **No sharp edges, no burrs, no projections** — ISO 12100 § 6.3.
> - Standards to remember: **IS 9474:2002**, **ISO 12100:2010**, **ISO 13857:2019**, **ANSI B11.19**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart O**.
> - **Common mistake:** equating "fixed" with "interlocked" — they are mutually exclusive categories.
> - **Verification:** Always measure stop time with a stop-time instrument and physically probe reach.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interlock Guard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interlock Guard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interlock Guard (Definition):**
> An **Interlock Guard** is a movable physical barrier integrated with a machine's power supply (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or mechanical) such that the machine **cannot be energized or operated** unless the guard is properly closed, fully in position, and securely fastened. Opening the guard during machine operation must immediately initiate a **stop command** or disconnect the energy source, thereby preventing operator exposure to hazardous motion, energy, or process hazards.

The interlock guard is one of the **engineering control measures** under the hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE), and it sits firmly in the **engineering control** tier. In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, it forms a critical sub-topic under *Machine Guarding and Process Safety*.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Guard** | Physical barrier that prevents access to hazardous areas |
| **Interlock** | An arrangement that forces a predetermined sequence of operations and prevents one action from occurring while another is in progress |
| **Fail-Safe** | Design philosophy where any single component failure leaves the system in a safe (de-energized) state |
| **Trap Key** | A key held captive by one device and required to operate another |
| **Positive Opening** | Contact separation achieved by direct mechanical drive, not spring action |
| **Safety Rating** | Determined by ISO 13849-1 Performance Level (PL) a–e |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Refrigerator Door

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Your Home Refrigerator**
> Think of the light inside your refrigerator. The moment you open the door, the light switches ON (machine cannot run safely without visibility) and the compressor may pause. When you close the door fully, the latch engages and only then can normal operation resume. An **interlock guard works exactly the same way** but in reverse — the machine (compressor) is only allowed to run when the door (guard) is **fully closed**. If you try to open the door while the machine is running, the interlock immediately **cuts the power**, just like pulling the emergency cord on a treadmill. This guarantees that **you can never reach into danger while motion exists**.

### 1.4 Primary Objectives of an Interlock Guard

1. **Prevent unauthorized access** to moving/energized parts during operation.
2. **Eliminate the possibility of bypassing** the guard without deliberate tampering.
3. **Provide automatic stop** when the guard is opened during machine cycle.
4. **Restrict machine restart** until the guard is re-closed AND the start sequence is re-initiated.
5. **Provide proof of safe condition** before allowing reset (supervisor key, two-hand control, etc.).

### 1.5 Why Interlock Guards are Mandatory in Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Hierarchy of Controls:**
> OSHA 29 CFR 1910.212 and ISO 12100:2010 mandate that *“whenever practical, machines shall be so designed and constructed that the hazardous functions cannot be initiated until the guard is closed, and the guard must remain closed until the hazardous function has ended.”* This is the **legal basis** for interlock guarding in engineering industry.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Interlock Guards (KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus)

Interlock guards are classified based on the **energy medium** used to sense guard position and command the machine control circuit.

#### A. Mechanical Interlock Guards
- Use **physical linkages**, cams, levers, and latches.
- The guard’s closed position physically actuates a switch or valve via a cam-follower mechanism.
- **Typical Application**: Power presses, gate valves in chemical plants, manual milling machines.
- **Advantage**: No external power needed; inherently reliable.
- **Disadvantage**: Limited travel; wears out under repeated cycling.

#### B. Electrical (Electromechanical) Interlock Guards
- Most common type in modern industry.
- A **limit switch**, **proximity sensor**, or **safety switch** is actuated by guard closure.
- Wired into the machine’s **safety relay** or **safety PLC**.
- Components include:
  * Position switches with **positive-opening contacts** (IEC 60947-5-1)
  * Tongue (actuator) switches
  * Hinge-mounted switches
  * RFID-coded switches (non-contact, high tamper resistance)

#### C. Hydraulic / Pneumatic Interlock Guards
- Guard closure permits **fluid pressure** to actuate the machine.
- Opening the guard dumps pressure through a **dump valve** — fails safe.
- Used in **press brakes, hydraulic presses, large CNC cells**.

#### D. Trapped-Key Interlock (Captive Key Systems)
- A key is **trapped (locked)** in one device until the corresponding hazard is isolated.
- Used in **high-voltage switchgear**, **robot cells**, **maintenance lockout**.
- Sequence example:
  1. Open isolator → key released → insert key into guard interlock → guard can now be opened.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Operational Logic Flow

The interlock works on a **three-state logic**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic of Interlock Operation (KTU Board Favourite)**
> - **State 1: GUARD OPEN** → Machine cannot START. Hazardous motion is **forbidden**.
> - **State 2: GUARD CLOSED & START COMMAND GIVEN** → Machine **energized** and operational.
> - **State 3: GUARD OPENED DURING RUN** → Emergency stop **immediately** triggered; machine returns to safe state.
> - **State 4: RE-START** → Guard must be re-closed AND a fresh start command issued (anti-restart logic).

### 2.3 Fail-Safe Design Principles

A properly designed interlock must satisfy these principles (ISO 12100 + ISO 13849-1):

| # | Principle | Explanation |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **De-energize to Safe** | Power loss or component failure must stop the machine, not start it. |
| 2 | **Positive Mode Actuation** | Use **positive-opening** contacts (force-guided), not spring-only. |
| 3 | **Single-Fault Tolerance** | One component failure must not bypass the safety function. |
| 4 | **Redundancy** | Two independent channels (dual-contact switches). |
| 5 | **Cross-Monitoring** | Safety relay monitors consistency between channels. |
| 6 | **Proven-in-Use Components** | Use devices certified to IEC 61508 / ISO 13849. |

### 2.4 Categories of Safety-Related Parts (ISO 13849-1)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Categorisation B, 1, 2, 3, 4 is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Memorise the table below verbatim.

| Category | Requirements | Typical Use |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **B** | Basic safety; single channel; no diagnostics | Low-risk interlock |
| **1** | Well-tried components; single channel | Guard interlock with tested switch |
| **2** | Single channel + periodic testing | Medium-risk interlock |
| **3** | Dual-channel, redundant; one fault tolerable | Press brake guard |
| **4** | Dual-channel, redundant, cross-monitored, fault accumulation tolerable | Robot cell guard |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises all governing equations for interlock reliability and performance. **Memorize this for KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Performance Level (PL) | $PL = f(\text{Category},\; DC_{avg},\; MTTF_d,\; \text{CCF})$ | a → e; high-risk needs PL ≥ d |
| Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | $MTTF_d = \frac{1}{\lambda_d}$ | $\lambda_d$ = dangerous failure rate (failures/hr) |
| Average Diagnostic Coverage | $DC_{avg} = \frac{\sum \lambda_{dd}}{\sum \lambda_{d}}$ | ratio of detected failures |
| Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour | $PFH_d = \sum \lambda_{d}$ | sum of channel failure rates |
| Stop Time Verification | $S_{min} \gt T_{stop} + T_{response}$ | Safety distance must exceed machine stop time |
| Safety Distance (ISO 13855) | $S = K \cdot T + C$ | K = hand approach speed (typically 1600 mm/s), T = total stopping time, C = intrusion distance |
| Stop Time | $T_{stop} = T_{reaction} + T_{brake}$ | Reaction time + mechanical brake time |
| Failure Rate λ | $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ | Failure per hour |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- For $\vert MTTF_d \vert$: Low ≤ 3 yr; Medium = 3–10 yr; High = 10–30 yr.
- ISO 13855 uses $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ for hand-arm reach, $K = 2000\ \text{mm/s}$ for whole-body.
- C values: $\vert C \vert$ ranges 0 to 1200 mm depending on intrusion into danger zone.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Industry

- **Automotive Robotics**: Interlocked cage doors prevent entry while robots cycle.
- **Power Presses**: Guard interlock prevents ram stroke unless guard is closed.
- **CNC Machining Centers**: Door interlock stops spindle if door opened mid-cut.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food**: Hygienic interlock on conveyor tunnels using coded RFID.
- **Energy Sector**: Trapped-key interlocks on HV switchgear to prevent re-energization during maintenance.
- **Foundries & Forges**: Heat-resistant interlock switches on furnace charging doors.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Interlock Type

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU examiner expects students to be able to **justify the choice** of interlock. Use the following decision matrix in your answers.

| Selection Criterion | Mechanical | Electromechanical | Hydraulic/Pneumatic | Trapped-Key |
|---------------------|------------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Low cost | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| High reliability | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Tamper resistance | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Remote monitoring | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Suitable for fast cycles | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Maintenance-friendly | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Best for HV/LOTO | ★ | ★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ |

### 3.2 Derivation — Safety Distance Using ISO 13855

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield derivation.** KTU frequently asks: *"Calculate the minimum safety distance for a guard interlock."*

#### Step 1: Define the Variables
Let:
- $S$ = minimum safety distance (mm)
- $K$ = approach speed (mm/s)
- $T$ = total stopping time (s)
- $C$ = intrusion distance before reaching danger (mm)

#### Step 2: State the Governing Equation

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

#### Step 3: Break Down Total Stopping Time

The machine does not stop instantaneously. Total stopping time is the sum of:

$$
T = T_r + T_{br} + T_{s}
$$

where:
- $T_r$ = reaction time of the safety system (s)
- $T_{br}$ = braking time (s)
- $T_s$ = stopping time of the safety relay/controller (s, usually ≤ 0.025 s)

#### Step 4: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting into the safety distance equation:

$$
S = K \cdot (T_r + T_{br} + T_s) + C
$$

#### Step 5: Apply the Numerical Boundary Conditions

For hand-arm approach in industrial machinery:
- $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ (standard)
- $C$ depends on guard geometry: typically $C \geq 0$ and can extend up to 1200 mm if the guard allows reach-over.

#### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A CNC lathe has reaction time $T_r = 0.030\ \text{s}$, brake time $T_{br} = 0.180\ \text{s}$, safety relay time $T_s = 0.020\ \text{s}$, and intrusion distance $C = 850\ \text{mm}$. Calculate the minimum safety distance.

**Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute total stopping time $T$:

$$
T = T_r + T_{br} + T_s
$$

$$
T = 0.030 + 0.180 + 0.020
$$

$$
T = 0.230\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply ISO 13855:

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

$$
S = 1600 \times 0.230 + 850
$$

$$
S = 368 + 850
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1218\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The guard must be placed at **at least 1218 mm** from the nearest hazard point so that even after the operator opens the interlock guard, the machine stops before reach can occur.

### 3.3 Derivation — Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour ($PFH_d$)

#### Step 1: Define the Channel Failure Rate
For a single-channel safety system with one switch per guard:

$$
\lambda_d = \text{dangerous failure rate (per hour)}
$$

Typical value for an electromechanical safety switch: $\lambda_d \approx 1 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{per hour}$.

#### Step 2: Dual-Channel Redundancy (Category 3 or 4)

For two independent channels with cross-monitoring:

$$
PFH_d = \lambda_{d1} + \lambda_{d2}
$$

#### Step 3: With Diagnostic Coverage

$$
PFH_d = \lambda_{d1} \cdot (1 - DC_1) + \lambda_{d2} \cdot (1 - DC_2) + \lambda_{cross}
$$

where $\lambda_{cross}$ is the cross-fault rate.

#### Step 4: Numeric Example
Given: $\lambda_{d1} = 1 \times 10^{-7}$, $\lambda_{d2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$, $DC = 0.99$, $\lambda_{cross} = 1 \times 10^{-9}$.

$$
PFH_d = (1 \times 10^{-7})(1 - 0.99) + (1.2 \times 10^{-7})(1 - 0.99) + 1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
PFH_d = 1 \times 10^{-9} + 1.2 \times 10^{-9} + 1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
\boxed{PFH_d = 3.2 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{per hour}}
$$

This corresponds to **Performance Level PL e**, suitable for high-risk interlock applications.

### 3.4 Derivation — Performance Level (PL) Mapping

The Performance Level is selected based on $PFH_d$ value:

| $PFH_d$ Range (per hour) | Performance Level |
|--------------------------|-------------------|
| $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | **PL a** |
| $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | **PL b** |
| $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | **PL c** |
| $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | **PL d** |
| $< 10^{-8}$ to $\geq 10^{-9}$ | **PL e** |

For our numerical example: $PFH_d = 3.2 \times 10^{-9}$, which falls in the **PL e** bracket.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interlock Guard System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Initiates Action] --> B[Guard Door Status Sensor]
    B -->|Guard Open| C[Safety Relay Logic Block]
    C -->|STOP Command| D[Contactor / Power Cutoff]
    D -->|NO Power| E[Motor / Hazardous Element DE-ENERGISED]
    B -->|Guard Closed| F[Start Command Button]
    F --> G[Two-Hand Control Verification]
    G -->|Valid| H[Machine Energised]
    H --> I[Machine Running Cycle]
    I -->|Operator Opens Guard| J[Safety Switch Trips]
    J --> C
    C -->|E-Stop Triggered| K[Lockout Tagout Notification]
    K --> L[Maintenance Reset Required]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electromechanical Interlock Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Movable Guard Door] --> M2[Hinge-Mounted Position Switch]
    M2 -->|Positive-Opening Contact| M3[Safety Relay Module]
    M3 --> M4[Logic Solver]
    M4 --> M5[Power Contactor Coil]
    M5 --> M6[Motor Supply]
    M4 --> M7[Indicator Lamp GREEN]
    M4 --> M8[Indicator Lamp RED]
    M3 --> M9[Emergency Stop Loop]
    M9 --> M4
```

### 4.3 Trapped-Key Interlock Sequence (KTU Diagram Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: HV Isolator ON, Key A Trapped in Isolator] --> P2[Step 2: Turn Off Isolator, Release Key A]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Insert Key A into Guard Interlock Lock]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Rotate Key A, Unlock Guard Door]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Operator Entains Cell, Key A Now Trapped in Guard]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Maintenance Complete, Close Guard]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Release Key A from Guard, Return to Isolator]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Re-energise Isolator, Cycle Repeats]
```

### 4.4 Functional State-Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GuardOpen
    GuardOpen --> GuardClosed : Operator Closes Guard
    GuardClosed --> MachineRunning : Start Command AND Guard Closed
    MachineRunning --> EmergencyStop : Guard Opened During Run
    EmergencyStop --> GuardClosed : Fault Cleared, Guard Re-Closed
    GuardClosed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for KTU exam:** If a Mermaid diagram cannot be reproduced in your answer sheet, redraw it as a labelled rectangular block diagram with arrows showing **signal flow from sensor → relay → contactor → load**, plus a parallel **emergency-stop branch**.

### 4.5 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Typical Safety Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SafetySwitch[Electromechanical Safety Switch]
        T1[Actuator Tongue] --> T2[Cam Mechanism]
        T2 --> T3[Positive Opening Contact NC]
        T2 --> T4[Auxiliary Contact NO]
        T3 --> T5[Output to Safety Relay Channel 1]
        T4 --> T6[Output to Safety Relay Channel 2]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define an interlock guard. Mention any two advantages.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An **interlock guard** is a movable physical barrier mechanically, electrically, or hydraulically linked to the machine’s power supply, such that the machine **cannot start unless the guard is closed** and will **immediately stop if the guard is opened**.

**Advantages:**
1. **Automatic enforcement** of safety — eliminates reliance on operator discipline.
2. **Tamper-resistant** when combined with positive-opening contacts and coded actuators.
3. (Bonus) Allows **higher productivity** with maintained safety compliance.
*(Answer any 2 — 1 + 1 + 1 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Distinguish between a guard and an interlock guard.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Guard | Interlock Guard |
|--------|-------|------------------|
| Function | Physical barrier preventing access | Barrier + control system preventing operation |
| Linkage to machine | None | Linked to power/control circuit |
| Reaction to opening | None (passive) | Machine stops automatically |
| Effectiveness | Depends on operator | Engineering control — enforced |
| Compliance | Meets basic guarding | Meets ISO 12100 / OSHA 1910.212 active guarding |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for distinction — Total 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) Explain in detail the classification of interlock guards based on the energy medium, with one industrial example for each type.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Interlock guards are classified into **four principal types** based on the energy medium that senses guard position and commands the machine control circuit:

1. **Mechanical Interlock Guards** *(2 marks)*
   - **Operating Principle:** Use **physical cam, lever, or latch** arrangements where guard closure physically actuates a switch or valve.
   - **Industrial Example:** Power-press guards in metal stamping industries where the ram cannot descend unless the mechanical latch is engaged.

2. **Electromechanical / Electrical Interlock Guards** *(2 marks)*
   - **Operating Principle:** A **limit switch, proximity sensor, or coded RFID actuator** is wired to a safety relay or safety PLC. On guard closure, the contacts close; on opening, they open, de-energising the contactor coil.
   - **Industrial Example:** CNC machining-centre door interlocks where spindle rotation is interlocked with door status.

3. **Hydraulic / Pneumatic Interlock Guards** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Operating Principle:** Guard closure permits **fluid pressure** to flow to the machine actuator; opening the guard dumps pressure via a **dump valve**, failing safe.
   - **Industrial Example:** Hydraulic press brakes where the ram cannot stroke unless the rear guard is hydraulically interlocked.

4. **Trapped-Key (Captive Key) Interlock Guards** *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Operating Principle:** A key is held captive in one device (e.g., HV isolator). Only by releasing the key and inserting it into another lock (e.g., guard) can the next step proceed.
   - **Industrial Example:** High-voltage switchgear panels in substations to prevent re-energisation while maintenance personnel are inside.

---

**(b) With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of an electromechanical interlock guard system. State its fail-safe design principles.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Electromechanical Interlock System:**

Operator → Guard Door → Position Switch (with Positive-Opening Contact) → Safety Relay Module → Logic Solver → Contactor Coil → Motor Power Supply.

**Working Description:**

1. When the **guard door is closed**, the actuator tongue enters the position switch, closing the normally-open contact and opening the normally-closed contact in a **positive mode**.
2. The signal goes to the **safety relay module**, which checks both channels and permits current flow to the **contactor coil**.
3. The contactor energises, supplying power to the **motor/hazardous element**.
4. When the operator **opens the guard door** during operation, the actuator withdraws → the positive-opening contact **forces open** → the safety relay drops out → the contactor de-energises → the motor stops.
5. **Anti-restart logic** requires the guard to be re-closed **AND** a fresh start command to be issued.

**Fail-Safe Design Principles:** *(3 marks)*
- **De-energise to Trip:** Loss of power → machine stops.
- **Positive Opening:** Contacts separated by direct mechanical force, not spring only.
- **Redundancy:** Two independent switch contacts.
- **Cross-Monitoring:** Safety relay continuously checks channel consistency.
- **Single-Fault Tolerance:** Any single failure must not bypass safety.
- **Proven Components:** Certified to IEC 61508 / ISO 13849-1.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) State and explain the ISO 13849-1 categories of safety-related parts of control systems. How are they applied to interlock guard design?**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

ISO 13849-1 defines **five categories (B, 1, 2, 3, 4)** that describe the **structural requirements** of safety-related parts, based on their **fault tolerance, diagnostics, and redundancy**:

1. **Category B — Basic Safety Principle** *(1 mark)*
   Single-channel design with no redundancy. Only basic, well-tried principles. Acceptable only for **low-risk** interlock guards.

2. **Category 1** *(1 mark)*
   Single-channel with **well-tried and proven components**. Used where mature technology (e.g., positive-opening switches) is sufficient.

3. **Category 2** *(1 mark)*
   Single-channel with **periodic automatic testing** (diagnostics). Failure detected at next test cycle. Suitable for medium-risk interlock guards.

4. **Category 3** *(2 marks)*
   **Dual-channel redundant** system. One fault is **tolerable** without loss of safety function. Most interlock guards for medium-high risk machines use this category.

5. **Category 4** *(2 marks)*
   Dual-channel redundant with **cross-monitoring** and ability to **accumulate faults** without loss of safety. Mandatory for high-risk robot cells and press brakes.

**Application to Interlock Guard Design:**
The category is selected by performing a **risk assessment** using ISO 12100 (severity × frequency × possibility of avoidance). Low-risk guarding → Category B/1; medium → 2/3; high → Category 4 with Performance Level PL d or PL e.

---

**(b) A CNC machining centre has a reaction time of 0.025 s, brake time of 0.150 s, safety relay response of 0.020 s, and intrusion distance of 950 mm. Using ISO 13855, calculate the minimum safety distance for the interlock guard. State the assumption made.**
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the given values:**
- Reaction time: $T_r = 0.025\ \text{s}$
- Brake time: $T_{br} = 0.150\ \text{s}$
- Safety relay response: $T_s = 0.020\ \text{s}$
- Intrusion distance: $C = 950\ \text{mm}$

**Step 2 — Compute total stopping time $T$:**

$$
T = T_r + T_{br} + T_s
$$

$$
T = 0.025 + 0.150 + 0.020 = 0.195\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply ISO 13855 formula:**

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (assuming hand-arm approach $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$):**

$$
S = 1600 \times 0.195 + 950
$$

$$
S = 312 + 950
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1262\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Assumption Made:** *(1 mark)* Hand-arm approach speed $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ per ISO 13855 (since the operator is reaching into the work zone of a CNC machining centre). For whole-body reach, the value would be $K = 2000\ \text{mm/s}$.

**Conclusion:** The interlock guard must be positioned at **minimum 1262 mm** from the nearest hazard point.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the total stopping time components** — Examiner expects you to write $T = T_r + T_{br} + T_s$, NOT just plug a single value.
> 2. **Not stating the assumption for K** — Always state that $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ for hand-arm reach.
> 3. **Confusing mechanical interlocks with electrical ones** in classification questions — list the operating principle for each.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in working questions — at least 2 marks reserved for diagram.
> 5. **No mention of fail-safe principles** when describing interlocks — examiner specifically looks for **positive-opening** and **de-energise-to-trip**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2 / Interlock Guard**

- **Definition:** Interlock guard = movable guard + control linkage such that machine cannot run unless guard is closed.
- **Hierarchy Position:** Engineering control (2nd tier in NEP 2020 hierarchy).
- **Four Types:** Mechanical, Electromechanical, Hydraulic/Pneumatic, Trapped-Key.
- **Three-State Logic:** Guard Open → No Start; Guard Closed + Start → Run; Guard Opened → E-Stop.
- **Fail-Safe Principles:** De-energise-to-trip, positive opening, redundancy, cross-monitoring, single-fault tolerance, proven components.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 12100:2010, ISO 13849-1 (Categories B, 1, 2, 3, 4 and PL a–e), ISO 13855 (Safety Distance), IEC 60947-5-1 (Positive-opening switches), OSHA 29 CFR 1910.212.
- **Safety Distance Formula:** $S = K \cdot T + C$, where $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ for hand-arm.
- **Total Stopping Time:** $T = T_r + T_{br} + T_s$.
- **Performance Level Mapping:** $PFH_d < 10^{-8}$ → PL e (highest), $\geq 10^{-5}$ → PL a (lowest).
- **Anti-Restart:** Guard must be re-closed **AND** fresh start command issued.
- **Industrial Applications:** Power presses, CNC machining centres, robotic cells, HV switchgear, hydraulic press brakes, conveyor systems.
- **Examiner's Pet Question:** *"Distinguish guard vs interlock guard"* and *"Calculate safety distance using ISO 13855."*
- **Remember the abbreviation LOTO** (Lockout Tagout) — directly linked to trapped-key interlock philosophy.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Guard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automatic Guard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Automatic Guard

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PESFT864 — Module 2):** *Automatic Guards — Types, Operating Principles, Selection Criteria, Maintenance & Testing, Standards (ISO 13849, ISO 13855, EN 60204-1).*

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Guard** is a *self-actuating protective device* that, without the need for continuous manual intervention by an operator, detects an unsafe condition or the intrusion of a person into a hazard zone and automatically:

1. **Initiates a stop command** to the machine's prime mover (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or mechanical), AND
2. **Prevents machine restart** until the hazard has been safely cleared and the protective state is re-established.

According to **ISO 12100:2010** (Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction), an automatic guard is a *"guard whose moving parts are not linked to the moving parts of the associated machinery; the functioning of which depends on an external event such as the presence or position of a person, or an applied force."*

In KTU valuation terms, examiners expect the answer to contain the three keywords: **self-actuating, hazard detection, fail-safe / positive-break.**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Automatic Sliding Gate at a Level Crossing

Imagine the automatic barrier at a **railway level crossing**:

- A train (the *hazard source*) approaches.
- A trackside sensor (the *trip mechanism*) detects the train.
- The barrier lowers automatically — no human closes it manually.
- Even if power fails, the barrier stays closed (by gravity / spring — *fail-safe*).
- The barrier cannot be lifted until the train fully passes AND the system is reset (positive *re-locking*).

An **automatic guard in a machine** behaves exactly the same way. The "train" is the **rotating blade / press ram / conveyor nip point**; the "sensor" is a **light curtain, pressure mat, or interlock switch**; the "barrier" is the **stop signal sent to the machine's controller**.

### 1.3 Standard Safety Metrics Used in KTU Answers

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Performance Level | **PL** | ISO 13849-1 discrete level (a, b, c, d, e) |
| Safety Integrity Level | **SIL** | IEC 62061 discrete level (1, 2, 3) |
| Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | **MTTFd** | ISO 13849-1 (years) |
| Diagnostic Coverage | **DC** | ISO 13849-1 (%) |
| Category | **Cat.** | ISO 13849-1 structural requirement (B, 1, 2, 3, 4) |
| Probability of Failure per Hour | **PFHd** | ISO 13849-1 (1/h) |
| Safety Distance | **S** | ISO 13855 (mm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Always quote the relevant ISO/EN standard number alongside the definition. A bare definition without a standard reference usually attracts only **½ to 1 mark** out of the allocated marks in Part A (3 marks).

### 1.4 Primary Classification of Automatic Guards

```text
Automatic Guards
├── 1. Mechanical Trip Guards
│      ├── Trip wires / cables
│      ├── Pressure-sensitive mats
│      └── Mechanical interlocks (hinged, sliding, lift-off)
├── 2. Electromechanical Guards
│      ├── Limit-switch interlocks
│      └── Solenoid-locked guards (with / without delay)
├── 3. Electronic / Electro-Sensitive Guards
│      ├── Light Curtains (AOPD – Active Opto-electronic Protective Device)
│      ├── Laser Scanners
│      ├── Safety Mats (pressure-sensitive, electronic)
│      └── Safety Edges / Bumpers
├── 4. Two-Hand Control Devices
│      ├── Type I (synchronous, anti-repeat)
│      └── Type III (synchronous + anti-repeat + concurrent)
└── 5. Hydraulic / Pneumatic Guards
       ├── Pressure-relief valves
       └── Pilot-operated check valves
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Of the above, **AOPD (Light Curtains)** and **Interlocked Guards with Guard Locking** are the most frequently tested in KTU university papers because they combine *electronics + mechanics + standards*.

### 1.5 Why Automatic Guards? — The Hierarchy of Controls

Per **ISO 12100:2010**, the designer must apply controls in this strict order:

$$H_{12100} = \text{Design Out} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Safeguarding} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Information for Use}$$

Automatic guards sit in the **second layer (Safeguarding)** — they protect the worker when the hazard *cannot be designed out* of the machine. They are a **passive protective measure from the operator's perspective** but an **active protective measure from the system's perspective**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of an Automatic Guard — The Six-Stage Logic

Every automatic guard, regardless of physical type, executes the same **6-stage operational logic** (this is a board-exam favourite as a "list and explain" 7-marker):

| Stage | Action | Failure Consequence if Skipped |
|---|---|---|
| **S1: Detection** | Sensor perceives the unsafe event (light beam broken, mat compressed, switch actuated). | Hazard undetected → guard useless. |
| **S2: Signal Conversion** | Physical event → Electrical / Hydraulic / Pneumatic signal. | No communication to controller. |
| **S3: Logic Evaluation** | Safety relay / PLC / Safety PLC checks if signal is valid (cross-monitoring, dual-channel). | Single-point failure may allow restart. |
| **S4: Stop Command** | Output relay de-energises → contactor drops → power cut to motor. | Machine continues → injury. |
| **S5: Holding** | Guard remains latched in safe state (mechanical spring, gravity, magnetic lock). | Guard could be defeated. |
| **S6: Reset** | Operator must perform deliberate, intentional reset (pushbutton, key switch). | Unexpected restart → secondary injury. |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Valuation Trick:** Examiners often award a **bonus ½ mark** if the student draws this table inside a labelled block diagram rather than as plain prose — *visual presentation = +½ to +1 mark* on 7-mark sub-questions.

## 2.2 ISO 13849-1 Performance Level (PL) — The Most Tested Framework

The **Performance Level (PL)** is a discrete level (a, b, c, d, e) used to specify the **reliability of a safety-related control function** of a guard. It is determined by **three parameters**:

$$\boxed{\;PL \;=\; f(\,Category,\; MTTF_d,\; DC_{avg}\,)\;}$$

### 2.2.1 The Three Determinants

| Parameter | Definition | KTU Shortcut |
|---|---|---|
| **Category (B, 1, 2, 3, 4)** | *Structural requirement* — resistance to faults and the behaviour under fault condition. | Cat-B < Cat-1 < Cat-2 < Cat-3 < Cat-4 |
| **MTTFd** | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure of each channel (Low ≤ 3 yrs · Medium = 3–10 yrs · High = 10–30 yrs). | "LMH" — Low, Medium, High |
| **DCavg** | Diagnostic Coverage average across all dangerous failures (None · Low ≥ 60% · Medium ≥ 90% · High ≥ 99%). | "NLH" — None, Low, Medium, High |

### 2.2.2 The PL Determination Grid (per ISO 13849-1:2023 Table 5)

| Category | MTTFd (each channel) | DCavg | → PL |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **B** | Low | None | **a** |
| **B** | Medium | None | **b** |
| **1** | High | None | **c** |
| **2** | Low | Low | **a** |
| **2** | Medium | Low | **b** |
| **2** | High | Low | **c** |
| **2** | Medium | Medium | **c** |
| **2** | High | Medium | **d** |
| **3** | Low | Low | **b** |
| **3** | Medium | Low | **c** |
| **3** | High | Low | **d** |
| **3** | Low | Medium | **b** |
| **3** | Medium | Medium | **d** |
| **4** | High | High | **e** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap Question:** *"A guard is built to Category 3 with MTTFd = High and DCavg = Low. What is its PL?"* The correct answer is **PL d**, **NOT** PL e. Students who skip the grid and write "Cat-3 = PL d" lose 1 mark.

## 2.3 The Safety Distance Equation — ISO 13855:2024

The **physical placement** of an automatic guard (especially light curtains, two-hand controls, and mats) is governed by the **safety distance** $S$:

$$\boxed{\;S \;=\; K \cdot T \;+\; C\;}$$

Where:

- $S$ = minimum safety distance from the hazard point to the detection plane (mm)
- $K$ = approach speed of the body / hand (mm/s) — typically $K = 1600 \,\text{mm/s}$ for walking reach, $K = 2000 \,\text{mm/s}$ for hand reach (per ISO 13855)
- $T$ = total stopping time of the machine (s) — **measured**, not assumed
- $C$ = intrusion distance before the guard can detect (mm)

### 2.3.1 Refined Form for Light Curtains (Vertical Sensing, ISO 13855)

$$S_{LC} \;=\; K \cdot (T_m + T_s) \;+\; C_{RO}$$

- $T_m$ = machine stopping time (s)
- $T_s$ = response time of the protective device itself (s)
- $C_{RO}$ = additional distance due to reach-over (mm), dependent on the height of the light curtain above the floor.

For reach-over situations, **C_RO** is given by ISO 13855 Table 1:

$$C_{RO} \;=\; \min\!\Big(\;1200,\; H - 15 \cdot d_{RO}\;\Big)$$

Where $H$ = height of the top beam and $d_{RO}$ = depth of reach-over.

### 2.3.2 Refined Form for Pressure-Sensitive Mats (Horizontal Sensing)

$$S_{mat} \;=\; K \cdot T \;+\; 1200\;\text{mm}$$

The constant **1200 mm** accounts for the length of a foot-step onto the mat.

## 2.4 The Complete KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this table in the exam:** Memorise the boxed (primary) equations. The unboxed relations are background derivations — quote, do not derive.

| # | Equation | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $\;S = K \cdot T + C\;$ | General safety distance (ISO 13855) | mm |
| **F2** | $\;S_{LC} = K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + C_{RO}\;$ | Light-curtain placement | mm |
| **F3** | $\;S_{mat} = K \cdot T + 1200\;$ | Pressure mat placement | mm |
| **F4** | $\;S_{THC} = K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + 250\;$ | Two-hand control placement | mm |
| **F5** | $\;C_{RO} = 1200 \;(\text{if } H \geq 1000)\;$ | Reach-over, top beam ≥ 1 m | mm |
| **F6** | $\;C_{RO} = 850 \;(\text{if } 900 \leq H < 1000)\;$ | Mid-height reach-over | mm |
| **F7** | $\;C_{RO} = 650 \;(\text{if } H < 900)\;$ | Low-height reach-over | mm |
| **F8** | $\;MTTF_d = \frac{1}{\lambda_d}\;$ | Reliability per channel | years |
| **F9** | $\;PFH_d = \sum_{i} \lambda_{d,i}\;$ | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | 1/h |
| **F10** | $\;DC = \frac{\lambda_{dd}}{\lambda_d} \times 100\%\;$ | Diagnostic Coverage | % |
| **F11** | $\;PL \leftrightarrow PFH_d\;$ mapping (see §2.5) | PL ↔ failure budget | — |

### 2.5 PL ↔ PFHd Mapping (Memorise This!)

| Performance Level | PFHd (1/h) — Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour |
|:---:|:---:|
| **a** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ |
| **b** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ |
| **c** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ |
| **d** | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ |
| **e** | $< 10^{-8}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "PL a is safer than PL e." It is the **opposite** — PL e is the **highest** integrity level, equivalent to SIL 3 in IEC 62061 terms.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Automatic Guard Application | Standard Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive Press Shops** | Light curtains + safety PLC for mechanical power presses | ISO 13849 PL e / Cat 4 |
| **Pharmaceutical Tablet Presses** | Interlocked guard doors with guard locking | EN 60204-1 §9.3 |
| **Robotic Welding Cells** | Laser area scanners + safety mats | ISO 10218-1 (Industrial robots) |
| **Woodworking Machinery** | Brake-band guards, kickback fingers | EN 1870 series |
| **Printing Presses** | Two-hand control + reach guards | ISO 12621 |
| **CNC Machining** | Sliding interlocked doors with magnetic-coded sensors | ISO 16090-1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Insight for KTU Viva:** When asked *"Give a real-world example of an automatic guard,"* the strongest answer is *"Light curtain on a hydraulic press brake with Category 4 / PL d architecture for finger protection."* It instantly signals command of both the theory (PL, Cat) and the practice (press brake = highest-finger-injury-risk machine).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation D1 — Safety Distance for a Vertical Light Curtain (Reach-Over Case)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **hydraulic press brake** uses a vertical **light curtain (AOPD, Type 4)** for finger protection. The following data are measured:

- Machine stopping time, $T_m = 90 \,\text{ms} = 0.090 \,\text{s}$
- Safety relay response time, $T_s = 20 \,\text{ms} = 0.020 \,\text{s}$
- Height of the topmost beam above the floor, $H = 1100 \,\text{mm}$
- Approach speed of the hand, $K = 2000 \,\text{mm/s}$ (hand reach, ISO 13855)
- Reach-over depth, $d_{RO} = 700 \,\text{mm}$

Find the **minimum safety distance** $S_{LC}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

For a vertical AOPD, ISO 13855 specifies:

$$S_{LC} \;=\; K \cdot (T_m + T_s) \;+\; C_{RO}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the stopping times.**

$$T_m + T_s \;=\; 0.090 + 0.020 \;=\; 0.110 \,\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reach-over intrusion $C_{RO}$.**

Using the ISO 13855 Table 1 formula:

$$C_{RO} \;=\; 1200 - (1200 - H) \cdot \frac{d_{RO}}{1000}$$

But the simpler tabular lookup: for $H = 1100 \,\text{mm}$, the row gives **$C_{RO} = 850 \,\text{mm}$** when $d_{RO} \leq 1000 \,\text{mm}$.

$$C_{RO} \;=\; 850 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into $S_{LC}$.**

$$S_{LC} \;=\; (2000) \cdot (0.110) + 850$$

$$S_{LC} \;=\; 220 + 850$$

$$\boxed{\;S_{LC} \;=\; 1070 \,\text{mm}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The light-curtain's **detection plane must be at least 1070 mm away** from the hazard point (ram edge). If mounted closer, an operator's hand could reach the pinch point *before* the press has fully stopped.

### 3.1.3 Verification — Check Against ISO 13855 Boundary Conditions

ISO 13855 states the **minimum** $S_{LC}$ is **100 mm** even if the formula yields a smaller value, and the formula is valid only when $S_{LC} \geq 500 \,\text{mm}$. Since:

$$S_{LC} = 1070 \,\text{mm} \;\geq\; 500 \,\text{mm} \quad ✓$$

the equation is valid and no adjustment is needed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation D2 — MTTFd of a Dual-Channel Automatic Guard

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

An interlocked guard door uses **two independent position switches** (Channel A and Channel B) wired to a Cat-3 safety relay.

- Channel A: failure rate, $\lambda_{dA} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{per hour}$
- Channel B: failure rate, $\lambda_{dB} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{per hour}$

Compute the **MTTFd of the dual-channel assembly** and the **PFHd**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Compute the combined dangerous failure rate (sum, since channels are in series for safety).**

$$\lambda_d^{combined} \;=\; \lambda_{dA} + \lambda_{dB} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-6} + 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MTTFd in years.**

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_d^{combined}} \;=\; \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}}\;\text{h} \;=\; 285714.29\;\text{h}$$

Convert hours to years (1 year = 8760 hours):

$$MTTF_d \;=\; \frac{285714.29}{8760}\;\text{years} \;\approx\; 32.62\;\text{years}$$

**Step 3 — Classify MTTFd per ISO 13849-1.**

- Low: $\leq 3$ years
- Medium: $3 < MTTF_d \leq 10$ years
- High: $10 < MTTF_d \leq 30$ years

Since $MTTF_d = 32.62$ years, this **exceeds the "High" band (max 30 years)**. Per ISO 13849-1 §4.5.4, when MTTFd for each channel is greater than 30 years, it is **capped at "High"** for the purpose of PL determination (the formula contribution saturates at 30 years).

$$\boxed{\;MTTF_d^{each\ channel} \;\rightarrow\; \text{High (capped at 30 yrs)}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compute PFHd.**

For a Cat-3 dual-channel system with DCavg = 60% (typical):

$$PFH_d \;\approx\; 2 \cdot \lambda_d^{each} \cdot (1 - DC_{avg}) \cdot T_{proof}$$

For this exercise, the simplified form per ISO 13849-1 gives:

$$PFH_d \;\approx\; \lambda_d^{combined} \;\approx\; 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{h}^{-1} \;=\; 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\;/\text{h}$$

This corresponds to **PL b** per the mapping in §2.5. If Category is upgraded to **Cat 4** with DCavg = High, the same hardware would deliver **PL e**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Which Guard for Which Hazard?

| Hazard Type | Energy Source | Recommended Automatic Guard | ISO 13849 Target PL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pinch point on power press** | Mechanical (kinetic) | Vertical light curtain + Cat-4 safety PLC | **PL e** |
| **Rotating shaft entanglement** | Mechanical (rotational) | Fixed interlocked guard + guard locking | **PL d** |
| **Laser cutting beam exposure** | Optical (Class 4 laser) | Interlocked enclosure door + guard locking | **PL d** |
| **Robotic arm sweep zone** | Electro-mechanical | Horizontal laser scanner + muting function | **PL d** |
| **Stored hydraulic energy release** | Hydraulic | Pilot-operated check valve + pressure switch | **PL c** |
| **Hot die burn (injection moulding)** | Thermal + Mechanical | Two-hand control + guard door interlock | **PL c** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation D3 — Selection of Approach Speed $K$

The choice of $K$ is **not arbitrary** — it is tied to the **reach geometry**:

$$K \;=\; \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 1600\;\text{mm/s}, & \text{walking reach (whole body), ISO 13855 Table A.1} \\[4pt] 2000\;\text{mm/s}, & \text{hand reach from above (top-down), ISO 13855 Table A.2} \\[4pt] 2500\;\text{mm/s}, & \text{leg reach (knee-down), Annex B} \end{array} \right.$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "1-6-2-0-2-5":** "**16-20-25**" — *Walk 1.6, Hand 2.0, Leg 2.5 m/s.* This single number triplet fetches a quick 1 mark on safety-distance problems.

### 3.4.1 Worked Variant — Horizontal Mat on a Conveyor

A conveyor has $T_m = 1.2 \,\text{s}$. A horizontal pressure mat is installed **around** the tail pulley (a nip point). Approach speed $K = 1600 \,\text{mm/s}$.

$$S_{mat} \;=\; K \cdot T_m + 1200$$

$$S_{mat} \;=\; 1600 \cdot 1.2 + 1200 \;=\; 1920 + 1200 \;=\; 3120 \,\text{mm}$$

So the mat's **inner edge** must start at least **3.12 m** from the nip point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automatic Guard System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HAZ[HAZARD SOURCE\nMachine / Press / Robot] -->|Mechanical motion| DANGER[DANGER ZONE\nPinch, Crush, Shear]
    SENSOR1[DETECTION ELEMENT\nLight Curtain / Mat / Switch] -->|Analog / Digital signal| LOGIC
    SENSOR2[SECONDARY CHANNEL\nRedundant Sensor] -->|Cross-check signal| LOGIC
    LOGIC[SAFETY LOGIC UNIT\nSafety Relay / Safety PLC] -->|Stop pulse| OUTPUT
    OUTPUT[OUTPUT ELEMENT\nContactor / Hydraulic Valve] -->|Power cut| HAZ
    LOGIC -->|Status feedback| HMI[OPERATOR HMI\nStatus LEDs / Alarm]
    OUTPUT -->|Confirmed stop| RESET[MANUAL RESET\nPushbutton / Key-switch]
    RESET -->|Intentional restart| LOGIC
    classDef hazard fill:#ffd5d5,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#d5f0d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#d5e0f0,stroke:#1f3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class HAZ,DANGER hazard
    class LOGIC,OUTPUT,SENSOR1,SENSOR2 logic
    class HMI,RESET safe
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two **parallel sensor channels** flowing into the **Safety Logic Unit** represent the **dual-channel / Category-3-or-4 architecture** mandated by ISO 13849 for high-integrity guards. The **manual reset** is intentionally placed *after* the stop confirmation — this enforces the **deliberate-recovery principle** and prevents automatic restart.

---

## 4.2 Classification Flow Diagram — Selecting the Correct Automatic Guard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Hazard Identified] --> Q1{Is the hazard\naccessible during\nnormal operation?}
    Q1 -- No --> NO_GUARD[No Guard Required\n(Task analysis only)]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can the hazard be\ndesigned out?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> DESIGN[Design Out\n(ISO 12100 Layer 1)]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{How often does\noperator enter\nhazard zone?}
    Q3 -- Rarely --> MECH[Mechanical Trip Guard\nInterlock Switch]
    Q3 -- Frequently --> Q4{What is the\nrequired access\nfrequency pattern?}
    Q4 -- Frequent / repetitive --> LC[Light Curtain / AOPD\nVertical Sensing]
    Q4 -- Floor-level reach --> MAT[Pressure-Sensitive Mat\nHorizontal Sensing]
    Q4 -- Both hands needed --> THC[Two-Hand Control\nType III]
    Q4 -- Guard cannot open\nuntil machine stops --> LOCK[Guard with Guard Locking\nSolenoid-locked interlock]
    LC --> PL[Determine PL per\nISO 13849-1 Grid]
    MAT --> PL
    THC --> PL
    LOCK --> PL
    MECH --> PL
    PL --> SD[Compute Safety\nDistance S per\nISO 13855]
    SD --> INSTALL[Install + Validate\n+ Train Operators]
    classDef dec fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#d5f0d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef def fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 dec
    class NO_GUARD,DESIGN,INSTALL endNode
    class MECH,LC,MAT,THC,LOCK,SD,PL def
```

---

## 4.3 State Machine — Automatic Guard Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MACHINE_RUN
    MACHINE_RUN --> INTRUSION_DETECTED : Hazard enters zone
    INTRUSION_DETECTED --> STOP_COMMAND_ISSUED : T_s response time
    STOP_COMMAND_ISSUED --> MACHINE_STOPPED : T_m stopping time
    MACHINE_STOPPED --> HAZARD_CLEARED : Operator withdraws
    HAZARD_CLEARED --> RESET_PRESSED : Operator pushes reset
    RESET_PRESSED --> MACHINE_RUN : Safety PLC confirms clear
    MACHINE_STOPPED --> EMERGENCY_HOLD : If fault detected
    EMERGENCY_HOLD --> MAINTENANCE : Lockout-tagout
    MAINTENANCE --> RESET_PRESSED : After repair + test
    MACHINE_RUN --> EMERGENCY_HOLD : Emergency stop pressed
    note right of MACHINE_STOPPED : Time spent here is governed\nby T_m, the measured\nmachine stopping time.
    note right of INTRUSION_DETECTED : T_s is the response time\nof the safety device\nitself (typically < 20 ms).
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Explain the working of an automatic guard with a diagram,"* this **state-machine diagram** scores full marks if you also **label the two critical timing parameters** ($T_s$ and $T_m$) on the transitions. A diagram without timing labels = 3 marks out of 7; with timing labels = 6–7 marks.

---

## 4.4 Sub-Architecture — Dual-Channel Cat-3 Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INPUT_A[Input Channel A\nSwitch Contact 1] --> RELAY_A[Safety Relay\nChannel A Coil]
    INPUT_B[Input Channel B\nSwitch Contact 2] --> RELAY_B[Safety Relay\nChannel B Coil]
    RELAY_A --> XOR{Cross-Monitor\nXOR Logic}
    RELAY_B --> XOR
    XOR -->|Both OK| OUT[Output Contactors\nK1 + K2 in series]
    XOR -->|Disagreement| FAULT[FAULT LED\nLatch + Disable]
    OUT --> MOTOR[Machine Motor\nPower Supply]
    FAULT --> MAINT[Manual Reset\nRequired]
    MAINT --> OUT
    classDef ok fill:#d5f0d5,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#ffd5d5,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#d5e0f0,stroke:#1f3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class INPUT_A,INPUT_B,RELAY_A,RELAY_B,XOR,OUT,MOTOR io
    class MAINT ok
    class FAULT err
```

**Reading aid:** The **XOR cross-monitor** detects any single-channel fault (e.g., one contact welded closed) and forces the system into the **FAULT state**, satisfying ISO 13849-1 Category 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define an automatic guard. List any four types with one-line applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1½ marks):** An automatic guard is a *self-actuating protective device* (per ISO 12100:2010) that detects an unsafe condition or the intrusion of a person into a hazard zone and automatically initiates a machine stop, preventing restart until the hazard is cleared — all without continuous operator intervention.

> **Four types (1½ marks — 0.4 each, any four):**
>
> 1. **Light Curtain (AOPD)** — Vertical sensing on power presses and robotic cells.
> 2. **Pressure-sensitive mat** — Floor-level access on conveyors and palletisers.
> 3. **Interlocked guard door** — Sliding/hinged doors on enclosures (lathes, CNC mills).
> 4. **Two-hand control** — Press brakes, injection moulding.
> 5. **Laser scanner** — Mobile platforms and AGVs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scoring Note:** A bare list without the definition → max 2/3. Definition without ISO reference → 2½/3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**State the safety distance equation per ISO 13855 and explain each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{\;S = K \cdot T + C\;}$$

- **$S$** = minimum safety distance from hazard point to detection plane (mm).
- **$K$** = approach speed of the operator's hand/body (mm/s) — $1600$ for walk, $2000$ for hand reach.
- **$T$** = total stopping time of the system (s) — sum of machine stopping time $T_m$ and safety device response time $T_s$.
- **$C$** = intrusion distance — the additional distance a person travels before the protective device physically prevents further approach.

> [!TIP]
> **Units Earn Marks:** Mentioning **mm/s** and **s** explicitly fetches an extra ½ mark because students commonly write "K = 2000" without units.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU ESE pattern:** Module 2 carries **one 14-mark question**, with internal choice. Each 14-marker has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, usually 7 + 7 marks.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]*

**A.** *(a)* Explain the **operating principle of an automatic guard** with a labelled block diagram. Discuss the **six stages** from hazard detection to manual reset. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A **power-press brake** is fitted with a **Category-4, Type-4 vertical light curtain**. The following data are given:

- $T_m = 110 \,\text{ms}$, $T_s = 15 \,\text{ms}$
- Top beam height $H = 1200 \,\text{mm}$
- Hand reach approach speed $K = 2000 \,\text{mm/s}$

Compute the **safety distance** $S_{LC}$ and comment on whether the design is compliant with ISO 13855. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Introduction (2 marks):**

An automatic guard protects the operator without requiring continuous manual action. Its operation proceeds through six well-defined stages, illustrated in the diagram below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Stage 1\nDETECTION] --> A2[Stage 2\nSIGNAL CONVERSION]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3\nLOGIC EVALUATION]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4\nSTOP COMMAND]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5\nHOLDING]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6\nDELIBERATE RESET]
    A6 --> A1
    classDef node fill:#d5e0f0,stroke:#1f3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 node
```

**Explanation of the six stages (5 marks — 1 each):**

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Detection** | A sensor (photo-electric, pressure, mechanical) perceives the unsafe event. |
| **2. Signal Conversion** | Physical event converted to electrical / hydraulic / pneumatic signal. |
| **3. Logic Evaluation** | Safety relay / PLC cross-checks both channels (dual-channel architecture). |
| **4. Stop Command** | Output relay de-energises; contactor drops; motor power cut. |
| **5. Holding** | Guard remains in safe state via spring, gravity, or magnetic lock. |
| **6. Deliberate Reset** | Operator performs explicit manual reset before restart. |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** Any failure in any stage must result in a **safe state** — this is the **fail-safe principle**.

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Given:** $T_m = 110 \,\text{ms} = 0.110\,\text{s}$; $T_s = 15 \,\text{ms} = 0.015\,\text{s}$; $H = 1200 \,\text{mm}$; $K = 2000\,\text{mm/s}$.

**Step 1 — Governing equation.**

$$S_{LC} \;=\; K \cdot (T_m + T_s) \;+\; C_{RO}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the stopping times.**

$$T_m + T_s \;=\; 0.110 + 0.015 \;=\; 0.125\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Look up $C_{RO}$.** Since $H = 1200\,\text{mm} \geq 1000\,\text{mm}$, ISO 13855 Table 1 gives:

$$C_{RO} \;=\; 850 \,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_{LC}$.**

$$S_{LC} \;=\; (2000) \cdot (0.125) + 850 \;=\; 250 + 850 \;=\; \boxed{1100 \,\text{mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Compliance check.**

- Minimum ISO 13855 floor: $S_{LC} \geq 500\,\text{mm}$. Here $S_{LC} = 1100 > 500$ ✓
- Validity range: formula valid for $S_{LC} \geq 500\,\text{mm}$ ✓
- Therefore, **the design is compliant with ISO 13855**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common error 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to add $T_s$ to $T_m$. Many students write $S = 2000 \times 0.110 + 850 = 1070$ mm — correct $T$-sum is **0.125 s**, not 0.110 s.
> 2. **Common error 2 (–1 mark):** Using $K = 1600\,\text{mm/s}$ for a *hand-reach* scenario. The 1600 value is only for *walking reach*.
> 3. **Common error 3 (–1 mark):** Failing to verify the validity condition $S \geq 500$ mm. Always end with a compliance statement.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**B.** *(a)* Describe the **Performance Level (PL)** concept as per ISO 13849-1. With the help of a table, explain how the **PL is determined from Category, MTTFd and DCavg**. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* An automatic guard uses **two electromechanical switches** in series, each with $\lambda_d = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{h}^{-1}$, monitored by a safety relay with **DCavg = 90%**. Determine the **MTTFd**, classify it per ISO 13849-1, and find the **achieved PL** assuming **Category 2** architecture. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Concept (3 marks):**

The **Performance Level (PL)** is a discrete level (a, b, c, d, e) specified per **ISO 13849-1** to quantify the reliability of a safety-related control function of a guard under reasonably foreseeable conditions. It depends on three structural/reliability parameters: **Category**, **MTTFd**, and **DCavg**.

**PL Determination Table (4 marks):**

| Category | MTTFd (each ch.) | DCavg | PL |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| B | Low | None | a |
| B | Medium | None | b |
| 1 | High | None | c |
| 2 | Low | Low | a |
| 2 | Medium | Low | b |
| **2** | **High** | **Low** | **c** |
| 2 | Medium | Medium | c |
| **2** | **High** | **Medium** | **d** |
| 3 | Low | Low | b |
| 3 | Medium | Low | c |
| 3 | High | Low | d |
| 3 | Low | Medium | b |
| **3** | **Medium** | **Medium** | **d** |
| 4 | High | High | e |

(Three rows emphasised because they are the *most commonly tested* cells.)

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Step 1 — Combined failure rate.**

$$\lambda_d^{combined} \;=\; 2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — MTTFd (per channel).**

$$MTTF_d^{each} \;=\; \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8760}\,\text{years} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.01752}\;\text{years} \;\approx\; 57.08\;\text{years}$$

Per ISO 13849-1 §4.5.4, this is **capped at High (30 years)**.

**Step 3 — Classify MTTFd per channel.** **High** (10 < MTTFd ≤ 30).

**Step 4 — DCavg classification.** 90% → **Medium** (60 ≤ DC < 90? Actually 60–90 = Medium; 90–99 = Medium-High). The 2023 standard clarifies **DC ≥ 90% = Medium**.

**Step 5 — Look up PL.** Category 2 + MTTFd = High + DCavg = Medium ⇒ **PL c**.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Achieved PL} = \mathbf{c}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse "DC = 90%" as **High** (it is Medium per ISO 13849-1:2023). Reading the **wrong row** of the table changes the answer from PL c to PL b — a **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Automatic guard = *self-actuating protective device* per **ISO 12100:2010**.
- [ ] **Six operational stages:** Detect → Convert → Logic → Stop → Hold → Deliberate Reset.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of controls (ISO 12100):** Design out → Safeguard → Information.
- [ ] **Primary safety-distance formula:** $S = K \cdot T + C$ (**ISO 13855**).
- [ ] **Approach speeds to remember:** Walk = **1600 mm/s**; Hand = **2000 mm/s**; Leg = **2500 mm/s**.
- [ ] **Light curtain variant:** $S_{LC} = K(T_m + T_s) + C_{RO}$.
- [ ] **Pressure mat variant:** $S_{mat} = K \cdot T + 1200$ mm.
- [ ] **Performance Level (PL) ladder:** a < b < c < d < e (PL e is the **highest**).
- [ ] **PL ↔ PFHd boundary values:** a=10⁻⁵, b=10⁻⁶, c=10⁻⁷, d=10⁻⁸, e<10⁻⁸ (per hour).
- [ ] **PL is a function of:** Category + MTTFd + DCavg — *not a single number*.
- [ ] **MTTFd bands:** Low ≤ 3 yrs; Medium 3–10 yrs; High 10–30 yrs (capped).
- [ ] **DCavg bands:** None < 60%; Low 60–90%; Medium 90–99%; High ≥ 99%.
- [ ] **MTTFd of a channel = 1 / $\lambda_d$**, where $\lambda_d$ is the dangerous failure rate.
- [ ] **Dual-channel guards:** Total $\lambda_d$ = sum of individual $\lambda_d$ values.
- [ ] **Category 4 + High DC + High MTTFd = PL e** (gold standard for press brakes, robots).
- [ ] **Validity condition for $S_{LC}$:** $S_{LC} \geq 500$ mm (always check and declare).
- [ ] **Fail-safe principle:** *Any single fault must lead to a safe state.*
- [ ] **Deliberate reset:** *Auto-restart after guard-clear is forbidden* by EN 60204-1.
- [ ] **Two-hand control placement:** $S_{THC} = K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + 250$ mm.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite quote:** *"According to ISO 13849-1..."* — always cite the standard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trip Guard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trip Guard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Trip Guard** is a category of *interlocking protective device* used in industrial machinery safety that **immediately and automatically de-energizes or disengages the hazardous motion of a machine** the moment an operator's body, clothing, or a foreign object crosses a pre-defined safety boundary or makes contact with a sensing element.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PESFT864 – Module 2: Machine Guarding & Control Devices), a trip guard is formally classified as a **"presence-sensing/motion-tripping safety device"** designed to convert hazardous machine energy into a safe state within a calculated **stop time** that is shorter than the human reaction time plus reach distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *"A Trip Guard is a protective mechanism that instantaneously trips (stops/cuts power to) a machine when an operator's hand, limb, or body part encroaches into the danger zone during a machine cycle, thereby preventing injury by ensuring the machine reaches a safe (stopped) state before the operator can be harmed."*

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Response Time** | **≤ 0.025 seconds (25 ms)** for high-hazard machinery | Time between trip actuation and power cutoff initiation |
| **Stopping Time (T)** | **0.5 – 1.5 seconds** (machine-dependent) | Time from cutoff signal to complete cessation of motion |
| **Safety Distance (Ds)** | **≥ 1.25 m** (typical OSHA minimum) | Minimum allowed operator reach beyond trip device |
| **Operating Force** | **≤ 5 Newtons** (light-touch) | Force needed to actuate wire/rod sensing element |
| **Reliability (B10d)** | **≥ 1,00,000 cycles** | Mean cycles before dangerous failure |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Imagine you are crossing a road at a railway crossing.** You cannot see the train approaching, but there is a **trip wire stretched across the track**. The instant your foot touches the wire, an alarm rings, the signal lights flash red, and the train brakes are automatically applied within milliseconds — long before the train can reach you.

In a factory setting, the **trip guard is exactly that trip wire**, but instead of a foot, it senses a **worker's hand reaching into a press die, a conveyor nip point, or a rolling mill roller**. The moment the hand crosses the safety plane, the machine receives an electrical "stop" command faster than the human hand can continue moving into the danger zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Logic (Single-Line Summary for Viva):**
> *Trip Guard = Sensing Element + Signal Transmission + Power Cutoff + Brake Engagement → Machine reaches a safe (stopped) state before the operator's body reaches the hazard point.*

### 1.4 Why Trip Guards are Critically Important in the KTU Context

- **Reduction of Operator Dependency**: Unlike fixed guards (which depend on the operator never reaching in), trip guards actively intervene when human behavior deviates from safety procedure.
- **Compliance with Statutory Requirements**: The Factories Act, 1948 (Section 21 & 22), Indian Standards IS 947, IS 12673, and international standards ANSI B11.1, OSHA 1910.217, and ISO 12100 mandate trip devices for specific high-risk machines.
- **Cost-Effective Engineering Control**: Listed in the **Hierarchy of Controls** above PPE and administrative controls — they are a hardware-engineered solution, not merely a behavioral instruction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Theory — The "Why" and "How" Behind Every Step

A trip guard operates on a **closed-loop fail-safe principle** with four sequential functional stages:

**Step 1 — Sensing Stage (Detection Layer)**
- A sensing element (wire, rod, light curtain, pressure mat, capacitive plate) defines the *trip plane* — an invisible boundary between the safe zone and the danger zone.
- When the operator's hand, arm, or tool crosses this plane, the sensing element is mechanically or electro-optically disturbed.
- *Why this works:* The trip plane is positioned at a calculated safety distance outside the physical hazard, giving the machine time to stop before the hand arrives at the danger point.

**Step 2 — Signal Conversion Stage (Interface Layer)**
- The mechanical disturbance (e.g., wire tension change) is converted into an electrical signal via a **limit switch, microswitch, or photoelectric sensor**.
- This signal is routed through a **Safety Relay (Category 3 or 4 architecture as per ISO 13849-1)** that performs self-diagnostic checks for redundancy.

**Step 3 — Logic & Verification Stage (Control Layer)**
- The safety relay verifies the signal using dual-channel redundancy (two independent contacts that must agree).
- If valid, a stop command is sent to the machine's main contactor or motor drive.
- *How this prevents failure:* Even if one channel fails (contact welded, wire broken), the second channel independently trips the machine — this is the principle of **redundancy with cross-monitoring**.

**Step 4 — Power Cutoff & Braking Stage (Actuation Layer)**
- The main contactor disconnects power to the motor; simultaneously, a mechanical or electrical brake engages.
- The machine decelerates and stops within its **measured stopping time (T)**.
- *Why brakes are necessary:* Simply cutting power to a flywheel or large inertia machine does not stop it fast enough; active braking is mandatory.

### 2.2 Classification of Trip Guards (Exam-Relevant Taxonomy)

| Type | Sensing Method | Best Application | Typical Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Trip Wire Guard** | Tensioned wire + limit switch | Power presses, rolling mills, conveyors | 0.05 – 0.10 s |
| **Mechanical Trip Rod Guard** | Rigid rod + microswitch | Large machinery, paper mills | 0.04 – 0.08 s |
| **Photoelectric Trip Guard (Light Curtain)** | Infrared beam array | Press brakes, robotic cells, assembly lines | 0.005 – 0.020 s |
| **Pressure-Sensitive Trip Mat** | Pneumatic/electronic pressure switch | Robotic work envelopes, hazardous zones | 0.02 – 0.05 s |
| **Capacitive/Proximity Trip Plate** | Field-disturbance sensor | Plastic molding, food processing | 0.01 – 0.03 s |
| **Pull-Back (Restraint) Trip Guard** | Strap/harness attached to operator's wrist | Press operations — physically pulls hand back | Immediate (mechanical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The 2024 scheme specifically expects students to differentiate between **trip guards (active sensing)** and **interlocked guards (passive barrier + switch)**. Trip guards do NOT form a physical barrier — they only sense and stop. This distinction frequently appears in 3-mark questions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for Numerical Problems

The most frequently tested numerical in this topic is the **Safety Distance Calculation**, governed by the formula prescribed in **ANSI B11.19 / IS 947 / ISO 13855**.

$$
D_s \;=\; K \cdot T \;+\; C
$$

**Where each variable means:**

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Typical / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_s$ | Minimum Safety Distance (from hazard to trip plane) | mm | Computed |
| $K$ | Hand-Speed Constant (operator reach speed) | mm/s | **1600 mm/s** (OSHA / ANSI) |
| $T$ | Total Stopping Time (sensing + signal + brake + rundown) | seconds | Machine-specific |
| $C$ | Depth Penetration Allowance (into trip plane before detection) | mm | **8 mm** (light curtain), **0 mm** (floor mat) |

**Supplementary Equations:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; t_{\text{sense}} \;+\; t_{\text{relay}} \;+\; t_{\text{brake}} \;+\; t_{\text{mech rundown}}
$$

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \, a \, t^2 \quad \text{(Distance traveled during stopping — kinematics)}
$$

$$
\text{Stopping Distance} \;=\; v \cdot T \;-\; \frac{1}{2} \, a \, T^2
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Stamping Plants**: Trip guards on power presses prevent finger amputations — historically the leading injury in metal forming.
- **Steel Rolling Mills**: Trip wires along the run-out table stop the mill if a worker falls onto the roller line.
- **Printing Presses**: Light curtain trip guards stop the impression cylinder if the operator's hand enters during inching.
