> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed Guard Fencing - Guard Construction - Guard Opening - Selection and Suitability: Lathe, Drilling, Boring, Milling, Grinding, Shaping, Sawing, Shearing, Presses, Forge Hammer, Flywheels, Shafts, Couplings, Gears, Sprockets, Wheels and Chains, Pulleys and Belts - Authorized Entry to Hazardous Installations - Benefits of Good Guarding Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Fixed Guard Fencing — Formal KTU Definition

A **Fixed Guard** is a permanent physical barrier that forms an integral part of a machine or its enclosure, designed to prevent operator access to hazardous areas during normal operation. Unlike movable or interlocking guards, a fixed guard is **not readily opened or removed without the use of tools**.

According to **ANSI B11.1, EN ISO 12100, and IS 9474**, a fixed guard is:

> "A guard that is permanently secured in its position (e.g., by welding, riveting, or by means of screws, nuts, or other fasteners that require the use of tools to remove it) and that does not depend on moving parts for its operation."

Fixed guards are also known as **Permanent Guards** or **Static Barriers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A guard is classified as *fixed* ONLY if removal requires a **tool**. If it can be opened by hand, it becomes a *movable guard* and demands **interlocking** by law.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Fence" Analogy

Imagine the danger zone of a machine as a **fierce, fast-spinning ceiling fan** in a closed room.

| Element | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Spinning Blades | Lathe chuck / milling cutter | Point of Operation |
| Room Walls | Welded steel enclosure | **Fixed Guard Fencing** |
| Locked Door | Bolted access panel | Permanently fixed barrier |
| Window with bars | Perforated mesh screen | **Aperture Guard** (restricted opening) |

The wall doesn't move, can't be lifted, and has no hinges — to enter, you must break it down (use a tool). This is **exactly** what a fixed guard does: it *engineers-out* the human-machine collision possibility by brute physical separation.

## 1.3 Classification of Machine Guards (KTU Hierarchy)

The broader family of machine guards, ranked from most rigid to most flexible, is:

1. **Fixed Guards** (this module's primary focus) — permanent, tool-required removal
2. **Interlocking Guards** — coupled to power, gate-open = machine-stop
3. **Adjustable Guards** — allow variation for stock size
4. **Self-Adjusting Guards** — move with material feed
5. **Two-Hand Control Devices** — operator must use both hands
6. **Presence-Sensing Devices** — light curtains, mats

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 emphasizes **Fixed Guards** because they are the **first line of defense** in the **Hierarchy of Controls**: *Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls (Guards) → Administrative Controls → PPE*.

## 1.4 Guard Construction — Materials, Standards & Geometry

### 1.4.1 Materials Used in Fixed Guard Construction

| Material | Application | Standard / Code |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel Sheet (MS) | General enclosures, panels | IS 2062 |
| Perforated Metal Sheet | Visibility + ventilation | IS 14201 |
| Weld Mesh (10 mm / 15 mm aperture) | Belt/pulley guards | IS 4940 |
| Polycarbonate (Lexan) | Transparent barriers, chip shields | ANSI Z87.1 |
| Cast Iron | Heavy forge hammer enclosures | IS 210 |
| Stainless Steel (SS 304/316) | Food, pharma, chemical plants | ASTM A240 |
| Expanded Metal | Lightweight guarding | IS 412 |

### 1.4.2 Construction Specifications (Per IS 9474 / EN 953)

- **Sheet Thickness:** $\geq \mathbf{1.6 \, mm}$ for MS panels up to 1 m²; thicker for larger spans
- **Weld Spacing:** $\leq \mathbf{100 \, mm}$ pitch for structural welds
- **Bolt Torque:** M10 bolts at $\mathbf{45 \, N \cdot m}$ minimum retention
- **Edge Treatment:** All sharp edges deburred; radius $\geq \mathbf{1.5 \, mm}$
- **Finish:** Powder-coated or hot-dip galvanized for corrosion resistance
- **Deflection Limit:** Maximum $\mathbf{10 \, mm}$ under $\mathbf{250 \, N}$ point load at mid-span

### 1.4.3 Guard Opening — The "Reach Distance" Concept

The **size of any opening** in a guard is not arbitrary. It is governed by the **safety distance formula** that prevents a person's hand or fingers from reaching the hazard before the machine stops (or that simply makes reaching physically impossible).

The **ISO 13857 / EN 294** formula relates opening size to reach distance:

$$ S = (K \times T) + C $$

Where:

- $S$ = Minimum safety distance from hazard to guard opening (mm)
- $T$ = Total stopping time of machine (s)
- $K$ = Hand/arm approach speed constant = **1600 mm/s** (regular) or **2500 mm/s** (fast reach)
- $C$ = Additional distance based on opening size (mm), per ISO 13857 Table

### 1.4.4 Standard Opening-to-Reach Mapping (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Opening Dimension (one side) | Reach Through Possible? | Minimum Reach Distance $C$ |
|---|---|---|
| $< 6 \, mm$ | No finger entry | $0 \, mm$ |
| $6 - 10 \, mm$ | Finger only | $\geq 10 \, mm$ |
| $> 10 - 25 \, mm$ | Finger to knuckle | $\geq 25 \, mm$ |
| $> 25 - 100 \, mm$ | Whole hand | $\geq 85 \, mm$ |
| $> 100 - 300 \, mm$ | Whole arm | $\geq 850 \, mm$ |
| $> 300 \, mm$ | Body entry — **forbidden** | Not permitted as guard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Any opening $\mathbf{> 300 \, mm}$ is treated as **unrestricted body access** and is illegal as a guard opening. The machine must instead be fully enclosed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Theoretical Logic of Fixed Guarding

Fixed guarding rests on three pillars of safety engineering theory:

### Pillar 1: **Hazard Isolation Principle**

The guard creates a **physical discontinuity** in the energy transfer path. The operator cannot simultaneously reach the hazard AND operate the machine because the guard occupies the space between them. This is the **safest engineering control** because it does not depend on human behavior.

### Pillar 2: **Defense in Depth**

A fixed guard serves as **primary containment**, working in conjunction with secondary measures:

$$\text{Total Safety} = \underbrace{G}_{\text{Guard}} + \underbrace{L}_{\text{Locking}} + \underbrace{S}_{\text{Signage}} + \underbrace{T}_{\text{Training}} + \underbrace{PPE}_{\text{Personal}}$$

If any single layer fails (e.g., guard removed), others still protect the worker.

### Pillar 3: **Risk Reduction Hierarchy (Per ISO 12100)**

$$\text{Risk}_{\text{reduced}} = \text{Risk}_{\text{inherent}} - (G \times \text{Reduction Factor})$$

Where the **reduction factor** for a properly designed fixed guard approaches **0.95 - 0.99**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Application | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = (K \times T) + C$ | Minimum safety distance for guard openings | ISO 13857 |
| 2 | $K = 1600 \, mm/s$ | Hand approach speed (normal reach) | EN 294 |
| 3 | $K = 2500 \, mm/s$ | Hand approach speed (rapid reach) | EN 294 |
| 4 | $\text{Mesh Aperture} \leq 30 \, mm$ for hand exclusion | Standard mesh guard | IS 4940 |
| 5 | $\text{Hole Diameter} \leq 6 \, mm$ for finger exclusion | Drilling guard aperture | ANSI B11.1 |
| 6 | $F_{\text{impact}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of ejected workpiece | Kinetic theory |
| 7 | $v_{\text{tip}} = \pi D n$ | Peripheral speed of rotating part (m/s) | Rotational dynamics |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{bolt}} \geq 45 \, N \cdot m$ for M10 | Guard fastener retention torque | IS 9474 |
| 9 | $\text{Deflection} \leq \frac{L}{200}$ | Maximum guard panel deflection | Structural design |
| 10 | $\text{Height}_{\text{guard}} \geq 1400 \, mm$ | Minimum pedestrian barrier height | IS 3691 |

### 2.2.1 Critical Subsidiary Equations

**Peripheral (tip) speed of a rotating hazard:**

$$v_{\text{tip}} = \pi D n$$

Where:
- $v_{\text{tip}}$ = tip speed in m/s
- $D$ = diameter in meters
- $n$ = rotational speed in rev/s

> *Any rotating part with $v_{\text{tip}} \geq 25 \, m/s$ is considered a **high-energy hazard** and demands a full enclosure, not just a partial guard.*

**Kinetic energy of ejected workpiece (used to size guard strength):**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

Where $E_k$ is in Joules, $m$ in kg, and $v$ in m/s.

A **10 kg workpiece** ejected at **20 m/s** carries:

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 10 \times 20^{2} = 2000 \, J$$

This is lethal kinetic energy (a bullet carries ~500-2000 J). Hence guards must be impact-rated.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fixed guards are mandated across virtually every industrial sector:

| Industry | Application | Critical Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Press lines, robotic cells | Punch press entrapment |
| Steel Mills | Rolling mills, forging | Flywheel ejection |
| Woodworking | Saw tables, shapers | Blade contact laceration |
| Textile | Carding machines, calendars | Rotating shaft entanglement |
| Food Processing | Dough mixers, slicers | Cutting blade amputation |
| Chemical | Reactor agitators | Rotating seal failure |

In every case, the fixed guard is the **engineered hardware** that implements the legal principle of **"safe by design"** embedded in the Factories Act 1948 (India), Section 21, and the OSH Code 2020.

## 2.4 Selection Logic — When Fixed vs. Other Guards

A fixed guard is selected when **ALL** of the following are true:

1. The machine operation is **continuous** (no frequent operator access during cycle)
2. The hazard is **constant** during normal operation
3. Maintenance access is **rare** and can be planned
4. Removing the guard for normal operation is **not required**
5. The guard does not interfere with **material flow**

If any of these fail → switch to **interlocking guard** instead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** "If you need to open the guard more than **once per shift**, it should not be a fixed guard." This is a frequently asked concept question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance from Reach & Stopping Time

The KTU-mandated safety distance formula is derived from kinematic motion analysis. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the Threat Window

A person begins reaching toward a hazard at time $t = 0$. The machine receives a stop signal at time $t = T_{r}$ (reaction time) and physically halts at $t = T_{r} + T_{b}$ (reaction + braking time). The total stopping time:

$$ T = T_{r} + T_{b} $$

### Step 2 — Hand Travel During Stop Window

During the stopping interval $T$, the hand can travel a distance:

$$ d_{\text{hand}} = K \times T $$

where $K$ is the hand approach speed in mm/s.

### Step 3 — Add Anatomical Clearance

After the machine stops, the fingers can still extend a distance $C$ beyond the hand-rest position to reach the hazard. This $C$ is determined by the guard opening size (per ISO 13857).

### Step 4 — Total Safety Distance

For the hand to JUST fail to reach the hazard:

$$ S_{\min} = d_{\text{hand}} + C $$

$$ \boxed{S = (K \times T) + C} $$

**Units:** $S$ in mm, $K$ in mm/s, $T$ in seconds, $C$ in mm.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A milling machine has a stopping time of 0.5 s. The guard has a slot opening of 25 mm. Calculate the minimum safety distance from the opening to the cutter. Use $K = 1600 \, mm/s$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters:
- $T = 0.5 \, s$
- $K = 1600 \, mm/s$
- Opening = 25 mm → From KTU Table in §1.4.4, $C = 25 \, mm$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$ S = (1600 \times 0.5) + 25 $$

$$ S = 800 + 25 $$

$$ S = 825 \, mm $$

Step 3 — Practical interpretation:

The guard opening must be at least **825 mm** away from the nearest cutter tooth. If this distance cannot be achieved, the opening must be reduced so that $C$ increases (e.g., to 85 mm → $S$ = 885 mm).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation problem**. Always show all three steps: parameter identification → substitution → engineering interpretation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Peripheral Speed Threshold for High-Energy Hazards

For rotating machine parts, the peripheral speed determines whether a guard must be a *partial* or *full enclosure*.

**Step 1:** Peripheral speed of a rotating component:

$$ v_{\text{tip}} = \pi D n $$

**Step 2:** International safety standard threshold (ANSI B11 / EN 953):

$$ v_{\text{tip}} \geq 25 \, m/s \implies \text{Full enclosure mandatory} $$

**Step 3:** Worked example — A grinding wheel with $D = 0.4 \, m$ running at $n = 3000 \, rpm$:

Convert $n$ to rev/s:

$$ n = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \, rev/s $$

Calculate $v_{\text{tip}}$:

$$ v_{\text{tip}} = \pi \times 0.4 \times 50 $$

$$ v_{\text{tip}} = 62.83 \, m/s $$

Since $62.83 \, m/s > 25 \, m/s$ → **Full enclosure required** with $\geq 12 \, mm$ thick steel plate capable of containing wheel fragments in case of burst.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Guard Type per Machine

This is the **core KTU Module 2 analytical tool** — mapping each machine to its appropriate fixed guard type.

| Machine | Primary Hazard | Recommended Fixed Guard Type | Critical Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lathe** | Chuck rotation, flying chips, entanglement | Enclosure with polycarbonate viewing window | Aperture $\leq 6 \, mm$ mesh at rear; chip shield thickness $\geq 3 \, mm$ |
| **Drilling** | Drill bit entanglement, ejected swarf | Telescopic sleeve + chuck guard | Guard opening $= $ drill bit diameter; cylindrical enclosure |
| **Boring** | Long tool whip, large swarf | Full perimeter enclosure with interlocked access | Tool clearance $\geq 50 \, mm$; polycarbonate window |
| **Milling** | Cutter teeth, chip ejection, table | Full enclosure, hinged for setup | Aperture $\leq 30 \, mm$ mesh; safety distance $S \geq 825 \, mm$ |
| **Grinding** | Wheel burst, sparks, eye damage | Full wheel guard (bi-pass), eye protection zone | Wheel guard $\geq 12 \, mm$ MS; angle of exposure $\leq 65°$ |
| **Shaping** | Ram reciprocation, tool shatter | Front rail + transparent side shields | Toe guard $\geq 150 \, mm$ high; transparent grade PC |
| **Sawing (Wood)** | Blade contact, kickback | Riving knife + blade guard + anti-kickback fingers | Blade guard must cover all teeth except cut zone |
| **Shearing** | Blade descent, finger amputation | Fixed front guard with finger slots | Slot width $\leq 6 \, mm$; depth = stock thickness |
| **Presses (Mechanical)** | Ram descent, die crush | Fixed barrier with light curtain or perimeter fence | Light curtain compliance per EN 61496 |
| **Forge Hammer** | Hot metal splash, hammer drop | Full perimeter guard with viewing window | Heat-resistant glass; height $\geq 1800 \, mm$ |
| **Flywheels** | Burst, entanglement | Full enclosure, sealed bearing housing | Burst containment per ASME stress calc |
| **Shafts** | Rotational entanglement, clothing wrap | Full enclosure along length, bearing covers | Covering $\geq 360°$ for unprotected sections |
| **Couplings** | Pinch point, projection | Bolt-on guard with sight port | Slot access $\leq 6 \, mm$ for lubrication |
| **Gears** | Meshing crush, entanglement | Full or perforated enclosure | Aperture $\leq 30 \, mm$ for hand exclusion |
| **Sprockets** | Chain engagement, nip points | Wrap-around guard on chain run | Covers upper span and both sprockets |
| **Wheels and Chains** | Chain derailment, spoke injury | Side shields + chain box | Sprocket coverage $\geq 180°$ |
| **Pulleys and Belts** | Belt nip, entanglement | Tunnel guard with inspection port | Belt guard $\geq 50 \, mm$ clearance from belt |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Guarding a Lathe (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Question:** Design the fixed guard system for a center lathe handling 1 m long workpieces.

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification:**
- Rotating chuck and workpiece
- Ejected hot chips
- Tailstock pinch
- Lead screw / feed rod entanglement

**Step 2 — Guard Selection:**
- **Rear enclosure:** MS sheet 1.6 mm, full height 1400 mm
- **Front:** Hinged polycarbonate (8 mm) chip shield
- **Bottom:** Wire mesh (10 mm aperture) for chip clearance but finger exclusion
- **Top:** Open (for long bar stock) — but with adjustable bar guard

**Step 3 — Safety Distance Verification:**

Assuming lathe stopping time $T = 0.4 \, s$:

$$ S = (1600 \times 0.4) + 85 = 640 + 85 = 725 \, mm $$

So the operator's normal reach position must be $\geq 725 \, mm$ from any rotating hazard, or the guard must provide that equivalent barrier.

**Step 4 — Compliance Check:**

- $\checkmark$ ANSI B11.1
- $\checkmark$ IS 9474
- $\checkmark$ Factories Act 1948 §21
- $\checkmark$ OSH Code 2020

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fixed Guard Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["HAZARD SOURCE"] --> node2["ENERGY AT POINT OF OPERATION"]
    node2 --> node3["FIXED GUARD BARRIER"]
    node3 --> node4{"CAN OPERATOR REACH HAZARD?"}
    node4 -- NO --> node5["SAFE OPERATION"]
    node4 -- YES --> node6["REJECT DESIGN"]
    node6 --> node7["REDESIGN OPENING OR DISTANCE"]
    node7 --> node3

    nodeA["MATERIAL SELECTION"] --> node1
    nodeB["ISO 13857 OPENING"] --> node4
    nodeC["FACTORIES ACT 1948"] --> node5
    nodeD["EN 953 STANDARD"] --> node3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Guard Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START MACHINE SELECTION"] --> q1{"FREQUENT OPERATOR ACCESS NEEDED?"}
    q1 -- YES --> alt1["USE INTERLOCKING GUARD"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{"HIGH ENERGY ROTATING PART?"}
    q2 -- YES --> full["FULL ENCLOSURE FIXED GUARD"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{"CHIP OR SPARK GENERATION?"}
    q3 -- YES --> chip["POLYCARBONATE SCREEN FIXED GUARD"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{"BELT OR CHAIN DRIVE?"}
    q4 -- YES --> tunnel["TUNNEL GUARD PERFORATED"]
    q4 -- NO --> q5{"STOCK ENTRY SLOT NEEDED?"}
    q5 -- YES --> slot["FIXED GUARD WITH ADJUSTABLE SLOT"]
    q5 -- NO --> basic["STANDARD FIXED PANEL GUARD"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Safety Distance Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1["STOPPING TIME T seconds"] --> cal["S = K TIMES T PLUS C"]
    inp2["HAND SPEED K = 1600 OR 2500 mm per second"] --> cal
    inp3["OPENING SIZE C"] --> cal
    cal --> out["SAFETY DISTANCE S millimeters"]
    out --> chk{"S ACHIEVABLE IN DESIGN?"}
    chk -- YES --> ok["DESIGN APPROVED"]
    chk -- NO --> red["REDUCE OPENING OR INCREASE BARRIER"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Machine-to-Guard Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    m1["LATHE"] --> g1["POLYCARBONATE CHIP SHIELD"]
    m2["DRILL"] --> g2["TELESCOPIC SLEEVE GUARD"]
    m3["MILL"] --> g3["FULL ENCLOSURE WITH MESH"]
    m4["GRINDER"] --> g4["BIPASS WHEEL GUARD"]
    m5["PRESS"] --> g5["PERIMETER FENCE PLUS LIGHT CURTAIN"]
    m6["FORGE HAMMER"] --> g6["HEAT RESISTANT ENCLOSURE"]
    m7["FLYWHEEL"] --> g7["BURST CONTAINMENT HOUSING"]
    m8["SHAFT"] --> g8["FULL LENGTH ROTATING SHAFT COVER"]
    m9["GEAR"] --> g9["PERFORATED GEAR ENCLOSURE"]
    m10["BELT PULLEY"] --> g10["TUNNEL GUARD WITH INSPECTION PORT"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Authorized Entry System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req["PERSONNEL REQUESTS ENTRY"] --> auth{"AUTHORIZED?"}
    auth -- NO --> deny["ENTRY DENIED"]
    auth -- YES --> ptp["PERMIT TO WORK ISSUED"]
    ptp --> lock["LOCKOUT TAGOUT APPLIED"]
    lock --> test["ZERO ENERGY VERIFICATION"]
    test --> esc["ESCORT ASSIGNED"]
    esc --> enter["SAFE ENTRY INTO HAZARD ZONE"]
    enter --> work["WORK EXECUTED"]
    work --> restore["ENERGY RESTORED AFTER CLEARANCE"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a fixed guard. List any four materials used in its construction.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fixed guard** is a permanent physical barrier permanently secured to a machine such that its removal requires the use of tools (per **ANSI B11.1 / IS 9474**).

Four materials used:
1. **Mild Steel (MS) Sheet** — general panels, thickness $\geq 1.6 \, mm$
2. **Polycarbonate (Lexan)** — transparent chip shields
3. **Perforated MS Sheet** — visibility + ventilation
4. **Weld Mesh** — belt/chain guards

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the ISO 13857 safety distance formula and define each term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$ S = (K \times T) + C $$

- $S$ = Minimum safety distance (mm)
- $K$ = Hand approach speed, $1600 \, mm/s$ normal or $2500 \, mm/s$ rapid
- $T$ = Total machine stopping time (s)
- $C$ = Additional reach distance based on guard opening (mm)

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (CHOICE 1)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the constructional features of a fixed guard fence. List the relevant Indian Standard codes.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A drilling machine has a stopping time of **0.3 seconds**. The chuck guard has an opening of **12 mm diameter**. Calculate the minimum safety distance. What guard modification would you suggest if this distance cannot be maintained?

#### Model Solution (a):

**Constructional Features of Fixed Guard Fencing:**

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Frame | $40 \times 40 \, mm$ MS angle or $50 \, mm$ pipe |
| Panel | MS sheet $\geq 1.6 \, mm$ or weld mesh $10 - 30 \, mm$ aperture |
| Fasteners | M10 bolts, torque $\geq 45 \, N \cdot m$, security-type nuts |
| Posts | $1500 \, mm$ height, embedded $300 \, mm$ in concrete |
| Finish | Powder-coated / hot-dip galvanized |
| Edges | Deburred, radius $\geq 1.5 \, mm$ |
| Signage | Hazard pictogram at every entry |

**Applicable Indian Standards:**
- **IS 9474** — Fixed and movable guards
- **IS 4940** — Weld mesh specifications
- **IS 3691** — Industrial fencing
- **IS 14201** — Perforated sheet
- **Factories Act 1948 §21**

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:**
- $T = 0.3 \, s$
- Opening diameter $= 12 \, mm$
- $K = 1600 \, mm/s$ (normal reach)

**For opening = 12 mm**, from ISO 13857 Table: $C = 10 \, mm$ (finger only reach)

**Calculation:**

$$ S = (1600 \times 0.3) + 10 $$

$$ S = 480 + 10 $$

$$ S = 490 \, mm $$

**Interpretation:**
The guard opening must be $\geq 490 \, mm$ from the chuck. If the physical layout does not permit this:

**Recommended Modification:**
Replace the **12 mm circular opening** with a **6 mm opening** (finger-exclusion threshold). This increases $C$ to a higher value AND — more importantly — **physically prevents finger entry entirely**, eliminating the need for distance calculation. Alternatively, install an **interlocking guard** instead.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (CHOICE 2)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe with neat sketches the suitable fixed guards for **i) Lathe and ii) Grinding Machine**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the safety requirements for guarding **flywheels, shafts, couplings, gears, and pulleys**.

#### Model Solution (a):

**i) Lathe Fixed Guards:**
- **Rear enclosure:** MS sheet 1.6 mm, height 1400 mm
- **Front chip shield:** 8 mm transparent polycarbonate, hinged
- **Bottom mesh:** Weld mesh 10 mm aperture to allow chip fall but exclude fingers
- **Chuck guard:** Encloses the rotating chuck area completely

**ii) Grinding Machine Fixed Guards:**
- **Wheel guard:** Bi-pass type covering $\geq 65\%$ of wheel periphery
- **Material:** Cast iron or MS plate $\geq 12 \, mm$
- **Tool-rest gap:** $\leq 3 \, mm$ (regulatory max)
- **Eye protection zone:** Transparent polycarbonate screen between operator and wheel

#### Model Solution (b):

| Component | Guard Type | Critical Specification |
|---|---|---|
| **Flywheel** | Full enclosure with burst containment | Designed for $1.5 \times$ max KE |
| **Shafts** | Full length cover (unbroken) | $\geq 360°$ coverage where accessible |
| **Couplings** | Bolt-on guard with sight hole | Sight hole $\leq 6 \, mm$ for lubrication |
| **Gears** | Perforated enclosure or full cover | Aperture $\leq 30 \, mm$; hand exclusion |
| **Pulleys & Belts** | Tunnel guard with inspection door | Belt clearance $\geq 50 \, mm$; door interlocked |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently **forget to mention the tool-rest gap of 3 mm** for grinding wheels — losing 2 marks instantly. Similarly, **sight-hole apertures for couplings** are a common omission. Always quote **numerical values** for openings and distances in 14-mark answers; qualitative answers alone score poorly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **fixed guard** is removed only with **tools** — otherwise it is movable/interlocked.
- The **safety distance formula** $S = (K \times T) + C$ is the heart of KTU Module 2.
- $K = 1600 \, mm/s$ (normal) or $2500 \, mm/s$ (rapid).
- **MS sheet thickness $\geq 1.6 \, mm$** is the baseline standard for fixed guard panels.
- **Mesh aperture $\leq 30 \, mm$** excludes hands; $\leq 6 \, mm$ excludes fingers.
- **Peripheral speed $\geq 25 \, m/s$** mandates a **full enclosure**, not a partial guard.
- **Tool-rest gap on grinder $\leq 3 \, mm$** — this is a high-frequency exam value.
- **Flywheel guards** must withstand **kinetic energy** $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$.
- **Press guards** typically combine perimeter fence + light curtain (EN 61496).
- **Authorized entry** requires **Permit-to-Work**, **LOTO**, **zero-energy verification**, and **escort**.
- If guard must be opened **>1 shift**, switch to **interlocking type**.
- Always cite **IS 9474, IS 4940, ANSI B11.1, EN ISO 12100, Factories Act 1948 §21**.
- **Belt/chain guards** must cover **upper span + both sprockets/pulleys**.
- **Polycarbonate thickness $\geq 8 \, mm$** for chip shields; **MS $\geq 12 \, mm$** for wheel guards.
- Always end a long answer with a **compliance-checklist table** referencing standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** For 14-mark questions, the **3-element structure** that always scores highest is: (1) **definition with standard reference**, (2) **numerical table of specifications**, (3) **diagram or schematic description + compliance statement**. Master this and you will consistently score above 12/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Welding and Oxygen Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gas Welding and Oxygen Cutting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Gas Welding — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Gas Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating them to the welding temperature with a flame obtained by the combustion of a fuel gas (such as acetylene, hydrogen, propane, or natural gas) with **oxygen (O₂)**. The heat source is purely chemical (exothermic oxidation reaction), and no electrical arc is involved. The most industrially significant variant is **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** because acetylene (C₂H₂) produces the highest flame temperature (~**3160 °C to 3480 °C**) among commercially used fuel gases. The American Welding Society (AWS) classifies gas welding under the broader family of **"Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding (OFW)"** processes, designated by the letter **"OF"** followed by the fuel-gas identifier (e.g., OFW-A for acetylene). ### 1.2 Oxygen Cutting — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Oxygen Cutting** (also called **Flame Cutting** or **Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting, OFC**) is a thermal severing process in which the metal is separated primarily by the **chemical reaction** of the base metal with a stream of **pure oxygen** issuing from a cutting torch. The process has two stages: (i) the metal is pre-heated to its ignition temperature by an oxy-fuel flame, and (ii) the high-pressure oxygen jet oxidizes (burns) the hot metal, while the molten oxide (dross) is blown away by the kinetic energy of the jet. It is governed by **ISO 9013:2017** for thermal cutting quality classification and is identified by the AWS designation **"OC"** (Oxygen Cutting). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Gas Welding Analogy — The Controlled Campfire:** Imagine you have a tiny, super-hot, precisely controllable campfire. By mixing a fuel gas (acetylene) with oxygen in the right ratio, you create a flame hot enough to melt steel on a kitchen table. The "candle" feeding this fire is a pressurized gas cylinder, and the "nozzle" is a specially designed torch that mixes the gases in exact proportions. Just as a chef controls flame size by adjusting a gas stove knob, a welder controls weld-pool temperature by tweaking two pressure regulators. **Oxygen Cutting Analogy — The Surgical Saw with Fire:** Think of oxygen cutting as a surgeon using a hot knife to slice through a thick slab of butter. First, you warm the butter just enough so it softens (pre-heating stage), then a thin, high-pressure stream of pure oxygen "burns" a narrow groove through it. The molten by-product is blasted out of the way by the jet, leaving a clean cut. Iron and low-carbon steel behave this way because they oxidize readily; stainless steel and aluminum stubbornly refuse — just as cold butter resists a knife. ### 1.4 Key Physical & Industrial Constants - **Flame temperature of Oxy-Acetylene:** **3160 °C – 3480 °C** (the hottest practical combustion flame in air-fuel engineering). - **Auto-ignition temperature of iron in pure oxygen:** **~870 °C to 950 °C** (critical for oxygen cutting). - **Oxygen cylinder pressure (fully charged at 15 °C):** **~15.2 MPa (≈152 bar / 2200 psi)**. - **Acetylene cylinder pressure (dissolved-acetone type):** **~1.7 MPa (≈17 bar / 250 psi)** maximum safe charging pressure. - **Standard acetylene flame ratios:** Neutral = **1.1 : 1** (O₂ : C₂H₂ by volume). - **Industry standard nozzle sizes:** **No. 1 to No. 90** (drilled in thousandths of inches). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Chemistry & Physics of Oxy-Acetylene Combustion The combustion of acetylene with oxygen proceeds in **two sequential stages**, and the engineer/welder exploits this by adjusting the oxygen-to-acetylene ratio to produce three distinct flame chemistries. **Stage 1 — Primary Combustion (Partial Oxidation):** $$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \quad + \text{Heat (Stage 1)}$$ This reaction occurs in the **inner cone** of the flame and is fuel-rich. The carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H₂) produced are themselves combustible and migrate outward. **Stage 2 — Secondary Combustion (Complete Oxidation):** $$4\,\text{CO} + 2\,\text{H}_2 + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad + \text{Heat (Stage 2)}$$ This reaction occurs in the **outer envelope** (called the *outer cone* or *envelope flame*), using atmospheric oxygen. **Combined Stoichiometric Equation (1 : 1 molar ratio, 2.5 : 1 by volume):** $$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \Delta H \approx -1300\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ ### 2.2 The Three Flame Types (High-Yield KTU Topic) The welder alters the **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio** at the torch to obtain one of three flame chemistries: | Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Volume Ratio | Inner Cone | Outer Envelope | Industrial Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Carburizing (Reducing)** | **< 1.1 : 1** | Long, white, feathered | Small, bluish | Hard-facing, welding high-carbon steel, brass | | **Neutral** | **≈ 1.1 : 1** | Short, bright, rounded | Sharp, blue, well-defined | **General-purpose welding of mild steel** | | **Oxidizing** | **> 1.1 : 1** | Short, pointed, purplish | Short, harsh | Welding of brass, bronze (Mn, Zn evaporates); also mild steel brazing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Sketch and label the three types of oxy-acetylene flames."* Memorize the ratio **1.1 : 1 = neutral** and the colour/shape descriptors above. ### 2.3 Equipment Train (High-Yield Diagram Topic) The gas welding/cutting equipment train consists of the following components, each a critical safety link: 1. **Cylinders** — colour-coded (oxygen: black body, white shoulder in many countries; acetylene: maroon in India/Europe). 2. **Pressure Regulators** — two-stage for oxygen (high inlet → constant working pressure), single-stage for acetylene. 3. **Hoses** — **green for oxygen, red for acetylene** (ISO 3821 / BS EN ISO 3821). 4. **Flashback Arrestors (FBAs)** — fitted at the torch inlet and at the regulator outlet; prevent flame propagation into the hoses/regulators. 5. **Torch (Blowpipe)** — injector-type for low-pressure acetylene, equal-pressure-type for high-pressure systems. 6. **Cutting Attachment / Nozzle** — for oxygen cutting only; has a central high-pressure oxygen orifice surrounded by preheat oxy-fuel ports. ### 2.4 Oxygen Cutting — The Sequential Process Logic For oxygen cutting to occur, **three thermodynamic conditions** must be satisfied: - **(C1)** The ignition temperature of the metal in pure oxygen must be **lower** than its melting point (true for iron: ignition ≈ **870 °C**, melting ≈ **1538 °C**). - **(C2)** The oxide formed must be **fluid enough** to be ejected by the oxygen jet (FeO satisfies this at cutting temperatures). - **(C3)** The combustion of the metal must be **exothermic** — it must release heat that sustains the cut (iron's oxidation releases ~**7.36 kJ/g**, providing ~70 % of the total cutting energy). > [!NOTE] > **Why stainless steel, cast iron, and aluminum resist oxygen cutting:** > - Stainless steel forms **Cr₂O₃**, a refractory (high-melting) oxide that blocks further oxidation. > - Cast iron has carbon that forms CO gas and produces a hard, irregular cut. > - Aluminum forms **Al₂O₃** (melting point 2072 °C), which caps the molten metal and refuses to be blown away. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heat released per m³ of C₂H₂ (stoichiometric) | $Q_{C_2H_2}$ | **kJ/m³** | Calculate gas consumption & cost | | 2 | Heat of combustion of acetylene | $\Delta H_c$ | **−1300 kJ/mol** or **~56,000 kJ/m³** | Energy budgeting | | 3 | Theoretical flame temperature | $T_{flame}$ | **°C / K** | Predict weld-pool superheat | | 4 | Neutral flame ratio | $R_N$ | **O₂/C₂H₂ = 1.1 : 1 (vol)** | Set torch valves | | 5 | Oxygen consumption (cutting) | $V_{O_2}$ | **m³/hr** | Size cylinder/capacity | | 6 | Cutting speed empirical | $v_c$ | **mm/min** | Estimate production rate | | 7 | Kerf width | $w_k$ | **mm** | Nesting & material yield | | 8 | Specific heat of iron (cutting) | $c_p$ | **0.46 kJ/(kg·K)** | Energy to pre-heat plate | | 9 | Heat released by iron oxidation | $Q_{Fe}$ | **7.36 kJ/g** | Sustains the cut | | 10 | Plate pre-heat energy | $Q_{pre}$ | **kJ** | $Q_{pre}=m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX/Markdown Rule:** When using absolute values or ratios inside tables, **never** use the pipe symbol `|`. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead — example: $\vert \Delta T \vert$ rather than `|ΔT|`. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Gas welding and oxygen cutting are foundational in: - **Structural steel fabrication** (especially for field erection). - **Pipe-line construction** (carbon-steel pipelines, repair sleeves). - **Heavy-equipment repair** (mining, shipyards). - **Demolition & salvage** (oxygen cutting of ship hulls, bridge girders). - **Art & sculpture** (bronze, copper welding). - **Automotive bodywork** (gas welding of thin sheet steel — still preferred over arc welding to avoid burn-through). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Total Heat Released by Oxy-Acetylene Combustion (Per m³ of Acetylene) We will derive the heat released per cubic metre of acetylene at standard temperature and pressure (**STP**: 0 °C, 101.325 kPa) for a neutral flame. **Step 1 — Stoichiometric equation (neutral):** $$2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The molar enthalpy of combustion (standard, liquid water product) is: $$\Delta H_c^\circ = -2600\ \text{kJ per 2 mol C}_2\text{H}_2$$ Therefore, **per mole of C₂H₂**: $$\Delta H_c^\circ = -1300\ \text{kJ/mol}$$ **Step 2 — Convert moles to volume at STP.** Using the ideal gas law at STP, **1 mole of any ideal gas = 22.414 L = 0.022414 m³**. **Step 3 — Compute heat released per m³ of acetylene:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{per m}^3} &= \frac{\Delta H_c^\circ}{V_m} \\ &= \frac{1300\ \text{kJ/mol}}{0.022414\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}} \\ &= 57{,}998.5\ \text{kJ/m}^3 \\ &\approx 58{,}000\ \text{kJ/m}^3 \\ &\approx 5.8 \times 10^4\ \text{kJ/m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result.** $$\boxed{Q_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} \approx 58{,}000\ \text{kJ/m}^3 \quad \text{(neutral flame)}}$$ For comparison: - Oxy-hydrogen: ~ **10,800 kJ/m³** (≈ 5× lower). - Oxy-propane: ~ **93,000 kJ/m³** (higher total energy but much lower flame temperature due to dilution by N₂ in air). > **Engineering Takeaway:** Acetylene is preferred over propane for welding (despite propane having higher total heat) because flame *temperature* — not total heat — controls melting efficiency. Temperature is what enables a localized melt pool. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Energy Required to Preheat a Steel Plate for Cutting **Problem:** Calculate the heat required to preheat a mild-steel plate of dimensions **1500 mm × 2000 mm × 25 mm** from ambient (25 °C) to ignition temperature (900 °C) for oxygen cutting. Use $c_p = 0.46\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$. **Step 1 — Compute the volume of the plate:** $$V = 1.5 \times 2.0 \times 0.025 = 0.075\ \text{m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute the mass of the plate:** $$\begin{aligned} m &= \rho \cdot V \\ &= 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 0.075\ \text{m}^3 \\ &= 588.75\ \text{kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the temperature rise:** $$\Delta T = 900 - 25 = 875\ \text{°C} \equiv 875\ \text{K}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the sensible-heat equation:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{pre} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\ &= 588.75\ \text{kg} \times 0.46\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)} \times 875\ \text{K} \\ &= 588.75 \times 0.46 \times 875 \\ &= 236{,}941.875\ \text{kJ} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to MJ:** $$\boxed{Q_{pre} \approx 237\ \text{MJ}}$$ **Step 6 — Compute equivalent acetylene consumption (assuming 60 % thermal efficiency of the preheat flame):** $$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_2} &= \frac{Q_{pre}}{Q_{\text{per m}^3} \times \eta} \\ &= \frac{236{,}942}{58{,}000 \times 0.60} \\ &= \frac{236{,}942}{34{,}800} \\ &= 6.81\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** A single 1500 mm × 2000 mm × 25 mm plate consumes ~**6.81 m³ of acetylene** just for preheat. This is why large oxy-fuel installations use propane or natural gas for preheat (cheaper) and reserve acetylene for high-temperature work. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Cutting Speed (Empirical KTU-style Estimate) A simplified empirical relationship used in industry for **low-carbon steel** is the **cutting-speed equation (ISO 9013 / ESAB charts simplified)**: $$v_c \approx \frac{K}{t}$$ where $v_c$ = cutting speed (mm/min), $t$ = plate thickness (mm), $K$ = material factor (≈ **350** for mild steel with oxygen purity ≥ **99.5 %**). **Example:** For a **20 mm** mild-steel plate: $$\begin{aligned} v_c &= \frac{350}{20} \\ &= 17.5\ \text{mm/min} \times 60 \\ &= 1050\ \text{mm/min} \\ &= 1.05\ \text{m/min} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{v_c \approx 1.05\ \text{m/min} \quad \text{for 20 mm mild steel}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fuel Gases | Parameter | Acetylene (C₂H₂) | Hydrogen (H₂) | Propane (C₃H₈) | Natural Gas (CH₄) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Flame temperature (°C) | **3160–3480** | 2050 | 2520 | 2400 | | Heat of combustion (kJ/m³) | 58,000 | 10,800 | 93,000 | 36,000 | | Specific gravity (air=1) | 0.91 | 0.07 | 1.52 | 0.55 | | Flame speed (m/s) | 8 | 9.4 | 3.7 | 3.8 | | Oxygen requirement (vol/vol) | 2.5 | 0.5 | 5.0 | 2.0 | | Suitability for welding | **Excellent** | Limited (low T) | Poor | Poor | | Suitability for cutting | Excellent | Limited | Good (cheaper) | Good | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Oxy-Acetylene Welding Equipment Train ```mermaid graph LR O2CYL[Oxygen Cylinder
Black Body, White Shoulder
Pressure 15.2 MPa] ACETY[C2H2 Cylinder
Maroon Body
Pressure 1.7 MPa] O2REG[Oxygen Regulator
Two-Stage, 0.3 MPa Working] ACREG[Acetylene Regulator
Single-Stage, 0.07 MPa Working] O2HOSE[Oxygen Hose
Green, ISO 3821] ACHOSE[Acetylene Hose
Red, ISO 3821] FBA1[Flashback Arrestor
Regulator End] FBA2[Flashback Arrestor
Torch End] TORCH[Torch Body
Injector Type, Brass] NOZ[Cutting or Welding Nozzle
Drilled, Copper] FLAME[Flame at Workpiece
Inner Cone + Outer Envelope] O2CYL --> O2REG ACETY --> ACREG O2REG --> FBA1 ACREG --> FBA1 FBA1 --> O2HOSE FBA1 --> ACHOSE O2HOSE --> FBA2 ACHOSE --> FBA2 FBA2 --> TORCH TORCH --> NOZ NOZ --> FLAME ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Oxygen Cutting Process Logic ```mermaid graph TD STEP1[Step 1: Ignite Pre-Heat Flame
O2 plus C2H2 mixed at nozzle] STEP2[Step 2: Pre-Heat Metal
Surface reaches 870 to 950 °C
Glowing cherry red] STEP3[Step 3: Press Lever
Pure O2 jet released at high pressure] STEP4[Step 4: Iron Oxidation Reaction
Fe plus O2 yields FeO plus Heat
Q equals 7.36 kJ per g] STEP5[Step 5: Dross Ejection
Molten FeO blown out by O2 jet
Forms cutting kerf] STEP6[Step 6: Continuous Progression
Torch moves at cutting speed v sub c] STEP7[Step 7: Complete Severance
Two pieces separated with kerf w sub k] STEP1 --> STEP2 STEP2 --> STEP3 STEP3 --> STEP4 STEP4 --> STEP5 STEP5 --> STEP6 STEP6 --> STEP7 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Three Flame Types (Visual Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD FLAME[Flame Selector at Torch] A[Carburizing Flame
O2 to C2H2 less than 1.1 to 1] B[Neutral Flame
O2 to C2H2 equals 1.1 to 1] C[Oxidizing Flame
O2 to C2H2 greater than 1.1 to 1] A --> A1[Inner Cone Long White Feathered
Outer Cone Small Bluish] B --> B1[Inner Cone Short Rounded Bright
Outer Cone Sharp Blue] C --> C1[Inner Cone Short Pointed Purple
Outer Cone Short Hissing] FLAME --> A FLAME --> B FLAME --> C ``` ### 4.4 Safety Hazard Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR HAZ[Hazard Source] HAZ --> H1[Backfire
Flame travels back into nozzle] HAZ --> H2[Flashback
Flame travels into hose and regulator] HAZ --> H3[Cylinder Explosion
Acetylene above 1.7 MPa decomposes] HAZ --> H4[Hose Burst
Age, oil contamination, kinking] HAZ --> H5[Oxygen Fire
Oil plus high pressure O2 equals violent ignition] H1 --> CTRL1[Use FBA, water seal, soak nozzle in water] H2 --> CTRL2[FBAs mandatory, check valves, hose check] H3 --> CTRL3[Never exceed 1.7 MPa, no free acetylene above 2 bar] H4 --> CTRL4[Inspect hoses annually, replace every 3 years] H5 --> CTRL5[No oil on O2 fittings, no grease, PTFE tape only] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Neutral Flame" in oxy-acetylene welding. State the O₂ : C₂H₂ volume ratio for a neutral flame.* **Model Answer:** A **neutral flame** is one in which the oxygen-to-acetylene ratio is exactly balanced, so that neither excess oxygen nor excess carbon is present in the weld zone. The combustion is chemically complete, producing a non-oxidizing, non-reducing atmosphere. **O₂ : C₂H₂ volume ratio = 1.1 : 1 (i.e., 1.1 volumes of oxygen per 1 volume of acetylene).** The flame has a short, bright, rounded inner cone and a sharp, well-defined blue outer envelope. It is the standard flame for general mild-steel welding. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List any **four** safety devices used in an oxy-fuel gas welding setup.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Pressure regulators** — reduce cylinder pressure to safe working pressure. 2. **Flashback arrestors (FBAs)** — fitted at regulator outlet and torch inlet to prevent flame propagation. 3. **Non-return (check) valves** — prevent reverse flow of gas into hoses. 4. **Hose connections with non-interchangeable fittings** (oxygen uses right-hand threads; acetylene uses left-hand threads) to prevent cross-connection. 5. *(Bonus)* Water seal / gas-failure safety device on acetylene lines. --- ### 📝 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — **[Choice 1]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 3]** **Q.A.** *(a)* With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **oxy-acetylene welding (OAW) equipment train**. List the function of each component. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **three types of oxy-acetylene flames** with sketches, indicating the O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio, flame appearance, and industrial application of each. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) The oxy-acetylene welding equipment train consists of the following components connected in series: 1. **Oxygen cylinder** — black body, white shoulder; filled to ~**15.2 MPa**. Stores high-purity (≥ 99.5 %) oxygen. 2. **Acetylene cylinder** — maroon body; contains acetylene dissolved in acetone (the acetone absorbs up to **~14 bar** safely). Maximum charging pressure **1.7 MPa**; above this, acetylene becomes unstable and may decompose explosively (C₂H₂ → 2 C + H₂). 3. **Oxygen pressure regulator** — two-stage type; reduces cylinder pressure to a stable working pressure (typically **0.2 – 0.4 MPa**). 4. **Acetylene pressure regulator** — single-stage type; reduces to a low working pressure (typically **0.05 – 0.1 MPa**). 5. **Oxygen hose** — green-coloured, right-hand threaded fittings. 6. **Acetylene hose** — red-coloured, left-hand threaded fittings (non-interchangeable). 7. **Flashback arrestors** — fitted on both regulator-outlet and torch-inlet ends; contain a sintered metal element that quenches a flame front. 8. **Torch (blowpipe)** — brass body with two needle valves (O₂ and C₂H₂) and a mixer. The injector type uses the oxygen venturi to draw acetylene into the mixing chamber. 9. **Welding nozzle** — copper, drilled with one or more orifices; sizes 1 – 90 according to plate thickness. ``` Sketch reference: SECTION 4.1 above (Mermaid block diagram). ``` --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) The three flames are differentiated by adjusting the acetylene valve relative to the oxygen valve: **1. Carburizing (Reducing) Flame:** - **O₂ : C₂H₂ < 1.1 : 1** (e.g., 0.9 : 1). - **Appearance:** Long, white, feathered inner cone (the "feather" is unburned carbon particles); small, bluish outer envelope. - **Chemistry:** Excess carbon; atmosphere is reducing (rich in CO and H₂). - **Use:** Welding of high-carbon steel, cast iron (with flux), and hard-facing; useful for adding carbon to the weld pool. **2. Neutral Flame:** - **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.1 : 1** (stoichiometric balance). - **Appearance:** Short, bright, rounded inner cone; sharp, well-defined blue outer envelope (about twice as long as the inner cone). - **Chemistry:** Complete combustion with no excess of either gas. - **Use:** **General-purpose welding of mild steel, low-carbon steel, stainless steel, copper, and aluminium.** This is the workhorse flame. **3. Oxidizing Flame:** - **O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1.1 : 1** (e.g., 1.5 : 1). - **Appearance:** Short, pointed, purplish inner cone; short, harsh, hissing outer envelope. - **Chemistry:** Excess oxygen; atmosphere is oxidizing. - **Use:** Welding of **brass and bronze** (oxidizes Zn and Mn impurities and evaporates them); also used for fusion welding of mild steel where slight surface oxidation is acceptable. **Not used for steel in critical applications.** ``` Sketch reference: SECTION 4.3 above (Three-flame block diagram). ``` --- ### 📝 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — **[Choice 2]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, Module 3]** **Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the **principle and procedure of oxygen cutting** of mild steel. State the conditions necessary for successful oxygen cutting. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A mild-steel plate of dimensions **1200 mm × 800 mm × 15 mm** is to be cut by the oxygen-cutting process. Calculate the **(i)** heat required to preheat the plate to ignition temperature of 870 °C from ambient 30 °C, and **(ii)** the volume of acetylene consumed assuming 50 % thermal efficiency. Given: $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 0.486\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, heat of combustion of C₂H₂ = **58,000 kJ/m³**. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Principle:** Oxygen cutting separates ferrous metal by a **chemical reaction** of the metal with a stream of pure oxygen, after the metal has been pre-heated to its ignition temperature. **Procedure (7 sequential steps):** 1. **Set-up:** Mount the cutting torch on the oxy-fuel gas train; verify regulator pressures (O₂ at ~0.3 MPa, C₂H₂ at ~0.07 MPa). 2. **Ignition:** Open acetylene valve, light with friction lighter, then open oxygen valve to establish a neutral pre-heat flame. 3. **Pre-heat:** Hold the flame 3 – 6 mm above the starting point of the cut; heat until the surface becomes **cherry red (~870 °C)**. 4. **Initiate cut:** Press the oxygen-cutting lever; pure oxygen jet issues through the central orifice at high pressure (~0.7 – 1.0 MPa). 5. **Oxidation:** Iron reacts exothermically with the oxygen jet: **Fe + ½ O₂ → FeO + 7.36 kJ/g**. 6. **Dross ejection:** Molten FeO is blown out of the kerf by the high-velocity oxygen jet, exposing fresh metal. 7. **Travelling:** Move the torch at a uniform cutting speed along the marked line; the cut proceeds automatically once initiated. **Three Necessary Conditions for Successful Oxygen Cutting:** - **Condition 1:** The ignition temperature of the metal in pure oxygen must be **below its melting point** (for iron: 870 °C ignition vs. 1538 °C melting — satisfied). - **Condition 2:** The metal oxide must be **molten and fluid** at the cutting temperature so it can be ejected by the jet (FeO melts at 1377 °C — satisfied). - **Condition 3:** The combustion of the metal must be **exothermic** to provide sustained heat for self-propagation (iron oxidation provides ~70 % of the energy — satisfied). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical **Given:** - Plate dimensions: 1200 mm × 800 mm × 15 mm - $T_i = 30\ \text{°C}$, $T_{ign} = 870\ \text{°C}$ - $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 0.486\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - $Q_{C_2H_2} = 58{,}000\ \text{kJ/m}^3$, $\eta = 50\ \% = 0.50$ **Step 1 — Compute plate volume:** $$V_p = 1.2 \times 0.8 \times 0.015 = 0.0144\ \text{m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute plate mass:** $$\begin{aligned} m &= \rho \cdot V_p \\ &= 7850 \times 0.0144 \\ &= 113.04\ \text{kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute temperature rise:** $$\Delta T = 870 - 30 = 840\ \text{K}$$ **Step 4 — Compute preheat heat:** $$\begin{aligned} Q_{pre} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\ &= 113.04 \times 0.486 \times 840 \\ &= 46{,}145.51\ \text{kJ} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{pre} \approx 46{,}146\ \text{kJ} \approx 46.15\ \text{MJ}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute acetylene volume required (with 50 % efficiency):** $$\begin{aligned} V_{C_2H_2} &= \frac{Q_{pre}}{Q_{C_2H_2} \cdot \eta} \\ &= \frac{46{,}145.51}{58{,}000 \times 0.50} \\ &= \frac{46{,}145.51}{29{,}000} \\ &= 1.591\ \text{m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{V_{C_2H_2} \approx 1.59\ \text{m}^3}$$ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mark-losing traps on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing flame ratios:** Students write "neutral = 1 : 1" instead of **1.1 : 1**. The 0.1 extra is for the secondary combustion's atmospheric oxygen. Lose 1 mark. > 2. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always state kJ, MJ, m³ clearly. Partial marks are lost for unitless answers. > 3. **Wrong colour of hoses:** Green = oxygen, Red = acetylene (BS EN ISO 3821). Some countries use different codes — but in India/Europe, this is the standard. > 4. **Cylinder pressure mis-stated:** Acetylene maximum = **1.7 MPa (17 bar)**, NOT 15 MPa. Charging above this causes explosive decomposition. Examiners test this. > 5. **Confusing "backfire" and "flashback":** Backfire = flame pops back into nozzle momentarily; Flashback = flame travels continuously into hose — a much more dangerous event. Examiners test the distinction. > 6. **Confusing "carburizing" with "oxidizing":** Carburizing = fuel-rich (excess C); Oxidizing = fuel-lean (excess O₂). Easy to mix up under exam stress. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Gas Welding & Oxygen Cutting** - **Gas welding = OFW (Oxy-Fuel Gas Welding); AWS code = OFW-A for acetylene.** - **Oxygen cutting = OC; relies on chemical oxidation of iron by pure O₂ jet.** - **Acetylene produces the hottest practical flame (~3160 °C–3480 °C).** - **Acetylene cylinder max safe pressure = 1.7 MPa (17 bar) due to instability risk above.** - **Three flames: Carburizing (< 1.1:1), Neutral (= 1.1:1), Oxidizing (> 1.1:1).** - **Neutral flame is the standard for mild-steel welding.** - **Hoses: Green = O₂ (right-hand thread), Red = C₂H₂ (left-hand thread) — non-interchangeable.** - **Flashback arrestors are MANDATORY on both regulator and torch ends.** - **Oxygen cutting needs: (1) T_ign < T_melt, (2) fluid oxide, (3) exothermic metal oxidation.** - **Iron ignition in O₂ ≈ 870 °C; iron melting ≈ 1538 °C — gap is exploited by the cut.** - **Heat of combustion of C₂H₂ ≈ 58,000 kJ/m³ (≈ 1300 kJ/mol).** - **Specific heat of iron ≈ 0.46 – 0.486 kJ/(kg·K); density ≈ 7850 kg/m³.** - **Pre-heat energy: $Q_{pre} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.** - **Cutting speed inversely proportional to thickness (empirical $v_c \approx 350/t$ for mild steel, mm/min when t in mm).** - **Kerf width ≈ 2 – 6 mm depending on plate thickness; account for it in nesting.** - **Dross = molten iron oxide (FeO) ejected during the cut.** - **Materials unsuitable for oxy-fuel cutting: stainless steel, cast iron, aluminium, copper — due to refractory oxides or low ignition temps.** - **Backfire = momentary; Flashback = sustained and dangerous.** - **Always use friction lighter; never use matches/oil lamps to light the torch.** - **Stand the cylinder upright, secured by chain; never use oil/grease on O₂ fittings.** ---

topics.title: Resistance Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resistance Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Resistance Welding (RW)** is a non-fusion, thermo-electric welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced at the faying surfaces (the contacting interface between the two workpieces) by the heat obtained from resistance to the flow of an electric current through the parts, with the application of pressure. The heat is generated *internally* within the workpiece itself, not from an external flame or arc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Resistance welding is one of the most widely used welding processes in mass production industries (automotive, sheet metal, white goods, aerospace). Unlike arc welding, it produces **no open arc, no spatter, and no filler metal**, making it inherently safer regarding radiation hazards — but introduces severe **electrical, crush, and burn hazards** that must be controlled under the Factories Act 1948 and Indian Electricity Rules 1956.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Toaster Principle")

Think of a **domestic electric toaster**:
1. You push bread into a slot → two hot metal plates press against the bread (this is the **electrode force / pressure**).
2. Inside, a nichrome wire resists the flow of electricity → the wire gets hot (this is **I²R heat generation**).
3. After a fixed time, a spring pops the bread up (this is the **weld time cycle**).
4. The bread's surface is now "welded" (toasted) exactly where contact occurred.

In resistance welding:
- The **bread** = your two metal sheets to be joined.
- The **nichrome wire** = the bulk metal (which has its own electrical resistance).
- The **hot plates** = water-cooled **copper electrodes** that carry current AND apply pressure.
- The **toast** = the **nugget** (the fused weld zone formed at the interface).

The genius is that the metal's *own* resistance to current flow is what creates the heat — so heat is generated *exactly* at the joint where you need it, not on the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because heat is generated at the interface (highest contact resistance), resistance welding is extremely **energy-efficient** (~80–90% electrical-to-thermal efficiency) compared to arc welding (typically 60–70%). This makes it ideal for high-volume production lines.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Law

The fundamental law governing all resistance welding processes is **Joule's Law of Heating**, discovered in 1841 by James Prescott Joule.

$$H = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t$$

Where each variable is a critical, *independently controllable* weld parameter:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Operator Action |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $H$ | Heat generated at the interface | **Joules (J)** or **Calories (cal)** | Result (cannot directly set) |
| $I$ | Welding current (RMS) | **kA** (kilo-Amperes) | Tapped on transformer |
| $R$ | Total resistance of the weld stack | **micro-ohms (μΩ)** | Determined by material, surface, pressure |
| $t$ | Weld time | **cycles** (1 cycle = 1/50 s in India) | Set on controller timer |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Do not confuse this with arc welding's heat equation. In arc welding, $H = V \cdot I \cdot t$ (the arc voltage is what matters). In resistance welding, **voltage across the workpiece is negligible** (typically 1–10 V), and heat is *purely* ohmic (I²R). This is a KTU favorite trick question.

---

## 1.4 Position in KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus

Within **Module 3 (Welding Safety)**, Resistance Welding is taught alongside Gas Welding, Arc Welding, and Special Welding Processes. The course outcomes typically mapped are:

- **CO3:** Identify hazards associated with various welding processes and recommend appropriate PPE and engineering controls.
- **CO4:** Apply statutory requirements (Factories Act, IS 818, IS 2879) to welding shop design and worker protection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Resistance Welding — Step by Step

When current is passed through two sheets pressed together, the total resistance $R$ is **not** just the bulk resistance. It is the sum of *five* distinct resistances acting in series:

$$R_{total} = 2R_{bulk} + 2R_{electrode\text{-}work} + R_{interface}$$

Let us break down *why* each term exists:

### Step 1 — The Two Bulk Resistances ($2R_{bulk}$)
Current must travel from each electrode, *through the body* of each sheet, down to the faying surface. The bulk metal offers resistance proportional to length and inversely to cross-sectional area. This is governed by:

$$R_{bulk} = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is electrical resistivity (Ω·m), $L$ is the path length (m), and $A$ is cross-section (m²). Since current enters one sheet and leaves the other, we get **two** such terms.

### Step 2 — The Two Electrode-Work Contact Resistances ($2R_{e-w}$)
The copper electrode does *not* molecularly fuse with steel. Contact occurs only at microscopic asperities (tiny peaks). The current must squeeze through these tiny points, creating localized high resistance → localized heating at the *electrode face*. This is why electrodes wear out — they overheat and deform.

### Step 3 — The Interface Resistance ($R_{interface}$)
This is the **most important** term. Where the two sheets touch, microscopic surface roughness, oxides, and contaminants mean current flows through a few small "a-spots." This is where the **weld nugget** actually forms. The interface resistance is what we *want* to be high (for heat), but also what we *control* via surface preparation and electrode force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept — Why Pressure Matters:** Higher electrode force flattens the asperities, which has two opposing effects: it *increases* the real area of contact (lowering $R$), but it *also* expels oxides and ensures a clean metallic bridge. The operator must find the **sweet-spot force** — too low gives a cold joint (lack of fusion), too high gives a thin, expulsion-prone joint (extrudes molten metal out, a serious burn and fire hazard).

### Step 4 — Time-Dependent Resistance Behavior
Resistance is *not* constant during a weld. A typical plot of $R$ vs. time has a characteristic shape:

1. **Initial high spike** — surface oxides and roughness give high contact resistance.
2. **Rapid drop** — as metal softens with heat, asperities collapse, resistance plummets.
3. **Steady low plateau** — bulk resistance dominates; metal is approaching melting.
4. **Slight rise at end** — molten nugget formation; resistivity of liquid metal is higher.

## 2.2 Types of Resistance Welding (The KTU "Big Four")

| Type | Weld Geometry | Current Range | Electrode Configuration | Typical Industry Use |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Spot Welding (RSW)** | Discrete circular nuggets | 5–20 kA | Two opposed cylindrical Cu electrodes | Automotive body-in-white, sheet metal cabinets |
| **Seam Welding (RSEW)** | Continuous leak-tight seam | 8–15 kA | Roller electrodes (like small wheels) | Fuel tanks, radiators, milk cans |
| **Projection Welding (RPW)** | Pre-formed embossments melt first | 8–18 kA | Flat platen electrodes | Cross-wire welding, nuts to plates |
| **Butt Welding (RBW)** | End-to-end joint in bars/rods | 20–200 kA | Jaws grip the two pieces | Wire drawing feedstock, chain links, rail joints |

Each is again subdivided:

- **Butt Welding** has two sub-types:
  - *Flash Butt Welding:* Pieces are brought close; arc flashes between them, melting tips progressively; then forged together suddenly. Used for large sections (rails, axles).
  - *Upset Butt Welding:* Pieces are clamped, current passed, then forced together under high pressure. Solid-state process, no flash.

- **Seam Welding** has subtypes by current interruption pattern:
  - *Roll Spot:* Like a series of overlapping spot welds.
  - *Continuous Seam:* Constant current, produces a hermetic seam.
  - *Intermittent / Pulsation:* Current on for 2–4 cycles, off for 1–2 cycles, prevents overheating of rollers.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — KTU Part B questions (14 marks) almost always require using at least 2 of these.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Engineering Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $H = I^{2} R t$ | Joule's Law | Total heat generated (Joules) |
| 2 | $H_{cal} = 0.239 \cdot I^{2} R t$ | $0.239$ = J→cal conversion | Useful when thermal data is in calories |
| 3 | $R_{total} = 2R_{b} + 2R_{e} + R_{i}$ | $b$=bulk, $e$=electrode, $i$=interface | Design optimization |
| 4 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Resistivity geometry | Estimating bulk resistance |
| 5 | $P = F / A$ | Electrode pressure | Determining electrode force |
| 6 | $Q_{nugget} = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T + m \cdot L_{f}$ | $c$=sp. heat, $L_f$=latent heat | Theoretical heat *required* |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = H_{used} / H_{generated}$ | Thermal efficiency (typically 0.5–0.9) | Process efficiency audit |
| 8 | $Weld\text{-}time = N_{c} / f$ | $N_c$=number of mains cycles, $f$=50 Hz in India | Converting cycles to seconds |
| 9 | $Nugget\text{ }diameter = 4\sqrt{t}$ (empirical, low-carbon steel) | $t$=sheet thickness in mm | Quick nugget size estimate |
| 10 | $I_{weld} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{t}$ | Empirical current sizing | Setting transformer tap |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule (KTU 2024 Exam Scripts):** Never use the vertical pipe $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside a table cell. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $(x)$ depending on context. Examiners award 0 if markdown rendering breaks.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Safety

| Process Parameter | Safety Consequence if Mismanaged |
|:---|:---|
| Too high current ($I$) | Expulsion of molten metal → **burns, fire, eye injury** |
| Too low current | Cold joint → **in-service failure** (structural collapse) |
| Too short time | Incomplete fusion → **cracking under cyclic load** |
| Too long time | Electrode pitting, water leak → **electrocution hazard** |
| Too high pressure | Electrode mushrooming, machine ram over-travel → **crush injury** |
| Too low pressure | Excessive flash/expulsion → **arc-eye from UV flash** |
| Inadequate cooling | Electrode melts → catastrophic short → **operator electrocution** |
| Poor ventilation | Ozone + NOₓ accumulation → **respiratory illness** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Joule's Law to Total Energy for a Spot Weld

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):**
A resistance spot welding machine operates at a secondary current of $I = 8000 \text{ A}$ (RMS). The total resistance of the weld stack is $R = 200 \ \mu\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6} \ \Omega$. The weld time is $t = 12$ cycles of 50 Hz supply. Find the total heat energy generated in **Joules** and in **calories**.

### Step 1 — Convert weld time from cycles to seconds

We know that in India the supply frequency is:

$$f = 50 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s}$$

Therefore the weld time in seconds is:

$$t = 12 \times 0.02 = 0.24 \text{ s}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* 1 electrical cycle of 50 Hz AC lasts 0.02 s. The timer in resistance welding controllers is set in cycles, not seconds, because 50 Hz mains has 100 zero-crossings per second — using cycles gives a clean integer time base synchronized to the supply.

### Step 2 — Apply Joule's Law of Heating

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t \\
  &= (8000)^{2} \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.24 \\
  &= 64{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0002 \times 0.24 \\
  &= 64 \times 10^{6} \times 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \\
  &= 3072 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* $(8000)^2 = 6.4 \times 10^7$. Multiply by $200 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ to get $1.28 \times 10^4$. Multiply by 0.24 to get $3072$.

### Step 3 — Convert Joules to calories (if required by question)

Using $1 \text{ cal} = 4.186 \text{ J}$, so $1 \text{ J} = 0.239 \text{ cal}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{cal} &= 0.239 \times 3072 \\
        &= 734.2 \text{ cal}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer (boxed)

> **$H = 3072 \text{ J} = 734.2 \text{ cal}$**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Balance for Nugget Formation

**Problem:** Two mild-steel sheets, each of thickness $t_s = 1.2$ mm and density $\rho_m = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, are spot-welded. The nugget diameter is $d = 5$ mm (assume cylindrical). Find the *minimum theoretical heat* required to form the nugget, given specific heat $c = 0.49 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, melting point rise $\Delta T = 1500 - 25 = 1475$ K, and latent heat of fusion $L_f = 272 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

### Step 1 — Compute nugget volume (cylindrical)

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{nugget} &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \times t_{sheet} \\
           &= \frac{\pi \times (0.005)^{2}}{4} \times 0.0012 \\
           &= \frac{3.1416 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4} \times 0.0012 \\
           &= 1.9635 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.0012 \\
           &= 2.356 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Compute nugget mass

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \rho_m \times V_{nugget} \\
  &= 7850 \times 2.356 \times 10^{-8} \\
  &= 1.849 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} \\
  &= 0.1849 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute heat to raise to melting point (sensible heat)

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{sensible} &= m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T \\
             &= 1.849 \times 10^{-4} \times 490 \times 1475 \\
             &= 1.849 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.2275 \times 10^{5} \\
             &= 133.6 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* $c = 0.49 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} = 490 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$. Multiply $c \cdot \Delta T = 490 \times 1475 = 7.2275 \times 10^5$ J/kg. Then multiply by mass.

### Step 4 — Compute latent heat for phase change

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{latent} &= m \cdot L_f \\
           &= 1.849 \times 10^{-4} \times 272{,}000 \\
           &= 50.3 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Total minimum heat required

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{min} &= Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} \\
        &= 133.6 + 50.3 \\
        &= 183.9 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

> **$Q_{min} = 183.9 \text{ J}$** is the *useful* heat. Comparing with the 3072 J generated in the previous derivation, thermal efficiency is:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{183.9}{3072} = 0.0598 = 5.98\%$$

This low number is *typical* for poorly designed welds. **A well-tuned spot weld has $\eta_{th} \approx 50$–$80\%$.** The implication: a lot of heat is being wasted to cooling water, surrounding metal, and radiation — a key safety concern, as it heats the electrodes and workplace.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Empirical Sizing of Welding Transformer

The welding transformer steps down the mains voltage (415 V three-phase) to a few volts at very high current. The relationship between primary and secondary is:

$$\frac{V_p}{V_s} = \frac{N_p}{N_s} = \frac{I_s}{I_p}$$

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 415 V welding transformer has a turns ratio of 80:1. Find (a) secondary voltage, (b) secondary current if primary draws 80 A, and (c) the power delivered to the weld stack if resistance is 250 μΩ.

### Part (a) — Secondary voltage

$$\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{V_p}{N_p / N_s} = \frac{415}{80} = 5.19 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b) — Secondary current

$$\begin{aligned}
I_s &= I_p \times \frac{N_p}{N_s} = 80 \times 80 = 6400 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (c) — Power to weld

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= I_s^{2} \times R \\
  &= (6400)^{2} \times 250 \times 10^{-6} \\
  &= 40.96 \times 10^{6} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= 10{,}240 \text{ W} = 10.24 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Safety Insight:** Even at 5.19 V secondary, the available current is lethal. The hazard is *not* voltage but **current** and **arc-flash / burn risk** at the secondary terminals. All secondary cabling must be heavily insulated, and operators must never wear conductive jewellery or wet gloves.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Resistance vs. Other Welding Processes

| Attribute | Resistance Welding | Arc Welding (SMAW) | Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene) | Laser Welding |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Heat source | I²R internal | External arc | External flame | Focused beam |
| Open arc / UV | **No** | Yes (severe) | No | Yes (Class 4 laser) |
| Filler metal | **Not required** | Required | Required (rod) | Optional |
| Operator skill level | Low (automated) | High | Medium | Very high |
| Automation suitability | **Excellent** | Moderate | Poor | Excellent |
| Typical safety priority | Electrical, crush | UV eye, fumes, electric shock | Combustion, asphyxiation | Laser, fume |
| Energy efficiency | **80–90%** | 60–70% | 30–50% | 30–40% |
| Sheet metal suitability | **Best for thin** | Limited | Poor | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Resistance Spot Welding Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M3PH[3-Phase 415V Mains] --> CB[Main Circuit Breaker]
    CB --> CTRL[Electronic Weld Controller]
    CTRL --> SCR[Thyristor / Ignitron Stack]
    SCR --> TX[Step-Down Welding Transformer]
    TX --> SEC[Secondary Loop Low V High I]
    SEC --> UPPER[Upper Copper Electrode Holder]
    SEC --> LOWER[Lower Copper Electrode Holder]
    UPPER --> WS[Work Stack Two Sheets]
    LOWER --> WS
    UPPER --> PC[Air or Hydraulic Pneumatic Cylinder]
    PC --> CTRL
    WCOOL[Chilled Water Recirculator] -. Cools .-> UPPER
    WCOOL -. Cools .-> LOWER
    WCOOL -. Cools .-> TX
    CTRL --> OP[Operator Console with Foot Switch]
    style M3PH fill:#fbb,stroke:#900
    style TX fill:#bbf,stroke:#009
    style WS fill:#bfb,stroke:#060
    style WCOOL fill:#ff9,stroke:#960
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red node** = power input (mains supply). Must be locked-out during maintenance (LOTO procedure).
- **Blue node** = transformer. Heavy, oil- or water-cooled; secondary must never be opened under load.
- **Green node** = the actual weld zone. Only accessible zone during operation; fully guarded.
- **Yellow node** = cooling water circuit. Critical for electrode life and electrical safety — water leak into electrodes causes phase-to-ground short.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Weld Cycle Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Loads Workpieces] --> B[Foot Pedal Pressed]
    B --> C{Pressure OK?}
    C -- No --> Z[Reject and Alarm]
    C -- Yes --> D[Squeeze Time Delay]
    D --> E[Electrodes Close and Clamp]
    E --> F[Weld Current ON I2R Heating]
    F --> G{Heat Sufficient?}
    G -- No --> H[Extend Weld Time or Current]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Hold Forge Phase Current Off Pressure Maintained]
    I --> J[Off Time Cooling]
    J --> K[Electrode Retract]
    K --> L[Weld Complete Inspect Nugget]
    L --> M{Defects?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Reclaim or Reject]
    M -- No --> O[Move to Next Station]
    style F fill:#fbb,stroke:#900
    style I fill:#bfb,stroke:#060
    style Z fill:#999,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Squeeze time** (typically 5–20 cycles): allows electrode pressure to stabilize *before* current flows. Critical for safety — prevents a "no-pressure arc" which would splatter molten metal.
- **Weld time** (3–30 cycles): current flows, I²R heating occurs.
- **Hold/forge time** (5–30 cycles): current OFF but pressure remains. Allows the nugget to solidify under pressure, preventing cracking.
- **Off time**: full release; operator can unload.

## 4.3 Hazard Mapping — Resistance Welding Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HZD [Identified Hazards per IS 818 and IS 2879]
        H1[Electrical shock from secondary loop]
        H2[Crush injury from pneumatic ram]
        H3[Arc flash from contactor failure]
        H4[Burn from expelled molten metal]
        H5[Eye injury from UV if contactor sticks]
        H6[Fume inhalation ozone and NOx]
        H7[Slip hazard from water cooling leak]
        H8[Noise from pneumatic exhaust]
    end
    subgraph CTRL [Engineering Controls]
        C1[Two-hand trip circuit]
        C2[Light curtain or fixed guard]
        C3[Contactor watchdog and redundancy]
        C4[Heat resistant gloves and apron]
        C5[Auto darkening welding helmet]
        C6[Local exhaust ventilation LEV]
        C7[Anti skid floor and drip tray]
        C8[Silencer on pneumatic exhaust]
    end
    H1 --- C1
    H2 --- C2
    H3 --- C3
    H4 --- C4
    H5 --- C5
    H6 --- C6
    H7 --- C7
    H8 --- C8
    style H1 fill:#f99
    style H2 fill:#f99
    style H3 fill:#f99
    style H4 fill:#f99
    style H5 fill:#f99
    style H6 fill:#f99
    style H7 fill:#f99
    style H8 fill:#f99
    style C1 fill:#9f9
    style C2 fill:#9f9
    style C3 fill:#9f9
    style C4 fill:#9f9

    style C6 fill:#9f9
    style C7 fill:#9f9
    style C8 fill:#9f9
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left column** is a hazard register — typical KTU question asks "List 5 hazards in resistance welding."
- The **right column** is the engineering control hierarchy (elimination → engineering control → administrative → PPE). KTU expects students to map hazard → control.
- Notice that **PPE (C4, C5)** is the *last* line of defense; primary controls are *guarding* and *interlocks*.
