We assume the worst case: the hand moves *until the moment* the protective device (light curtain) detects it.

### Step 2 — Define the stopping time of the press

When the protective device is tripped, the time from the trip signal to the ram coming to rest is the **total stop time $T_s$**:

$$T_s = T_{detect} + T_{logic} + T_{brake}$$

where $T_{detect}$ is the sensor response time, $T_{logic}$ is the relay/PLC scan time, and $T_{brake}$ is the mechanical brake engagement time.

### Step 3 — How far does the hand travel during $T_s$?

The hand keeps moving during the entire stopping time. The distance travelled is:

$$D_{hand} = V_h \cdot T_s$$

### Step 4 — Add the safety penetration factor

The light curtain has a finite "penetration" before detection — the hand can cross a small additional distance $d_{pf}$ before the curtain reliably trips. This is the **penetration factor** (also called *depth of penetration* or *approach distance*).

$$D_{hand,total} = V_h \cdot T_s + d_{pf}$$

### Step 5 — Equate to the safety distance

The **safety distance $D_s$** (how far the light curtain must be placed from the die) must be **at least** equal to the distance the hand can travel after the trip:

$$\boxed{D_s = V_h \cdot T_s + d_{pf}}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

If $D_s$ is *less* than this value, the hand could reach the die **before** the ram stops → injury. Therefore, the **inequality form** is the one used in design:

$$D_s \ge V_h \cdot T_s + d_{pf}$$

This is the foundational equation for **all safeguarding devices** on power presses: light curtains, safety mats, two-hand controls, and even guard interlocks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Truth Table for Three-Mode Operation

Let us derive the truth table for the **clutch relay coil C** under the three modes. Define inputs:

- $M$ = Mode selector (00 = Off, 01 = Inch, 10 = Single, 11 = Continuous)
- $H_1, H_2$ = Two-hand buttons (1 = pressed)
- $G$ = Guard closed (1 = closed)
- $E$ = E-stop released (1 = released, 0 = pressed)
- $F$ = Flywheel at speed (1 = up to speed)
- $I$ = Inch button (1 = pressed)
- $AR$ = Anti-repeat satisfied (1 = both hands previously released)

We model the *clutch energise* signal in each mode. The relay coil $C$ follows:

| Mode $M$ | $H_1$ | $H_2$ | $G$ | $E$ | $F$ | $I$ | $AR$ | $C$ (Clutch) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00 (Off) | x | x | x | x | x | x | x | **0** | Mode off — no clutch permitted |
| 01 (Inch) | x | x | x | 1 | 1 | 1 | x | **1** | Clutch follows $I$ directly (no anti-repeat) |
| 01 (Inch) | x | x | x | 0 | x | x | x | **0** | E-stop pressed |
| 10 (Single) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **1** | All conditions satisfied, single stroke starts |
| 10 (Single) | 1 | 0 | x | 1 | x | 0 | x | **0** | Both hands not pressed |
| 10 (Single) | 1 | 1 | 0 | x | x | 0 | x | **0** | Guard open |
| 11 (Cont.) | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **1** | Continuous, all safeties met |
| 11 (Cont.) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0** | Anti-repeat: must release and re-press |

This truth table is what a **KTU examiner** expects you to draw for a 14-mark "Explain the operating modes with logic" question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stopping Time of a Flywheel–Clutch–Brake System

The flywheel stores kinetic energy:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the flywheel (in $\mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$) and $\omega$ is the angular speed in rad/s.

For a motor running at $n$ RPM:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} = \frac{\pi n}{30} \quad [\mathrm{rad/s}]$$

The brake must dissipate this energy (plus the energy added by the motor during the stop) in the braking time $t_{stop}$. The average brake torque required is:

$$T_{brake} = \frac{I \cdot \omega}{t_{stop}} - T_{motor,avg}$$

The term $T_{motor,avg}$ accounts for the fact that the motor (if not disconnected) keeps delivering torque during the stop. In a fail-safe design, the motor is **disconnected simultaneously with brake application** (via the same contactor), so $T_{motor,avg} \approx 0$, simplifying to:

$$T_{brake} \approx \frac{I \cdot \omega}{t_{stop}}$$

### Worked Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Given:** Flywheel moment of inertia $I = 25 \, \mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$, motor speed $n = 1440 \, \mathrm{RPM}$, required stop time $t_{stop} = 0.10 \, \mathrm{s}$. Motor is disconnected during braking. Find the required brake torque.

**Step 1:** Compute $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{\pi \cdot 1440}{30} = 48\pi \approx 150.8 \quad \mathrm{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the brake torque:

$$T_{brake} = \frac{25 \times 150.8}{0.10} = \frac{3770}{0.10} = 37{,}700 \quad \mathrm{N \cdot m}$$

**Step 3:** Comment:

$$T_{brake} = 37.7 \, \mathrm{kN \cdot m}$$

This is the **minimum** brake torque. In practice, a safety factor of **1.5 to 2.0** is applied, so the selected brake would be rated for **56 – 75 kN·m**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Clutch Solenoid Current and Power Dissipation

The clutch solenoid coil has resistance $R_{coil}$ (typical 30 – 80 Ω for 110 V DC). The inrush current is:

$$I_{inrush} = \frac{V}{R_{coil}}$$

Once the armature pulls in, the inductance drops, and a **holding current** is maintained at lower voltage using an economiser circuit:

$$I_{hold} \approx 0.3 \cdot I_{inrush}$$

The power dissipated during holding is:

$$P_{hold} = I_{hold}^2 \cdot R_{coil}$$

This is why solenoid coils are rated for **intermittent duty** (e.g., 10% duty cycle) or have separate hold coils — to avoid thermal burn-out.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Safeguarding Devices

| Safeguard | Response Time | Suitability for High-Speed Press | Cost | Operator Convenience |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Two-hand control | ~50 ms | Moderate (≤ 100 SPM) | Low | High (no obstruction) |
| Light curtain | ~10–20 ms | High (≤ 400 SPM) | Medium–High | Very High |
| Safety mat | ~30–50 ms | Low (≤ 60 SPM) | Medium | High |
| Guard interlock switch | ~15 ms | High | Low | Medium (must open guard) |
| Pullback (cable) | Operator-dependent | Low | Low | Low |

This table is a high-yield KTU comparison question (commonly asked for 7 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule**: every node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `PPower`, `PClutch`, `PBrake`) — no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are used as standalone node names. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text, with no bold/italic markers.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Power Press Electric Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[OPERATOR LAYER Two-Hand Station Selector Switch Foot Switch Guard Interlock E-Stop Light Curtain]
    B[LOGIC LAYER Mode Selector Relay Anti-Repeat Relay Timer 0.5 s Centrifugal Switch NC Contacts]
    C[DRIVE LAYER Clutch Contactor Brake Contactor Solenoid Coil]
    D[POWER LAYER 3-Phase Induction Motor Flywheel DOL Starter Control Transformer 110V AC]
    E[MECHANICAL LAYER Crankshaft Ram and Slide Die Area]
    F[BRAKE MECHANICAL Spring Applied Brake Disc Failsafe on Power Loss]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    C -. energises .-> E
    F -. always-on passive .-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** Information flows top-down from the operator's input through the logic layer, which decides whether to energise the clutch coil. The brake is shown as a **dotted** (passive) influence on the mechanical layer — it acts *whenever* the clutch coil is *not* energised, regardless of any active command.

## 4.2 State Diagram for the Three Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ModeOff
    ModeOff --> ModeInch : Selector at INCH
    ModeOff --> ModeSingle : Selector at SINGLE
    ModeOff --> ModeContinuous : Selector at CONTINUOUS

    ModeInch --> ModeOff : Selector turned
    ModeInch --> ModeOff : EStop pressed
    ModeInch --> ModeOff : Inch button released
    ModeInch --> BrakeApply : Any trip condition

    ModeSingle --> BrakeApply : One complete stroke
    ModeSingle --> BrakeApply : Two hand release
    ModeSingle --> BrakeApply : EStop or Guard open

    ModeContinuous --> BrakeApply : EStop pressed
    ModeContinuous --> BrakeApply : Guard open
    ModeContinuous --> BrakeApply : Stop button pressed
    ModeContinuous --> BrakeApply : Anti repeat violated

    BrakeApply --> ModeSingle : Reset and reselect
    BrakeApply --> ModeContinuous : Reset and reselect
    BrakeApply --> ModeInch : Selector at INCH
```

**Reading the diagram:** Any mode transitions to `BrakeApply` on a trip condition. From there, the operator must re-arm the press by returning the selector to OFF and re-selecting the desired mode. This is the **inherent safety lockout** of the system.

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Single-Stroke Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant LS as Logic Relays
    participant CC as Clutch Contactor
    participant BR as Brake
    participant CR as Crankshaft and Ram

    Op->>LS: Press Two Hand Buttons within 0.5s
    LS->>LS: Check Guard E-Stop Flywheel Anti-Repeat
    LS->>CC: Energise Clutch Coil
    CC->>BR: Release Brake Spring
    BR-->>CR: Ram begins downward stroke
    CR->>CR: Stroke 0 to 180 degrees
    CR->>LS: Limit switch at TDC Top Dead Centre
    LS->>CC: De-energise Clutch Coil
    CC->>BR: Apply Brake Spring
    BR-->>CR: Ram stops at TDC
    Note over LS,CR: Anti-Repeat: Both hands MUST be released before next cycle
    Op->>LS: Release both hands
    Op->>LS: Re-press both hands
    LS->>CC: Energise again, new cycle
```

## 4.4 Ladder Logic Interpretation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L1 Control Phase] --> R1[NC E-Stop]
    R1 --> R2[NC Guard Limit Switch]
    R2 --> R3[NC Centrifugal Switch Motor at Speed]
    R3 --> R4[Mode Selector Contact SINGLE]
    R4 --> R5[NC Two Hand Button 1 Anti-Repeat]
    R5 --> R6[NC Two Hand Button 2 Anti-Repeat]
    R6 --> R7[NO Two Hand Button 1 Start]
    R7 --> R8[NO Two Hand Button 2 Start]
    R8 --> C1[Clutch Contactor Coil]
    C1 --> L2[L2 Neutral]

    C1 -. sealing contact NC .-> R7
    C1 -. sealing contact NC .-> R8
```

**Reading the diagram:** Current flows from `L1` to `L2` through the chain. The clutch coil `C1` only energises if **every** NC contact is closed (i.e., the safety is in its *safe* state) AND both NO hand buttons close simultaneously. The dotted line from `C1` back to `R7`/`R8` shows the **sealing (latching) contact** that maintains the clutch engaged after the buttons are released — but the sealing path is via the NC buttons, so wedging a button down breaks the seal and enforces anti-repeat.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]

**Define the term "fail-safe by de-energisation" as applied to the clutch and brake of a power press. Why is this principle used?**

**Model Answer:**

"Fail-safe by de-energisation" means that the **brake is applied by spring force, and the brake is *released* by an electrically energised solenoid**. In normal operation, the solenoid is energised, overcoming the spring, releasing the brake and engaging the clutch. If power is lost — or any safety device trips — the solenoid de-energises, the spring snaps the brake onto the disc, and the clutch disengages automatically.

**Why used:** A spring is a *passive* energy source. It does not require electricity to "do work." Therefore, *any* failure (power failure, broken wire, E-stop, guard open, two-hand release) results in the **safe state** — the brake on, the ram stopped. This is the foundational reason every power press uses spring-applied, electrically-released brakes.

*(Examiner's note: Use the exact phrase "spring-applied, electrically released" for full marks.)*

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]

**What is "anti-repeat" protection on a power press? Briefly explain how it is implemented using relay logic.**

**Model Answer:**

Anti-repeat protection prevents the operator from initiating a *continuous* cycle by wedging a weight or block on one of the two-hand buttons. It requires the operator to **release both hand buttons** before the press will accept a *new* stroke command.

**Implementation:** A sealing (latching) contact of the clutch relay is wired in **series with the normally-closed (NC) contact of each hand button**. To seal the relay (i.e., keep it ON after the buttons are released), both NC contacts must be closed — which only happens when both hand buttons are *released*. If an operator wedges one button down, its NC contact is *open*, the sealing path is broken, and the relay cannot latch. The press therefore cannot continue stroking.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]

#### (a) [7 Marks] With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a power press electric control system. Identify the role of each layer.

**Model Answer:**

The power press electric control system is organised into **four functional layers** as shown below:

| Layer | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Operator Layer | Two-hand station, selector switch, foot switch, E-stop, guard interlock switch, light curtain | Accepts human input and translates it into electrical signals |
| 2. Logic Layer | Control relays, contactors, mode-selector relay, anti-repeat relay, timer, centrifugal switch contacts | Implements the AND-of-safeties logic; decides whether clutch is permitted |
| 3. Drive Layer | Clutch contactor, brake contactor, clutch solenoid coil, rectifier | Provides the *power* interface — energises/de-energises the clutch and brake |
| 4. Power Layer | 3-phase induction motor, flywheel, DOL starter, control transformer (110 V AC) | Supplies the rotational energy stored in the flywheel |
| 5. Mechanical Layer | Crankshaft, connecting rod, ram (slide), die area | The actual moving parts performing the metal-forming operation |

**Why layered?** The layering separates the **logic decision** (which must be safe) from the **power delivery** (which is dangerous). All safety decisions happen in the low-voltage logic layer, and the high-power drive layer is only energised if the logic layer "permits" it. This separation makes the system both safe and easy to troubleshoot.

*(Examiner's note: Marks are awarded for naming all 4–5 layers, listing 3+ components per layer, and stating the "fail-safe by de-energisation" principle at the drive layer.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the operating logic for "Single Stroke" mode. Draw the truth table and the sequence of events from operator input to ram stopping at TDC.

**Model Answer:**

In **Single Stroke** mode, one complete press cycle (one full revolution of the crankshaft) occurs for *each* valid operator input. The cycle proceeds as follows:

1. **Pre-conditions:** Selector at SINGLE, E-stop released, guard closed, flywheel at speed, anti-repeat satisfied (both hands previously released).
2. **Operator action:** Both hand buttons pressed **within 0.5 s** of each other.
3. **Logic check:** All NC safety contacts in series are closed, all NO hand contacts close. The clutch contactor coil energises.
4. **Mechanical:** Clutch solenoid pulls in, releasing the brake and engaging the flywheel-to-crank connection. The ram begins its downward stroke.
5. **Top Dead Centre (TDC):** A cam-operated limit switch at the top of the crankshaft rotation trips, breaking the sealing path of the clutch relay.
6. **Stop:** Clutch coil de-energises, brake spring applies, ram stops at TDC.
7. **Anti-repeat:** To initiate another cycle, the operator must **release both hand buttons first** (closing the NC contacts and re-arming the anti-repeat circuit) and then press them again.

**Truth Table (Single Stroke Mode):**

| Guard | E-Stop | Flywheel Speed | Anti-Repeat | Hand 1 | Hand 2 | Clutch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | x | x | x | x | x | **0** |
| x | 0 | x | x | x | x | **0** |
| x | x | 0 | x | x | x | **0** |
| x | x | x | 0 | x | x | **0** |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **0** (idle) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0** (one hand only) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0** (one hand only) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** (cycle starts) |

*(Examiner's note: 7 marks are split roughly as: 2 marks for sequence, 2 marks for truth table, 2 marks for anti-repeat explanation, 1 mark for diagram/clarity.)*

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks] *(Alternative Choice)*

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the construction and working principle of a "spring-applied, electrically released" combined clutch–brake unit used in power presses. Why is this the universally adopted design?

**Model Answer:**

A combined clutch–brake unit consists of:

- A **clutch plate** mounted on the flywheel, free to slide axially.
- A **brake disc/drum** mounted on the gear or crankshaft, stationary when the press is idle.
- A **thrust plate** carrying friction linings, moved by a solenoid through a lever mechanism.
- A set of **strong compression springs** that push the thrust plate toward the brake drum.

**Working principle:**

- **Energised state (clutch engaged, brake released):** The solenoid pulls the lever against the spring force. This pulls the thrust plate *away* from the brake drum (brake released) and pushes the clutch plate *into* contact with the flywheel face (clutch engaged). Power flows from flywheel → clutch → crankshaft → ram.

- **De-energised state (clutch disengaged, brake applied):** When the solenoid is de-energised, the spring force pushes the thrust plate *into* the brake drum (brake applied) and pulls the clutch plate *away* from the flywheel (clutch disengaged). The ram stops, and the crankshaft is held by the brake.

**Why universally adopted:** This is the textbook example of **fail-safe design**. The spring is a passive, always-available energy source. Any failure of the *active* element (electricity, solenoid, wire) results in the *safe* state — brake on. There is no "failed-dangerous" mode. This is mandated by safety engineering principles for all hazardous machinery.

*(Examiner's note: Use the phrase "single fault safety" or "passive fail-safe" for higher marks.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] A power press flywheel has moment of inertia $I = 30 \, \mathrm{kg \cdot m^2}$ and runs at $n = 960 \, \mathrm{RPM}$. The motor is disconnected instantaneously when braking starts. Calculate: (i) the angular speed, (ii) the kinetic energy stored, (iii) the average brake torque required to stop the flywheel in 0.15 s, (iv) the recommended brake torque with a factor of safety of 1.8.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Angular speed:**

$$\omega = \frac{\pi n}{30} = \frac{\pi \times 960}{30} = 32\pi \approx 100.53 \quad \mathrm{rad/s}$$

**(ii) Kinetic energy stored:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times (100.53)^2$$

$$E_k = 15 \times 10{,}106.4 = 151{,}596 \quad \mathrm{J} \approx 151.6 \quad \mathrm{kJ}$$

**(iii) Average brake torque for 0.15 s stop:**

Using $T_{brake} = \dfrac{I \omega}{t_{stop}}$ (motor disconnected):

$$T_{brake} = \frac{30 \times 100.53}{0.15} = \frac{3015.9}{0.15} = 20{,}106 \quad \mathrm{N \cdot m}$$

$$T_{brake} \approx 20.1 \quad \mathrm{kN \cdot m}$$

**(iv) Recommended brake torque with FOS = 1.8:**

$$T_{recommended} = 1.8 \times 20{,}106 = 36{,}191 \quad \mathrm{N \cdot m}$$

$$T_{recommended} \approx 36.2 \quad \mathrm{kN \cdot m}$$

**Engineering comment:** The selected brake should be rated at *at least* **36.2 kN·m** of dynamic torque. In practice, the chosen standard size is usually the next size up (e.g., 40 kN·m) to accommodate brake-lining wear over the brake's service life.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for each numerical answer, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for comment, 1 mark for the FOS application. Common mistake: students forget to disconnect the motor from the calculation. If they include motor torque, they get wrong answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing clutch-engage and brake-apply logic.** Many students write that "clutch is spring-applied" — wrong. The **brake** is spring-applied; the **clutch is engaged by spring force** but **released electrically** in the *combined* unit. Mark this clearly in the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting anti-repeat.** When explaining the two-hand control, students often describe the *simultaneity* requirement but forget the *release-before-restart* requirement (anti-repeat). Both must be mentioned for full marks.
> 3. **Mixing up the two-hand buttons with E-stop.** The two-hand control is a *start* device, not a *stop* device. E-stop is a separate, hardwired, normally-closed, red palm-button circuit.
> 4. **Numerical errors in flywheel calculations.** Using $n$ in RPM directly in $\omega$ formulas (without $\pi/30$ conversion) is the most common mistake. Always convert RPM → rad/s first.
> 5. **Omitting the fail-safe principle.** A press control without an explicit "fail-safe by de-energisation" sentence in the answer is treated as incomplete by the examiner. Always include this phrase.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **power press** is a mechanical press with a flywheel–clutch–brake system; the **electric control** decides clutch engagement and ensures fail-safe braking.
- The foundational safety equation is the **AND of all safeties**: $C = S_1 \cdot S_2 \cdot S_3 \cdot S_4 \cdot S_5 \cdot S_6 \cdot S_7$.
- **Clutch–brake design:** **Spring-applied, electrically released** → fail-safe by de-energisation. Any loss of signal = brake ON = safe state.
- **Three operating modes:** INCH (jog), SINGLE STROKE (one cycle per actuation), CONTINUOUS (auto-repeats). The mode selector is the *first* decision in the logic chain.
- **Two-hand control:** Both hand buttons must be pressed within **$\Delta t = 0.5 \, \mathrm{s}$** of each other; this is implemented by an off-delay timer.
- **Anti-repeat:** Operator must **release both hands** before the next cycle is accepted; enforced by NC contacts of hand buttons in the sealing path.
- **E-stop** is a separate, hardwired, NC, red palm-button on a dedicated loop; pressing it must drop the clutch coil *instantly*.
- **Guard interlock** is an NC switch in series with the clutch coil; opening the guard = coil de-energises = brake applies.
- **Light curtain** muting is done only during the upward (non-hazardous) portion of the stroke; this requires precise cam-switch timing.
- **Safety distance inequality:** $D_s \ge V_h \cdot T_s + d_{pf}$, with $V_h = 1.6 \, \mathrm{m/s}$.
- **Flywheel formulas:** $\omega = \pi n / 30$, $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$, $T_{brake} = I \omega / t_{stop}$.
- **Stopping time $T_s$** is the sum of detection time, logic time, and brake time; it must be kept **as short as possible** to allow higher SPM (strokes per minute) operation.
- **Daily try-out:** The operator must test the brake at the start of every shift with hands *outside* the die area.
- **Energy isolation (LOTO — Lock-Out Tag-Out):** All press maintenance requires electrical isolation (main switch off and locked) and mechanical isolation (ram blocked) before any work inside the die area.
- **Standards (general, exam-safe phrasing):** The control design is guided by the **hierarchy of controls** — Elimination, Substitution, Engineering Controls (guarding, two-hand, light curtain), Administrative Controls (training, SOPs), and PPE. Electric controls belong to the **engineering control** layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line recall (write this in your exam margin):**
> *Power press electric control = spring-applied brake + solenoid-released clutch + AND-of-safeties logic + two-hand + anti-repeat + E-stop + guard interlock — all working on the fail-safe-by-de-energisation principle.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Press Set Up and Die Removal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Press Set Up and Die Removal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Press?

A **Power Press** is a mechanical or hydraulic machine used in the manufacturing industry to shape, cut, form, punch, blank, bend, or draw sheet metal and other materials through the application of a significant compressive force. The force is generated by a flywheel driven by an electric motor, which transmits energy through a clutch and crankshaft/eccentric mechanism to a reciprocating slide (ram).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A power press is defined as a machine used for various metal forming operations (such as blanking, piercing, bending, drawing, and coining) where the energy stored in a flywheel is released via a clutch to drive a ram downward against a die to deform or cut the workpiece.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, "Power Press Set Up and Die Removal" is studied under industrial safety because the **set-up** (preparation) and **die removal** (changeover/storage) phases represent the **highest-risk periods** of operation, accounting for a large percentage of serious injuries and fatalities in press shops worldwide.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Giant Stamp" Mental Model)

Imagine a **giant cookie-cutter stamp** that comes down from the ceiling with tremendous force. The "stamp" is the **punch (upper die)**, the "cutting board" is the **die (lower die)**, and the dough is the **metal sheet**.

- **Setting up the press** is like carefully positioning the cookie cutter, adjusting the depth of the cut, lubricating the surfaces, and ensuring the "stop" mechanisms are perfectly placed so the dough doesn't slip.
- **Removing the die** is like carefully lifting the heavy, sharp metal tooling out of the machine without injuring your fingers — using proper slings, lifting aids, lockout devices, and storage cradles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Setup and Die Removal are the Most Dangerous Operations:**
> Per KTU safety modules and the Factory Act 1948 (India) / OSHA 29 CFR 1910.211 standards, approximately **60–70%** of all power press injuries occur during setup, die change, troubleshooting, and material handling — not during normal production. This is because the operator is in close physical proximity to the point of operation when guards are often removed or disabled.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU Standard Definition |
|------|------------------------|
| **Ram (Slide)** | The vertically moving member that carries the upper die (punch). |
| **Bed (Bolster)** | The stationary lower table of the press that holds the lower die. |
| **Shut Height** | The distance from the press bed to the ram face with the ram at its bottom dead center (BDC). |
| **Stroke** | The vertical distance traveled by the ram per revolution, equal to **2 × crank radius**. |
| **Point of Operation** | The area where the punch and die meet to perform work on the material. |
| **Tonnage Capacity** | The maximum force (in tons) the press frame, bed, and slides are rated to safely withstand. |
| **Die Set** | The assembly of upper and lower die components mounted in the press. |
| **Stripper** | A device that removes the workpiece or scrap from the punch on the upstroke. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Referenced in KTU Press Safety:**
> - **1 Ton (T) ≈ 8.9 kN ≈ 2000 lbs-force (US)**
> - Standard human finger pinch force tolerance: **~1000 N** before bone fracture.
> - Safe reach distance from point of operation: minimum **0.5 m (500 mm)** for mechanical press operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Power Press (Components Involved in Setup)

A typical mechanical power press used in KTU industrial case studies consists of:

- **Frame**: C-type (open-front) or straight-side (closed) construction.
- **Flywheel**: Stores rotational kinetic energy, typically running continuously.
- **Clutch**: Engages the flywheel to the crankshaft (full-revolution or part-revolution type).
- **Brake**: Stops the crankshaft/ram precisely at the top (or desired position).
- **Crankshaft / Eccentric Shaft**: Converts rotary motion into reciprocating ram motion.
- **Ram (Slide)**: Carries the upper die shoe.
- **Bed / Bolster**: Holds the lower die shoe, usually with T-slots for clamping.
- **Die Cushion**: A hydraulic or pneumatic cylinder under the bed that provides blank-holding force during drawing operations.
- **Stripper / Knockout**: Removes the part from the punch.
- **Feeding Mechanism**: Manual, roll feed, or automatic transfer.

## 2.2 Power Press Set-Up Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)

The setup sequence must follow a logical, KTU-approved sequence to prevent crushing, shearing, and struck-by injuries.

### Step 1: Pre-Setup Safety Protocol
- Isolate the energy source using **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** procedures.
- Depressurize all hydraulic/pneumatic circuits.
- Verify that the main disconnect is OFF and tagged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LOTO Rule (29 CFR 1910.147 / Indian Factory Rules):** Every energy-isolating device must have a **personal padlock** and tag applied by the authorized setup person. No two workers should share a lock on the same device.

### Step 2: Clean the Work Area and Press Surfaces
- Remove all scrap, oil, and previous workpiece material.
- Wipe the bolster, ram face, and T-slots.
- Inspect T-bolts, clamps, and die shoes for damage or wear.

### Step 3: Inspect the Die Set
- Visual inspection for cracks, chipped cutting edges, worn stripper springs, and damaged guide pins/bushings.
- Measure die dimensions: punch-to-die clearance, cutting edge wear, sharpening status.
- Check stripper spring force rating (should be within design specification).

### Step 4: Position the Lower Die on the Bolster
- Use appropriate lifting device (overhead crane, die cart, or chain block rated for the die weight).
- Align die with press centerline using press center marks.
- Clamp using **T-bolts, heel blocks, and wedge clamps**.
- Tighten bolts in a **diagonal cross-pattern sequence** to avoid distortion.

### Step 5: Mount the Upper Die on the Ram
- Lift the upper die using a sling attached to the ram-shank hole or proper lifting eye.
- Insert the ram shank into the slide, engage the gibs/wedges.
- Adjust to set proper **shut height**.

### Step 6: Adjust Shut Height
- Shut height = (Die height when closed) + (Stripper height) + small clearance (0.05–0.10 mm).
- Turn the **screw-down adjustment** on the press until the ram bottoms out with correct clearance.
- Lock the adjustment.

### Step 7: Set Stroke Length
- The stroke must be the **minimum required** to clear the part and feed the stock.
- Reducing stroke reduces energy release and brake wear.

### Step 8: Adjust Clutch and Brake
- Set the part-revolution clutch to disengage at the desired ram position.
- Set the brake to stop the ram precisely at top dead center (TDC) for setup.
- **Trip test the press** at slow speed first (inch mode if available).

### Step 9: Install Point-of-Operation Guards
- Fixed perimeter guards, light curtains, two-hand controls, or presence-sensing devices.
- Verify all guards are interlocked and functional.

### Step 10: First Stroke Test
- With hands OUT of the point of operation, perform one dry stroke.
- Inch the press through full cycle.
- Verify trip mechanism, brake, and guards work correctly.

### Step 11: First Part Production
- Feed one piece manually using a feeding tool (tongs, tweezers, or shuttle).
- Inspect the part for burrs, distortions, correct dimensions.
- Only then proceed to production mode.

## 2.3 Die Removal Procedure (Reverse-Logic Sequence)

Die removal is essentially the setup procedure in **reverse**, with extra emphasis on safe handling and storage.

### Step 1: Stop the Press
- Cycle to TDC and engage the brake.
- Push the red emergency stop button.

### Step 2: Apply LOTO
- Disconnect main power, lock and tag.

### Step 3: Remove Material and Scrap
- Clear all stock, finished parts, and scrap from the die area.

### Step 4: Remove Guards and Feeding Equipment
- Lower or swing away guards.
- Disconnect roll feed or transfer system.

### Step 5: Loosen Upper Die
- Release ram gibs and wedges.
- Support the upper die with a sling or crane before loosening.
- Lower the die onto a die cart or cradle — never allow the die to free-fall.

### Step 6: Loosen Lower Die
- Loosen T-bolts in reverse diagonal pattern.
- Lift the die straight up using the sling.

### Step 7: Inspect and Clean Dies
- Wipe down with rust-preventive oil.
- Inspect for wear, chips, or breakage.
- Tag the die with a status label (sharp, dull, needs repair, scrap).

### Step 8: Store in Designated Die Storage Rack
- Store on **Die Rack with proper cradles**.
- Heavier dies on lower shelves.
- Avoid stacking dies on top of each other.
- Maintain a **Die Inventory Logbook**.

### Step 9: Remove LOTO
- Only after the area is clean and no personnel are in the die space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Storage Safety Rule:** Each die should be stored in its own cradle, labeled, and logged. A typical injury scenario in KTU case studies is a die falling from a storage rack onto a worker's foot — always wear **safety shoes (steel toe)** during die handling.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | Application |
|---------|---------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **Press Tonnage Required** | $F = L \times t \times S$ | $F$ = Force, $L$ = Perimeter of cut, $t$ = Material thickness, $S$ = Shear strength | $F$ in N, $L$ in mm, $t$ in mm, $S$ in MPa | Blanking, piercing, shearing |
| **Shear Strength of Mild Steel** | $S \approx 0.7 \times UTS$ | $UTS$ = Ultimate Tensile Strength | MPa | Material selection |
| **Stripping Force** | $F_s = (0.005 \text{ to } 0.03) \times F$ | $F_s$ = Stripper force, $F$ = Cutting force | N | Stripper spring sizing |
| **Kinetic Energy of Flywheel** | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | $I$ = Moment of inertia, $\omega$ = Angular velocity | Joules | Press capacity, energy balance |
| **Stroke Length** | $S_t = 2r$ | $r$ = Crank radius | mm | Press setup, energy use |
| **Shut Height Relation** | $SH = D_{closed} + h_{stripper} + c$ | $D_{closed}$ = Closed die height, $h_{stripper}$ = Stripper height, $c$ = Clearance (0.05–0.10 mm) | mm | Die setup |
| **Die Closing Speed (Mechanical Press)** | $v_c \leq 25 \text{ mm/s}$ | $v_c$ = Closing speed | mm/s | Safety threshold for hand feeding |
| **Safe Reach Distance** | $D_{reach} \geq 0.5 \text{ m}$ | — | m | Operator safety zone |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always include units in your calculations. A common deduction is missing units, even when the numerical answer is correct.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power press setup and die removal protocols are applied in:
- **Automotive body panel manufacturing** (stamping lines for doors, hoods, fenders).
- **White goods / appliance industry** (refrigerator shells, washing machine tubs).
- **Sheet metal fabrication shops** (HVAC ducts, electrical enclosures).
- **Aerospace component manufacturing** (sheet metal brackets, fuselage panels).
- **Coin and medal production** (blanking presses).

In all these sectors, **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** principles, originally from Toyota Production System, are used to reduce setup time while maintaining safety — this is a favorite KTU case study topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Press Tonnage Required for Blanking

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):** A circular blank of diameter **60 mm** is to be cut from a mild steel sheet of thickness **3 mm**. The shear strength of mild steel is **345 MPa**. Calculate the tonnage required on the power press. If the press has a capacity of **200 kN**, is it sufficient?

### Step 1: Identify the Cutting Perimeter
The blank is circular, so the cut goes around the full circumference of the circle.

$$
L = \pi \times D
$$

where $L$ is the perimeter of cut and $D$ is the diameter of the blank.

$$
L = \pi \times 60 \text{ mm} = 188.496 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Sheet Thickness
Given directly:

$$
t = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3: Identify the Shear Strength
Given as:

$$
S = 345 \text{ MPa} = 345 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Cutting Force Formula
The force required to shear the material along the perimeter is:

$$
F = L \times t \times S
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Values

$$
F = 188.496 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm} \times 345 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 6: Compute Numerically

$$
F = 188.496 \times 3 = 565.488 \text{ N/mm}
$$

$$
F = 565.488 \times 345 = 195{,}093.36 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 7: Convert to kN and Tons

$$
F \approx 195.09 \text{ kN}
$$

Converting to metric tons (1 ton ≈ 8.9 kN):

$$
F = \frac{195.09}{8.9} \approx 21.92 \text{ tons}
$$

### Step 8: Compare with Press Capacity
Press capacity = 200 kN. Required force = 195.09 kN.

Since 195.09 kN < 200 kN, **the press is sufficient**, with a safety margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = \frac{200 - 195.09}{200} \times 100\% = 2.45\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Safety Practice:** Although a small margin is mathematically OK, KTU and industry best practice require a **minimum 20–25% safety margin** for press tonnage. A margin below 5% is unsafe due to material variability, thickness fluctuations, and double-blank feeding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shut Height Adjustment

**Problem Statement:** A die set has a closed height of **180 mm**, a stripper plate of height **25 mm**, and a required clearance of **0.10 mm**. Calculate the required press shut height.

### Step 1: State the Shut Height Formula

$$
SH = D_{closed} + h_{stripper} + c
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Given Values

$$
SH = 180 \text{ mm} + 25 \text{ mm} + 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Result

$$
SH = 205.10 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Verify Against Press Specifications
The press's maximum and minimum shut height must encompass this value. The press should have a published range (e.g., 180–260 mm). Since 205.10 mm lies within 180–260 mm, **the die is compatible** with the press.

## 3.3 Derivation: Flywheel Energy Availability

**Problem Statement:** A flywheel has a moment of inertia of $I = 12 \text{ kg·m}^2$ and rotates at $\omega = 30 \text{ rad/s}$. Calculate the available energy for a single press stroke. If the stroke consumes 8000 J, is the flywheel adequate?

### Step 1: Apply the Rotational Kinetic Energy Formula

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
E = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times (30)^2
$$

### Step 3: Compute

$$
E = 6 \times 900 = 5400 \text{ J}
$$

### Step 4: Compare with Required Energy
Required = 8000 J; Available = 5400 J. Since 5400 J < 8000 J, **the flywheel is inadequate**, and the press will bog down (lose speed) significantly. The flywheel's moment of inertia must be increased.

To find required $I$:

$$
I_{required} = \frac{2E}{\omega^2} = \frac{2 \times 8000}{900} = 17.78 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Setup vs. Removal — Safety Critical Steps

| Phase | Setup Step | Removal Step | Common Hazard | KTU Safety Standard |
|-------|-----------|--------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | LOTO applied | LOTO applied | Stored energy release | OSHA 1910.147 / Indian Factory Rule 60 |
| 2 | Bolster cleaned | Scrap removed | Slips, trips | ISO 45001 housekeeping |
| 3 | Die inspection | Die inspection | Hidden cracks | Visual + NDT (where applicable) |
| 4 | Lower die clamped | Lower die unbolted | Die fall | Crane + sling rated ≥ die weight |
| 5 | Upper die mounted | Upper die loosened | Crush injury | Ram block at BDC, never below die weight |
| 6 | Shut height set | — | Off-center load | Ram adjustment per OEM manual |
| 7 | Stroke adjusted | — | Excess energy | Minimum stroke for part clearance |
| 8 | Guards installed | Guards removed last | Pinch at point of operation | Light curtain + two-hand trip |
| 9 | Test stroke | Die stored | Unexpected ram motion | Trip test before production |

## 3.5 Derivation: Operator Reach Safety Distance

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the safe reach distance for a mechanical press with a closing speed of $v_c = 20 \text{ mm/s}$ and operator reaction time of $t_r = 0.4 \text{ s}$. The press has a stopping time of $t_s = 0.2 \text{ s}$.

### Step 1: Total Approach Time

$$
t_{total} = t_r + t_s
$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$
t_{total} = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 3: Closing Distance in This Time

$$
d_{closing} = v_c \times t_{total} = 20 \times 0.6 = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Add the Minimum Hand Withdrawal Distance
A standard safety margin of **500 mm** is applied to ensure the operator can withdraw fully before pinch.

$$
D_{safe} = 500 + 12 = 512 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, operator's hand should not be closer than **0.512 m** to the point of operation during stroking.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **two-hand trips** and **light curtains** are mandated in KTU 2024 safety engineering — they enforce this 0.5 m reach distance electronically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Power Press Major Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Motor] --> B[Flywheel]
    B --> C[Clutch]
    C --> D[Brake]
    D --> E[Crankshaft / Eccentric]
    E --> F[Ram Slide]
    F --> G[Upper Die Punch]
    G --> H[Point of Operation]
    H --> I[Lower Die]
    I --> J[Bolster Bed]
    K[Die Cushion] --> J
    L[Feeding Mechanism] --> H
    M[Safety Guards] --> H
    N[LOTO System] --> A
    N --> C
    N --> K
```

**Interpretation:** The block diagram shows the power transmission path from the motor through energy storage (flywheel), energy release (clutch), motion conversion (crankshaft), and tool action (ram + dies). All control inputs (LOTO, safety guards) are overlaid as parallel safety layers that can interrupt the energy flow at any point.

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Power Press Set-Up Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start] --> S2[Apply LOTO]
    S2 --> S3[Clean Press Bed and Area]
    S3 --> S4[Inspect New Die Set]
    S4 --> S5{Inspection OK?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Tag Die for Repair]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Lift Lower Die onto Bolster]
    S7 --> S8[Clamp Lower Die T-Bolts]
    S8 --> S9[Mount Upper Die on Ram Shank]
    S9 --> S10[Adjust Shut Height]
    S10 --> S11[Set Stroke Length]
    S11 --> S12[Adjust Clutch and Brake]
    S12 --> S13[Install Safety Guards]
    S13 --> S14[Inch Test Dry Stroke]
    S14 --> S15{Any Issue?}
    S15 -->|Yes| S16[Reset and Inspect]
    S15 -->|No| S17[First Part Test]
    S17 --> S18[Release LOTO Begin Production]
    S6 --> S19[End]
    S18 --> S19
    S16 --> S12
```

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Die Removal Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Start Production Stop] --> R2[Cycle to TDC and Brake]
    R2 --> R3[Apply LOTO and De-Energize]
    R3 --> R4[Remove Stock and Scrap]
    R4 --> R5[Remove Guards and Feeders]
    R5 --> R6[Support Upper Die with Sling]
    R6 --> R7[Loosen Ram Gibs and Wedges]
    R7 --> R8[Lower Upper Die to Cradle]
    R8 --> R9[Loosen Lower Die T-Bolts]
    R9 --> R10[Lift Lower Die with Crane]
    R10 --> R11[Clean and Oil Both Dies]
    R11 --> R12[Inspect Die Condition]
    R12 --> R13[Tag Die Status]
    R13 --> R14[Store in Die Rack]
    R14 --> R15[Update Die Inventory Log]
    R15 --> R16[Remove LOTO]
    R16 --> R17[End]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Safety Interlock Layers (KTU Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Layer 1 Fixed Guards] --> L2[Layer 2 Light Curtains]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Two Hand Trip]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Presence Sensing]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5 Pullbacks]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 6 Safety Mats]
    L6 --> L7[Layer 7 Emergency Stop]
    L7 --> L8[Layer 8 LOTO]
    L8 --> L9[Final Stop and Brake]
```

**Interpretation:** Multiple independent safety layers are stacked so that if one fails, the next still protects the operator. This is the **Hazard Control Hierarchy** taught in KTU 2024 scheme: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Die Storage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DS1[Receiving Bay] --> DS2[Inspection Station]
    DS2 --> DS3{Status Decision}
    DS3 -->|Sharp Ready| DS4[Active Die Rack]
    DS3 -->|Dull| DS5[Sharpening Queue Rack]
    DS3 -->|Damaged| DS6[Repair Rack]
    DS3 -->|Scrap| DS7[Scrap Bin]
    DS4 --> DS8[In Production]
    DS8 --> DS9[After Production]
    DS9 --> DS3
    DS5 --> DS10[External Sharpening Vendor]
    DS10 --> DS2
    DS6 --> DS11[In House Tool Room]
    DS11 --> DS2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define a power press. List any **four** common operations performed on a power press.

**Model Answer:**

A **power press** is a mechanical or hydraulic machine used to apply high compressive force via a reciprocating ram and die set to shape, cut, or form sheet metal and other materials. The energy is typically stored in a flywheel and released through a clutch mechanism.

**Four common operations performed on a power press:**
1. **Blanking** – cutting a flat piece of material from a sheet using a punch and die.
2. **Piercing** – making holes in a sheet metal.
3. **Bending** – deforming the sheet along a straight line.
4. **Drawing** – pulling a flat blank into a die cavity to form a cup-shaped part.
5. *(Any four of blanking, piercing, bending, drawing, coining, embossing.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is **LOTO**? Why is it essential during power press setup and die removal?

**Model Answer:**

**LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is a safety procedure used to ensure that dangerous machines are properly shut off and cannot be re-energized before the completion of maintenance or setup work. It involves:
- **Lockout:** placing a physical padlock on the energy-isolating device (main disconnect, hydraulic valve, etc.).
- **Tagout:** attaching a tag with the worker's name, date, and reason for the lockout.

**Importance during power press setup and die removal:**
1. The press cannot be accidentally energized while the operator's hands are in the point of operation.
2. Stored energy in the flywheel, hydraulic accumulator, or pneumatic system is released.
3. It is a **legal requirement** under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 and Indian Factories Act Rule 60.
4. It prevents crushing, amputation, and struck-by injuries — the leading causes of power press fatalities.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for setting up a power press** for a new die set. Highlight the safety precautions at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular blank of **75 mm diameter** is to be cut from a **4 mm thick** mild steel plate with a **shear strength of 360 MPa**. Calculate:
  (i) The cutting force required
  (ii) The press tonnage needed (in tons)
  (iii) If the available press has a capacity of **300 kN**, determine the safety margin. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — Setup Procedure:

| Step No. | Action | Safety Precaution |
|----------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Stop press and apply **LOTO** | Each worker uses personal padlock; verify zero energy state. |
| 2 | **Clean** the bolster, ram face, T-slots | Avoid slips; remove all scrap and oil. |
| 3 | **Inspect the die set** for cracks, wear, and chipping | Discard or repair any damaged die. |
| 4 | **Lift lower die** using sling/cart | Use lifting device rated ≥ die weight; keep hands clear. |
| 5 | **Position** on bolster, align with press center | Use center mark guides; never place hands under suspended die. |
| 6 | **Clamp with T-bolts** in diagonal pattern | Use torque wrench to specified value. |
| 7 | **Mount upper die** on ram shank | Use proper sling; engage gibs/wedges. |
| 8 | **Set shut height** with screw-down | Verify stroke clearance. |
| 9 | **Adjust stroke** to minimum required | Reduces energy and brake wear. |
| 10 | **Adjust clutch and brake** for top stop | Trip test in inch mode. |
| 11 | **Install safety guards** | Light curtain, fixed perimeter guard, two-hand trip. |
| 12 | **Dry run test** (hands out of zone) | Watch for irregular motion. |
| 13 | **First part test** with feed tool | Inspect for burrs and correct geometry. |
| 14 | **Release LOTO**, start production | Ensure all personnel clear. |

**Key safety principles:** LOTO, no hands in point of operation during stroking, minimum stroke principle, guarding, die inspection, proper rigging.

#### Solution (b) — Numerical:

**(i) Cutting Force:**

Perimeter of cut:

$$
L = \pi \times D = \pi \times 75 = 235.62 \text{ mm}
$$

Cutting force:

$$
F = L \times t \times S
$$

$$
F = 235.62 \times 4 \times 360
$$

$$
F = 339{,}292.8 \text{ N} \approx 339.29 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Tonnage in Metric Tons:**

$$
T = \frac{F}{8.9} = \frac{339.29}{8.9} = 38.12 \text{ tons}
$$

**(iii) Safety Margin:**

Available press = 300 kN. Required = 339.29 kN.

Since required > available, **the press is NOT sufficient** for this operation.

**Result:** A larger press with capacity ≥ 339.29 kN (or 40 tons) is needed. The press is unsuitable, indicating either press reselection or a die redesign (e.g., progressive die, or multiple smaller blanks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **die removal procedure** from a power press. List **five safety rules** to be followed during die handling and storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a power press** and the role of the flywheel, clutch, and brake in its operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — Die Removal:

**Die Removal Procedure:**

1. **Stop the press** at TDC and engage the brake.
2. **Apply LOTO** on the main disconnect, hydraulic/pneumatic valves.
3. **Remove stock, finished parts, and scrap** from the die area.
4. **Remove guards, feeding equipment, and rolls** from the work area.
5. **Attach sling to upper die** and support its weight with a crane before loosening.
6. **Loosen ram gibs and wedges**; lower the upper die onto a die cart.
7. **Loosen T-bolts** on the lower die in reverse diagonal pattern.
8. **Lift lower die** with a crane/sling rated for the die weight.
9. **Clean both dies** with a non-abrasive solvent; apply rust-preventive oil.
10. **Inspect the dies** for wear, cracks, chipping.
11. **Tag the die** with a status label (Sharp, Dull, Needs Repair, Scrap).
12. **Store in designated die rack** on a proper cradle, label side out.
13. **Update the die inventory log** with date, operator, and condition.
14. **Remove LOTO** only after the press area is verified clean.

**Five Safety Rules for Die Handling and Storage:**

1. **Always use LOTO** before any die handling.
2. **Never place hands** under a suspended die — use only slings and cranes rated for the die weight.
3. **Wear PPE** — safety shoes, gloves, safety glasses, and hard hat.
4. **Store dies in proper racks/cradles** — never stack directly on each other or on the floor.
5. **Maintain a die inventory log** and tag every die with its current status.
6. *(Bonus)* Inspect dies for damage before and after each use.

#### Solution (b) — Functional Architecture:

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):**

```
Motor → Flywheel → Clutch → Brake → Crankshaft → Ram → Upper Die
                                                   ↘
                                                Point of Operation
                                                   ↙
                                                Lower Die → Bolster
```

**Role of Each Component:**

1. **Motor:** Provides continuous rotational power to the flywheel. Runs continuously; does not engage/disengage the press cycle.

2. **Flywheel:** Stores rotational kinetic energy during idle periods. This stored energy is released in a single pulse during each press stroke. The flywheel's large moment of inertia ensures that the press can deliver high force without the motor being stalled.

   $$
   E_{flywheel} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2
   $$

3. **Clutch:** The engagement device that connects the flywheel's stored energy to the crankshaft. Two main types:
   - **Full-revolution clutch** — engages for a full turn; ram cannot stop mid-stroke.
   - **Part-revolution clutch** — can be disengaged at any point; allows inching and emergency stopping.

4. **Brake:** Stops the crankshaft and ram at the desired position (usually TDC). Critical for setup and emergency stops. A worn brake is one of the most common causes of press accidents.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Forgetting LOTO** — always state it first in any setup/removal procedure.
> 2. **Missing units in numericals** — Newton-meters or Joules must accompany the energy answer.
> 3. **Confusing blanking and piercing** — blanking produces the part, piercing produces the hole.
> 4. **Not checking press capacity** — a press must always be rated ≥ the calculated cutting force.
> 5. **Skipping die inspection** — most students omit this critical step.
> 6. **Forgetting safety margin** — KTU expects a 20–25% safety margin in press tonnage selection.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Power Press:** Mechanical/hydraulic machine that uses a flywheel-stored energy pulse to drive a ram against a die to shape, cut, or form material.
- **Setup** is the **most dangerous** phase of press operation; ~60–70% of injuries occur here.
- **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is the **#1 safety step** — always applied first and removed last.
- The **eleven setup steps**: LOTO → Clean → Inspect → Position lower die → Clamp → Mount upper die → Shut height → Stroke → Clutch/brake → Guards → Test stroke → First part.
- **Shut Height Formula:** $SH = D_{closed} + h_{stripper} + 0.05–0.10 \text{ mm}$.
- **Cutting Force Formula:** $F = L \times t \times S$ (N).
- **Press Tonnage Selection Rule:** Press capacity must be ≥ 1.20 × calculated cutting force (20% safety margin).
- **Closing Speed** of mechanical press at point of operation must be ≤ **25 mm/s** for hand feeding.
- **Operator Reach Distance:** minimum **0.5 m** from point of operation.
- **Die Storage Rules:** Store on cradles, never stack directly, label, log inventory, segregate by status (sharp/dull/repair/scrap).
- **PPE Required:** Safety shoes (steel toe), gloves, safety glasses, hard hat, hearing protection.
- **Safety Hierarchy:** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering Controls → Admin Controls → PPE.
- **Indian Legal References:** Factories Act 1948 (Sections 21, 22, 60), Indian Factory Rules, ISO 45001, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.211 (Mechanical Power Presses), 1910.147 (LOTO).
- **KTU Hot Words:** Point of Operation, Ram, Bolster, Shut Height, Stroke, Tonnage, LOTO, SMED, Guarding Hierarchy, Flywheel Energy, Die Cushion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection and Maintenance - Metal Sheers - Press Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection and Maintenance - Metal Sheers - Press Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Inspection – The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inspection (as per IS 18001 & KTU 2024 Safety Engineering Syllabus):**
> *Inspection* is the systematic, planned, and documented examination of equipment, machinery, plant, and workplace conditions to determine their conformity with statutory requirements, manufacturer specifications, and safe operating limits. It is a **proactive safety control** that detects deterioration *before* it leads to failure.

- **Purpose:** Detect wear, cracks, misalignment, loose fasteners, hydraulic leaks, electrical faults, and guarding deficiencies.
- **Frequency:** Statutory inspections (annual), routine inspections (daily/weekly), and non-routine inspections (post-incident).
- **Three Legal Pillars in India:** Factories Act 1948 (Sections 6, 7, 21A), The Kerala Factories Rules, and BIS Standards (e.g., IS 1840 for presses, IS 11054 for guillotine shears).

## 1.2 Maintenance – The KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance (ISO 13373 & KTU Industrial Safety Module):**
> *Maintenance* is the combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions intended to retain an item, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform its required function.

- **Classification Hierarchy (Essential for KTU Board Exam):**
  1. **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Time-based / scheduled (e.g., every 500 hours).
  2. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Condition-based using vibration, thermography, oil analysis.
  3. **Corrective / Breakdown Maintenance:** Run-to-failure; least preferred in safety-critical machinery.
  4. **Predictive > Preventive > Corrective** (in safety and cost efficiency ranking).

## 1.3 Metal Shears (Shearing Machines) – Intuitive Overview

A **Metal Shear** is a machine tool that uses a sharp, hardened blade (the **upper blade / moving blade**) to cut sheet or plate metal against a stationary **lower blade / fixed blade**, much like a giant paper cutter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – The Giant Guillotine:**
> Imagine a paper guillotine on a workshop desk. Now scale it up 1,000 times and harden the blades to Rockwell C 58–62. That is essentially a **guillotine shear**. The metal sheet is the paper, the upper blade is the moving knife, and the operator's fingers are the "paper" – which is exactly why safety devices (hold-downs, finger guards) are non-negotiable.

**Industrial Types of Metal Shears (Mandatory for KTU Board):**
- **Guillotine Shear** – Vertical ram, straight cut, most common.
- **Alligator Shear** – Hinged "jaws" like a crocodile, used in scrap yards.
- **Power Squaring Shear** – Precision plate cutting with squaring arm.
- **Throatless Shear** – Open-frame for curve cutting.
- **Rotary Shear / Slitter** – Continuous strip cutting (roller principle).
- **Punching & Nibbling Shear** – Profile cutting via reciprocating punch.

## 1.4 Press Brakes – Intuitive Overview

A **Press Brake** is a machine that bends sheet metal by forcing it between a **punch (top tool)** and a **die (bottom tool)**. It is the "folding machine" of the metal-fabrication world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy – The Wall-Edge Folder:**
> Think of creasing a thick cardboard box along a line. Your thumb is the **punch**, the table edge is the **die**, and the bending force is supplied by your hand. A press brake does this with **tonnes of hydraulic or mechanical force** on sheets up to 6 m long and 25 mm thick. Without proper guarding, the punch-die gap becomes a guillotine for human hands.

**Three Bending Methods (Syllabus Critical):**
- **Air Bending (Coining-incomplete):** Punch contacts material only at three points; die width is ~8× to 12× material thickness. Most common.
- **Bottoming:** Punch forces material fully into the die cavity; higher tonnage, more accurate angle.
- **Coining:** Punch embosses the material into the die; produces sharp radii, requires 5× to 10× air-bending tonnage.

## 1.5 Key Physical Quantities (BIS / IS Standards)

- **Shear Strength (τ) of Mild Steel:** **300 – 350 N/mm²** (typical).
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (Sₜ) of Mild Steel:** **400 – 460 N/mm²**.
- **Standard Die Opening for Air Bending:** **8 × t to 12 × t** (where t = sheet thickness).
- **Punch Tip Radius:** typically **1 × t or less** for soft materials.
- **Blade Clearance in Shears:** **0.05 × t to 0.10 × t** per side (for mild steel).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 3 | Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inspection – The Five-Step Logic Chain

1. **Plan:** Decide WHAT to inspect, WHEN, and WHO (competent person).
2. **Observe:** Visual + functional (e.g., check blade sharpness, guard interlock).
3. **Measure:** Use gauges, dial indicators, micrometers, crack-detection dyes.
4. **Record:** Logbook entry → maintenance management system.
5. **Act:** Trigger PM, replace part, or shut down if unsafe.

**Inspection Points Specific to Shears & Press Brakes:**
- Blade sharpness, chip-free edges, blade gap.
- Hold-down pressure gauge reading.
- Hydraulic oil level & temperature.
- Light curtain / two-hand control response time.
- Emergency stop circuit continuity (must drop out within ≤ 0.5 s).
- Foot pedal shroud integrity.
- Electrical earthing resistance (**≤ 1 Ω** for machinery).

## 2.2 Maintenance – Why the Order Matters

| Type | Trigger | Cost Implication | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrective | Failure | High (downtime) | **Highest Risk** |
| Preventive | Time/Hours | Medium | Medium |
| Predictive | Condition | Lowest (long-term) | **Lowest Risk** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Which maintenance strategy is best for safety-critical presses?"*, the correct KTU-recommended answer is **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** because it predicts failure *before* it endangers the operator.

## 2.3 Metal Shears – Operational Theory

The shearing process is **shear-cutting without chip formation** (unlike machining). Failure occurs along the **shear plane** at ≈ 45° to the sheet plane when shear stress (τ) reaches the material's shear strength.

**Three Phases of Shearing (Essential Drawing Knowledge):**
1. **Elastic Deformation Phase:** Punch contacts sheet, plastic zone begins.
2. **Plastic Deformation Phase:** Sheet rotates; sheared edge begins to form.
3. **Fracture Phase:** Cracks from both blades meet → clean separation.

**Critical Parameters:**
- **Shear Angle (α):** Inclination of upper blade to lower blade; **typically 0.5° to 2.5°** to prevent binding.
- **Clearance (c):** Gap per side; controls cut quality and tonnage.
- **Hold-down Force:** Prevents sheet lifting; ≈ 2–5 % of shear force.

## 2.4 Press Brakes – Operational Theory

**Bending Process:** Tensile outer fibres stretch, compressive inner fibres compress. The neutral axis shifts *toward the inside* of the bend by a factor of **0.33 to 0.5 × t** depending on material hardness (K-factor).

**Springback (θ_s):** When the punch lifts, the elastic recovery causes the bend angle to *open* slightly. Compensation is done by **over-bending** the punch angle.

## 2.5 KTU 2024 – High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE (Kerala Board Exams):** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables. Below is the unified formula sheet for Module 3 of PESFT864.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shear Cutting Force (General)** | $P = \tau \cdot t \cdot L$ | N | τ = shear strength, t = thickness, L = cut length |
| 2 | **Shear Tonnage (Mild Steel, Practical)** | $P = \dfrac{0.7 \cdot S_t \cdot t \cdot L}{1000}$ | kN | 0.7 = empirical shear/tensile ratio |
| 3 | **Shear Force per mm of cut (M.S.)** | $F_{mm} = 0.7 \cdot S_t \cdot t$ | N/mm | Multiply by cut length L |
| 4 | **Air-Bending Tonnage (V-Die)** | $P = \dfrac{C \cdot S_t \cdot t^{2} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V}$ | kN | C = die-opening factor (≈ 1.33 for V = 8t) |
| 5 | **Bottoming Tonnage** | $P = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot S_t \cdot t^{2} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V}$ | kN | ≈ 1.5 × air-bending |
| 6 | **Coining Tonnage** | $P = \dfrac{S_t \cdot t \cdot L}{1000}$ | kN | Direct material embossing |
| 7 | **Inside Bend Radius (Air Bending)** | $R_i = \dfrac{V}{2} \cdot \text{offset}$ | mm | Function of die width V |
| 8 | **Bend Allowance (BA)** | $BA = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot (90 + K \cdot SP) \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t) + 0.5 \cdot t$ | mm | K = K-factor (0.33 soft to 0.5 hard) |
| 9 | **Bend Deduction (BD)** | $BD = (R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) - (R_i + K \cdot t) \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} + 0.5 \cdot t$ | mm | Flat-pattern length compensation |
| 10 | **Neutral Axis Shift** | $NA = K \cdot t$ from inner surface | mm | K = 0.33 soft, 0.5 hard |
| 11 | **Springback Angle** | $\theta_{s} = \dfrac{0.044 \cdot S_y \cdot V}{E \cdot t}$ | degrees | S_y = yield, E = Young's modulus |
| 12 | **Hydraulic Cylinder Force** | $P = p \cdot A = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2}$ | N | p = pressure (Pa), D = piston dia |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sheet metal industry:** Automotive body panels, HVAC ducts, electrical enclosures.
- **Aerospace:** Lightweight aluminium press-brake components.
- **Construction:** Roof decking, structural flashings.
- **Shipbuilding:** Plate cutting by large hydraulic guillotines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Statistic (for KTU Essay-type answers):**
> A 6 mm × 3 m mild-steel plate cut on a guillotine requires ≈ **75 tonnes** of shearing force, while air-bending the same plate on a press brake requires ≈ **100–120 tonnes** – which is why press brakes are typically the *largest* machine in any fabrication shop and the *most dangerous* unguarded.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 | Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Shearing Force on a Guillotine

**Statement:** A hydraulic guillotine shear is used to cut a mild-steel plate of thickness *t* = 10 mm, length *L* = 2500 mm. Ultimate tensile strength *S_t* = 420 N/mm². Determine the required shearing force *P* in kN and tonnes.

### Step 1 – Identify the working stress
The shearing stress τ is the *practical* resistance offered by the material along the sheared face. For mild steel, it is empirically taken as **70 % of ultimate tensile strength**.

$$
\tau = 0.7 \cdot S_t
$$

### Step 2 – Substitute numerical value

$$
\tau = 0.7 \times 420 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 294 \text{ N/mm}^{2}
$$

### Step 3 – Compute sheared area
The sheared area is the product of the cut length *L* and the plate thickness *t*:

$$
A = t \cdot L
$$

$$
A = 10 \text{ mm} \times 2500 \text{ mm} = 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 – Apply fundamental shear equation

$$
P = \tau \cdot A
$$

$$
P = 294 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \times 25{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

### Step 5 – Evaluate

$$
P = 7{,}350{,}000 \text{ N} = 7350 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 6 – Convert to tonnes-force (engineering unit used on shop floors)

$$
P_{\text{tonne}} = \dfrac{P}{9.81} = \dfrac{7350}{9.81} \approx 749.2 \text{ tonnes-force}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Insight:** This single calculation (≈ 750 tonnes) is why a 10 mm × 2.5 m guillotine is rated as a **1000-tonne-class machine**. Always quote in *both* kN and tonnes for full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Air-Bending Tonnage on a Press Brake

**Statement:** A press brake is to bend a 6 mm mild-steel sheet (*S_t* = 410 N/mm²), length *L* = 3000 mm, in a V-die of width *V* = 48 mm (i.e., *V* = 8*t*). Compute the required tonnage in kN and tonnes-force.

### Step 1 – Select the formula from the KTU Formula Sheet (Row 4)

$$
P = \dfrac{C \cdot S_t \cdot t^{2} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V}
$$

### Step 2 – Determine the die-opening factor *C*
For an air bend with *V* = 8*t*, empirical tables give **C ≈ 1.33**.

$$
C = 1.33
$$

### Step 3 – Substitute all values

$$
P = \dfrac{1.33 \times 410 \times (6)^{2} \times 3000}{1000 \times 48}
$$

### Step 4 – Evaluate numerator step by step

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.33 \times 410 &= 545.3 \\
6^{2} &= 36 \\
545.3 \times 36 &= 19{,}630.8 \\
19{,}630.8 \times 3000 &= 58{,}892{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 – Evaluate denominator

$$
1000 \times 48 = 48{,}000
$$

### Step 6 – Final division

$$
P = \dfrac{58{,}892{,}400}{48{,}000} \approx 1226.9 \text{ kN}
$$

### Step 7 – Convert to tonnes-force

$$
P_{\text{tonne}} = \dfrac{1226.9}{9.81} \approx 125.1 \text{ tonnes-force}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Expectation:** Always state the **die width (V)**, the **material grade**, the **factor C**, and the **assumption (air-bending, not coining)** before computing. Marks are awarded for *context*, not just numbers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Bend Allowance (for Flat-Pattern Layout)

**Statement:** Bend a 4 mm aluminium sheet (K-factor 0.4) to a 90° angle, inside radius *R_i* = 8 mm, springback *SP* = 2°. Find the Bend Allowance (BA).

### Step 1 – Write the KTU Standard Formula (Row 8 of Cheat Sheet)

$$
BA = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{180}\right) \cdot (90 + K \cdot SP) \cdot (R_i + K \cdot t) + 0.5 \cdot t
$$

### Step 2 – Substitute K = 0.4, SP = 2°, R_i = 8, t = 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \cdot SP &= 0.4 \times 2 = 0.8 \\
(90 + K \cdot SP) &= 90.8 \text{ degrees} \\
K \cdot t &= 0.4 \times 4 = 1.6 \text{ mm} \\
(R_i + K \cdot t) &= 8 + 1.6 = 9.6 \text{ mm} \\
0.5 \cdot t &= 0.5 \times 4 = 2.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Compute the angle-to-radian factor

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{180} \times 90.8 = 1.5851 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 4 – Multiply by the radius term

$$
1.5851 \times 9.6 = 15.22 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5 – Add the flat-stock term

$$
BA = 15.22 + 2.0 = 17.22 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Air vs Bottoming vs Coining

| Parameter | Air Bending | Bottoming | Coining |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tonnage | **1 × (baseline)** | ≈ 1.5 × | **5 × to 10 ×** |
| Angle Accuracy | ± 1° (springback) | ± 0.25° | ± 0.1° |
| Inside Radius | Wide (8t to 12t) | Moderate | Sharp (< 0.5t) |
| Tool Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Operator Risk | High (open gap) | Medium | High (high force) |
| KTU Typical Use | General fabrication | Production runs | Precision parts |

## 3.5 Safety Thresholds from IS Standards (Recall Matrix)

| Standard | Equipment | Key Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| IS 1840 | Power Presses | Two-hand control ≤ 0.5 s response |
| IS 11054 | Guillotine Shears | Hold-down force ≥ 0.05 × shear force |
| IS 15068 | Press Brakes | Light curtain Type 4 / SIL 3 |
| IS 18001 | OHSMS | Documentation cycle ≤ 12 months |
| Factory Rules (Kerala) | All guarded machinery | Annual thorough inspection by Competent Person |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
