# Module 3 | Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PESFT864]
**Define Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance. State two advantages of each in the context of metal-shearing and press-brake machinery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Time-based or usage-based maintenance activities (lubrication, blade-gap check, hydraulic-filter change) performed at fixed intervals to *prevent* failure. (1 mark)
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Condition-based maintenance using vibration analysis, oil sampling, thermography, ultrasonic crack detection to *predict* remaining useful life. (1 mark)
- **Advantages in shears/press brakes:**
  - PM → less downtime, predictable spare-part consumption, statutory compliance.
  - PdM → catches blade chipping and hydraulic-pump degradation *before* unsafe failure; lowers lifetime cost. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PESFT864]
**List any six inspection points you would verify during a weekly safety check of a hydraulic guillotine shear.**

**Model Answer (3 marks – ½ mark per point):**

1. Blade sharpness and chip-free condition of cutting edges.
2. Blade clearance (gap) measured with feeler gauge (target 5–10 % of sheet thickness).
3. Hold-down pressure gauge reading vs. manufacturer setting.
4. Hydraulic oil level, temperature, and leak-tightness of hoses.
5. Functioning of light curtain / two-hand control (response ≤ 0.5 s).
6. Emergency-stop circuit continuity and foot-pedal shroud integrity.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern)

### QUESTION A – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, PESFT864]

#### (a) [7 marks]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a hydraulic press brake. List the major safety devices fitted on a modern CNC press brake as per IS 15068.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *Refer to Section 4.2 above – the three blocks of Top Beam/Punch, Bending Zone, Hydraulic System, plus Back Gauge and Safety PLC.*

**Operation Steps (2 marks):**
1. Sheet is loaded against the **back gauge** (set to the desired flange length).
2. **Hydraulic cylinders** (typically two, on each end of the ram) push the **punch** down.
3. Punch forces the sheet into the **V-die** → plastic deformation → bend is formed.
4. At the end of stroke, the **crowning system** compensates for ram deflection across the length.
5. The ram retracts; CNC stores the bend sequence for the next part.

**Major Safety Devices per IS 15068 (2 marks):**
- **Light curtain (Type 4 / SIL 3)** with 14 mm resolution – optically covers the punch front.
- **Two-hand control** with synchronous anti-tie-down circuit.
- **Foot-pedal deadman** with rigid shroud preventing accidental activation.
- **CNC Safe Stop** – ram decelerates if the light curtain is broken.
- **Pressure-relief valve** at 1.05 × working pressure.
- **E-stop mushroom button** at every operator station (red on yellow).

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A press brake is to bend 8 mm mild-steel sheets (Ultimate tensile strength S_t = 410 N/mm²) of length 4 m in an air-bending operation using a V-die of width 64 mm. Calculate the required bending tonnage in kN and in tonnes-force. State the die-width factor used and the springback compensation angle for a 90° bend if the yield strength is 250 N/mm² and Young's modulus is 2 × 10⁵ N/mm².**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify parameters:**
- t = 8 mm, L = 4000 mm, S_t = 410 N/mm², V = 64 mm (V = 8t, so C = 1.33).

**Step 2 – Apply air-bending formula:**

$$
P = \dfrac{C \cdot S_t \cdot t^{2} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V}
$$

**Step 3 – Substitute:**

$$
P = \dfrac{1.33 \times 410 \times 8^{2} \times 4000}{1000 \times 64}
$$

**Step 4 – Numerator:**

$$
1.33 \times 410 = 545.3 \quad ; \quad 8^{2} = 64
$$

$$
545.3 \times 64 = 34{,}899.2
$$

$$
34{,}899.2 \times 4000 = 139{,}596{,}800
$$

**Step 5 – Denominator:**

$$
1000 \times 64 = 64{,}000
$$

**Step 6 – Final tonnage in kN:**

$$
P = \dfrac{139{,}596{,}800}{64{,}000} \approx 2181.2 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 7 – Convert to tonnes-force:**

$$
P_{\text{tonne}} = \dfrac{2181.2}{9.81} \approx 222.3 \text{ tonnes-force}
$$

**Step 8 – Springback angle (using the Cheat-Sheet Row 11):**

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{0.044 \cdot S_y \cdot V}{E \cdot t}
$$

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{0.044 \times 250 \times 64}{2 \times 10^{5} \times 8}
$$

$$
\theta_{s} = \dfrac{704}{1{,}600{,}000} \approx 0.00044 \text{ rad} = 0.0252^{\circ}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The press brake must deliver ≈ **2181 kN ≈ 222 tonnes-force**, and the punch must be set ≈ **0.03° sharper than 90°** to compensate for springback.

---

### QUESTION B – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3, PESFT864]

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Describe in detail the three phases of metal shearing. Explain the role of blade clearance, shear angle, and hold-down device in producing a clean cut and in ensuring operator safety.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three Phases of Shearing (3 marks – 1 mark each):**
1. **Elastic-Plastic Deformation:** The upper blade presses; the sheet yields locally; no separation yet.
2. **Plastic Penetration & Rotation:** The sheet rotates about a pivot near the cut line; cracks initiate at both blade tips at ≈ 45° to the sheet surface.
3. **Fracture & Separation:** The two cracks from upper and lower blades meet → clean cut, leaving a small burr.

**Role of Process Parameters (4 marks):**
- **Blade Clearance (c):** *Too small* → excessive tonnage, blade chipping, blunt edges. *Too large* → ragged cut, large burr, sheet deformation. **Optimum = 5–10 % of t per side for mild steel.** Correct clearance reduces operator strain and tonnage requirement.
- **Shear Angle (α):** Tilting the upper blade by 0.5°–2.5° reduces *peak* cutting force by ≈ 30 % (cutting begins progressively from one end). It also prevents the offcut from jamming between blades, which is a major pinch-point hazard.
- **Hold-Down Device:** Presses the sheet firmly before the blade descends, preventing *sheet flip-up* (a major cause of operator hand injuries). Per IS 11054, hold-down force ≥ 5 % of total shear force. Modern hold-downs use **hydraulic pressure-regulated cylinders** that clamp only during the stroke.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A hydraulic guillotine shear is rated for 8 mm × 3000 mm mild steel (S_t = 420 N/mm²). Calculate (i) the theoretical shear force in kN, (ii) the required motor power if the stroke takes 4 seconds, and (iii) the expected cycle time if a 12 mm × 3000 mm plate is to be cut instead (neglect the motor-power limit). Comment on the safety implications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Shear force for 8 mm plate:**

$$
P_{8} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 420 \times 8 \times 3000}{1000}
$$

$$
P_{8} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 420 \times 24{,}000}{1000} = \dfrac{7{,}056{,}000}{1000} = 7056 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 – Motor power for 4-second stroke:**

$$
\text{Power} = \dfrac{P \cdot v}{1000} = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{1000 \cdot t_s}
$$

$$
\text{Power} = \dfrac{7056 \times 3000}{1000 \times 4} = 5292 \text{ kW} \text{ (peak)}
$$

**Step 3 – Shear force for 12 mm plate:**

$$
P_{12} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 420 \times 12 \times 3000}{1000} = \dfrac{10{,}584{,}000}{1000} = 10584 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 – Cycle time for 12 mm plate (stroke length unchanged, force ∝ t):**

$$
t_{s,12} = t_{s,8} \times \dfrac{P_{12}}{P_{8}} = 4 \times \dfrac{10584}{7056} = 4 \times 1.5 = 6 \text{ s}
$$

> **Safety Implications (commentary for 2 marks):**
> Cutting 12 mm plate requires **50 % more force** and **50 % more time** than the rated 8 mm capacity. This is *outside* the machine's rated capacity → **risk of overload, blade chipping, hydraulic-seal blow-out, and ram stall**. Strict operator discipline and an **overload-protection interlock** (which the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates) must prevent the operator from attempting this cut.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalise Heavily:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.7 empirical factor** in shear-force calculation → loses **1 mark** per instance.
> 2. **Not converting kN → tonnes-force** → KTU Board often awards a dedicated 1 mark for the unit conversion; missing it costs full credit on the final answer.
> 3. **Using |P| with literal pipe symbols in tables** – in KTU 2024 digital evaluation, the system can mis-parse the table. **Always use `\vert P \vert`** in markdown/LaTeX.
> 4. **Mixing up C factors** – using the *bottoming* formula where *air-bending* is specified (or vice-versa) → wrong answer, **no partial credit**.
> 5. **Omitting the die-width *V* in the air-bending formula** – the most common single error in Module 3.
> 6. **Neglecting springback compensation** – KTU 2024 marks the springback angle calculation as a *separate* sub-question (often 2 marks); omitting it loses those marks.
> 7. **Drawing the press-brake diagram with the punch pointing *up* (i.e., confusing with a press)** – examiners deduct 1 mark for "incorrect process understanding."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 – "Inspection & Maintenance – Metal Sheers – Press Brakes" — KTU PESFT864 — Quick-Recall List**

### A. Inspection
- [ ] Inspection = planned, documented examination (statutory + routine + post-incident).
- [ ] Three pillars of Indian inspection law: **Factories Act 1948**, **Kerala Factories Rules**, **BIS Standards**.
- [ ] Competent Person must conduct **annual thorough examination** of all guarded machinery.
- [ ] Daily visual check of **blades, guards, lubrication, E-stop, light curtain, foot-pedal shroud**.

### B. Maintenance
- [ ] Order of preference: **Predictive > Preventive > Corrective** (for safety-critical machinery).
- [ ] Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) is *mandatory* before any maintenance on shears or press brakes.
- [ ] Hydraulic oil analysis + vibration analysis = core PdM techniques for these machines.

### C. Metal Shears
- [ ] Shear force: $P = \tau \cdot t \cdot L$ → for mild steel, $\tau = 0.7 S_t$.
- [ ] **Blade clearance** = 5–10 % of t per side (mild steel); 8–12 % for stainless steel.
- [ ] **Shear angle** = 0.5°–2.5° tilts the upper blade; reduces peak force by ~30 %.
- [ ] **Hold-down** force ≥ 5 % of shear force (IS 11054).
- [ ] Safety: light curtain + two-hand control + foot-pedal shroud + E-stop.

### D. Press Brakes
- [ ] Air-bending tonnage: $P = \dfrac{C \cdot S_t \cdot t^{2} \cdot L}{1000 \cdot V}$ with **C ≈ 1.33** for *V* = 8t.
- [ ] **Coining tonnage** = 5× to 10× air-bending tonnage.
- [ ] **Bend Allowance** depends on K-factor (0.33 soft → 0.5 hard) and inside radius.
- [ ] **Springback** = elastic recovery after ram lifts; compensate by over-bending (~0.02°–0.05° typical).
- [ ] Safety: **light curtain Type 4, two-hand control, CNC safe stop** (per IS 15068).

### E. Numerical Constants to Memorise
- [ ] Mild-steel **shear strength** ≈ 300 N/mm².
- [ ] Mild-steel **tensile strength** ≈ 400–460 N/mm².
- [ ] **9.81** = conversion from kN to tonnes-force.
- [ ] **0.7** = empirical shear/tensile strength ratio.
- [ ] **1.33** = air-bending die-opening factor for V = 8t.
- [ ] **1.5** = bottoming/air-bending force ratio.

### F. KTU 2024 Hot-Words
- [ ] **LOTO** (Lockout-Tagout) – must appear in every maintenance answer.
- [ ] **Competent Person** – must be named in every inspection answer.
- [ ] **Risk Assessment** + **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE).
- [ ] **IS 1840** (presses), **IS 11054** (guillotines), **IS 15068** (press brakes), **IS 18001** (OHSMS).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Working Safety in Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hot Working Safety in Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Hot Working?

**Hot Working** is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation of the metal is carried out at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature**. At this elevated temperature, the metal becomes soft, ductile, and exhibits significantly lower flow stress, enabling large deformations without fracturing the workpiece.

Mathematically, the hot working condition is satisfied when:

$$T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \times T_{melt} \quad (\text{in Kelvin})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Hot working is defined as the process of plastic deformation of metals at temperatures sufficiently high enough so that recrystallization occurs simultaneously with deformation, thereby eliminating strain hardening effects.

---

## 1.2 What is Forging?

**Forging** is a manufacturing process in which compressive forces are applied to a heated metal workpiece (billet, ingot, or blank) using a hammer, press, or specialized tooling (dies) to shape it into the desired geometry while refining its grain structure and enhancing mechanical properties.

### Types of Forging

| Type | Description | Typical Products |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| **Open-Die Forging** | Workpiece deformed between flat or simple shaped dies; metal flows freely | Large shafts, rings, discs |
| **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** | Metal confined within die cavities to form complex shapes | Connecting rods, crankshafts, gears |
| **Drop Forging** | Hammer dropped onto workpiece using board or steam hammer | Automotive components |
| **Press Forging** | Slow continuous squeezing using hydraulic/mechanical press | Large structural parts |
| **Upset Forging** | Cross-sectional area increased by compressive force along axis | Bolt heads, fasteners |
| **Roll Forging** | Workpiece passed through contoured rotating rolls | Tapered shafts, leaf springs |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clay vs. Cold Butter" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine trying to mould a stick of cold butter with your hands — it cracks, breaks, and resists shape change. This is **cold working**.
> Now place the same butter in warm sunlight — it becomes pliable, soft, and you can shape it effortlessly into any form. This is **hot working**.
> In forging, the heated metal is the "warm butter," and the hammer/press is your "hand." The critical insight is that while the metal is being deformed, new crystals form inside it (recrystallization), so it never hardens — it stays soft and workable throughout the process.

---

## 1.4 Hot Working Temperature Ranges for Common Engineering Metals

| Metal | Melting Point (°C) | Hot Working Range (°C) | Typical Forging Color |
|-------|-------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Carbon Steel (0.2–0.6% C)** | 1490–1530 | **850 – 1230** | Bright red to yellow |
| **Stainless Steel (304)** | 1400–1450 | **900 – 1200** | Dull red to orange |
| **Aluminium Alloys** | 475–660 | **350 – 480** | Faint red glow |
| **Copper Alloys (Brass)** | 900–940 | **600 – 800** | Cherry red |
| **Titanium Alloys** | 1660–1720 | **900 – 1150** | Bright cherry red |
| **Magnesium Alloys** | 410–650 | **200 – 400** | No visible glow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Thresholds:**
> - Human skin suffers **second-degree burns** at contact temperatures above **70 °C** for sustained contact, and **instant pain** above **45 °C**.
> - Forging furnaces operate between **800 °C – 1250 °C**, making thermal exposure the **#1 hazard** in forging shops.

---

## 1.5 Why Forging Safety Demands Special Attention

Forging combines **three simultaneous lethal hazards**:

1. **Extreme thermal energy** (radiation, convection, contact burns, fire)
2. **Massive mechanical kinetic energy** (hammers weighing 500 kg – 50,000 kg dropping from 1–5 m)
3. **High decibel noise** (105 – 130 dB(A), well above the OSHA permissible limit of **90 dB(A) for 8 hours**)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:**
> Most students describe forging hazards as "just heat and heavy machinery." Examiners award full marks only when students recognize the **triad of thermal-mechanical-acoustic hazards** plus **secondary hazards** (fumes, scale particles, ergonomic strain, hydraulic failure).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hot Working Process Chain — A Step-by-Step Breakdown

The forging workflow is a **5-stage process chain**, and safety analysis must address **each stage independently**:

### Stage 1: Material Receipt & Storage
- Raw billets/ingots inspected for cracks, surface defects
- Stored in designated areas, segregated by alloy type

### Stage 2: Heating (Furnace Operation)
- **Fuel-fired furnaces** (gas/oil): produce CO, NOₓ, SO₂
- **Induction furnaces**: produce electromagnetic field exposure (low frequency)
- **Electric resistance furnaces**: high electrical hazard risk

### Stage 3: Transfer to Die
- Worker transports hot billet using tongs, robotic arms, or conveyors
- **Burns, drop hazards, ergonomic injuries** peak here

### Stage 4: Forging (Hammer/Press Action)
- Deformation under high impact or static load
- **Die fragments, flying scale, mechanical failure** peak here

### Stage 5: Trimming, Cooling & Inspection
- Hot trimming press, water/oil quenching
- **Thermal shock, scale inhalation, slip hazards**

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — Forging Load Estimation

For **closed-die forging**, the maximum forging load is estimated using:

$$F_{max} = K_f \cdot A_{proj} \cdot \sigma_{flow}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $F_{max}$ | Maximum forging force | N (Newton) or kN |
| $K_f$ | Force multiplication factor (geometry-dependent) | dimensionless, **1.5 – 3.0** |
| $A_{proj}$ | Projected area of forging (including flash) | mm² |
| $\sigma_{flow}$ | Material flow stress at forging temperature | MPa |

### Die Closing Velocity & Kinetic Energy (For Hammer Forging)

The kinetic energy delivered by the ram must satisfy:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2} \geq V_{forging} \cdot \sigma_{flow} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$$

Where:

- $m$ = mass of the falling ram (kg)
- $v$ = velocity of ram at impact (m/s)
- $V_{forging}$ = volume of forging material (m³)
- $h_0$ = initial height, $h_f$ = final height of workpiece (m)

For a **drop hammer** with ram height $H$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot H}$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ is the gravitational acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> This equation is critical for safety engineers sizing **energy absorption systems** in foundations. A 5000 kg ram dropped from 3 m delivers approximately:
> $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times (2 \times 9.81 \times 3) = 147{,}150 \text{ J} \approx 147 \text{ kJ}$$
> This is enough kinetic energy to fatally injure any unprotected operator — confirming why **physical guarding** around hammers is non-negotiable.

---

## 2.3 Noise Level Estimation in Forging

The sound pressure level from a hammer impact can be approximated by:

$$L_{p} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where $I$ = acoustic intensity (W/m²) and $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² (reference intensity).

For impulsive forging noise, the **peak sound pressure** often exceeds:

$$L_{peak} = 120 - 135 \text{ dB(C)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Permissible Exposure Limits (per OSHA / Indian Factories Rules):**
> - **90 dB(A)** for 8 hours continuous exposure
> - Every **+5 dB(A) doubling of noise** halves the permissible exposure duration
> - At **130 dB(A)**, any exposure — even momentary — causes **permanent hearing damage**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------|------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt}$ | $T$ | K | Hot vs. cold working threshold |
| 2 | $F_{max} = K_f \cdot A_{proj} \cdot \sigma_{flow}$ | Force, area, stress | N, mm², MPa | Press capacity sizing |
| 3 | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Mass, velocity | kg, m/s, J | Hammer energy calculation |
| 4 | $v = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Gravity, drop height | m/s², m | Drop hammer velocity |
| 5 | $\sigma_{flow} = \sigma_0 \left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon_0}}\right)^{m} \cdot \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{-n}$ | Stress, strain rate, temperature | MPa, s⁻¹, K | Flow stress temperature dependence |
| 6 | $L_p = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Intensity | dB | Noise level calculation |
| 7 | $Q_{heat} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy | J | Furnace heating load |
| 8 | $P_{hyd} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_{ram}} \cdot 10$ (with safety factor 10) | Hydraulic pressure | Pa | Hydraulic press safety |
| 9 | $TWL = 16.5 \log_{10}(t) + 90$ | Time, level | dB(A) | OSHA permissible noise vs. duration |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Heat radiation | W | Furnace radiant heat flux |

> The vertical bar used in absolute value notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) has been avoided in the table to preserve markdown table integrity. Instead, $\vert \cdot \vert$ is reserved for inline math only.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hammer Velocity at Impact

### Given:
- A drop hammer of mass $m$ is raised to a height $H$ above the workpiece
- The hammer is released and falls freely under gravity

### To Derive:
- The velocity of the hammer at the moment of impact with the workpiece

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

We begin with the **conservation of mechanical energy**. The hammer starts from rest at height $H$, with no initial kinetic energy, and possesses only potential energy relative to the impact point.

$$E_{potential,initial} = m \cdot g \cdot H$$

At the instant of impact (just before touching the workpiece), all of this potential energy has been converted into kinetic energy (assuming no air resistance — a valid assumption for short drop heights).

$$E_{kinetic,impact} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

By conservation of energy:

$$m \cdot g \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

We now solve for $v$. First, cancel the mass $m$ from both sides (a fundamental assumption that gravity acts equally on all parts of the system):

$$g \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot v^{2}$$

Multiply both sides by 2 to isolate the velocity-squared term:

$$2 \cdot g \cdot H = v^{2}$$

Take the square root of both sides to obtain the magnitude of the impact velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot H}$$

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{2 \, g \, H}}$$

### Numerical Validation:

For a steam hammer with $H = 2.5 \text{ m}$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.5} = \sqrt{49.05} \approx 7.00 \text{ m/s}$$

This velocity is equivalent to approximately **25.2 km/h** — fast enough to be lethal to any human in its path.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Kinetic Energy Delivered by a Drop Hammer

### Given:
- Hammer mass $m = 5000$ kg
- Drop height $H = 2.5$ m
- Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Compute impact velocity using the derived formula:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.5}$$

$$v = \sqrt{49.05}$$

$$v \approx 7.00 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $v$ into the kinetic energy formula $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times (7.00)^{2}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times 49.0$$

$$E_k = 2500 \times 49.0$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 122{,}500 \text{ J} = 122.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to equivalent units for intuition:

$$122{,}500 \text{ J} = \frac{122{,}500}{4184} \approx 29.3 \text{ kcal of energy}$$

This is roughly equivalent to the **kinetic energy of a 1-tonne car travelling at 50 km/h** — illustrating why hammer forging zones are strictly exclusion areas.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Severity vs. Likelihood in Forging

| Hazard Category | Severity (Consequence) | Likelihood (Frequency) | Risk Score (S × L) | Required Control |
|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Die fragment ejection | Catastrophic (fatal) | Occasional | **HIGH** | Full perimeter guarding |
| Burn from hot billet | Serious (3rd-degree) | Frequent | **HIGH** | Aluminized PPE, tongs |
| Hearing loss from impact | Chronic disability | Frequent | **HIGH** | Acoustic enclosure + ear plugs/muffs |
| Carbon monoxide poisoning | Catastrophic | Remote | **MEDIUM** | Exhaust ventilation, gas monitors |
| Hydraulic hose burst | Serious | Remote | **MEDIUM** | Burst-valve protection, regular inspection |
| Ergonomic back injury | Moderate | Frequent | **MEDIUM** | Mechanical handling aids |
| Slips on oil/coolant | Minor to Moderate | Occasional | **LOW-MED** | Anti-slip flooring, drainage |
| Fire from oil spill + hot scale | Catastrophic | Remote | **MEDIUM** | Class B extinguishers, no oil near furnaces |
| Crush injury during die change | Catastrophic | Occasional | **HIGH** | Lockout-Tagout (LOTO), die blocks |

---

## 3.4 Critical Safety Threshold — Temperature of Workpiece vs. Burn Injury

The relationship between contact temperature and burn severity is logarithmic in clinical terms:

| Workpiece Temperature | Skin Contact Time for 2nd-Degree Burn | PPE Required |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------------|--------------|
| **50 °C** | > 10 minutes | Standard leather gloves |
| **70 °C** | ~ 1 second | Aluminized gloves (Type A) |
| **100 °C** | < 0.1 second | Full aluminized suit + visor |
| **500 °C** | Instant — no pain (nerve destroyed) | Remote handling only |
| **1000 °C+** | Instantaneous third-degree burn | No human contact permitted |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The most dangerous scenario is **500–800 °C** because:
> 1. The metal is hot enough to cause instant tissue destruction
> 2. It is **below visible glow** in daylight, so workers misjudge its temperature
> 3. Pain receptors are immediately destroyed, so the worker does not withdraw the hand
>
> This is why **pyrometers** and **infrared cameras** are mandatory for temperature verification.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define hot working. List any four forging processes.**

**Model Answer:**

Hot working is a metal forming operation carried out at a temperature above the recrystallization temperature of the metal (typically $T \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt}$ in Kelvin), where simultaneous deformation and recrystallization eliminate strain hardening.

**Four forging processes:**

1. **Open-die forging** — deformation between flat/simple dies with free material flow
2. **Closed-die forging** — material confined in die cavities to produce complex shapes
3. **Drop forging** — vertical hammer impact for rapid deformation
4. **Upset forging** — compression along axis to increase cross-section (used for bolt heads)

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for correctly listing four processes)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State any three thermal hazards in forging operations.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Direct contact burns** from hot billets, dies, and forged components (surface temperatures 800 °C – 1250 °C)
2. **Radiant heat exposure** from furnaces and hot workpieces, causing heat stress, dehydration, and chronic skin damage
3. **Inhalation of hot fumes and combustion gases** such as CO, NOₓ, SO₂ from fuel-fired furnaces, leading to respiratory illness

> *(3 marks: 1 mark each for stating three distinct hazards with brief elaboration)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**a) Explain the different types of forging operations with neat sketches and state the safety hazards associated with each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Forging Types and Their Hazards:**

| Forging Type | Process Description | Primary Safety Hazards |
|--------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Open-Die Forging** | Workpiece deformed between flat dies; operator controls shape manually | (i) Burn injuries from manual handling; (ii) Crush injuries during tilting; (iii) Flying scale particles |
| **Closed-Die Forging** | Material forced into cavity dies under high pressure | (i) Die fragment ejection; (ii) Flash burns; (iii) Hydraulic press failure; (iv) Noise above 130 dB |
| **Drop Forging** | Hammer (500 – 50,000 kg) dropped from 1 – 5 m height | (i) Catastrophic impact injuries if guarding fails; (ii) Foundation vibration; (iii) Extreme noise; (iv) Scale projection |
| **Press Forging** | Hydraulic/mechanical press squeezes workpiece slowly | (i) Pinch points; (ii) Hydraulic hose burst; (iii) Slow crush injuries during die change |
| **Upset Forging** | Axial compression increases cross-section | (i) Billet slippage causing ejection; (ii) Recoil injuries; (iii) Tongs slippage |
| **Roll Forging** | Workpiece passes through contoured rolls | (i) Entanglement in rotating rolls; (ii) Nip-point injuries |

**Safety Hazards — Generalized Classification:**

- **Thermal:** Burns, heat stress, fires from oil contact with hot scale
- **Mechanical:** Impact, crushing, entanglement, projectiles (die fragments)
- **Acoustic:** Impulsive noise causing NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss)
- **Chemical:** CO, NOₓ, oil mist, scale dust inhalation
- **Ergonomic:** Repetitive strain, manual handling of hot billets
- **Electrical:** Furnace controls, induction heating systems

> *(7 marks: 2 for classification, 3 for type-wise hazard table, 2 for generalized hazards)*

---

**b) Discuss the various engineering and administrative control measures used to ensure safety in hot forging operations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Engineering Controls (5 marks distributed):

1. **Furnace Exhaust Ventilation:** Local exhaust hoods with airflow ≥ **0.5 m/s** capture velocity to remove CO, NOₓ, and oil mist before they reach the breathing zone.

2. **Machine Guarding:** Fixed perimeter guards with interlocked gates around hammers and presses; light curtains create virtual barriers; pressure mats detect intrusion.

3. **Acoustic Enclosures:** Forging hammers enclosed in sound-absorbing booths achieving **20 – 30 dB(A) attenuation**, reducing exposure to below 90 dB(A).

4. **Automated Material Handling:** Robots, conveyors, and manipulators remove human presence from the hot transfer zone.

5. **Hydraulic Safety Systems:** Burst-valve protection, pressure relief valves, dual hydraulic lines, and accumulator-based emergency stop systems.

6. **Fire Suppression:** Class B (CO₂/dry powder) extinguishers near oil stores; automatic sprinklers rated for 200 °C activation; **no oil storage within 3 m of furnace**.

### Administrative Controls (2 marks distributed):

1. **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO):** Mandatory energy isolation during die change, maintenance, and cleaning. Each energy source tagged by authorized personnel only.

2. **Permit-to-Work System:** Hot work permits issued before any cutting, welding, or grinding near furnaces.

3. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs):** Documented step-by-step procedures for forging, furnace operation, emergency response.

4. **Training & Competency:** Operators certified on specific equipment; minimum 40 hours of safety induction; annual refresher courses.

5. **Medical Surveillance:** Annual audiometry, vision tests, dermatological screening, and pulmonary function tests.

6. **Emergency Preparedness:** Emergency stop buttons within 3 m of every workstation; evacuation drills quarterly; burn first-aid stations every 30 m.

> *(7 marks: 5 for engineering controls with technical detail, 2 for administrative controls)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Retest]**

**a) A drop hammer of mass 8,000 kg falls from a height of 3.5 m onto a hot steel billet. Calculate: (i) the velocity at impact, and (ii) the kinetic energy delivered. What does this imply for safety guard design? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mass of hammer, $m = 8000$ kg
- Drop height, $H = 3.5$ m
- Gravitational acceleration, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Velocity at impact:**

Using conservation of energy:

$$m \cdot g \cdot H = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

Canceling $m$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot H}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.5}$$

$$v = \sqrt{68.67}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 8.29 \text{ m/s} \approx 29.8 \text{ km/h}}$$

**(ii) Kinetic energy delivered:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^{2}$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 8000 \times (8.29)^{2}$$

$$E_k = 4000 \times 68.74$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 274{,}960 \text{ J} \approx 275 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Equivalent real-world comparison:**
$$E_k = 275{,}000 \text{ J} \approx \text{kinetic energy of a 1.5-tonne car at 50 km/h}$$

**Implications for Safety Guard Design:**

1. **Guard Strength:** Guards must withstand at least **2× the maximum kinetic energy** with a safety factor of 2, requiring impact resistance of **≥ 550 kJ**. Only reinforced steel plate (≥ 12 mm thickness) with backing structures can absorb this.

2. **Guard Coverage:** Full perimeter enclosure with **no gap exceeding 50 mm**; light curtains must cut power within **≤ 50 milliseconds** of intrusion detection.

3. **Energy Absorption:** Foundation pits must be sized to absorb residual energy; pit mass ≥ **5× ram mass** recommended.

4. **Operator Exclusion Zone:** No human presence within **3 m** of hammer during operation; cycle time delays ≥ **5 seconds** after impact for acoustic/kinetic decay.

5. **Redundancy:** Dual independent braking systems (mechanical + electrical) must engage if primary fails; emergency stops must be within **1.5 m reach** of every operator position.

> *(7 marks: 2 for velocity, 2 for kinetic energy, 3 for safety implications with quantitative reasoning)*

---

**b) Describe the personal protective equipment (PPE) required for forging operators. Justify each item based on the specific hazard addressed. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| PPE Item | Specification | Hazard Addressed | Justification |
|----------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Hard Hat with Chin Strap** | ISI-marked, Type 2 | Falling scale, overhead crane impact | Hot scale particles ejected during forging can cause skull fractures; chin strap prevents dislodgement during rapid head movements |
| **Face Visor (IR-rated, Shade 5–7)** | Polycarbonate with IR coating | Infrared radiation, flying particles | Furnace IR radiation causes cataract and retinal burns; visor also stops scale fragments travelling at > 50 m/s |
| **Ear Plugs + Muffs (Dual)** | NRR ≥ 33 dB combined | Noise 120–135 dB(C) | Single protection insufficient above 110 dB; dual protection achieves OSHA-compliant < 90 dB at eardrum |
| **Aluminized Heat-Reflective Suit** | ASTM F955 compliant | Radiant heat 5–15 kW/m² | Reflects > 90% of radiant heat; rated for sustained exposure up to 1000 °C ambient |
| **Aluminized Gloves (Type A)** | Gauntlet length to elbow | Contact burns from billets | Tested for 30-second contact at 800 °C without heat transfer to skin |
| **Steel Toe Boots + Metatarsal Guards** | IS 15298 compliant | Falling billets, heavy tooling | A 10 kg billet falling 1 m has 100 J energy — sufficient to fracture foot bones; boots rated for 200 J impact |
| **Flame-Resistant Undergarments** | Nomex IIIA equivalent | Flash fires | Prevents cotton ignition that would melt onto skin; self-extinguishes within 2 seconds of flame removal |
| **High-Visibility Vest** | EN ISO 20471 Class 2 | Crane-vehicle proximity | Crane operators need clear visibility of ground personnel to prevent struck-by accidents |

**Justification Principle:**
Each PPE item is selected based on a **specific hazard-energy pathway analysis** rather than generic compliance. The hierarchy is: *head → eyes/face → hearing → body → hands → feet.*

> *(7 marks: 5 marks for tabular PPE specification, 2 marks for justification principle)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in Hot Working Safety Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "hot working" with "hot machining":** Hot working refers to plastic deformation; machining is material removal. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are mixed.
>
> 2. **Listing hazards without classifying them:** Students write "burns, noise, fire" without grouping into *thermal / mechanical / acoustic / chemical / ergonomic*. Examiners explicitly look for **structured categorization** to award full marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the recrystallization condition:** When defining hot working, omitting the phrase "**above recrystallization temperature**" costs 1 full mark on definition questions.
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in hammer energy problems:** Many students forget to multiply by **g = 9.81** (using 10 incorrectly) or fail to take square root correctly. Always show every algebraic step.
>
> 5. **Writing "PPE is enough":** Examiners **severely penalize** any answer that begins with PPE as the primary control. Always start with elimination/substitution, then engineering, then admin, then PPE.
>
> 6. **Missing Indian regulatory references:** When asked about legal framework, students forget to cite **Indian Factories Act 1948 (Section 21–41)**, **Indian Electricity Rules**, or **IS 2969 (Forging Safety Code)**. Examiners award bonus marks for citing standards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

### ✅ Essential Concepts to Remember:

- [ ] Hot working requires $T \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt}$ (in Kelvin)
- [ ] Recrystallization occurs **simultaneously** with deformation — no strain hardening
- [ ] Six major forging types: open-die, closed-die, drop, press, upset, roll
- [ ] Forging temperatures for steel: **850 °C – 1230 °C** (cherry red to yellow)
- [ ] Hammers drop from **1–5 m** with masses up to **50,000 kg**
- [ ] Drop hammer velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gH}$
- [ ] Kinetic energy: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ — typical values **100–500 kJ**
- [ ] Forging force: $F_{max} = K_f \cdot A_{proj} \cdot \sigma_{flow}$
- [ ] Forging noise: **120–135 dB(C)** peak — dual hearing protection mandatory
- [ ] **Five hazard categories:** Thermal, Mechanical, Acoustic, Chemical, Ergonomic

### ✅ Safety Control Hierarchy (Top to Bottom — Always Apply):

- [ ] **Level 1 — Elimination:** Can forging be replaced by cold forming or casting?
- [ ] **Level 2 — Substitution:** Use robotic forging for hazardous parts
- [ ] **Level 3 — Engineering:** Guards, ventilation, acoustic enclosures, automation
- [ ] **Level 4 — Administrative:** LOTO, SOPs, training, permits, medical surveillance
- [ ] **Level 5 — PPE:** Aluminized suits, IR visors, dual ear protection, metatarsal boots

### ✅ PPE Checklist for Forging Operators:

- [ ] Hard hat + chin strap
- [ ] IR-rated face visor (Shade 5–7)
- [ ] Ear plugs + muffs (dual, NRR ≥ 33 dB)
- [ ] Aluminized suit + flame-resistant undergarments
- [ ] Type A aluminized gauntlet gloves
- [ ] Steel toe + metatarsal boots
- [ ] High-visibility vest (in crane zones)

### ✅ Legal/Regulatory References to Cite:

- [ ] **Indian Factories Act, 1948** — Sections 21, 22, 36, 41 (Safety provisions)
- [ ] **IS 2969** — Code of practice for safety in forging industry
- [ ] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.211** — Definitions (forging terminology, US reference)
- [ ] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** — Occupational noise exposure
- [ ] **NFPA 484** — Combustible metals (magnesium forging safety)
- [ ] **ILO-OSH 2001** — Guidelines on occupational safety management systems

### ✅ Frequently Asked Numerical Problem Types:

- [ ] Hammer impact velocity from drop height
- [ ] Kinetic energy delivered to workpiece
- [ ] Press tonnage estimation using $F_{max}$
- [ ] Noise exposure time calculations using OSHA permissible limit graph
- [ ] Furnace heating energy using $Q = mc_p \Delta T$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:**
> In 14-mark questions, always use **at least one diagram/table** in your answer. Examiners award partial marks generously when answers are visually structured. Avoid prose-heavy paragraphs — examiners have only 2–3 minutes per answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Rolling Mill Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hot Rolling Mill Operation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hot Rolling Mill Operation** is a primary metal forming process in which a heated metal workpiece (typically steel, aluminum, or copper) is plastically deformed by passing it between counter-rotating cylindrical rolls at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal, thereby reducing its cross-sectional area and elongating its length while continuously refining its grain structure. The recrystallization temperature is the temperature at which new strain-free grains nucleate and grow, replacing the deformed grains from prior mechanical work. For low-carbon steels, this is typically between **0.5 Tm and 0.7 Tm** (where **Tm** is the melting point in Kelvin), usually in the range of **900°C to 1250°C** for hot rolling operations. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The safety perspective of hot rolling mill operation is paramount. This includes personnel safety (burns, mechanical hazards, noise), process safety (mill fires, hydrogen explosions, scale accumulation), and equipment safety (roll breakage, bearing failure, coupling failures). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine rolling out dough to make a roti or chapati on a flat surface using a rolling pin. The rolling pin applies compressive force, flattening the dough ball into a thin, wide disc. A hot rolling mill operates on the **exact same principle**, but at an industrial scale, with enormous mechanical force, and on red-hot metal. **Key analogies:** - **Heated dough = Hot billet/slab** → softens, becomes pliable - **Rolling pin = Work rolls** → applies compression - **Multiple passes = Sequential reduction** → grain by grain refinement - **Cooling the dough = Air/water cooling on runout table** → locks in the refined structure - **Stretching/tearing = If passes are too aggressive** → defects like edge cracks > [!TIP] > The "heat" in hot rolling is not just a comfort factor — it is a **scientific necessity**. Cold metal is too hard and brittle to deform without fracturing. Heating above the recrystallization temperature allows the metal's internal crystalline structure to continuously "heal" itself during deformation, preventing work-hardening and allowing massive reductions (often **> 90%** in a single sequence). ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants) | Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Hot rolling temperature range | **1100°C – 1250°C** (steel) | Above austenite region | | Recrystallization temp (low C-steel) | **~ 900°C** | Lower bound for hot work | | Typical reduction per pass | **10% – 40%** | Depends on draft, friction | | Total reduction in sequence | **80% – 95%** | Billet to final product | | Roll surface speed (finishing) | **5 – 25 m/s** | High productivity | | Reheating furnace exit temp | **1150°C ± 50°C** | Process window | | Coiling temperature (strip) | **550°C – 700°C** | Metallurgical requirement | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hot Rolling Process Flow — Step-by-Step A hot rolling mill is not a single machine; it is an **integrated production line** consisting of multiple stations. The typical sequence is: ### Step 1: Stock Yard and Inspection - Incoming raw material (slabs, billets, or blooms) is stored in the stockyard. - Each piece is inspected for surface defects, dimensional accuracy, and chemical composition (via PMI – Positive Material Identification). - **Safety note:** Crane operations in stockyard require certified operators; rigging failures are a leading cause of fatal accidents. ### Step 2: Reheating Furnace (Pusher / Walking Beam / Rotary Hearth) - Stock is heated to the required rolling temperature (typically **1150°C – 1250°C** for steel). - Furnace types: - **Pusher furnace:** Hydraulic rams push stock through; simple but causes bottom-surface scratching. - **Walking beam furnace:** Stock is lifted and stepped forward — best for heavy slabs with minimal marking. - **Rotary hearth furnace:** Circular, high efficiency, used for direct reduction. - Furnace atmosphere may be oxidizing (air-fuel) or reducing (controlled). - **Safety hazards:** Furnace explosions (if unburnt gas accumulates), CO poisoning, refractory collapse, scale ignition. ### Step 3: Descaling (Hydraulic / Steam / Mechanical) - A high-pressure water jet (typically **100 – 400 bar**) or steam blast removes the oxide scale (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄, FeO) formed during heating. - Scale, if not removed, gets embedded into the rolled product, causing surface defects. - **Safety hazard:** High-pressure water jet injection injuries, flying scale particles. ### Step 4: Roughing Mill (R1, R2, Reversing or Continuous) - Performs the major cross-sectional reduction (often **50% – 80% total reduction**). - Roll gap is large; forces are very high (up to **several MN**). - Reversing mills allow multiple passes on the same stand; continuous mills use tandem stands. ### Step 5: Intermediate Mill (Pre-finishing) - Bridges the gap between roughing and finishing. - Further reduces cross-section; refines grain structure. - Often equipped with **edgers** to control width and prevent edge cracking. ### Step 6: Finishing Mill (Last 4–7 Stands, Tandem) - Achieves the final dimensional tolerance (e.g., **±0.05 mm** for thin strip). - Stands are closely coupled; interstand tension and speed control are critical. - **Finishing temperature** is precisely controlled (typically **850°C – 950°C** exit). ### Step 7: Runout Table and Cooling - Strip is cooled on a water-spray runout table. - Coiling temperature is controlled to achieve the desired metallurgical phase (e.g., austenite-to-ferrite transformation in low-carbon steel at **~ Ar₃**). - **Safety hazards:** Steam plumes (visibility zero), slip-fall on wet surfaces, high noise. ### Step 8: Coiling / Shearing - Strip is coiled in downcoilers (mandrel-type) or cut to length on cooling beds. - Coil handling: overhead cranes, upenders, coil cars. ## 2.2 Types of Rolling Mill Stands (Critical for KTU) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Focus:** Students must be able to draw and label these stand configurations. | Stand Type | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **2-High** | Two rolls, one above the other; reversing or non-reversing | Small mills, roughing | | **3-High** | Three rolls (top, middle, bottom); rolling in both directions without reversing | Older billet mills | | **4-High** | Two small work rolls supported by two large backup rolls | Modern strip mills | | **6-High / Cluster** | Additional intermediate rolls for flatness control | Tandem cold mills | | **Planetary** | Two large backup rolls surrounded by many small work rolls | Heavy reduction in a single pass | | **Universal** | Horizontal + vertical edger rolls | Beam and rail rolling | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > These are the **high-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on hot rolling. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta h = h_0 - h_1$ | Draft (reduction in thickness) | mm | | 2 | $r = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0}$ | Fractional reduction (draft ratio), 0 < r < 1 | dimensionless | | 3 | $\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_1}\right)$ | True (logarithmic) strain | dimensionless | | 4 | $L_1 = L_0 \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_1}$ | Elongation (constant volume) | mm | | 5 | $P_{avg} = \sigma_f \cdot b \cdot L_c$ | Average rolling force (simplified) | N | | 6 | $L_c = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ | Contact length (arc of contact) | mm | | 7 | $T = \mu \cdot P_{avg} \cdot R$ | Rolling torque | N·m | | 8 | $\mu = 0.8 \cdot \left(1.05 - 0.0005 \cdot T_{roll}\right)$ | Coefficient of friction (Siebel, $T_{roll}$ in °C) | dimensionless | | 9 | $P = \dfrac{2 \cdot T}{D}$ | Rolling power (per roll) | W | | 10 | $kW = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{9550}$ | Mill motor power | kW | | 11 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m \cdot e^{Q/RT}$ | Flow stress (hot deformation) | MPa | Where: - $h_0, h_1$ = entry and exit thickness - $b$ = width of strip - $R$ = work roll radius - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction - $\sigma_f$ = material flow stress - $K, n, m$ = material constants - $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol) - $R$ = Universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**) - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the contact-length formula, $L_c$ uses $\Delta h$, NOT $h_1$. Students frequently substitute incorrectly. The formula assumes circular geometry, so $L_c \approx \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ is valid only for small reductions where the arc is approximately equal to the chord. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Hot rolling mill safety engineering is critical in: - **Integrated steel plants** (e.g., SAIL, Tata Steel, JSW): Thousands of workers in hot zones daily. - **Aluminum rolling** (Hindalco, NALCO): Lower temperatures but similar safety profile. - **Rail and structural mills** (long product mills): Heavy machinery, higher fatality risk. - **Compliance:** Factories Act 1948, IS 10690 (Safety of Rolling Mills), OSHA 29 CFR 1910.261 (Pulp & Paper — historical), and Indian Electricity Rules. The safety engineer's role is to identify the **locus of hazard** at each station and deploy a hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Contact Length ($L_c$) from Circular Geometry When a work roll of radius $R$ contacts a strip being reduced from $h_0$ to $h_1$, the contact area forms an arc. For small reductions, this arc approximates a chord. **Starting point (geometry):** The work roll center is at height $R$ above the neutral axis. The entry point is at height $h_0/2$ above neutral axis; the exit point is at height $h_1/2$. The vertical distance from the roll center to the exit point is: $$y_{exit} = R - \dfrac{h_1}{2}$$ The vertical distance from the roll center to the entry point is: $$y_{entry} = R - \dfrac{h_0}{2}$$ The horizontal contact distance (chord half-length) is found using the Pythagorean theorem on the roll surface: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 + y_{entry}^2 = R^2$$ Substituting $y_{entry} = R - h_0/2$: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 + \left(R - \dfrac{h_0}{2}\right)^2 = R^2$$ Expanding the square: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 + R^2 - R \cdot h_0 + \dfrac{h_0^2}{4} = R^2$$ Canceling $R^2$ on both sides: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 - R \cdot h_0 + \dfrac{h_0^2}{4} = 0$$ Solving for $(L_c/2)^2$: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 = R \cdot h_0 - \dfrac{h_0^2}{4}$$ Factoring the right side: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{h_0}{4} \cdot (4R - h_0)$$ For hot rolling, $h_0 \ll R$ (typical: $h_0$ = 20 mm, $R$ = 400 mm), so $4R - h_0 \approx 4R$: $$\left(\dfrac{L_c}{2}\right)^2 \approx \dfrac{h_0}{4} \cdot 4R = R \cdot h_0$$ Therefore: $$\dfrac{L_c}{2} \approx \sqrt{R \cdot h_0}$$ Note: $h_0$ here is the **vertical drop** at the entry side. The actual vertical drop is $h_0 - h_1 = \Delta h$, but at the entry point the strip is at full thickness. By the more rigorous derivation considering both entry and exit contact points: $$L_c = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$$ This is the **standard form** used in KTU textbooks. > [!NOTE] > **Why the approximation is valid:** With $R/h_0 \approx 20$, the error from neglecting $h_0^2/4$ is less than **1.3%**, which is well within engineering tolerance. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rolling Force from Slab Method (Simplified) In the slab method (Orowan / Hitchcock formulation), the roll is treated as a wedge pressing into the strip. A free-body element is analyzed along the arc of contact. **Force equilibrium on a slab element of width $b$ and arc length $dx$:** The horizontal forces acting on the element are: - Roll pressure $p$ on top (varies along arc) - Friction $\tau = \mu p$ at the roll–strip interface - Horizontal tension $\sigma_x$ on the entry face - Horizontal tension $(\sigma_x + d\sigma_x)$ on the exit face - Bending stresses (ignored in simplified version) **Horizontal equilibrium:** $$\frac{d\sigma_x}{dx} \cdot h = \frac{dp}{dx} \cdot (h - h(x))$$ Wait — this is getting complex. The **simplified KTU-level result** (often quoted without full derivation): $$P_{total} = \sigma_{avg} \cdot b \cdot L_c$$ Where $\sigma_{avg}$ is the average flow stress in the deformation zone, incorporating: - Plane-strain compression: $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sigma_y \approx 1.155 \cdot \sigma_y$ - Friction hill: $\sigma_{avg}$ increases as $\mu$ and $L_c/h_m$ increase. **Final working formula for KTU problems:** $$P_{total} = 1.15 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot L_c \cdot f(\mu, L_c/h_m)$$ Where $f(\mu, L_c/h_m)$ is the friction-hill correction factor (often 1.0 – 1.5 for hot rolling). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Neutral Point and Forward Slip The **neutral point** is the location along the arc of contact where the strip velocity equals the roll surface velocity. Forward of this point, the strip moves slower than the roll (roll "drags" the metal); behind it, the strip moves faster (roll "restrains" the metal). The strip speed at exit is therefore slightly higher than the roll surface speed — this is **forward slip**. **Velocity relationship at exit:** $$v_{strip,exit} = v_{roll,surface} \cdot (1 + s)$$ Where $s$ = forward slip (typically **0.02 – 0.10**, i.e., **2% – 10%**). **Expression derived by Ekelund (simplified):** $$s = \dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{r}{1-r}}\right) - \dfrac{1}{2} \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-r}\right)}{2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{h_1}}} \cdot \text{(correction terms)}$$ For KTU purposes, a simpler operational form: $$s \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0}\right) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{h_0}}$$ **Worked Numerical Example (KTU style):** A hot rolling mill has the following operating data: - $h_0$ = **25 mm** (entry thickness) - $h_1$ = **18 mm** (exit thickness) - $R$ = **350 mm** (work roll radius) - Roll speed $N$ = **60 rpm** **Step 1: Draft** $$\Delta h = h_0 - h_1 = 25 - 18 = 7 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Reduction ratio** $$r = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0} = \dfrac{7}{25} = 0.28 \text{ (i.e., 28\%)}$$ **Step 3: Contact length** $$L_c = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{350 \times 7} = \sqrt{2450} \approx 49.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4: True strain** $$\varepsilon = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_1}\right) = \ln\left(\dfrac{25}{18}\right) = \ln(1.389) \approx 0.329$$ **Step 5: Contact angle** $$\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta h}{R}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{7}{350}} = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414 \text{ rad} \approx 8.1°$$ **Step 6: Roll surface velocity** $$v_{roll} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \cdot (2 \times 0.350) \cdot \dfrac{60}{60} = \pi \cdot 0.7 = 2.20 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 7: Forward slip (assume 4%)** $$v_{strip,exit} = v_{roll} \cdot (1 + 0.04) = 2.20 \times 1.04 = 2.288 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 8: Elongated length** If $L_0 = 5$ m: $$L_1 = L_0 \cdot \dfrac{h_0}{h_1} = 5 \times \dfrac{25}{18} \approx 6.94 \text{ m}$$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hot Rolling Force — Friction Hill Effect The pressure required at the roll gap rises in the entry zone (back tension) and falls in the exit zone (front tension). The peak pressure is higher than the plane-strain yield due to friction. **Friction hill formula (simplified):** $$p_{peak} = \sigma_y' \cdot e^{\mu \cdot L_c / h_m}$$ Where $h_m = (h_0 + h_1)/2$ is the mean thickness. **Numerical example with friction hill:** Given: $\sigma_y'$ = **180 MPa**, $\mu$ = **0.3**, $L_c$ = **49.5 mm**, $h_m$ = **21.5 mm**. $$\dfrac{\mu \cdot L_c}{h_m} = \dfrac{0.3 \times 49.5}{21.5} \approx 0.691$$ $$p_{peak} = 180 \times e^{0.691} = 180 \times 1.996 \approx 359.3 \text{ MPa}$$ This shows that the local peak pressure (~**359 MPa**) is **2x** the plain yield stress, illustrating why **roll wear, surface spalling, and roll bending** are critical concerns in hot rolling. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hot Rolling Mill Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow) > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Since physical engineering drawings of a rolling mill stand cannot be rendered as Mermaid, this section uses a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** to map all sub-systems, their safety interlocks, and the path of material flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Stockyard
Raw Slabs Billets] --> A2[Crane Handling
Lift to furnace] A2 --> A3[Reheating Furnace
1150C to 1250C] A3 --> A4[Descaling Unit
High Pressure Water] A4 --> B1[Roughing Mill
R1 R2 Stands] B1 --> B2[Intermediate Mill
Edger Included] B2 --> B3[Finishing Mill
Tandem 4 to 7 stands] B3 --> C1[Runout Table
Water Spray Cooling] C1 --> C2[Coiler or Shear
Downcoiler] C2 --> D1[Finished Product
Strip Coil or Sheet] A3 -. Hot Zone Heat Stress .-> S1[Safety Interlock 1
Gas Detection CO Alarm] A4 -. High Pressure Water .-> S2[Safety Interlock 2
Pressure Relief Valves] B1 -. Pinch Points .-> S3[Safety Interlock 3
Light Curtain Guard] B3 -. Scale Ejection .-> S4[Safety Interlock 4
Mill Enclosure Hoods] C1 -. Slip Hazard Steam .-> S5[Safety Interlock 5
Drainage Non Skid Floor] C2 -. Coil Handling .-> S6[Safety Interlock 6
Crane Load Cell] ``` ## 4.2 Process Parameter Control Loop (Automatic Gauge Control) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Entry Thickness Sensor
X Ray Gauge] --> P2[Controller PLC] P2 --> P3[Roll Gap Actuator
Hydraulic Screwdown] P3 --> P4[Roll Bending System
Roll Deflection] P4 --> P5[Exit Thickness Sensor
X Ray Gauge] P5 --> P6{Within Tolerance} P6 -- No --> P2 P6 -- Yes --> P7[Continue Rolling] ``` ## 4.3 Hot Rolling Safety Hazard Map (Zone-wise) | Zone | Primary Hazards | Engineering Controls | PPE Required | |---|---|---|---| | Stockyard | Crane drops, falling loads | Load cells, anti-collision | Hard hat, boots | | Furnace | Burns, CO, explosion | LEL detectors, interlocks | Aluminized suit | | Descaling | Water jet injection | Bleed valves, barriers | Face shield | | Roughing Mill | Pinch, scale, mechanical | Light curtains, guards | Gloves, visor | | Finishing Mill | Roll breakage, fire | Burst detection, hoods | Heat-resistant suit | | Runout | Steam, slip | Drainage, anti-skid | Non-slip boots | | Coiler | Coil collapse | Upender interlocks | Safety harness | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q1. Define the "recrystallization temperature" and explain its significance in hot rolling. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The recrystallization temperature is the minimum temperature at which new strain-free grains nucleate and grow in a deformed metal, replacing the work-hardened structure. For low-carbon steels, it is typically around **900°C**, i.e., approximately **0.5 Tm** to **0.7 Tm** (Tm = absolute melting point). **Significance (2 marks):** 1. **Allows large reductions without cracking** — At temperatures above recrystallization, the metal continuously "heals" its grain structure during deformation, preventing work-hardening accumulation and allowing reductions of 80–95% in a single sequence. 2. **Lower flow stress and rolling force** — Hot deformation requires much less force than cold deformation, reducing mill motor power requirements. 3. **Refines grain structure** — Controlled cooling after hot rolling produces fine, equiaxed grains with improved mechanical properties. > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Confusing recrystallization temperature with the melting point. Recrystallization is a solid-state phenomenon; the metal remains solid. Also, do not write "recrystallization temperature is fixed" — it depends on the prior cold work, time, and alloying. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q2. List FOUR major safety hazards in a hot rolling mill and the corresponding engineering control for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Hazard | Engineering Control | |---|---|---| | 1 | Burns from hot metal/scale | Mill enclosures, water curtains, scale hoods | | 2 | Pinch point at roll gap | Light curtains, fixed guards, trip wires | | 3 | Scale ejection injury | Mill hoods, ejection shields | | 4 | Furnace gas explosion | LEL detectors, automatic gas shut-off valves | | 5 | Noise (> 100 dB) | Acoustic enclosures, hearing protection zones | (Any four with correct pairing = full marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice Question, May 2024]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q. A. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **4-high reversing hot rolling mill stand**. List its advantages over a 2-high stand. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction (3 marks):** A 4-high stand has four rolls arranged vertically: - **Two work rolls** (small diameter, typically 400–700 mm) in the middle, in direct contact with the strip. - **Two backup rolls** (large diameter, typically 1200–1500 mm) above and below, supporting the work rolls to prevent bending under high rolling force. - Work rolls are driven by electric motors through gearboxes and pinion stands. - Hydraulic or mechanical **screwdown** mechanism sets the roll gap. - **Roll bending jacks** apply counter-deflection to maintain flatness. **Working (3 marks):** - Hot slab enters the roll gap from the entry side table. - Hydraulic pressure forces work rolls toward each other via the backup rolls. - Plastic deformation reduces the slab thickness. - Strip exits on the other side, is transferred through a **roller table**, and (in reversing mills) is fed back for the next pass. - Automatic Gauge Control (AGC) maintains exit thickness. **Advantages over 2-high (1 mark):** - Smaller work roll diameter allows lower **contact area → higher pressure** for given force, improving reduction per pass. - Backup rolls prevent work roll bending → better **thickness uniformity** and **flatness**. - Higher **stiffness** → improved dimensional accuracy. --- **Q. A. (b)** A hot rolling mill reduces a steel slab from **40 mm to 12 mm** in 5 passes through a stand of work roll radius **400 mm**. The roll speed is **50 rpm**, and the width of the strip is **1500 mm**. Calculate: - (i) The total reduction and true strain. - (ii) The contact length in the first pass (assuming equal passes). - (iii) The exit strip velocity, assuming forward slip = **4%**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Total reduction and true strain (2 marks)** Total draft: $\Delta h_{total} = h_0 - h_1 = 40 - 12 = 28$ mm Fractional reduction: $r = \dfrac{28}{40} = 0.70$ or **70%** True strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(40/12) = \ln(3.333) = 1.204$ **Step 2: Contact length in first pass (3 marks)** Assuming **equal draft per pass**: $\Delta h_{per\,pass} = 28/5 = 5.6$ mm $h_0$ (first pass entry) = 40 mm $h_1$ (first pass exit) = $40 - 5.6 = 34.4$ mm Contact length: $$L_c = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h} = \sqrt{400 \times 5.6} = \sqrt{2240} \approx 47.33 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3: Exit strip velocity (2 marks)** Roll diameter: $D = 2R = 800$ mm = 0.8 m Roll surface velocity: $$v_{roll} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N = \pi \times 0.8 \times \dfrac{50}{60} = 2.094 \text{ m/s}$$ Exit strip velocity with forward slip $s = 0.04$: $$v_{strip,exit} = v_{roll} \cdot (1 + s) = 2.094 \times 1.04 = 2.178 \text{ m/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** use the average thickness $(h_0+h_1)/2$ in the contact-length formula. Use $\Delta h$, not $h_m$. > - **Do NOT** forget to convert mm to m when computing roll velocity in m/s. > - **Forward slip is dimensionless** — do not write it as a percentage (4%) in formulas; convert to 0.04. > - **Assume constant volume** unless told otherwise; this gives the length-elongation relation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q. B. (a)** Explain the **hazards and safety measures** at each station of a hot rolling mill, from the stockyard to the coiler. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (4 stations × ~1.75 marks each):** **1. Stockyard (1.75 marks)** - *Hazards:* Crane drops, falling slabs, forklift accidents, dust. - *Controls:* Certified crane operators, load cell interlocks, exclusion zones, dust suppression, FRS (Fall Restraint System) for stacking. **2. Reheating Furnace (1.75 marks)** - *Hazards:* Gas explosion, CO poisoning, refractory burn-through, furnace door opening burns. - *Controls:* LEL/CO detectors, automatic gas shut-off, emergency furnace depressurization, furnace door interlocks, proper PPE (aluminized heat-protective clothing). **3. Rolling Stands (1.75 marks)** - *Hazards:* Pinch points at roll gap, scale ejection, roll breakage (catastrophic), bearing failure. - *Controls:* Fixed guards, light curtains, trip wires, mill enclosure hoods, scale blow-off suppression, roll-burst detection sensors, automatic mill stop on abnormal vibration. **4. Runout Table & Coiler (1.75 marks)** - *Hazards:* Steam plume (zero visibility), slip on wet floor, coil collapse during handling. - *Controls:* Proper drainage and non-skid flooring, fog-cannon suppression, sight-glass cameras, automated coil handling with interlocks, exclusion zones. --- **Q. B. (b)** Define **forward slip** in hot rolling. Derive an expression for **rolling torque** and compute the **mill motor power** for the following data: - Average rolling force = **2.5 MN** (2,500,000 N) - Work roll radius = **400 mm** - Roll speed = **50 rpm** - Coefficient of friction = **0.3** - Number of driven rolls = **2** (both work rolls driven) - Overall efficiency = **90%** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Forward slip definition (1 mark)** Forward slip is the phenomenon in which the exit velocity of the rolled strip is slightly greater than the tangential velocity of the work roll surface. It arises because the neutral point (where strip and roll velocities are equal) is shifted toward the exit side due to friction. **Step 2: Torque derivation (3 marks)** The rolling torque is the moment required to rotate the work roll against the frictional resistance of the strip. The frictional force per unit width on the roll surface is $\mu p$ (where $p$ is the local roll pressure). Integrating over the arc of contact: $$T = \int \mu \cdot p \cdot R \cdot b \cdot d\theta$$ For a simplified uniform pressure model: $$T = \mu \cdot P_{avg} \cdot R$$ The total torque on a single roll: $$T_{roll} = 0.5 \cdot \mu \cdot P_{total} \cdot R$$ **Step 3: Numerical computation (3 marks)** Given: $P_{total} = 2.5 \times 10^6$ N, $R = 0.4$ m, $\mu = 0.3$. Torque on each work roll (both driven): $$T = 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.4 = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}$$ Angular velocity: $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 50}{60} = 5.236 \text{ rad/s}$$ Power per roll: $$P_{roll} = T \cdot \omega = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 5.236 = 7.854 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 785.4 \text{ kW}$$ Total power for 2 rolls (before efficiency): $$P_{gross} = 2 \times 785.4 = 1570.8 \text{ kW}$$ Motor power (after efficiency): $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{1570.8}{0.90} \approx 1745.3 \text{ kW} \approx \textbf{1.75 MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** omit the 0.5 factor in $T = 0.5 \cdot \mu \cdot P \cdot R$. This arises from the moment arm being the average lever of the friction force (half the contact arc), not the full roll radius. > - **Do NOT** forget the efficiency factor — mill motor power is always greater than the calculated rolling power. > - **Do NOT** compute power per roll and forget to multiply by 2 when both work rolls are driven. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme** ### A. Core Concepts - Hot rolling is performed **above the recrystallization temperature** (typically **1100°C – 1250°C** for steel). - The metal is **continuously recrystallized** during deformation, preventing work-hardening. - Total reduction in a single sequence can be **80–95%** in long-product mills. - Constant-volume assumption: $A_0 L_0 = A_1 L_1$ → for flat rolling, $h_0 L_0 = h_1 L_1$. ### B. Critical Formulas (Must Memorize) - $L_c = \sqrt{R \cdot \Delta h}$ — contact length. - $r = \Delta h / h_0$ — fractional reduction. - $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_1)$ — true strain. - $P = 1.15 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot b \cdot L_c$ — simplified rolling force. - $T = 0.5 \cdot \mu \cdot P \cdot R$ — rolling torque per roll. - $v_{strip,exit} = v_{roll} \cdot (1 + s)$ — forward slip relation. ### C. Stand Types - **2-high:** Simple, low accuracy. - **4-high:** Industry standard for strip; small work rolls, large backup rolls. - **Planetary:** Massive single-pass reductions (up to 95%). ### D. Safety Stations (In Order) 1. **Stockyard** → Crane safety, exclusion zones. 2. **Reheating furnace** → Gas detection, burn protection. 3. **Descaling** → High-pressure water safety. 4. **Roughing mill** → Pinch point guards, scale ejection hoods. 5. **Finishing mill** → Roll-burst detection, fire suppression. 6. **Runout table** → Slip prevention, steam management. 7. **Coiler** → Automated handling, coil collapse prevention. ### E. Common Examiner Traps - Confusing $\Delta h$ with $h_m$ in contact-length formula. - Forgetting to convert mm → m in velocity/motor power. - Mixing forward slip (dimensionless) with percentage. - Treating rolling torque as $T = \mu P R$ instead of $T = 0.5 \mu P R$. - Forgetting mill efficiency in motor power. ### F. Real-World Mapping - Hot strip mill: SAIL Bokaro, Tata Jamshedpur, JSW Vijayanagar. - Long product mill: SAIL Bhilai, RINL Vizag. - Aluminum: Hindalco Hirakud, Vedanta Jharsuguda. - Reference standards: **IS 10690**, **OSHA 1910.261**, **Factories Act 1948 (India)**.

topics.title: Safe Guards in Hot Rolling Mills - Hot Bending of Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safe Guards in Hot Rolling Mills - Hot Bending of Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Hot Rolling Mill?

A **Hot Rolling Mill** is a heavy industrial facility consisting of two or more counter-rotating work rolls mounted in a robust stand, through which heated metallic stock (ingots, blooms, billets, slabs) is plastically deformed **above its recrystallization temperature** to reduce cross-sectional area, alter the grain structure, and impart the desired mechanical properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recrystallization Temperature (T_recryst):** For carbon steels, hot rolling is typically carried out at **1100 °C – 1250 °C** (above T_recryst ≈ 0.4 × T_melt ≈ 723 K). At this temperature, dynamic recrystallization prevents work hardening and keeps flow stress low (~30–80 MPa for mild steel).

### 1.2 What is Hot Bending of Pipes?

**Hot Bending of Pipes** is a thermo-mechanical forming process in which a pipe (carbon steel, alloy steel, or stainless steel) is heated locally or globally to **700 °C – 1000 °C** and plastically deformed into a bend of prescribed radius, typically using one of the following methods:

| Method | Heat Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Induction Bending** | High-frequency induction coil | Long-radius bends in heavy pipelines |
| **Hot Rotary Draw Bending** | Oxy-fuel / furnace | Tight-radius process piping |
| **Mandrel Bending (Hot)** | Furnace + mandrel support | Thin-wall boiler tubes |
| **Compression Bending** | Furnace | Large diameter bends |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cooked Pasta & Hot Straw" 🌀

> **Hot Rolling Mill** 🍝
> Imagine rolling out pasta with a hand-cranked machine. Now imagine the dough is a **glowing red-hot steel billet** and the rollers are made of hardened tool steel weighing **hundreds of tons**. Instead of a kitchen counter, the work rolls are surrounded by **flying sparks, boiling water, hydraulic oil, and 1500 °C scale**. Every moving surface, every splash, every flying scale particle is a **hazard**. The safeguards are the **guard rails, the emergency trip-wires, and the heat shields** that keep the cook (operator) safe.

> **Hot Bending of Pipes** 🥤
> Think of trying to bend a hot plastic straw just taken out of a hot drink — it bends easily and holds its shape on cooling. Now scale that up to a **6-inch steel pipe at 850 °C** being forced around a 5×D radius die by a **hydraulic ram exerting 200 tonnes of force**. The molten oxide scale flies off, the hydraulic hose carries oil at **210 bar**, and the moment the operator loses concentration — burns, crushing injuries, or hydraulic injection wounds occur. Safeguards are the **heat shields, hose-burst valves, light curtains, and PPE** that intervene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Distinct Process Domains – One Common Safety Philosophy**
> Although the forming physics differs, both processes share the *hierarchy-of-controls* logic: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**. The difference is only in the *specific* hazard energy vectors (thermal, mechanical, hydraulic, gravitational).

### 1.4 Core Hazard Categories at a Glance

| Domain | Dominant Hazard Energy | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Kinetic / gravitational | Roll nip points, shears, coiler mandrels, crane movement |
| **Thermal** | Radiant + Convective heat | 1100 °C steel, scale ejection, induction coil stray fields |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized fluid | Bending ram cylinder at **100–300 bar**, accumulator discharge |
| **Electrical** | Arc / induction | Induction coil RF leakage, motor control centres |
| **Chemical** | Lubricant / scale | Oil mist, inhalation of Wüstite (FeO) scale dust |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hot Rolling Mill — Hazard Mapping

A modern hot rolling mill consists of **reheating furnace → roughing stand → intermediate stand → finishing stand → run-out table → coiler**. Each zone carries a distinct hazard signature.

#### 2.1.1 Reheating Furnace Zone

- **Hazard:** Furnace explosion (gas leak + ignition), hot charge ingress, door opening scalding.
- **Heat source:** Natural gas / pulverized coal flame at **1200 °C – 1350 °C**.
- **Critical safety:** Burner management system (BMS), flame failure trip, **NFPA 85 / IS 14604** compliance.

#### 2.1.2 Descaling & Roughing Zone

- **Hazard:** High-pressure water jet (descaling pump at **150–200 bar**) + steam plume + scale ejection.
- **Engineering safeguard:** Descaling box enclosure with **interlocked access doors**, pressure relief valves, water curtain.

#### 2.1.3 Finishing Mill & Run-Out Table

- **Hazard:** Roll-nip entanglement (the most lethal hazard in any rolling mill — historical fatality data shows >40 % of rolling-mill deaths occur at the **roll bite**).
- **Engineering safeguard:** **Fixed perimeter guards**, **trip-wire systems** (cable-pull E-stops), **light curtains**, **safety mats**, and **two-hand control** for inching.

#### 2.1.4 Coiler Zone

- **Hazard:** Rotating mandrel (180–600 RPM), strip edge cuts, pinch points, flying coil.
- **Engineering safeguard:** Wrapping belt interlock, coil car anti-collision sensors, hydraulic mandrel locking.

### 2.2 Hot Pipe Bending — Hazard Mapping

| Sub-process | Specific Hazard | Energy Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Pipe loading into bender | Crushing during crane placement | Pipe mass 500–5000 kg |
| Induction heating | RF burns, coil water leak | Coil current 5–20 kA at 1–10 kHz |
| Bending ram stroke | Pinch / shear at die | Hydraulic pressure **100–300 bar** |
| Cooling / quenching | Steam explosion | Water contact with 850 °C pipe |
| Unloading | Dropped pipe, swing | Crane + suspended hot load |

### 2.3 Hierarchy of Controls — Applied

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hot-Point:** Examiners consistently award higher marks to answers that **categorize safeguards by hierarchy level** rather than listing them flatly.

1. **Elimination** – e.g., automate charging to remove manual crane work.
2. **Substitution** – use water-based vs. oil-based lubricant to remove fire load.
3. **Engineering Controls** – physical guards, light curtains, pressure-relief valves, thermal shields.
4. **Administrative Controls** – LOTO (Lock-Out / Tag-Out), Permit-to-Work, SOPs, training.
5. **PPE** – aluminized heat suits (rated to **1000 °C radiant**, ISO 11612), face shields, safety boots.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> **📘 Exam Cheat Sheet — Memorize These Six Equations**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Terms | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | $q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot \bigl(T_{hot}^{4} - T_{worker}^{4}\bigr)$ | $q$ = radiant heat flux (W/m²), $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0.6–0.85 for oxidized steel), $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) | Determine PPE thermal rating; design of heat shields |
| **F-2** | $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \cdot T_{melt} \;[\text{K}]$ | $T_{melt}$ for pure Fe ≈ 1811 K → $T_{recryst} ≈ 723$ K (450 °C) | Confirm hot-working regime (>0.6 T_melt in K) |
| **F-3** | $F_{ram} = P_{hyd} \cdot A_{piston}$ | $F$ = ram force (N), $P$ = hydraulic pressure (Pa), $A$ = piston area (m²) | Sizing hose-burst valves; bending tonnage |
| **F-4** | $Q_{conv} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $h$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (10–500 W/m²K), $A$ = exposed pipe area | Cooling time before safe handling |
| **F-5** | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $\sigma_b$ = bending stress, $M$ = bending moment, $c$ = outer radius, $I$ = second moment of area | Mandrel sizing; bend ovalization check |
| **F-6** | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A}$ | Time for pipe to cool from hot to safe-handle temp (~60 °C) | Define exclusion-zone duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Radiative Heat Flux:** A typical **1100 °C steel slab** radiates approximately **190 kW/m²**. A human can tolerate only **~1.6 kW/m²** without protection for a few seconds — this 100× ratio is the *fundamental justification* for every physical heat shield, light curtain, and PPE requirement in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radiant Heat Flux from a Hot Steel Slab (F-1)

**Problem:** A freshly-rolled steel slab sits on the run-out table at **1100 °C (1373 K)**. A worker without PPE stands **1.5 m** away at skin temperature **34 °C (307 K)**. The slab has surface emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.78$ (oxidized scale). Estimate the radiant heat flux incident on the worker's face.

**Step 1 — State Stefan-Boltzmann Law (for two parallel surfaces):**

$$
q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot \bigl(T_{hot}^{4} - T_{cold}^{4}\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Insert constants:**

* $\varepsilon = 0.78$
* $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W} / \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4$
* $T_{hot} = 1373 \; \text{K}$
* $T_{cold} = 307 \; \text{K}$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_{hot}^4$:**

$$
T_{hot}^{4} = (1373)^{4}
$$

Intermediate computation:

$$
1373^{2} = 1{,}885{,}129
$$

$$
1373^{4} = (1{,}885{,}129)^{2} \approx 3.554 \times 10^{12} \; \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T_{cold}^4$:**

$$
