T_{cold}^{4} = (307)^{4}
$$

$$
307^{2} = 94{,}249
$$

$$
307^{4} = (94{,}249)^{2} \approx 8.883 \times 10^{9} \; \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Take difference (note $T_{cold}^4$ is negligible vs. $T_{hot}^4$):**

$$
T_{hot}^{4} - T_{cold}^{4} \approx 3.554 \times 10^{12} - 0.00888 \times 10^{12} \approx 3.545 \times 10^{12} \; \text{K}^{4}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into F-1:**

$$
q = 0.78 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (3.545 \times 10^{12})
$$

$$
q = 0.78 \times 5.67 \times 3.545 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
q = 15.67 \times 10^{4} \;\; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,q \approx 1.567 \times 10^{5} \;\text{W/m}^{2} = 156.7 \;\text{kW/m}^{2}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

A bare worker receives **~157 kW/m²**. Tolerable limit (ISO 13732-1) for unprotected skin is **1.6 kW/m²** — exposure must be **< 2 seconds**. This justifies:

* **Fixed heat shields** between the run-out table and walkway.
* **Mandatory aluminized PPE** rated to ISO 11612 (radiant heat transfer index ≥ 18 s).
* **Minimum approach distance** calculated from inverse-square law:

$$
q_{worker} = \frac{A_{slab}}{2 \pi r^{2}} \cdot q_{emit}
$$

Solving for $r$ at tolerable flux of 1.6 kW/m²:

$$
r = \sqrt{\frac{A_{slab} \cdot q_{emit}}{2 \pi \cdot q_{tolerable}}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Ram Force in Pipe Bending (F-3)

**Problem:** An induction-bending machine bends a **6-inch (168.3 mm OD)** carbon-steel pipe at **850 °C**. The hydraulic cylinder has a piston **diameter = 250 mm** and operates at a working pressure of **210 bar**. Calculate the ram force.

**Step 1 — List known quantities:**

* $D_{piston} = 250 \; \text{mm} = 0.250 \; \text{m}$
* $P_{hyd} = 210 \; \text{bar} = 210 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} = 2.10 \times 10^{7} \; \text{Pa}$

**Step 2 — Compute piston area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.250)^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0625}{4} = \frac{0.19635}{4} \approx 0.04909 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply F-3:**

$$
F = P \cdot A = (2.10 \times 10^{7}) \times (0.04909)
$$

$$
F = 1.0309 \times 10^{6} \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F \approx 1.03 \; \text{MN} \;\; (\text{≈ } 105 \; \text{tonnes-force})\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety implication:**

A **105-tonne** force at the die makes **hose-burst protection** and **light-curtain interlocks** non-negotiable. The F-3 calculation directly justifies the **relief-valve setting** at **~115 %** of working pressure (= 241.5 bar) and the requirement for **safety-burst sleeves** on all high-pressure hoses (per **ISO 4413**).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recrystallization Temperature Confirmation (F-2)

**Problem:** Confirm whether hot rolling of mild steel at **1100 °C** is thermodynamically in the hot-working regime.

**Step 1 — Convert melting point of iron to Kelvin:**

$$
T_{melt} = 1538 \;^{\circ}\text{C} + 273 = 1811 \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply F-2:**

$$
T_{recryst} = 0.4 \times 1811 = 724.4 \; \text{K} = 451.4 \; ^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Define hot-working threshold (rule of thumb):**

$$
T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \cdot T_{melt} \;[\text{K}]
$$

$$
T_{hot,min} = 0.6 \times 1811 = 1086.6 \; \text{K} = 813.6 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare working temperature:**

$$
T_{work} = 1100 \;^{\circ}\text{C} = 1373 \; \text{K} > 1086.6 \; \text{K} \;\; ✅
$$

**Conclusion:** Mild-steel hot rolling at 1100 °C occurs well above the hot-working threshold, ensuring dynamic recrystallization and low flow stress.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safeguard Selection Logic

| Hazard Observed | First-Line Engineering Control | Administrative Backup | PPE (Last Resort) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roll-nip entanglement | Fixed perimeter guard + trip wire | LOTO during roll change | Avoid loose clothing |
| Scale ejection from descaling box | Enclosed descaling cabinet | Exclusion-zone marking | Face shield (EN 166) |
| Hot pipe radiant heat | Heat shield / water curtain | Job-rotation; cool-down timing | Aluminized suit (ISO 11612) |
| Hydraulic hose burst | Hose-burst sleeve + relief valve | Pre-use hose inspection | Hydraulic-resistant gloves |
| Crane drop of hot pipe | Anti-two-block, load-moment indicator | Banksman + certified slings | Safety boots (steel toe) |
| Induction coil RF burn | Faraday cage + interlocks | Only certified operators enter | Insulated gloves (Class 2) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Safeguards Architecture — Hot Rolling Mill + Hot Pipe Bending (Combined Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hot Rolling Mill Process] --> B1[Reheating Furnace Zone]
    A --> B2[Roughing Mill Zone]
    A --> B3[Finishing Mill + Run-Out Table]
    A --> B4[Coiler Zone]

    C[Hot Pipe Bending Process] --> D1[Pipe Loading + Crane]
    C --> D2[Induction Heating Zone]
    C --> D3[Bending Ram + Die Zone]
    C --> D4[Cooling + Unloading Zone]

    B1 --> HZ1[Furnace Explosion Hazard]
    B2 --> HZ2[Descaling Water + Scale Hazard]
    B3 --> HZ3[Roll-Nip + Hot Slab Hazard]
    B4 --> HZ4[Rotating Mandrel Hazard]

    D1 --> HZ5[Dropped Load + Crush Hazard]
    D2 --> HZ6[RF Burn + Coil Water Hazard]
    D3 --> HZ7[Pinch + Hydraulic Burst Hazard]
    D4 --> HZ8[Steam Explosion Hazard]

    HZ1 --> SF1[Engineering Controls]
    HZ2 --> SF1
    HZ3 --> SF1
    HZ4 --> SF1
    HZ5 --> SF1
    HZ6 --> SF1
    HZ7 --> SF1
    HZ8 --> SF1

    SF1 --> SF1A[Fixed Guards + Light Curtains]
    SF1 --> SF1B[Trip Wires + E-Stops]
    SF1 --> SF1C[Heat Shields + Water Curtains]
    SF1 --> SF1D[Pressure Relief + Hose Sleeves]
    SF1 --> SF1E[Interlocks on Doors + Coils]

    SF1 --> SF2[Administrative Controls]
    SF2 --> SF2A[LOTO + Permit to Work]
    SF2 --> SF2B[SOP + Operator Training]
    SF2 --> SF2C[Inspection + Maintenance Schedules]

    SF2 --> SF3[PPE Last Line]
    SF3 --> SF3A[Aluminized Heat Suit ISO 11612]
    SF3 --> SF3B[Face Shield + Safety Helmet]
    SF3 --> SF3C[Heat-Resistant Gloves]
    SF3 --> SF3D[Steel-Toe Safety Boots]

    SF3 --> SF4[Standards Compliance]
    SF4 --> SF4A[Indian Factories Act 1948]
    SF4 --> SF4B[IS 8369 Rolling Mill Safety]
    SF4 --> SF4C[ISO 4413 Hydraulic Safety]
    SF4 --> SF4D[ISO 13849 Control System Safety]
    SF4 --> SF4E[ISO 11612 PPE for Heat]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy-of-Controls Pyramid — Applied to Both Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[ELIMINATE Hazard at Source] --> L2
    L2[SUBSTITUTE with Safer Process] --> L3
    L3[ENGINEERING Controls - Guards, Shields, Interlocks] --> L4
    L4[ADMINISTRATIVE Controls - LOTO, SOP, Permit] --> L5
    L5[PPE - Aluminized Suit, Face Shield, Gloves]

    L1 -.- N1[Most Effective]
    L5 -.- N2[Least Effective]
```

### 4.3 Hot Pipe Bending — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pipe Storage Yard] --> P2[Conveyor + Crane Lift]
    P2 --> P3[Pre-heat Furnace OR Induction Coil]
    P3 --> P4[Pipe Reaches 850 C Target]
    P4 --> P5[Hydraulic Bending Ram Engages]
    P5 --> P6[Mandrel Supports Inner Wall]
    P6 --> P7[Bend Verified by Laser Scanner]
    P7 --> P8[Air Cool or Water Quench]
    P8 --> P9[Inspection + Hydrotest]
    P9 --> P10[Finished Bend to Storage]

    P5 -. Hydraulic Burst Risk .-> S1[Hose-Burst Sleeve + Relief Valve]
    P5 -. Pinch Point .-> S2[Light Curtain + Two-Hand Control]
    P4 -. Stray RF .-> S3[Faraday Cage + Interlocked Door]
    P8 -. Steam Explosion .-> S4[Controlled Quench + Exclusion Zone]
    P7 -. Overbend .-> S5[Load Cell Feedback Auto-Stop]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each *dotted red arrow* represents a **hazard vector** and its corresponding **safeguard**. In the KTU exam, you may be asked to draw a similar diagram for any process — memorize this *hazard → safeguard* annotation pattern; it consistently earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1.** Define a hot rolling mill. List any **four major hazards** encountered in the roughing and finishing zones. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hot rolling mill** is a heavy industrial facility in which metallic stock heated above its recrystallization temperature is plastically deformed between counter-rotating work rolls to achieve a desired cross-section and mechanical properties.

Four major hazards:
1. **Roll-nip entanglement** between counter-rotating rolls (mechanical hazard).
2. **Radiant heat exposure** from slabs at 1100 °C–1250 °C (thermal hazard).
3. **Scale (FeO) ejection** at high velocity during descaling (mechanical + thermal).
4. **Run-out table pinch points** between rollers and stock (mechanical hazard).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for four hazards = 1 + 2 = 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2.** What are the **engineering safeguards** provided during induction bending of hot pipes? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Faraday cage enclosure** around the induction coil to prevent RF burns and confine stray electromagnetic field.
2. **Cooling-water interlocks** — bending cycle cannot start unless coil-water flow and temperature are within safe limits.
3. **Light-curtain / safety-mat perimeter** around the bending die to detect operator intrusion and trigger hydraulic block.
4. **Hose-burst protection sleeves** on all high-pressure hydraulic hoses (working pressure ~210 bar).
5. **Load-cell feedback** to auto-stop ram travel if bending force exceeds design envelope.

*(Any 3 well-explained points = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice:** Question A OR Question B must be attempted. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Both choices are provided below.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hot Rolling Mill Focus `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

##### **Q.A (a)** Discuss the **mechanical and thermal hazards** in a hot rolling mill. Categorize them by energy domain and indicate the typical magnitude (temperature, pressure, or rotational speed). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanical Hazards:**
1. **Roll-nip points** — pinch points at roll bite; rolls rotate at **50–600 RPM** with enormous torque (up to 10 MN·m on finishing stands).
2. **Gear-box / pinion stand** — exposed gear trains with peripheral speeds up to **15 m/s**.
3. **Coiler mandrel** — rotates at **180–600 RPM**; entanglement hazard during threading.
4. **Shears / croppers** — high-energy cutting strokes for billet trimming.
5. **Crane movement of hot ingots** — gravitational + kinetic energy of suspended load (up to 50 t).

**Thermal Hazards:**
1. **Radiant heat** from slabs at **1100 °C–1250 °C** emitting ~157 kW/m² (derived in §3.1).
2. **Convective heat** from hot scale-laden water plume at the descaling box.
3. **Molten metal splash** during scale ejection or furnace charging.
4. **Burns from contact** with hot rolls (surface temp ~200 °C–400 °C).
5. **Steam explosions** if cooling water contacts hot metal unintentionally.

*(Allocate ~3.5 marks mechanical + ~3.5 marks thermal. Use **magnitude figures** to demonstrate quantitative understanding.)*

---

##### **Q.A (b)** Explain the **engineering and administrative safeguards** in a hot rolling mill with reference to relevant **Indian Standards / ISO clauses**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Engineering Safeguards:**
1. **Fixed perimeter guards** around all rotating parts (per **IS 8369**).
2. **Trip-wire / pull-cable E-stops** along roller tables (actuation distance < 0.5 s).
3. **Light curtains and safety mats** at access points.
4. **Two-hand control** for inching operations on rolls.
5. **Burner Management System (BMS)** with flame-failure trip (NFPA 85 / IS 14604).
6. **Hydraulic accumulators** with safety block valves on coiler mandrel.
7. **Water-curtain heat shields** between run-out table and walkway.

**Administrative Safeguards:**
1. **Lock-Out / Tag-Out (LOTO)** during roll change or maintenance (per **OSHA 1910.147** equivalent Indian provisions).
2. **Permit-to-Work** for furnace entry, hot work near hydraulics.
3. **SOP + toolbox talks** at shift start.
4. **Operator competency certification** as per **Skill Council for Green Jobs** guidelines.
5. **Predictive maintenance schedule** for rolls, gear-box, hydraulics.

*(Allocate ~4 marks engineering + ~3 marks administrative. Naming standards earns extra marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Hot Pipe Bending Focus `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

##### **Q.B (a)** Describe the **hot bending process of pipes**. Explain the **safety requirements** during induction heating and the bending ram stroke. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process Description:**
A pipe is loaded onto the bender via overhead crane. An **induction coil** (5–20 kA at 1–10 kHz) heats a narrow band to **800 °C–950 °C**. The pipe is then advanced while a **hydraulic ram** pushes it against a curved die, producing a controlled bend. An internal **mandrel** prevents wall collapse and ovalization. The bend is air-cooled or water-quenched, then inspected and hydrostatically tested.

**Safety Requirements — Induction Heating:**
1. **Faraday-cage enclosure** to confine RF leakage (operator exposure limit: **10 W/m²** per ICNIRP).
2. **Cooling-water interlocks** — coil fails safe if flow < minimum or temperature > setpoint (e.g., 45 °C).
3. **Door interlock** — coil de-energizes if any access door opens.
4. **PPE:** Insulated gloves Class 2 (per **IS 4770**), face shield with IR filter.

**Safety Requirements — Bending Ram Stroke:**
1. **Light-curtain perimeter** — operator intrusion triggers immediate hydraulic block (response < 30 ms per **ISO 13849 PL d**).
2. **Hose-burst sleeves** on all hydraulic hoses (per **ISO 4413**).
3. **Pressure-relief valve** set at 115 % of working pressure.
4. **Load-cell feedback** with auto-stop on force exceedance.
5. **Two-hand control** for manual override mode.

*(Allocate ~2 marks process + ~2.5 marks induction safety + ~2.5 marks ram safety.)*

---

##### **Q.B (b)** Discuss the **types of PPE** required for hot bending operations. State the relevant **Indian/ISO standards** and minimum performance ratings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| PPE Item | Standard | Minimum Rating | Hazard Addressed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminized heat suit (jacket + trousers) | **ISO 11612** | Radiant heat transfer index (RHTI) ≥ **18 s** at 20–40 kW/m² | Radiant heat from 850 °C pipe |
| Face shield with IR filter | **EN 166 / IS 8521** | Temperature range **-30 °C to 250 °C** | IR radiation + flying scale |
| Heat-resistant gloves (cuff type) | **EN 407 / IS 6153** | Contact heat Level 2 (≥ 250 °C, 15 s) | Hot pipe handling |
| Steel-toe safety boots | **IS 15298 / EN ISO 20345** | Impact 200 J, Compression 15 kN | Dropped pipe / scale |
| Ear protection | **IS 6229 / EN 352** | SNR ≥ 25 dB | Hydraulic pump + induction noise (~95 dBA) |
| Respiratory mask | **IS 9473 / EN 149** | FFP2 (filtering ≥ 94 %) | Scale dust + oil mist |

**Additional Notes:**
- PPE selection must follow the **hierarchy of controls** — PPE is the *last line of defence*.
- PPE must be **inspected before each shift** and replaced if damaged.
- **Training** on donning, doffing, and limitations of PPE is mandatory under the **Indian Factories Act §71**.

*(Allocate marks per category — table is worth ~4 marks, narrative ~3 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Board Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Hierarchy of Controls** — Listing PPE first or skipping Engineering/Administrative layers costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **No Quantitative Data** — Failing to cite temperatures (1100 °C), pressures (210 bar), or rotational speeds (180–600 RPM) reduces marks in the *"Discuss"* questions.
> 3. **Missing Indian Standards** — Examiners reward **IS codes / ISO clauses** by name (IS 8369, ISO 11612, IS 15298). Always cite at least 2 standards.
> 4. **Confusing PPE ratings** — Mixing up *EN 388* (mechanical) and *EN 407* (thermal) gloves is a common error.
> 5. **No "Hazard → Safeguard" Mapping** — Vague answers that don't link each hazard to a *specific* safeguard lose the *"engineering judgment"* mark.
> 6. **Skipping Administrative Controls** — Many students only discuss guards and PPE, ignoring LOTO, Permit-to-Work, and SOPs — this costs ~2 marks.
> 7. **Wrong use of "Hot Work Permit"** — Hot bending of pipes *is* hot work; mention permit-to-work explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅

> 📝 **High-Density Revision Card — Print This Section**

- ☐ Hot rolling mill = plastic deformation of metal **above recrystallization temperature** (>0.6 T_melt in K).
- ☐ Typical hot rolling temp for mild steel: **1100 °C – 1250 °C**.
- ☐ **Roll-nip entanglement** = #1 fatality cause in rolling mills → fixed guards + trip wires + light curtains mandatory.
- ☐ Radiant heat flux from 1100 °C slab ≈ **157 kW/m²** → 100× human tolerance → heat shields + aluminized PPE essential.
- ☐ Hot pipe bending temp: **700 °C – 1000 °C**; bending ram pressure: **100–300 bar**.
- ☐ Hydraulic ram force: $F = P \cdot A$ — always confirm hose-burst sleeve and relief valve.
- ☐ Recrystallization temperature formula: $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \cdot T_{melt}$ (in K).
- ☐ **Hierarchy of Controls (top → bottom):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ☐ **PPE for heat:** Aluminized suit per **ISO 11612**, RHTI ≥ 18 s; face shield per **EN 166**; gloves per **EN 407**.
- ☐ **Safety boots:** **IS 15298** (200 J impact, 15 kN compression).
- ☐ **Hydraulic safety:** **ISO 4413** (general), **ISO 13849 PL d** (control system).
- ☐ **Indian Factories Act 1948 §71, §72** govern PPE provision and worker training.
- ☐ **Permit-to-Work** required for furnace entry, hot work, confined space.
- ☐ **LOTO** mandatory during roll change, die change, hydraulic maintenance.
- ☐ Induction bending requires **Faraday cage + door interlock + cooling-water interlock**.
- ☐ Always map **hazard → safeguard → standard** in long answers.
- ☐ Cite at least **2 IS / ISO codes** in every 14-mark answer.
- ☐ Cool-down time for hot pipe (F-6): $t_{cool} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h \cdot A}$ — use to define exclusion-zone duration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards and Control Measures - Safety in Gas Furnace Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazards and Control Measures - Safety in Gas Furnace Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gas Furnace:** An industrial heating appliance that generates thermal energy through the controlled combustion of a gaseous fuel (typically **Natural Gas (CH₄)**, **LPG (C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀)**, or **producer gas**) mixed with air inside a refractory-lined combustion chamber, with the hot gases used for process heating, melting, or steam generation.

A **gas furnace operation** encompasses the entire lifecycle of activities involved in safe running of such equipment — from fuel delivery, burner ignition, combustion monitoring, heat recovery, to flue gas exhaust. From a Safety Engineering standpoint, KTU 2024 Scheme defines the safe operation regime as one where the **fire triangle** (Fuel + Oxygen + Ignition Source) is maintained strictly within engineered envelope limits, with redundant interlocks to prevent any uncontrolled release of energy or toxic species.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Hazard Philosophy:** Industrial gas furnaces present a converging hazard matrix — **thermal, chemical, mechanical, and asphyxiation** hazards simultaneously. The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *hazard recognition* followed by *engineering control hierarchy* (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a giant industrial "pressure cooker" running on highly flammable natural gas. In your home kitchen, you can see the flame, smell leaks instantly, and shut off the valve by hand in seconds. But an industrial furnace:

- Burns at **1200 °C – 1700 °C** (invisible radiant heat)
- Uses gas at pressures of **2–5 bar** (much higher than domestic supply)
- Operates in enclosed halls where leaks can accumulate unnoticed
- Runs 24/7 with minimal human supervision

So the safety challenge is: **you cannot "see" or "smell" a disaster coming until it is too late.** Therefore, every parameter — gas pressure, airflow, flame presence, temperature, oxygen level — must be monitored automatically, and failures must trigger **fail-safe shutdowns** automatically. Think of it like an airplane's autopilot: the human operator sets the destination, but the safety systems ensure that if *anything* deviates, the system defaults to the safest possible state.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook — The "SAFE-FIRE" Acronym for Gas Furnace Hazards:**
> **S**udden explosions · **A**sphyxiation · **F**lame flashbacks · **E**xcessive temperatures · **F**urnace puffback · **I**ncomplete combustion (CO) · **R**efractory failure · **E**xhaust emissions

## 1.3 Primary Physical Constants & Standards

The following constants and reference values are central to gas furnace safety calculations:

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFRₛ)** for methane = **17.2 : 1** (by mass)
- **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** of methane = **5%** by volume in air
- **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)** of methane = **15%** by volume in air
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** of methane = **537 °C (1000 °F)**
- **Flammability Limits of LPG (Propane)** = **2.1% to 9.5%** in air
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for CO** = **50 ppm** (8-hr TWA, OSHA)
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) for CO** = **1200 ppm**
- **Minimum Pilot Flame Detection Current** = typically **5 µA** (flame rectification)
- **NFPA 54** (National Fuel Gas Code) — governing standard for gas-burning equipment
- **IS 4246** (Indian Standard for Industrial Gas Furnaces)
- **NFPA 86** — Standard for Ovens and Furnaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any gas concentration between LEL and UEL forms an **explosive mixture**. Furnace enclosures must therefore be ventilated to keep any leak well below **25% of LEL** (the standard alarm trigger point).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hazards in Gas Furnace Operation

The hazards can be categorized along four engineering axes:

### 2.1.1 Fuel-Side Hazards (Upstream of Burner)
1. **Gas Leaks** — from corroded pipes, loose flanges, or faulty valves.
2. **High-Pressure Release** — regulator failure causing over-pressurization.
3. **Odor Fade / Loss of Odorant** — mercaptan (the smell agent) being absorbed by certain pipe materials, making leaks undetectable by smell.
4. **Liquid Carryover** in LPG systems — liquid entering the regulator causing vapor lock and pressure surges.

### 2.1.2 Combustion-Side Hazards (At Burner)
1. **Puffback / Flashback** — flame traveling back into the gas train.
2. **Impingement** — flame striking cold surfaces causing incomplete combustion.
3. **Flashback** — flame speed exceeding gas velocity, propagating upstream.
4. **Flame Failure** — sudden flame extinction with continued gas flow (creates explosive accumulation).

### 2.1.3 Combustion-Product Hazards (Downstream)
1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO) generation** — indicates incomplete combustion (insufficient air).
2. **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)** — high-temperature oxidation of atmospheric N₂.
3. **Refractory degradation** — releasing toxic fumes (e.g., silica dust, chrome compounds).
4. **Condensate corrosion** — water + SOx forming sulfuric acid in cooler sections.

### 2.1.4 Operational & Environmental Hazards
1. **Burn injuries** — radiant heat exposure to operators.
2. **Confined Space Asphyxiation** — oxygen displacement by leaked gas.
3. **Explosions** — within the furnace or in the flue passage.
4. **Noise** — burner noise up to **110 dB(A)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hierarchy of Controls (per KTU 2024 / OSHA):**
> 1. **Elimination** — substitute hazardous fuel (e.g., electric furnace)
> 2. **Substitution** — use cleaner fuel (e.g., LNG instead of coal gas)
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — flame arrestors, flame scanners, interlocks
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — SOPs, training, permits
> 5. **PPE** — heat-resistant gloves, face shields, respirators

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Burner Management

A modern gas furnace operates on a **Burner Management System (BMS)** sequence, typically following the **NFPA 85 Boiler Combustion System Safety Standard**:

### Pre-Purge Phase
- Combustion chamber is purged with fresh air (typically **4 to 6 air changes**) to remove any residual flammable mixture.
- **Purpose:** ensure combustible gas concentration is below **25% LEL** before ignition.

### Ignition Trial Phase
- Pilot flame is established using a high-energy igniter (spark or hot surface).
- Pilot is proven by **flame rectification** or **UV/IR flame scanner**.
- **Trial-for-ignition time** is limited to **10 seconds** (per NFPA 85).

### Main Flame Establishment
- Main gas valve opens after pilot is proven.
- Flame must be detected within a defined **flame failure response time (FFRT)** — typically **1 to 4 seconds**.

### Running Phase (Steady-State)
- Continuous monitoring of:
  - Flame signal strength
  - Furnace pressure (typically **−2 to −5 mmWC** negative pressure)
  - Air-fuel ratio
  - Stack temperature
  - Combustion efficiency

### Emergency Shutdown (ESD)
Triggered by any of:
- Flame failure (FFRT exceeded)
- Low air pressure / loss of combustion air
- High furnace pressure
- Loss of pilot
- Low gas pressure
- Power failure
- High stack temperature
- **All ESDs must be Fail-Safe (De-energize to Trip)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard / Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric air for CH₄ | $\mathrm{AFR_s} = \dfrac{2(32)+8}{16} = 4$ mol air/mol fuel = **17.2 kg air/kg fuel** | Mass basis | Combustion calculations |
| Theoretical CO₂ in flue gas | $\%\mathrm{CO_2} = \dfrac{\text{mol CO}_2}{\text{mol total flue gas}} \times 100$ | Volume % | Combustion efficiency check |
| Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{\text{Heat Input} - \text{Stack Losses}}{\text{Heat Input}} \times 100$ | % | Furnace performance |
| Stack Loss (Siegert) | $L_s = \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{\mathrm{CO_2\%}} \times K$ | % | Empirical flue gas loss |
| Excess Air Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}} = 1.10 \text{ to } 1.30$ typical | Dimensionless | Burner tuning |
| Flame Velocity (CH₄) | $v_f \approx \mathbf{0.4 \; m/s}$ at stoichiometric | m/s | Flashback risk |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature (CH₄) | $T_{AIT} = \mathbf{537\;°C}$ | °C | Safety limit |
| Gas Pressure Drop in Train | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ (Hagen-Poiseuille, laminar) | Pa | Pipe sizing |
| LEL Trigger for Alarm | $C_{alarm} = 0.25 \times \mathrm{LEL}$ | % vol | Gas detector calibration |
| Oxygen Deficiency Alarm | $O_2 < \mathbf{19.5\%}$ | % vol | Confined space entry |
| Furnace Draft (Negative) | $P_{draft} = -2 \text{ to } -5 \text{ mmWC}$ | mmWC | Pressure control |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the BMS sequence:** **"P-P-I-M-R-E"** → **P**urge, **P**ilot, **I**gnite, **M**ain flame, **R**un, **E**mergency shutdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gas furnace safety systems are the cornerstone of:
- **Steel re-rolling mills** (reheating furnaces)
- **Glass manufacturing** (tank furnaces operating at 1500 °C)
- **Cement plants** (pre-heater / kiln burners)
- **Petrochemical cracking units**
- **Food processing** (drying ovens)
- **Power plants** (auxiliary boilers)

Failure to control these hazards has historically caused catastrophic losses — for instance, the **2005 Texas City refinery explosion** (15 deaths) was attributed to a faulty **burner management system** and **flame failure detection** failure. Hence, KTU 2024 Scheme places significant emphasis on **engineering control logic** in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Methane Combustion

The complete combustion of methane is represented as:

$$
\mathrm{CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + \text{Heat}}
$$

Since air contains approximately **21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume** (and roughly 23% O₂ / 77% N₂ by mass), we calculate air requirement on a **molar basis** first, then convert to mass.

### Step 1: Oxygen Requirement
1 mole of CH₄ requires 2 moles of O₂.

### Step 2: Air Requirement (Molar)
Since air contains 21% O₂ by volume:

$$
n_{air} = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \dfrac{2}{0.21} = 9.524 \; \text{mol air per mol CH}_4
$$

### Step 3: Mass Conversion
Molecular weights: CH₄ = 16 kg/kmol, Air ≈ 28.97 kg/kmol.

$$
\mathrm{AFR_{molar\ basis}} = 9.524 \times 28.97 = 275.9 \; \text{kg air per kmol CH}_4
$$

### Step 4: Mass Air-Fuel Ratio
$$
\mathrm{AFR_{mass}} = \dfrac{275.9}{16} = 17.24 \;\dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{AFR_s \approx 17.2 \; kg \; air / kg \; CH_4}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** For every 1 kg of natural gas burned, **17.2 kg of air** is required for complete combustion. In practice, **15–30% excess air** is supplied to ensure full combustion and prevent CO formation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Excess Air Ratio and Flue Gas Composition

If actual air supplied = $A_a$ and stoichiometric air = $A_s$, then:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{A_a}{A_s}
$$

For $\lambda = 1.20$ (20% excess air) with methane, the flue gas composition per 100 mol of fuel is:

### Step 1: O₂ Supplied
$$
n_{O_2,\,supplied} = 2 \times 1.20 = 2.4 \; \text{mol}
$$

### Step 2: O₂ Consumed
All C → CO₂ (1 mol), all H → H₂O (2 mol). O₂ needed = 2 mol. Excess = $2.4 - 2 = 0.4$ mol O₂.

### Step 3: N₂ from Air
$$
n_{N_2} = 9.524 \times 1.20 \times 0.79 = 9.03 \; \text{mol N}_2
$$

### Step 4: Total Flue Gas Moles
$$
n_{total} = 1(\mathrm{CO_2}) + 2(\mathrm{H_2O}) + 0.4(\mathrm{O_2}) + 9.03(\mathrm{N_2}) = 12.43 \; \text{mol}
$$

### Step 5: Volume Percentages
$$
\%\mathrm{CO_2} = \dfrac{1}{12.43} \times 100 \approx 8.05\%
$$
$$
\%\mathrm{O_2} = \dfrac{0.4}{12.43} \times 100 \approx 3.22\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Flue gas composition (CH}_4, 20\% \text{ excess air)} = 8.05\%\;\mathrm{CO_2},\; 16.1\%\;\mathrm{H_2O},\; 3.22\%\;\mathrm{O_2},\; 72.6\%\;\mathrm{N_2}}
$$

> **Note:** This composition is what a flue gas **Orsat analyzer** should read in a healthy furnace. **Higher O₂** = excess air wasted (lower efficiency). **Higher CO** = incomplete combustion (safety hazard).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LEL Alarm Trigger Calculation

The gas detection system must alarm before the atmosphere reaches the LEL. Industry standard is **25% of LEL**.

For methane, LEL = **5% by volume** in air.

$$
C_{alarm\,set\,point} = 0.25 \times \mathrm{LEL} = 0.25 \times 5\% = 1.25\% \; \text{CH}_4 \; \text{by volume}
$$

This is the **first alarm level**. A second alarm at **50% LEL** triggers evacuation, and **100% LEL** triggers automatic process shutdown.

For LPG (LEL = 2.1%):
$$
C_{alarm} = 0.25 \times 2.1\% = 0.525\% \; \text{LPG}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Gas detectors must be **calibrated annually** and **bump-tested** (functional check) every **90 days** per **IS 15105 / IEC 60079-29-2**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Control Measure

| **Hazard** | **Root Cause** | **Engineering Control** | **Administrative Control** | **PPE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gas leak | Pipe corrosion, loose flange | Automatic gas shut-off valve; LEL detectors; **flame arrestors** | Permit-to-work; routine inspection | SCBA, gas monitor |
| Explosion | Accumulated gas + ignition | Explosion vents; **inert gas purging**; explosion suppression system | Hot work permit; gas testing | Flame-retardant coveralls |
| CO poisoning | Insufficient air / poor mixing | **Air-fuel ratio controller**; O₂ trim; CO analyzer | Combustion tuning schedule | CO dosimeter |
| Flashback | Flame velocity > gas velocity | **Flame arrester** in gas train; flashback arrestor at burner | Burner maintenance log | Face shield |
| Asphyxiation | O₂ displacement in confined space | Forced ventilation; **O₂ sensors** with alarm | Confined space entry permit | SCBA / supplied air |
| Burn injury | High surface temperature | Insulation; **interlocks on access doors** | Warning signs; lockout-tagout | Aluminized suit, gloves |
| Refractory failure | Thermal cycling | Periodic inspection; **thermocouples** | Refractory life tracking | Dust mask |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Combustion Air Requirement for a Reheating Furnace

**Given:**
- Furnace rated heat input: $Q = 5 \;\mathrm{MW}$
- Fuel: Natural gas (assume pure CH₄, calorific value = **55.5 MJ/kg**)
- Excess air: **20%**

**Step 1: Fuel Consumption**
$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{Q}{\mathrm{CV}} = \dfrac{5}{55.5} = 0.0901 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2: Stoichiometric Air**
$$
\dot{m}_{air,s} = 0.0901 \times 17.2 = 1.549 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3: Actual Air with 20% Excess**
$$
\dot{m}_{air,a} = 1.2 \times 1.549 = 1.859 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Combustion Products**
$$
\dot{m}_{flue} = 0.0901 + 1.859 - 0 \; (\text{no ash}) = 1.949 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5: Volumetric Flow at Standard Conditions (assuming flue gas density ≈ 1.3 kg/m³)**
$$
\dot{V}_{flue} = \dfrac{1.949}{1.3} \approx 1.50 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Design Ventilation/Exhaust Fan Capacity (with 1.25 safety factor):**
$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_{fan} = 1.5 \times 1.25 = 1.875 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \;\; (\approx \; 6750 \; \text{m}^3/\text{hr})}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Burner Management System (BMS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Supply Main Valve] --> B[Manual Ball Valve]
    B --> C[Pressure Regulator]
    C --> D[Solenoid Valve 1 - Safety Shut Off]
    D --> E[Solenoid Valve 2 - Tight Shut Off]
    E --> F[Gas Pressure Switch - Low / High]
    F --> G[Burner Orifice]
    G --> H[Combustion Air Blower]
    H --> I[Air Pressure Switch]
    I --> J[Flame Scanner UV or IR]
    J --> K[BMS Controller PLC]
    K --> L{Flame Proven Question}
    L -- Yes --> M[Main Flame Stable - Running Mode]
    L -- No --> N[Alarm and Emergency Shutdown]
    M --> O[Stack Temperature Monitor]
    O --> P[Furnace Pressure Transmitter]
    P --> Q[Continuous Operation]
    Q --> R[Furnace Process Load]
    N --> S[Gas Isolation - Fail Safe Closed]
    S --> T[Alarm Horn and Beacon]
    T --> U[Operator Notification]
    style K fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#ff9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#99ff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** Every block in yellow is an **active decision node**. The flow direction shows how gas, air, and signals converge at the **PLC** (Programmable Logic Controller). Red blocks indicate **fail-safe pathways** — when triggered, they default to a safe state (gas closed, no ignition).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Furnace Safety Interlock Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["Sensor Inputs"]
        I1[Gas Pressure Low]
        I2[Gas Pressure High]
        I3[Air Pressure Low]
        I4[Flame Failure]
        I5[Furnace Over Pressure]
        I6[Stack Temp High]
        I7[O2 Sensor Low 19.5 percent]
        I8[LEL Detector Above 25 percent]
    end
    
    subgraph Logic["Safety PLC - SIL 2 or SIL 3"]
        LOGIC{ANY Trip Condition Active Question}
    end
    
    subgraph Outputs["Actuator Outputs"]
        O1[Close Solenoid Valve 1]
        O2[Close Solenoid Valve 2]
        O3[Stop Combustion Blower]
        O4[Sound Alarm Siren]
        O5[Flash Beacon]
        O6[Vent Furnace - Dampers Open]
    end
    
    I1 --> LOGIC
    I2 --> LOGIC
    I3 --> LOGIC
    I4 --> LOGIC
    I5 --> LOGIC
    I6 --> LOGIC
    I7 --> LOGIC
    I8 --> LOGIC
    
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O1
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O2
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O3
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O4
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O5
    LOGIC -- YES Trip --> O6
```

> **Engineering Note:** This is a **2-out-of-2 voting architecture** (basic) for small furnaces. Critical furnaces use **2-out-of-3 voting** for higher availability. Safety Integrity Level (SIL) per **IEC 61508** is critical in BMS design.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Burner Operating Sequence

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> PRE_PURGE : Start Command
    PRE_PURGE --> PILOT_IGNITION : Purge Complete, 4 air changes
    PILOT_IGNITION --> PILOT_PROVEN : Flame Detected within 10 sec
    PILOT_IGNITION --> LOCKOUT : No Flame, Trial Timeout
    PILOT_PROVEN --> MAIN_IGNITION : Open Main Valve
    MAIN_IGNITION --> RUNNING : Main Flame Stable
    MAIN_IGNITION --> LOCKOUT : Flame Failure FFRT exceeded
    RUNNING --> RUNNING : All Parameters Normal
    RUNNING --> LOCKOUT : Any Trip Signal
    LOCKOUT --> OFF : Manual Reset after Root Cause Analysis
    LOCKOUT --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> This state diagram directly maps to **NFPA 85 sequence logic**. KTU examiners often ask students to draw or describe this sequence for 7-mark questions.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Gas Train with Safety Devices (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source - City Gas or LPG] --> B[Vibration Eliminator]
    B --> C[Manual Ball Valve - 1]
    C --> D[Strainer Filter]
    D --> E[Pressure Regulator]
    E --> F[Solenoid Valve 1 - Safety]
    F --> G[Solenoid Valve 2 - Tight Shut Off]
    G --> H[Leak Test Valve]
    H --> I[Manual Ball Valve - 2]
    I --> J[Gas Pressure Switch]
    J --> K[Burner Head with Flame Arrester]
    K --> L[Combustion Chamber]
    L --> M[Heat Exchanger / Process Load]
    M --> N[Flue Stack]
    style F fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#333
```

> **Two Solenoid Valves in Series** is the industry-standard requirement — they form a **double block and bleed** arrangement. Even if one leaks, the other isolates gas. This is mandated by **NFPA 86 / IS 15105**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: List any SIX major hazards associated with the operation of an industrial gas furnace.**

**Model Answer (Key Points for 3 marks):**

1. **Gas leaks** leading to fire/explosion (formation of explosive mixture within LEL-UEL range).
2. **Puffback / Flashback** — flame propagating back into the gas train.
3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO) generation** from incomplete combustion.
4. **Asphyxiation** in confined spaces due to oxygen displacement.
5. **Burn injuries** from high radiant heat and surface temperatures.
6. **Furnace explosion** due to accumulation of unburnt gas + delayed ignition.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, the KTU examiner expects **at least 4–5 distinct points** in concise bullet form. Each valid point = 0.5–1 mark.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define the term "Burner Management System (BMS)" and state its primary purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Burner Management System (BMS)** is a safety-critical control system that manages the safe start-up, operation, and shutdown of gas-fired furnace burners. It comprises sensors (flame scanners, pressure switches, temperature transmitters), logic controllers (PLCs/SIS), and final control elements (solenoid valves, dampers).

**Primary Purpose:** To **prevent uncontrolled combustion**, **flame failures**, and **explosions** by ensuring that fuel is admitted only when all pre-conditions for safe combustion are satisfied, and that any abnormal condition triggers a **fail-safe shutdown** within the prescribed **Flame Failure Response Time (FFRT)**.

> **Examiner Note:** Use the phrase "fail-safe shutdown" — it carries weight in marking.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Modeled on KTU ESE)

---

### **Question B-A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the sequence of operation of a Burner Management System (BMS) for an industrial gas furnace. Mention the relevant Indian/International standards.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction [1 mark]:**
The BMS is the heart of furnace safety, mandated under **NFPA 85 (Boiler Combustion Systems)** and **IS 15105**. It follows a strict state-machine logic.

**Operating Sequence [5 marks]:**

1. **Pre-Purge (4–6 air changes):** Combustion air blower runs; furnace pressure maintained at −2 to −5 mmWC; air pressure switch must prove airflow within **30 seconds**.

2. **Pilot Ignition Trial:** Spark ignitor energizes; pilot solenoid opens for **10 seconds max**. Pilot must be proven by **flame rectification** (signal > 5 µA).

3. **Main Flame Establishment:** Main gas valve opens **only after pilot is proven**. **Flame Failure Response Time (FFRT)** = 1–4 seconds.

4. **Running Mode:** Continuous monitoring of flame signal, gas pressure, air pressure, furnace pressure, stack temperature, and O₂ trim.

5. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD):** Any trip → both solenoid valves close within **< 1 second**. Alarms sound. **Manual reset only** after root cause analysis.

**Standards [1 mark]:**
- **NFPA 85** – Boiler Combustion System Safety
- **NFPA 86** – Ovens and Furnaces
- **IS 15105** – Indian Standard for gas burners
- **IEC 61508** – Functional Safety (SIL ratings)

**Diagram [Required for full marks — see Section 4.3 above].**

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A gas furnace operates on natural gas (CH₄) with **15% excess air**. Calculate: (i) the actual air-fuel ratio by mass, (ii) the flue gas composition (volume %), and (iii) the mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel. Assume complete combustion.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Fuel = CH₄; Excess air = 15%; CV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/kg; AFRₛ = 17.2 kg/kg.

**Step (i): Actual Air-Fuel Ratio [2 marks]**
$$
\mathrm{AFR_{actual}} = 1.15 \times 17.2 = 19.78 \; \text{kg air / kg fuel}
$$

**Step (ii): Flue Gas Composition [3 marks]**
Per kg of fuel: CH₄ supplied = 1 kg = 62.5 mol. O₂ supplied = $2 \times 1.15 \times 62.5 = 143.75$ mol. N₂ = $143.75 \times (79/21) = 540.8$ mol.
O₂ consumed = 125 mol (2 × 62.5). Excess O₂ = $143.75 - 125 = 18.75$ mol.
CO₂ produced = 62.5 mol. H₂O produced = 125 mol.
Total flue gas = $62.5 + 125 + 18.75 + 540.8 = 747.05$ mol.

$$
\%\mathrm{CO_2} = \dfrac{62.5}{747.05} \times 100 \approx 8.37\%
$$
$$
\%\mathrm{H_2O} = \dfrac{125}{747.05} \times 100 \approx 16.73\%
$$
$$
\%\mathrm{O_2} = \dfrac{18.75}{747.05} \times 100 \approx 2.51\%
$$
$$
\%\mathrm{N_2} = \dfrac{540.8}{747.05} \times 100 \approx 72.39\%
$$

**Step (iii): Mass of CO₂ produced per kg fuel [2 marks]**
$$
m_{CO_2} = 62.5 \times 44 = 2750 \; \text{g} = 2.75 \; \text{kg CO}_2 \;/\;\text{kg fuel}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\mathrm{AFR} = 19.78$, Flue gas = **8.37% CO₂, 16.73% H₂O, 2.51% O₂, 72.39% N₂**, $m_{CO_2} = 2.75$ kg/kg fuel.

---

### **Question B-B: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various **engineering control measures** employed for safety in gas furnace operations. Explain the role of flame arrestors, flashback arrestors, and double block-and-bleed valves with a labeled diagram of the gas train.

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Engineering Controls [3 marks]:**

| Control Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Fuel Supply Control** | Double block-and-bleed valves, pressure regulators, leak-test valves |
| **Combustion Control** | BMS, flame scanners, air-fuel ratio controllers |
| **Ignition Safety** | Spark ignitors, pilot flame monitors, flame arrestors |
| **Pressure/Vacuum Safety** | Pressure relief valves, explosion vents, draft controllers |
| **Detection & Alarm** | LEL detectors, CO analyzers, O₂ sensors, UV/IR flame scanners |
| **Emergency Isolation** | ESD buttons, remote shut-off valves, fire dampers |

**Specific Devices [4 marks]:**

1. **Flame Arrester:** A porous metal element (typically sintered bronze or corrugated steel) placed in the gas line. It allows gas to pass but **absorbs heat** from any flame attempting to travel upstream, cooling it below ignition temperature. Stops flashbacks.

2. **Flashback Arrestor:** Specifically designed to **stop flame propagation** in oxy-fuel systems; uses a **quenching mesh** with apertures smaller than the **quenching distance** of the gas (for CH₄ ≈ 2.5 mm).

3. **Double Block and Bleed (DBB) Valves:** Two **solenoid valves in series** with a **leak-test (bleed) valve** between them. The bleed valve allows safe pressure testing of valve integrity. Industry-standard for all industrial gas trains — required by **NFPA 86**.

**Diagram (Gas Train) [Already provided in Section 4.4].** Marks awarded for labeling: **Strainer, Regulator, Solenoid V1 & V2, Pressure Switch, Leak Test Valve.**

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **safety audit checklist** for a gas-fired industrial furnace. Your answer must include at least **10 distinct inspection items** with their **acceptance criteria**.

**Model Solution:**

A gas furnace safety audit is conducted quarterly per **IS 15105 / NFPA 86**. The checklist includes:

| # | Item | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gas pressure regulator operation | Output pressure within ±5% of setpoint |
| 2 | Solenoid valve leak test | Bleed rate < 0.1% of flow per minute |
| 3 | Flame scanner calibration | Signal ≥ 8 µA on steady flame |
| 4 | Low gas pressure switch trip | Trips at 80% of operating pressure |
| 5 | High gas pressure switch trip | Trips at 110% of operating pressure |
| 6 | Air pressure proving switch | Trips within 5 s of blower stop |
| 7 | Pilot trial-for-ignition time | ≤ 10 seconds |
| 8 | FFRT (Flame Failure Response Time) | ≤ 4 seconds |
| 9 | Furnace pressure switch | Trips at +5 mmWC / −8 mmWC |
| 10 | LEL gas detector calibration | Response within 30 s to test gas |
| 11 | Combustion analyzer reading | O₂ 2–4%, CO < 100 ppm |
| 12 | Emergency stop button function | ESD closes valves within 1 s |
| 13 | Insulation integrity | Surface temp < 60 °C |
| 14 | Stack emission compliance | PM < 50 mg/Nm³, NOx < 350 mg/Nm³ |

**Conclusion:** Every checklist item must have a **documented evidence** (calibration certificate, photo, signature) — KTU marks value **documentation** over vague statements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LEL with UEL** — students often write UEL when asked for LEL. Remember: LEL = **Lower** = the **lean** (low fuel) side; UEL = **Upper** = the **rich** (high fuel) side.
>
> 2. **Writing only 4 hazards** when 6 are asked — examiners deduct proportionally. Aim for **1.5× the asked quantity**.
>
> 3. **Not drawing the BMS sequence diagram** in 7-mark questions — even a hand-drawn rough state diagram can earn **2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing "Fail-Safe" directions** — the BMS must fail to the **SAFE** state (gas off, blower off). Wrong answer = 0 marks for that sub-part.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — KTU specifically penalizes missing units (e.g., writing only "17.2" instead of "17.2 kg air / kg fuel").
>
> 6. **Mixing up "Puffback" and "Flashback"** — Puffback is the furnace explosion from accumulated gas; Flashback is the flame traveling into the gas line. Different mechanisms.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning standards** (NFPA / IS / IEC) — examiners reward **standard references** as evidence of depth.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Gas Furnace Safety:**

### **Hazards (must-know 6)**
- Gas leaks and explosive mixture formation
- Puffback (delayed ignition in furnace)
- Flashback (flame into gas train)
- Incomplete combustion → CO poisoning
- Asphyxiation (O₂ < 19.5%)
- Thermal burn injuries

### **Key Numerical Values (must memorize)**
- LEL of CH₄ = **5%**, UEL = **15%** in air
- LEL of LPG = **2.1%**, UEL = **9.5%** in air
- AFRₛ for CH₄ = **17.2 kg/kg**
- AIT of CH₄ = **537 °C**
- CO PEL = **50 ppm**, IDLH = **1200 ppm**
- Gas detector alarm = **25% LEL**
- O₂ deficiency alarm = **19.5%**
- Pre-purge = **4–6 air changes**
- Pilot trial = **10 s max**
- FFRT = **1–4 s**

### **Engineering Controls (must know 8)**
- Double block-and-bleed valves
- Flame arrestor / flashback arrestor
- LEL gas detector + alarm
- UV/IR flame scanner
- Flame rectification
- Pressure regulators + pressure switches
- BMS with PLC + SIL rating
- Emergency shutdown (ESD) system

### **Standards (must reference)**
- **NFPA 85** – Boiler combustion safety
- **NFPA 86** – Ovens and furnaces
- **IS 15105** – Industrial gas burners (India)
- **IEC 61508** – Functional safety / SIL
- **NFPA 54** – National fuel gas code
- **IS 4246** – Industrial gas furnaces
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252** – Welding/cutting gas safety

### **BMS Sequence Mnemonic**
**P-P-I-M-R-E** → **P**urge → **P**ilot → **I**gnite → **M**ain flame → **R**un → **E**SD

### **Hierarchy of Controls Mnemonic**
**E-S-E-A-P** → **E**liminate → **S**ubstitute → **E**ngineering → **A**dministrative → **P**PE

### **Failure Mode Mnemonic for BMS Trips**
**GAF-PTS-OL** → **G**as pressure → **A**ir pressure → **F**lame failure → **P**ressure (furnace) → **T**emperature (stack) → **S**tand-by trip → **O**₂ low → **L**EL high

> **Final Tip:** When answering KTU questions on this topic, **always structure** your answer as: (1) Definition/Concept → (2) Diagram → (3) Standard reference → (4) Numerical justification → (5) Conclusion. Examiners reward this logical flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cupola
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cupola

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cupola Furnace (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **cupola** is a cylindrical, vertical, shaft-type melting furnace employed in foundry engineering for the continuous melting of ferrous metals — principally **cast iron** (grey iron, ductile iron, malleable iron), and occasionally **copper alloys** and **ferro-alloys**. The cupola operates on the principle of **counter-current heat exchange** between ascending combustion gases (from coke combustion) and descending solid charge (metal, coke, flux), enabling continuous charging, melting, and tapping operations.

The term *cupola* is derived from the Italian word *cupola*, meaning **"dome"** or **"small cup"**, owing to the rounded charging opening at the top of early designs. In the context of occupational safety engineering, the cupola is classified as a **Class-A High-Hazard Thermal Plant** under the Factory Act, 1948 (India), and falls under the regulatory purview of the **Indian Boilers Act** and **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** emissions norms for particulate matter and gaseous effluents.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stacked Sandwich Furnace"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Cupola is Like a Layered Sandwich Toaster**
>
> Imagine you have a tall vertical cylindrical toaster. You keep dropping slices of bread (metal), butter (flux), and heating elements (coke) from the top in alternating layers. Hot air (from a blower) is blown in from the bottom. As the bread descends, it gets progressively toasted (melted), drips down as molten liquid, and collects at the bottom — from where it can be poured out. The exhaust smoke rises up through the unfired top layers, preheating them. This **counter-current flow** is the essence of the cupola.

The cupola can be physically visualized as a **large vertical steel chimney (typically 4–8 m tall)** lined with refractory bricks, where controlled combustion of metallurgical coke melts descending iron scrap, pig iron, and returns.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **1400°C – 1600°C** |
| Internal Diameter (Shell) | **0.5 m – 3.5 m** |
| Stack Height | **4 m – 8 m** |
| Coke-to-Metal Ratio | **1 : 6 to 1 : 10** |
| Air Blast Pressure | **0.5 – 1.5 kPa (5–15 cm H₂O)** |
| Melting Rate (Small cupola) | **0.5 – 2 tonnes/hour** |
| Melting Rate (Large cupola) | **10 – 30 tonnes/hour** |
| Air Volume Required | **70 – 100 Nm³ per tonne of iron** |
| Thermal Efficiency | **30% – 50%** |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Cupola Furnaces

Cupolas are classified into the following primary categories based on blast air temperature and lining chemistry:

1. **Cold Blast Cupola** — Air injected at ambient temperature (most common, simpler design).
2. **Hot Blast Cupola** — Pre-heated air (200°C–500°C) using recuperators; higher thermal efficiency (~50–60%).
3. **Acid Lined Cupola** — Silica-based refractory; suitable for acidic slags (most common).
4. **Basic Lined Cupola** — Dolomite/magnesite lining; suitable for desulfurization and basic slags.
5. **Water-Cooled Cupola** — Continuous water-cooled shell above the tuyeres for prolonged campaign life.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** In the *Safety in Engineering Industry* course, the cupola is studied **primarily from the perspective of occupational hazards** — burns, fume inhalation, explosions, and ergonomic risks — rather than detailed metallurgical operations. Students must connect each component of the cupola with the **specific hazard it generates** and the corresponding **engineering / administrative control measure**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Cupola Furnace

A modern cupola is composed of the following principal structural zones (top to bottom):

### 2.1.1 Stack Zone (Charging Zone)
- Located between the **charging door** and the **upper tuyeres level**.
- Houses the descending solid charge (metal + coke + flux layers).
- Exhaust gases rise through this zone, **preheating the charge** (counter-current heat exchange).
- Typically accounts for **~40% of total heat transfer** in the cupola.

### 2.1.2 Melting Zone (Combustion Zone)
- Located at the level of the **tuyeres** (air injection ports).
- Peak temperature zone (1600°C – 1800°C).
- Coke combustion releases **CO, CO₂**, and radiant heat.
- Metal charge melts and trickles down through the coke bed.

### 2.1.3 Hearth Zone (Well)
- Lower cylindrical well below the tuyeres.
- Collects **molten iron** and **liquid slag** (which floats on top due to lower density).
- Hearth depth typically **300 – 600 mm**.

### 2.1.4 Tapping Zone
- **Tapping Spout** — front-bottom outlet for molten iron, continuously or intermittently tapped.
- **Slag Notch** — separate outlet slightly above the tapping spout for slag removal.

### 2.1.5 Bottom (Drop Bottom)
- Hinged or removable bottom door for **cleaning, slag dumping**, and **campaign termination**.
- Supported by a **prop** during operation; released to drop the bed at end of campaign.

---

## 2.2 Component-Wise Engineering Analysis

| Component | Material of Construction | Function | Associated Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Outer Shell | Mild Steel Plate (10–25 mm) | Structural support | Heat deformation |
| Refractory Lining | Silica bricks / Fireclay / Dolomite | Insulation, containment | Thermal runaway, erosion |
| Tuyeres | Cast iron / Copper-cooled | Air injection | Backfire, explosion |
| Charging Door | Steel with refractory lining | Material charging | Burns, falling objects |
| Blower System | Centrifugal/Roots blower | Air supply | Noise (>85 dB), electrical |
| Stack & Spark Arrestor | Steel + refractory | Exhaust path | Fire, sparks |
| Drop Bottom | Steel hinged plate | Bed drop | Hot mass ejection |
| Water Cooling System (if any) | Copper tubes / Spray nozzles | Shell cooling | Steam burns, leakage |

---

## 2.3 Operational Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Sequence Matters in Safety Engineering**
> Each step in cupola operation has an **inherent hazard profile**. Understanding the sequence helps in pre-emptive risk assessment (JSA / HIRA — Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment).

**Step 1 — Pre-Heating (Bed Coke Ignition)**
A **bed of coke** is ignited at the bottom using wood shavings, oil-soaked waste, or gas burners. The blower is started **gradually** to avoid sudden ignition flashback.

**Step 2 — Bed Establishment**
Additional coke is charged to form a bed of **300–600 mm height above the tuyeres**. The bed acts as the **fuel reservoir** and the **melting medium**.

**Step 3 — Charging (Alternating Layers)**
The charging sequence is critical and follows a strict cyclic pattern:
- **Metal Layer** (iron scrap + pig iron + foundry returns): 100–200 kg per charge
- **Coke Layer** (metallurgical coke, fixed carbon >85%): as per coke ratio
- **Flux Layer** (limestone CaCO₃, dolomite, fluorspar): 2–4% of metal weight

The flux decomposes to **CaO + CO₂**, where CaO combines with silica, alumina, and other impurities to form **fusible slag** (CaSiO₃, CaAl₂O₄).

**Step 4 — Combustion & Melting**
Air from tuyeres reacts with incandescent coke:

$$ C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ CO_2 + C \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

$$ C + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol} $$

**Step 5 — Tapping**
Molten iron flows out through the tapping spout into **ladles** at **1350°C–1450°C**.

**Step 6 — Slag Removal**
Slag is tapped intermittently through the slag notch — viscosity controlled by flux composition.

**Step 7 — End-of-Campaign Drop**
The bottom is released, and the entire bed (coke + slag + un-melted charge) drops out for cooling and disposal.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are derived from first principles of combustion stoichiometry and metallurgical mass balance, and are verifiable against standard foundry engineering references.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Coke Ratio} = \dfrac{M_c}{M_m}$ | Coke-to-metal mass ratio; $M_c$ = coke mass, $M_m$ = metal mass | dimensionless (1:8 typical) |
| 2 | $\text{Melting Rate (MR)} = \dfrac{M_m}{t}$ | Tonnes of metal melted per hour | t/h |
| 3 | $\text{Air Requirement} = 70 \text{ to } 100 \times M_m$ | Stoichiometric + excess air for combustion | Nm³ / t of iron |
| 4 | $\text{Carbon Combustion Rate} = \dfrac{n_C \times M_{CO_2}}{V_{air}}$ | Mol CO₂ per Nm³ air | mol/Nm³ |
| 5 | $\text{Thermal Efficiency} \eta = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of useful heat to total calorific input | % |
| 6 | $\text{Excess Air Ratio} = \dfrac{O_{2,actual}}{O_{2,stoich}}$ | Determines completeness of combustion | dimensionless (1.2–1.6) |
| 7 | $\text{Stack Loss} = m_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{stack} - T_{amb})$ | Heat lost via exhaust flue gases | kW |
| 8 | $\text{Slag Basicity} = \dfrac{\%CaO + \%MgO}{\%SiO_2 + \%Al_2O_3}$ | Slag chemistry control index | dimensionless (0.8–1.4) |
| 9 | $\text{Tapping Temperature Drop} \Delta T = T_{hearth} - T_{tap}$ | Heat loss between hearth and ladle | °C (~50–100°C) |
| 10 | $\text{Specific Coke Consumption} = \dfrac{M_c}{M_m}$ | kg of coke per tonne of iron | kg/t |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** The **vertical pipe symbol** $\vert$ is mathematically ambiguous in many foundry equations. Always use $\mid$ or explicit parentheses like $(CaO / SiO_2)$ for ratio quantities to avoid parser/valuation errors.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cupola furnaces are the **backbone of small and medium-scale iron foundries** across the world. They are preferred over electric induction furnaces for:

1. **Lower Capital Investment** — a 5 t/h cupola costs ~₹2–4 crore vs. ~₹6–10 crore for an equivalent induction furnace.
2. **Continuous Operation** — supports high-volume production runs (e.g., automotive castings, pipe fittings).
3. **Flexibility in Charge Mix** — accepts wide range of scrap grades and returns.
4. **Established Technology** — operators, refractories, and coke are widely available.

**Industrial Applications:**
- Automotive cylinder blocks, brake drums, flywheels
- Pipe fittings, valve bodies, manhole covers
- Agricultural machinery components
- Cast iron rolls for steel mills

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for Coke Combustion

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the theoretical minimum volume of air required to completely combust 1 kg of metallurgical coke (assumed 85% fixed carbon, 15% ash + moisture).

### Step 1 — Write the Combustion Equation
Carbon reacts with oxygen to form carbon dioxide:

$$ C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 $$

### Step 2 — Compute Molar Masses
- Carbon (C): **12 g/mol**
- Oxygen (O₂): **32 g/mol**
- Carbon Dioxide (CO₂): **44 g/mol**

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric Mass Ratio
From the balanced equation:

$$ \dfrac{M_{O_2}}{M_C} = \dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg C} $$

This means **2.667 kg of pure O₂** are required to burn **1 kg of carbon** completely.

### Step 4 — Adjust for Coke Purity
Since coke contains **85% fixed carbon**, the actual carbon per kg of coke is:

$$ M_C = 1 \text{ kg} \times 0.85 = 0.85 \text{ kg C} $$

### Step 5 — Compute O₂ Requirement per kg of Coke

$$ M_{O_2} = 0.85 \times 2.667 = 2.267 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg coke} $$

### Step 6 — Convert to Air Volume
Air contains **23.2% O₂ by mass** (equivalent to **21% by volume**). At STP, 1 kmol of air occupies 22.4 Nm³.

$$ M_{air} = \dfrac{2.267}{0.232} = 9.77 \text{ kg air / kg coke} $$

Volume of air at NTP:

$$ V_{air} = \dfrac{M_{air}}{\rho_{air}} = \dfrac{9.77}{1.293} = 7.56 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{kg coke} $$

### Step 7 — Apply Excess Air (Industrial Practice)
Industrial cupolas operate with **20%–50% excess air** to ensure complete combustion. Taking **30% excess**:

$$ V_{air,actual} = 7.56 \times 1.30 = 9.83 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{kg coke} $$

### Step 8 — Final Cupola Air Requirement
For a coke ratio of **1:8** (i.e., 125 kg coke per tonne of iron):

$$ V_{air} = 125 \times 9.83 = 1228.75 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{tonne of iron} $$

This is **higher** than the empirical 70–100 Nm³ range, indicating significant sensible heat losses through the stack — a key **energy efficiency concern** that justifies hot-blast cupola designs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Loss (Flue Gas Heat Loss)

The dominant heat loss in a cupola is via the hot stack gases. Let us derive it.

### Step 1 — Identify Flue Gas Components
For complete combustion of carbon with 30% excess air, flue gas composition per kg of coke:

- **CO₂ produced**: $\frac{0.85 \times 44}{12} = 3.117$ kg
- **O₂ (excess)**: $2.267 \times 0.30 = 0.680$ kg
- **N₂ from air**: $(9.77 \times 0.77) = 7.523$ kg

**Total flue gas mass per kg coke**: $m_g = 3.117 + 0.680 + 7.523 = 11.32$ kg

### Step 2 — Assume Specific Heat of Flue Gas
Average $c_p \approx 1.10$ kJ/(kg·K) (for mixed CO₂ + O₂ + N₂)

### Step 3 — Stack Temperature Assumption
Typical cupola stack temperature $T_{stack} = 600°C = 873$ K, ambient $T_{amb} = 30°C = 303$ K.

### Step 4 — Compute Heat Loss per kg Coke

$$ Q_{stack} = m_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{stack} - T_{amb}) $$

$$ Q_{stack} = 11.32 \times 1.10 \times (873 - 303) $$

$$ Q_{stack} = 11.32 \times 1.10 \times 570 $$

$$ Q_{stack} = 7097.6 \text{ kJ/kg coke} $$

### Step 5 — Convert to kW for 1 t/h Iron Production
With 125 kg coke per tonne iron and 1 t/h production:

$$ Q_{stack, hourly} = 125 \times 7097.6 = 887200 \text{ kJ/h} $$

$$ Q_{stack, kW} = \dfrac{887200}{3600} = 246.4 \text{ kW} $$

This **246 kW loss** represents roughly **20–25%** of total coke calorific input — emphasizing why **stack heat recovery (recuperators, hot-blast systems)** is a primary focus in modern safety-and-efficiency retrofits.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard–Component–Control Triad

| Cupola Zone | Hazard Identified | Cause/Mechanism | Engineering Control | Administrative/PPE Control |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Charging Door | Burns, Falling Objects | Manual charging of hot materials | Automated charging skip hoist; interlock guards | Restricted entry during charging; heat-resistant gloves, face shield |
| Tuyere Zone | Backfire Explosion | Air–coke ratio upset, water ingress | Flame arrestors, water detection sensors | Pre-shift inspection checklist; lockout-tagout |
| Tap Hole Area | Splashes, Molten Metal Burns | Tapping stream instability | Trough preheating; emergency dam | Spill containment; aluminized suit, face visor |
| Slag Notch | Caustic Burns, Steam Explosion | Wet tools contacting slag | Dry tool pre-heating station | Hot work permit, designated slag pit |
| Stack | Fire, Spark Emission | Hot particulates in flue gas | Spark arrestor, wet scrubber | Stack emission monitoring (CEMS) |
| Blower Room | Noise (>90 dB), Vibration | Continuous blower operation | Acoustic enclosure, vibration isolators | Ear protection (muffs + plugs), audiometry |
| Drop Bottom | Hot Mass Ejection | Premature bottom release | Mechanical safety prop, double-latch | Exclusion zone (≥15 m); siren before drop |
| Ladle Transfer | Tipping, Spillage | Ladle crane failure | Ladle pre-checks, dual hoist cables | Trained rigger, banksman; emergency ladle stand |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A cupola has a melting rate of **5 tonnes/hour**, a coke ratio of **1:8**, and operates with **30% excess air**. Calculate the hourly air requirement in Nm³/h.

### Solution:

Given:
- Melting rate = 5 t/h
- Coke ratio = 1:8 → Coke required = $\frac{5}{8}$ = **0.625 t/h = 625 kg/h**

From Section 3.1, air required per kg coke = **9.83 Nm³/kg coke**

$$ \text{Hourly Air} = 625 \times 9.83 = 6143.75 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} $$

**Answer: ~6144 Nm³/h** (≈ 102.4 Nm³/min, requiring a blower of capacity ~110 Nm³/min at 1.2 kPa pressure).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cupola Functional Architecture & Hazard Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid block diagrams are used here as the topic involves physical 3D geometry (a vertical cylindrical furnace) that cannot be rendered as a flow chart. The diagram below maps the **functional flow of materials, energy, and hazards** through the cupola — a Board Exam-acceptable schematic representation.

```mermaid

    C[Coke Bunker] -->|Metallurgical Coke| B
    D[Flux Hopper Limestone Dolomite] --> B
    B -->|Layered Charge| E[Charging Door Top of Cupola]
    E -->|Counter-current Preheating| F[Stack Zone Pre-heat Zone]
    F -->|Descending Charge| G[Melting Zone Tuyere Level 1600C]
    H[Roots Blower] -->|Forced Air Blast| I[Tuyeres]
    I -->|Combustion Air| G
    G -->|C plus O2 Combustion| J[Flue Gases CO2 CO N2]
    J -->|Rising Hot Gases| F
    J -->|Exhaust| K[Spark Arrestor Stack]
    K -->|Particulate Removal| L[Wet Scrubber Bag Filter]
    L -->|Cleaned Emission| M[Atmosphere via Tall Stack]
    G -->|Molten Iron Trickles Down| N[Hearth Well Collection]
    O[Flux Layer] -->|Slag Formation CaSiO3| P[Slag Layer Floats on Iron]
    N -->|Tap Hole| Q[Tapping Ladle]
    P -->|Slag Notch| R[Slag Pit Granulation]
    Q -->|Transfer| S[Pouring Station Foundry Floor]
    G -. Hot Surface Burns .-> Z1[Safety Hazard Zone 1]
    K -. Spark Emission Fire Risk .-> Z2[Safety Hazard Zone 2]

    Z3 --> X
    Z4 --> X
    Z5 --> X
```

## 4.2 Schematic Process Flow — Counter-Current Heat Exchange

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DESC["DESCENDING CHARGE - COLD TO HOT"]
        D1[Top Cold Charge 25C] --> D2[Preheated 400C] --> D3[Red Hot 800C] --> D4[Molten Droplets 1500C]
    end
    subgraph ASC["ASCENDING GASES - HOT TO COOL"]
        A1[Tuyere Zone 1800C] --> A2[Mid Stack 1000C] --> A3[Top Stack 500C] --> A4[Exit to Scrubber 200C]

    ASC -. Preheating .-> DESC
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked to *draw or describe* the cupola, KTU examiners typically award **3 marks for a labeled diagram** and **2 marks for the working principle**. Always include: **(i) Shell, (ii) Lining, (iii) Tuyeres, (iv) Charging door, (v) Tap hole, (vi) Slag hole, (vii) Drop bottom, (viii) Blower, (ix) Stack**. Missing any three of these typically loses full marks.

---
