topics.title: Foundry Production - Cleaning and Finishing Foundry Processes - Heat Treatment Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foundry Production - Cleaning and Finishing Foundry Processes - Heat Treatment Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cleaning and Finishing in Foundry Production

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Cleaning** in foundry production refers to the set of post-casting operations performed to remove adhering sand, excess metal, surface impurities, and gating/risering remnants from a cast component. **Finishing** is the subsequent set of operations that improve the surface quality, dimensional accuracy, and aesthetic appearance of the cleaned casting to meet the design specifications and service requirements.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding):**
Think of it like making a clay idol (the casting) and then performing the *post-creation rituals*. Right after the idol comes out of the mould, it is rough, sandy, and has extra clay sticking out (the gates and runners). *Cleaning* is like scraping off the rough sand, trimming the extra bits, and washing the idol. *Finishing* is like sanding it smooth, painting it, and polishing it until it looks beautiful and is ready to be worshipped (placed in service).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cleaning and Finishing are mandatory auxiliary processes in any foundry. They account for nearly **30–45% of the total casting cost** and significantly influence the final mechanical properties and dimensional integrity of the cast product.

---

### 1.2 Heat Treatment Operations in Foundry

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Heat Treatment** is a controlled sequence of heating and cooling operations applied to cast metals (ferrous and non-ferrous) in the solid state to alter their internal microstructure and thereby obtain desired mechanical, physical, and chemical properties such as hardness, strength, ductility, toughness, and wear resistance.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine baking a cake. You mix the ingredients, pour the batter into a mould, and put it in the oven at a specific temperature for a specific time. After baking, you let it cool. If you cool it fast (quench) vs. slow (anneal), the texture changes — one becomes soft and spongy, the other firm and crunchy. *Heat treatment of castings is exactly this — but for metals.* By controlling *how hot* we heat the metal, *how long* we hold it, and *how fast* we cool it, we engineer the internal grain structure to behave exactly as needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box — Key Heat Treatment Terms**
> - **Austenitizing:** Heating the steel above the upper critical temperature (Ac₃ for hypo-eutectoid / Acₘ for hyper-eutectoid) to form a uniform austenitic structure.
> - **Soaking/Holding:** Maintaining the casting at the austenitizing temperature to ensure uniform temperature and complete phase transformation.
> - **Quenching:** Rapid cooling in a medium (water, oil, brine, polymer) to suppress equilibrium transformation.
> - **Tempering:** Reheating a hardened steel to a temperature below Ac₁ to improve toughness at the cost of some hardness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Emphasis:** For every heat treatment process, students must mention the **three Ts** — **Temperature, Time (soaking duration), and Transformation medium (cooling rate)**. Missing any one of these guarantees a 2-mark deduction in the University Exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cleaning Operations — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

After a casting is shaken out of the mould, it carries:
- **Adhering sand** (sand particles fused to the surface)
- **Gates, risers, sprues, and runners** (excess feeding metal)
- **Flash and fins** (thin metal projections at parting lines)
- **Surface scale and oxide layers** (formed at elevated temperatures)

Cleaning operations are performed in the following logical sequence:

**Step 1: Decoring / Shakeout**
- The casting is separated from the mould material using vibrating screens, knockout grates, or rotary decanters.
- *Why:* To recover the moulding sand for reuse and free the casting for further processing.

**Step 2: Gate and Riser Removal**
- Performed using **chipping hammers, abrasive cut-off saws, hydraulic shears, band saws, or oxy-acetylene torches**.
- *Why:* To remove the metal that was used as feeding channels, since these are not part of the functional component.

**Step 3: Sand and Scale Removal**
- Methods include **shot blasting, sand blasting, tumbling, water blasting, and wire brushing**.
- *Why:* Adhering sand weakens surface integrity and degrades fatigue life.

**Step 4: Surface Defect Repair**
- Weld deposition, plugging, or impregnation is done to repair porosity, blowholes, or cracks.
- *Why:* Critical defects propagate under service loads and cause catastrophic failure.

**Step 5: Grinding and Surface Improvement**
- Performed on **bench grinders, swing-frame grinders, portable grinders, or robotic finishing cells**.
- *Why:* To achieve the surface finish and dimensional tolerance required.

---

### 2.2 Finishing Operations — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

Finishing is typically a **value-added** operation that turns a sound casting into a market-ready product:

- **Polishing and Buffing:** Using abrasive belts and cloth wheels to achieve mirror-like finishes.
- **Painting and Coating:** Anti-corrosion primers and powder coatings.
- **Shot Peening:** A controlled surface treatment that introduces compressive residual stresses, enhancing fatigue life.
- **Machining:** Final dimensional accuracy on lathes, milling machines, or CNC centres.
- **Inspection:** Dimensional check, surface roughness measurement, NDT (Dye Penetrant, Radiography, Ultrasonic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industries like automotive, aerospace, and hydraulic machinery, finishing processes directly determine the fatigue strength, corrosion resistance, and sealing capability of cast components. A rough surface is a crack initiator.

---

### 2.3 Heat Treatment Operations — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

The major heat treatment operations studied under KTU Module 4 are:

#### **A) Annealing**
- **Purpose:** Soften the casting, relieve internal stresses, refine grain structure, improve machinability.
- **Process:** Heat to Ac₃ + 30–50 °C → Soak → Furnace cool (very slow).
- **Resulting Microstructure:** Coarse pearlite + ferrite (for hypo-eutectoid steel).

#### **B) Normalizing**
- **Purpose:** Refine grain, improve uniformity, enhance mechanical properties.
- **Process:** Heat to Ac₃ + 50 °C → Soak → Air cool (moderate rate).
- **Resulting Microstructure:** Fine pearlite + ferrite.

#### **C) Hardening (Quenching)**
- **Purpose:** Achieve maximum hardness.
- **Process:** Heat to Ac₃ + 30–50 °C → Soak → Quench rapidly in water/oil/brine/polymer.
- **Resulting Microstructure:** Martensite (BCT — Body Centered Tetragonal).

#### **D) Tempering**
- **Purpose:** Reduce brittleness of martensite while retaining adequate hardness.
- **Process:** Reheat to 150–650 °C → Soak → Cool.
- **Result:** Toughened martensite + fine carbides.

#### **E) Stress Relieving**
- **Purpose:** Remove residual stresses from casting, welding, or machining.
- **Process:** Heat to 550–650 °C → Soak → Slow cool.

#### **F) Spheroidizing**
- **Purpose:** Produce globular carbides for maximum machinability (e.g., in tool steels).
- **Process:** Prolonged heating just below Ac₁ → Slow cool.

#### **G) Case Hardening (Surface Treatments)**
- **Carburizing, Nitriding, Cyaniding, Carbonitriding, Induction Hardening, Flame Hardening.**
- *Why:* Hard, wear-resistant surface with a tough, ductile core.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hardness Conversion (BHN to UTS, approx.) | $UTS \approx 3.45 \times BHN$ | MPa | Estimating ultimate tensile strength from Brinell hardness |
| 2 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $\frac{dT}{dt} = -k(T - T_{medium})$ | °C/s | Quench severity analysis |
| 3 | Heat Transfer to Quench Medium | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | J | Energy extracted during quenching |
| 4 | Cooling Rate (approximate) | $CR = \dfrac{T_{aust} - T_{medium}}{t_{cooling}}$ | °C/s | Quench intensity parameter |
| 5 | Gross / Net Hardenability Index (DI) | $DI = \bar{H} \times \dfrac{\text{quench severity}}{H}$ | — | Jominy end-quench test interpretation |
| 6 | Ideal Critical Diameter (Gross) | $\log D_I = \log D_{I\text{-base}} + J$ | inches/mm | Hardenability correlation |
| 7 | Total Heat Content of Steel | $H = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$ | J | Furnace load calculation |
| 8 | Time to Cool to Half Temperature | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ | s | Newton's cooling integration |
| 9 | Distance from Quenched End (Jominy) | $J_{x} = \text{function of cooling rate at point } x$ | mm | Hardenability curve |
| 10 | Tempering Parameter (Hollomon–Jaffe) | $HP = T (\log t + C)$ | — | Tempered hardness correlation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Reminder:** Always state the **assumptions and boundary conditions** when applying the cooling law formulas. Marks are specifically reserved for stating initial and final temperatures, the medium used, and the surface condition of the casting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Newton's Law of Cooling Applied to Quenching

**Statement of the Law:**
The rate of change of temperature of a body is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the body and its surrounding medium.

**Step 1: Mathematical Statement**
Let $T(t)$ be the temperature of the casting at time $t$, and $T_m$ be the temperature of the quenching medium (assumed constant for a large bath).

The rate of cooling is written as:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -k (T - T_m)$$

**Step 2: Sign Convention**
The negative sign indicates that the casting temperature *decreases* with time (cooling process). The constant $k > 0$ is the cooling rate constant, dependent on the medium (water, oil, brine) and surface heat transfer coefficient.

**Step 3: Separation of Variables**
Rearranging to separate $T$ and $t$:

$$\frac{dT}{T - T_m} = -k \, dt$$

**Step 4: Integration on Both Sides**
Integrating from initial temperature $T_0$ at $t = 0$ to temperature $T$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \frac{dT}{T - T_m} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 5: Evaluation of Integrals**
The left-hand side yields the natural logarithm, and the right-hand side yields $-kt$:

$$\ln(T - T_m) \Big|_{T_0}^{T} = -k t$$

Applying the limits:

$$\ln(T - T_m) - \ln(T_0 - T_m) = -k t$$

**Step 6: Combine the Logarithms**

$$\ln \left( \frac{T - T_m}{T_0 - T_m} \right) = -k t$$

**Step 7: Exponentiation to Isolate Temperature**

$$\frac{T - T_m}{T_0 - T_m} = e^{-k t}$$

**Step 8: Final Solution**

$$T(t) = T_m + (T_0 - T_m) e^{-k t}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- When $t = 0$: $T(0) = T_0$ ✓ (initial condition satisfied)
- When $t \to \infty$: $T(\infty) = T_m$ ✓ (casting reaches medium temperature)
- The cooling is **exponential** — fastest at the start, progressively slower.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Cool to Half the Temperature Difference

**Step 1: Define Half-Cooling**
Half-cooling occurs when the excess temperature equals half of the initial excess temperature.

$$T(t_{1/2}) - T_m = \frac{1}{2}(T_0 - T_m)$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Solution**

$$\frac{1}{2}(T_0 - T_m) = (T_0 - T_m) e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

**Step 3: Cancel the Common Term**

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$

**Step 4: Take Natural Logarithm**

$$\ln \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = -k t_{1/2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Half-Cooling Time**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$$

**Engineering Utility:** The half-cooling time is a useful metric in **quench tank sizing** and **martensite transformation kinetics**, since the formation of martensite begins when the casting crosses the Mₛ (martensite start) temperature, which is typically when approximately 50% of the temperature drop has occurred for shallow-hardening steels.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance During Quenching

**Step 1: Define the Total Energy Extracted**
The total heat energy extracted from the casting during quenching from austenitizing temperature $T_A$ to the medium temperature $T_m$:

$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_A - T_m)$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the casting (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of the metal (J/kg·°C, **≈ 460 J/kg·°C for steel**)
- $T_A$ = austenitizing temperature (°C)
- $T_m$ = medium temperature (°C)

**Step 2: Power Demand of the Quench Tank**
If the quench is completed in time $\Delta t$ (s), the average heat extraction rate is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{Q}{\Delta t} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_A - T_m)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3: Quench Severity (Grossmann H-value)**
Grossmann introduced a dimensionless number $H$ to rank the cooling power of quench media:

$$H = \frac{h}{2 k_{steel}}$$

Where:
- $h$ = surface heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·°C)
- $k_{steel}$ = thermal conductivity of steel (**≈ 45 W/m·°C**)

**Reference values of H (commonly tested in KTU):**

| Medium | H-value (1/inch) |
|---|---|
| Still Air | 0.02 |
| Still Oil | 0.30 |
| Still Water | 1.00 |
| Brine (5% NaCl) | 2.00 |
| Circulated Water | 4.00 |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Cleaning Methods (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Method | Principle | Application | Surface Finish Achieved | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tumbling** | Rotational abrasion of castings with each other and abrasive media | Small, robust castings | Moderate | Low |
| **Shot Blasting** | High-velocity steel shot impacts surface | All ferrous castings | Good | Moderate |
| **Sand Blasting** | Abrasive sand propelled by compressed air | Large castings, sand-sensitive parts | Good | Moderate |
| **Wire Brushing** | Manual mechanical brushing | Localized cleaning | Average | Very Low |
| **Grinding** | Abrasive wheel removes excess metal | Gate/riser cut-off regions | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Hydro-blasting** | High-pressure water jets | Delicate castings, sand-sensitive | Very Good | High |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Heat Treatment Selection (Based on Casting Requirement)

| Required Property | Recommended Heat Treatment | Resulting Microstructure |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum hardness | Hardening + Tempering (low temp) | Martensite + ε-carbide |
| High toughness | Tempering at high temperature (550–650 °C) | Tempered martensite + fine carbides |
| Soft, machinable structure | Full Annealing or Spheroidizing | Coarse pearlite / Globular carbides |
| Uniform refined grain | Normalizing | Fine pearlite + ferrite |
| Hard surface, tough core | Case Hardening (Carburizing + Quenching) | Case: Martensite; Core: Pearlite-Ferrite |
| Stress relief (no structural change) | Stress Relieving | Unchanged (only residual stress removed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flowchart — Cleaning and Finishing Sequence in Foundry

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the end-to-end post-casting workflow as taught in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shakeout from Mould] --> B[Decoring and Sand Recovery]
    B --> C[Decore Casting Inspection]
    C --> D[Gate Riser Sprue Removal]
    D --> E[Chiseling or Abrasive Cutoff]
    E --> F[Shot or Sand Blasting]
    F --> G[Surface Defect Repair Welding]
    G --> H[Grinding and Deburring]
    H --> I{Inspection Required Finish}
    I --> J[Surface Polishing Buffing]
    I --> K[Shot Peening]
    I --> L[Painting Coating]
    J --> M[Final Dimensional Inspection]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Ready for Dispatch]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each rectangular block represents a unit operation, while the diamond block `I` is the decision point where the route depends on whether the casting requires an aesthetic, fatigue-resistant, or corrosion-protected surface.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Treatment Process Train

The Mermaid block diagram below represents the functional architecture of a generic heat treatment cycle, with each block representing a critical transformation stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Casting Input] --> P2[Pre Heating Stage]
    P2 --> P3[Austenitizing Furnace]
    P3 --> P4[Soaking Zone Holding Time]
    P4 --> P5[Quench Tank Medium Selection]
    P5 --> P6[Post Quench Tempering Furnace]
    P6 --> P7[Controlled Cooling Zone]
    P7 --> P8[Hardness and Microstructure Testing]
    P8 --> P9[Accepted Treated Casting Output]
    P8 --> P10[Rejected Casting Reprocessing]
    P10 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic:** The closed feedback loop from `P8` (testing) back to `P3` (re-austenitizing) represents the quality-control reheat cycle, where castings failing to meet hardness or microstructure specifications are sent back for re-treatment. This is a critical KTU 2024 concept often asked for **3–5 mark short notes**.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Quench Medium

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Casting Ready for Quench] --> S2{Steel Composition Type}
    S2 --> S3[Plain Carbon Steel] --> S4[Water or Brine]
    S2 --> S5[Alloy Steel] --> S6[Oil or Polymer]
    S2 --> S7[High Alloy Tool Steel] --> S8[Air or Salt Bath]
    S4 --> S9[Quench Performed]
    S6 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Tempering to Reduce Brittleness]
    S10 --> S11[Final Inspection]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** This kind of decision-flow diagram is frequently rewarded in KTU valuation. Drawing even a simple decision tree for process selection earns 2–3 bonus marks for visual clarity and logical structure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Austenitizing and Soaking:** Students often use these terms interchangeably. *Austenitizing* is the *act of reaching* the austenitic phase, while *soaking* is the *duration held* at that temperature. Both are distinct marks-worthy points.
> 2. **Wrong Cooling Medium Recommendation:** Recommending water quenching for alloy steels is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. The cooling rate must match the steel's hardenability.
> 3. **Omitting the 'Why':** Listing processes without stating the engineering purpose (e.g., *why tempering is required after hardening*) loses 1–2 marks in the descriptive part.
> 4. **Mixing Cleaning and Finishing Definitions:** Many students blur these. *Cleaning* removes *unwanted* material; *Finishing* adds or modifies the *intended* surface. Examiner deducts for this.
> 5. **Forgetting Units and Numerical Substantiation:** Every formula in Section 2.4 must be quoted with units. Numerical substitution steps must be shown.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define cleaning and finishing in foundry practice. List any four common cleaning operations.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Cleaning** refers to the removal of sand, oxides, gates, risers, and other unwanted material from the casting surface after shakeout.
- **Finishing** refers to the subsequent operations that improve surface quality and dimensional accuracy.
- Four common cleaning operations are:
  1. **Shot blasting** — propelling steel shot at high velocity to remove sand and scale.
  2. **Grinding** — using abrasive wheels to remove gates, risers, and flash.
  3. **Tumbling** — rotating castings in a barrel with abrasive media for general cleaning.
  4. **Chiseling / Chipping** — manual removal of gates and sprues using a chisel and hammer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is annealing? Differentiate between full annealing and stress-relief annealing.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Annealing** is a heat treatment process in which a metal is heated to a specific temperature, held (soaked), and then cooled slowly to obtain a refined, softened, and stress-free structure.
- **Full Annealing:** Heating above Ac₃, soaking, and furnace cooling. Produces coarse pearlite + ferrite; used to improve machinability and softness.
- **Stress-Relief Annealing:** Heating to a temperature below Ac₁ (≈ 550–650 °C), soaking, and slow cooling. No phase transformation occurs; only residual stresses are relieved.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*Answer both parts:*
**(a)** Explain in detail the various cleaning operations performed on castings, with emphasis on shot blasting and grinding.
**(b)** Describe the full annealing process for a low-carbon steel casting, including the temperature ranges, microstructure changes, and resulting mechanical properties.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Cleaning Operations (7 Marks):**

Cleaning operations are categorized into:
- **Mechanical Cleaning:** Tumbling, shot blasting, sand blasting, wire brushing, grinding, and chiseling.
- **Thermal Cleaning:** Oxy-acetylene flame cleaning to remove sand and organic matter.
- **Chemical Cleaning:** Acid pickling, alkaline cleaning, and salt bath descaling.

**Shot Blasting (3 Marks):**
- Uses high-velocity steel shot (typically **0.5–2.0 mm diameter**) propelled by a centrifugal wheel rotating at **1500–3500 rpm**.
- Removes adhering sand, scale, and oxide layers; also induces compressive residual stresses (similar to shot peening).
- Equipment: **Table-type, hanger-type, tumble-blast, and continuous conveyor shot-blast machines.**
- Advantages: High productivity, uniform finish, improved fatigue strength.
- Limitations: Not suitable for very thin or delicate castings.

**Grinding (2 Marks):**
- Performed on **bench grinders, swing-frame grinders, or portable angle grinders**.
- Uses bonded abrasive wheels of **Al₂O₃ or SiC** with grit sizes ranging from **24 (coarse) to 120 (fine)**.
- Used for: gate/riser removal, surface defect removal, weld dressing, and achieving dimensional accuracy.
- **Safety Note:** Use of proper PPE (goggles, gloves) and dust extraction systems is mandatory.

**(b) Full Annealing of Low-Carbon Steel (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Heating:**
- The casting is heated slowly (to avoid thermal shock) to a temperature of **Ac₃ + 30–50 °C**.
- For low-carbon steel, Ac₃ ≈ 723–850 °C; hence austenitizing temperature ≈ **850–900 °C**.

**Step 2 — Soaking:**
- Held at this temperature for a duration of approximately **1 hour per inch of cross-sectional thickness** (or 2.5 minutes per mm).
- Purpose: To ensure uniform temperature throughout the cross-section and complete austenitization.

**Step 3 — Furnace Cooling:**
- The casting is cooled inside the furnace at a rate of **20–30 °C per hour** down to about 600 °C, then air-cooled.
- Slow cooling allows the formation of **coarse pearlite + ferrite** at the austenite grain boundaries.

**Step 4 — Resulting Microstructure and Properties:**

| Property | As-Cast | After Full Annealing |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness (BHN) | 180–220 | 120–150 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 450–550 | 350–420 |
| Ductility (% Elongation) | 8–12% | 22–28% |
| Machinability | Poor | Excellent |
| Internal Stresses | High | Negligible |

**Engineering Significance:** Full annealing is widely used in foundries producing carbon-steel castings (e.g., locomotive frames, valve bodies, gear blanks) where subsequent machining is extensive and residual stresses from solidification must be eliminated.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Answer both parts:*
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the Jominy End-Quench Test for hardenability. How is it used to select the quenching medium for a given steel?
**(b)** List and explain any three surface heat treatment processes used for cast components, with their industrial applications.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Jominy End-Quench Test (7 Marks):**

**Principle:**
The Jominy end-quench test is the **standard method (ASTM A255)** for determining the hardenability of a steel — i.e., its ability to form martensite when quenched.

**Specimen Preparation:**
- A cylindrical specimen of **25 mm diameter × 100 mm length** is machined from the steel.
- It is normalized first to ensure uniform microstructure.
- It is then austenitized at the specified temperature (≈ 850–900 °C) for a standard soaking time of **30–45 minutes**.

**Quenching Procedure:**
- The specimen is rapidly transferred to the Jominy fixture.
- A controlled water jet of **height 65 mm, orifice 12.5 mm diameter, water temperature 24 ± 5 °C** impinges on the **bottom end-face** of the specimen.
- The specimen is held for **at least 10 minutes**, after which it is fully cooled.

**Hardness Measurement:**
- After cooling, two parallel flats (≈ 0.4 mm deep) are ground along the length of the specimen.
- Rockwell hardness (HRC) is measured at intervals of **1.5 mm (up to 12 mm), 3 mm (up to 25 mm), and 6 mm (above 25 mm)** from the quenched end.
- A **Jominy curve** is plotted: Hardness (HRC) vs. Distance from quenched end (mm).

**Interpretation and Quench Medium Selection:**
- The end in contact with water cools fastest → highest hardness.
- The far end cools slowly → lower hardness.
- The **shape of the Jominy curve** indicates hardenability:
  - *Steep drop* → low hardenability → requires **brine/water quench** (e.g., plain carbon steels).
  - *Gradual drop* → high hardenability → can be quenched in **oil or air** (e.g., alloy steels like EN24, 4340).

**Use in Quench Medium Selection:**
1. Identify the **critical cooling rate** required at the TTT nose for martensite formation.
2. Compare with the cooling rate provided by water/oil/air at 700 °C.
3. Select the medium that just exceeds the critical cooling rate to avoid cracking.

**(b) Three Surface Heat Treatment Processes (7 Marks):**

**1. Carburizing (2.5 Marks):**
- **Process:** Heating low-carbon steel casting in a carbon-rich atmosphere (charcoal, CO, methane) at **900–950 °C** for several hours.
- **Result:** Carbon diffuses into the surface layer, raising surface carbon content to 0.8–1.2% to a depth of 1–2 mm.
- **Followed by:** Quenching and tempering to form hard martensitic case.
- **Application:** Gears, camshafts, piston pins, sprockets, railway crossings.

**2. Induction Hardening (2.5 Marks):**
- **Process:** The casting is placed inside a copper coil carrying high-frequency AC current. Eddy currents heat the surface rapidly (within seconds) above Ac₃.
- **Quenching:** Immediate water spray quench.
- **Result:** Hard, wear-resistant surface (depth 1–5 mm) with tough, ductile core.
- **Application:** Crankshafts, axle journals, cam lobes, splined shafts.

**3. Flame Hardening (2 Marks):**
- **Process:** Oxy-acetylene flame is moved over the surface, rapidly heating it to austenitizing temperature, followed by water spray.
- **Result:** Surface hardness of 55–60 HRC to a depth of 2–4 mm.
- **Application:** Large cast components like crane wheels, gear teeth, rail ends, rolling mill rolls.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Cleaning** removes *unwanted* material (sand, gates, scale); **Finishing** improves *intended* surface (polish, paint, peen).
- [ ] **Shot blasting** uses high-velocity steel shot; **sand blasting** uses abrasive silica/alumina particles propelled by air.
- [ ] **Tumbling** is the most economical method for small, robust castings.
- [ ] **Annealing** → Soft + ductile; **Normalizing** → Uniform refined grain; **Hardening** → Maximum hardness; **Tempering** → Tough + reasonably hard.
- [ ] For every heat treatment, mention the **3 Ts — Temperature, Time, Transformation Medium**.
- [ ] **Martensite** is the hardest and most brittle microstructure → must be tempered for industrial use.
- [ ] **Austenitizing temperature** for plain carbon steel = Ac₃ + 30–50 °C ≈ **850–900 °C**.
- [ ] **Soaking time** rule of thumb = **1 hour per inch of thickness** (or **2.5 min/mm**).
- [ ] **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $T(t) = T_m + (T_0 - T_m) e^{-k t}$ → exponential decay.
- [ ] **Half-cooling time:** $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$ — used in quench severity analysis.
- [ ] **Jominy end-quench test (ASTM A255)** is the standard for hardenability measurement.
- [ ] **Grossmann H-values** rank quench media: Brine (2.0) > Water (1.0) > Oil (0.3) > Air (0.02).
- [ ] **Hardness–Strength correlation:** $UTS \approx 3.45 \times BHN$ (MPa).
- [ ] **Case hardening** = hard surface + tough core; suited for gears, cams, axles.
- [ ] **Safety in finishing:** Always use PPE (goggles, gloves, respirators) and ensure dust extraction in grinding and blasting cells.
- [ ] **Heat treatment furnace** must be calibrated for temperature uniformity (±5 °C of set point).
- [ ] **Quench cracking** is the most common defect — caused by excessive cooling rate or improper tempering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In Part B (14-mark) questions, always structure the answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Procedure with temperatures → (iii) Microstructure → (iv) Properties → (v) Industrial application**. This five-step pattern matches the KTU marking scheme and maximizes scoring under valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electro Plating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Electroplating — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroplating** is an **electrochemical surface-engineering process** in which a thin, adherent, and uniform metallic coating is deposited onto a conductive substrate (cathode) by passing a **direct electric current (DC)** through an electrolytic solution containing dissolved ions of the coating metal. The process is governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and is widely used for corrosion protection, wear resistance, electrical conductivity enhancement, and decorative finishing in engineering industries.

**Key Industry-Standard Terminology (KTU Glossary):**
- **Anode**: The positive electrode — usually the plating metal (soluble anode) or an inert electrode.
- **Cathode (Workpiece)**: The negative electrode — the part to be plated.
- **Electrolyte**: An aqueous ionic solution (e.g., acid/salt bath) containing metal ions.
- **Current Density (J)**: Expressed in **A/dm²**, the most critical process parameter.
- **Throwing Power (TP)**: The ability of the bath to deposit uniform thickness on irregular shapes.
- **Faraday's Constant (F)**: **96,485 C/mol** of electrons.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of electroplating as **"giving a metal object a brand-new metallic skin, applied atom-by-atom using electricity."**

Imagine a rusted old iron gate. Instead of painting it (which sits *on top* of the surface), you want to fuse a permanent, shiny chromium "armor" *into* the metal itself. You hang the gate in a chemical bath (the electrolyte), connect it to the **negative terminal** of a battery, and drop in a chunk of chromium connected to the **positive terminal**. When you switch on the power, positively charged chromium ions migrate through the liquid, get "magnetized" toward the gate, and bond tightly to its surface — one atomic layer at a time. The rust is sealed, the gate shines, and you've essentially **re-skinned the metal from the inside out**.

This is electroplating — *manufacturing a new surface using electrochemistry.*

---

## 1.3 Industrial Significance

Electroplating is a **high-hazard industrial activity** (hence its inclusion in the Safety in Engineering Industry syllabus). It involves:
- Hazardous chemicals (cyanides, acids, chromates)
- High DC currents and voltages
- Flammable hydrogen evolution
- Toxic fumes and wastewater

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Any question on electroplating in Safety Engineering MUST link the technical process to **occupational health hazards, PPE requirements, effluent treatment, and statutory safety norms** (Factories Act, OSHA, EPA).

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant ($F$) | **96,485** | C/mol |
| Faraday's Constant (rounded) | **96,500** | C/mol |
| Standard current density (decorative Cr) | 10 – 30 | A/dm² |
| Standard current density (hard Cr) | 30 – 60 | A/dm² |
| Operating voltage (typical) | 2 – 12 | V DC |
| Bath operating temperature | 25 – 70 | °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Electrochemistry — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Ionization in the Electrolyte
The electrolyte (e.g., CuSO₄ for copper plating) dissociates:
$$\text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-}$$

### Step 2: Migration Under Applied DC Potential
- **Cu²⁺ ions** (cations) → move toward the **cathode (workpiece)**.
- **SO₄²⁻ ions** (anions) → move toward the **anode**.

### Step 3: Reduction at the Cathode (The Plating Reaction)
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}_{(s)}$$
This reaction deposits pure copper atoms onto the workpiece surface.

### Step 4: Oxidation at the Anode (For Soluble Anode)
$$\text{Cu}_{(s)} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$$
The anode dissolves, replenishing the metal ions in the bath (electrolyte self-maintenance).

### Step 5: Hydrogen Evolution (Parasitic Reaction — KTU Safety Point!)
At the cathode, **hydrogen gas may evolve** as a competing side reaction:
$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} \uparrow$$
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hydrogen evolution is the **primary explosion hazard** in electroplating shops — a key KTU 2024 safety examination point.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step (Engineering Logic)

- **Why DC and not AC?** → DC ensures unidirectional ion migration; AC would oscillate ions back and forth, causing no net deposition.
- **Why low voltage (2–12 V)?** → Higher voltages cause excessive heating, hydrogen evolution, and "burning" of the deposit (rough, porous coatings).
- **Why controlled current density?** → Low J → poor adhesion, slow plating; High J → dendritic (rough, spiky) deposits.
- **Why electrolyte agitation?** → Prevents ion depletion at cathode surface (concentration polarization).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU 2024 numerical problems on electroplating will be solved using these five formulas.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal deposited | grams (g) | **Faraday's First Law (core KTU formula)** |
| 2 | $t_{dep} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M}$ | Time required to deposit given mass | seconds (s) | Production time calculation |
| 3 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Current efficiency | % | **Faraday's Second Law application** |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Coating thickness | cm or μm | Quality control |
| 5 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | A/dm² | Process parameter setting |

**Where:**
- $I$ = current in **Amperes (A)**
- $t$ = time in **seconds (s)**
- $M$ = molar mass of the deposited metal (**g/mol**)
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons transferred per ion)
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\rho$ = density of deposited metal (**g/cm³**)
- $A$ = surface area of workpiece (**dm² or cm²**)
- $m$ = mass deposited (**g**)

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap (KTU):** Surface area must be in **dm²** when using current in A and current density in A/dm². If thickness is required in **μm**, multiply cm result by 10,000.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Plating Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Chrome, Zinc, Nickel | Corrosion + wear + aesthetics |
| Electronics | Gold, Silver, Tin | Conductivity, solderability |
| Aerospace | Cadmium, Hard Chrome | Fatigue + corrosion resistance |
| Marine | Zinc (sacrificial) | Cathodic protection |
| Decorative | Gold, Silver, Rhodium | Jewellery, cutlery |
| Tooling | Hard Chrome, Electroless Ni | Wear + hardness |

---

## 2.5 Key Engineering Parameters Affecting Plating Quality

1. **Current density (J)** — directly controls deposition rate
2. **Bath temperature** — affects ion mobility and grain structure
3. **pH of electrolyte** — critical for bath stability
4. **Agitation** — uniform deposition
5. **Concentration of metal ions** — prevents polarization
6. **Additives (brighteners, levelers)** — control grain size and reflectivity
7. **Substrate surface preparation** — determines adhesion
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law (KTU Core Derivation)

### Statement
> *"The mass of a substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge) passed through the electrolyte."*

**Mathematical form:**
$$m \propto Q$$

$$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

where $Z$ is the *electrochemical equivalent* (g/C).

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1:** One mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant.
$$Q_{1\ \text{mol }e^-} = N_A \cdot e = F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$$

where:
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number $= 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- $e$ = electronic charge $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$

**Step 2:** To deposit 1 mole of a metal of valency $n$, we need $n$ moles of electrons.
$$Q_{1\ \text{mol metal}} = n \cdot F$$

**Step 3:** For $w$ grams of metal (where $w$ = mass, $M$ = molar mass), the number of moles is:
$$N = \dfrac{w}{M}$$

**Step 4:** Total charge required:
$$Q = N \cdot n \cdot F = \dfrac{w}{M} \cdot n \cdot F$$

**Step 5:** Solving for mass $w$ (the standard KTU formula):
$$w = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

$$\boxed{w = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important formula** in the KTU 2024 electroplating syllabus. Memorize it perfectly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Thickness Formula

Given the mass of deposit and the geometry, we can compute coating thickness.

**Step 1:** Volume of deposited metal:
$$V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 2:** For a uniform coating of thickness $T$ on surface area $A$:
$$V = A \cdot T$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two:
$$A \cdot T = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for thickness:
$$\boxed{T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}}$$

**Combined formula (substituting Faraday's equation):**
$$T = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

---

## 3.3 KTU-Style Solved Numerical Example (14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]:**
> A steel component with a surface area of **200 cm²** is to be electroplated with a **0.025 mm** thick layer of nickel. Calculate the **time required** if a current of **5 A** is passed through the plating bath. Assume **100% current efficiency**.
>
> Given:
> - Density of nickel ($\rho$) = **8.9 g/cm³**
> - Atomic mass of Ni ($M$) = **58.7 g/mol**
> - Valency of Ni ($n$) = **2**
> - $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**
> - Current efficiency ($\eta_c$) = **100%**

**Step 1: Calculate mass of nickel required.**
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot T$$
$$m = 8.9 \times 200 \times \left(0.025 \times 10^{-1}\right)$$

Note: $0.025\ \text{mm} = 0.0025\ \text{cm}$
$$m = 8.9 \times 200 \times 0.0025$$
$$m = 4.45\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's First Law for time.**
$$t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M \cdot \eta_c}$$
$$t = \dfrac{4.45 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{5 \times 58.7 \times 1}$$

**Step 3: Numerator calculation.**
$$4.45 \times 2 = 8.9$$
$$8.9 \times 96{,}485 = 858{,}716.5$$

**Step 4: Denominator calculation.**
$$5 \times 58.7 = 293.5$$

**Step 5: Final time calculation.**
$$t = \dfrac{858{,}716.5}{293.5} = 2926.0\ \text{s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to minutes (engineering convention).**
$$t = \dfrac{2926.0}{60} \approx 48.77\ \text{minutes}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 48.77\ \text{minutes} \ (\approx 49\ \text{min})}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Plating Quality

| Parameter Increased | Effect on Plating | KTU Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Current density ($J$) | Faster deposition, but rough/dendritic | Hydrogen ↑ → explosion risk ↑ |
| Temperature | Better conductivity, smoother | Bath evaporation, fume hazard |
| Agitation | Thicker, more uniform | None |
| pH deviation | Rough, pitted, or no deposition | Acid burns, cyanide release |
| Current efficiency < 100% | Slower, more H₂ produced | Hydrogen + heat = fire risk |
| Contaminants | Pitting, porosity, peeling | Toxic metal salt exposure |

---

## 3.5 Pre-Plating Process Engineering (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!NOTE]
> Surface preparation accounts for **70% of plating quality** — KTU examiners often test this.

| Step | Process | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degreasing** (alkaline / solvent) | Remove oils, grease |
| 2 | **Rinsing** | Remove degreaser residue |
| 3 | **Pickling** (acid dip, e.g., H₂SO₄ / HCl) | Remove rust, scale, oxides |
| 4 | **Rinsing** | Remove acid |
| 5 | **Activation** (dilute acid) | Expose fresh metal surface |
| 6 | **Electroplating** | Apply the coating |
| 7 | **Rinsing + Drying** | Prevent water spots / staining |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Electroplating Setup (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[DC Power Supply 0 to 12V] -->|Positive Terminal| nodeB[Anode Plating Metal]
    nodeA -->|Negative Terminal| nodeC[Workpiece Cathode]
    nodeB -->|Oxidation releases M2 plus| nodeD[Electrolyte Bath Acid or Salt Solution]
    nodeD -->|Metal ions M2 plus migrate| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Reduction M2 plus 2e to M solid| nodeE[Metallic Coating on Workpiece]
    nodeD -->|2H plus 2e to H2 gas| nodeF[Hydrogen Evolution Side Reaction]
    nodeF -->|Safety Hazard| nodeG[Ventilation and Extraction System]
    nodeD -->|Filtration and Circulation| nodeH[Filtration Pump and Agitator]
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|Temperature Control| nodeI[Heater and Thermostat 25 to 70C]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Complete Electroplating Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Raw Workpiece] --> stepB[Mechanical Cleaning Sanding or Polishing]
    stepB --> stepC[Alkaline Degreasing]
    stepC --> stepD[Water Rinse 1]
    stepD --> stepE[Acid Pickling Rust and Scale Removal]
    stepE --> stepF[Water Rinse 2]
    stepF --> stepG[Activation Dip Dilute Acid]
    stepG --> stepH[Rinse 3]
    stepH --> stepI[Electroplating Tank with DC]
    stepI --> stepJ[Water Rinse 4]
    stepJ --> stepK[Drying Hot Air or Centrifuge]
    stepK --> stepL[Finished Plated Component]
    stepL --> stepM{Quality Inspection}
    stepM -->|Pass| stepN[Dispatch]
    stepM -->|Fail| stepO[Stripping and Replating]
    stepO --> stepH
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Safety Hazards Mapping in Electroplating

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hazard1[Electroplating Plant] --> h1A[Chemical Hazards]
    hazard1 --> h1B[Electrical Hazards]
    hazard1 --> h1C[Mechanical Hazards]
    hazard1 --> h1D[Environmental Hazards]
    h1A --> h1A1[Cyanide Salts]
    h1A --> h1A2[Chromic Acid H2CrO4]
    h1A --> h1A3[Acidic Fumes]
    h1A --> h1A4[Alkaline Burns]
    h1B --> h1B1[DC Shock Low Voltage High Current]
    h1B --> h1B2[Short Circuits in Bath]
    h1B --> h1B3[Anode and Cathode Bus Bars]
    h1C --> h1C1[Fume Extraction Failure]
    h1C --> h1C2[Slippery Floors from Splashes]
    h1D --> h1D1[Heavy Metal Effluent]
    h1D --> h1D2[Wastewater with Cyanide]
    h1D --> h1D3[Sludge Disposal]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Electrochemical Reactions in a Plating Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Anode Positive Electrode] -->|M to M2 plus 2e Oxidation| blockB[Electrolyte Solution]
    blockB -->|M2 plus ions migrate to cathode| blockC[Cathode Negative Workpiece]
    blockB -->|SO4 2- or similar anions| blockA
    blockC -->|M2 plus 2e to M Reduction = Plating| blockD[Metal Coating Deposited]
    blockB -->|Hydrogen Evolution 2H plus 2e to H2| blockE[Safety Hazard Gas]
    blockE -->|Explosive above 4 percent| blockF[Explosion Risk in Enclosed Tank]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid diagrams above follow the **node ID alphanumeric rule** strictly. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone identifiers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define electroplating. State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Electroplating** is the process of depositing a thin, adherent layer of a metal onto the surface of a conductive object (cathode) by passing a direct electric current through an electrolyte containing ions of the metal to be deposited. It is used for corrosion protection, wear resistance, and decorative purposes.

> **Faraday's First Law:** *"The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge) passed through the electrolyte."*
> $$m \propto Q \implies m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$
> where $Z$ is the electrochemical equivalent of the substance.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What are the major safety hazards associated with electroplating? List any four.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Chemical hazards** — exposure to cyanide salts, chromic acid, strong acids/alkalis (skin burns, inhalation injuries).
2. **Electrical hazards** — DC shock (low voltage but high current), short circuits in electrolyte.
3. **Hydrogen explosion** — evolution of H₂ gas at the cathode can form explosive mixtures above **4% LEL**.
4. **Heavy metal effluent** — toxic wastewater containing Cr, Ni, Cd, Pb contaminates environment.
5. **Fume inhalation** — acid mists, alkaline aerosols cause respiratory diseases.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **electrochemistry of electroplating**. Discuss the role of anode, cathode, and electrolyte with suitable reactions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass article of surface area **400 cm²** is to be gold-plated to a thickness of **5 μm**. Calculate the **time required** at a current of **2.5 A** and **current efficiency of 90%**.
Given: Au atomic mass = **197 g/mol**, valency = **3**, density = **19.3 g/cm³**, $F$ = **96,500 C/mol**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Electroplating is an electrochemical deposition process in which a metallic coating is applied to a conductive substrate using an external DC source and a suitable electrolyte.

**Schematic Description (2 marks):**
The setup consists of:
- **Anode** (positive terminal) — made of the plating metal (e.g., gold, copper, nickel)
- **Cathode** (negative terminal) — the workpiece to be plated
- **Electrolyte** — aqueous solution of a salt of the plating metal
- **DC power source** — typically **2–12 V**, controlled current

**Reactions (3 marks):**

**At the anode (oxidation — metal dissolves):**
$$\text{M}_{(s)} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$$

**At the cathode (reduction — plating):**
$$\text{M}^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow \text{M}_{(s)}$$

**In the electrolyte (e.g., for gold — using potassium gold cyanide bath):**
$$\text{KAu(CN)}_2 \rightarrow \text{K}^+ + \text{Au(CN)}_2^-$$
$$\text{Au(CN)}_2^- \rightarrow \text{Au}^+ + 2\text{CN}^-$$

**Role of Anode:** Supplies metal ions to the bath (replenishment).
**Role of Cathode:** Site of metal deposition.
**Role of Electrolyte:** Provides mobile ions and conducts current.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A continuous DC current maintains a steady ion flux, ensuring uniform, adherent deposition on the cathode.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Surface area $A = 400\ \text{cm}^2 = 4\ \text{dm}^2$
- Thickness $T = 5\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}$
- Current $I = 2.5\ \text{A}$
- Current efficiency $\eta_c = 90\% = 0.90$
- $M = 197\ \text{g/mol}$, $n = 3$, $\rho = 19.3\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $F = 96{,}500\ \text{C/mol}$

**Step 1: Mass of gold required.**
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot T$$
$$m = 19.3 \times 400 \times (5 \times 10^{-4})$$
$$m = 19.3 \times 400 \times 0.0005$$
$$m = 19.3 \times 0.2 = 3.86\ \text{g}$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law (modified for efficiency).**
$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \eta_c}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3: Rearranging for time $t$.**
$$t = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{I \cdot M \cdot \eta_c}$$

**Step 4: Substitute values.**
$$t = \dfrac{3.86 \times 3 \times 96{,}500}{2.5 \times 197 \times 0.90}$$

**Step 5: Numerator.**
$$3.86 \times 3 = 11.58$$
$$11.58 \times 96{,}500 = 1{,}117{,}470$$

**Step 6: Denominator.**
$$2.5 \times 197 = 492.5$$
$$492.5 \times 0.90 = 443.25$$

**Step 7: Final time calculation.**
$$t = \dfrac{1{,}117{,}470}{443.25} \approx 2521.0\ \text{seconds}$$

**Step 8: Convert to minutes.**
$$t = \dfrac{2521.0}{60} \approx 42.02\ \text{minutes}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 42\ \text{minutes}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **various safety hazards** in an electroplating industry and the **preventive measures** to control them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain **Faraday's Second Law of Electrolysis**. A current of **4 A** is passed through a solution of **CuSO₄ for 30 minutes**. Calculate the mass of copper deposited. Given: Cu atomic mass = **63.5 g/mol**, valency = **2**, $F$ = **96,500 C/mol**. Assume **100% current efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Chemical Hazards (2 marks):**
- **Cyanide poisoning** — used in gold, silver, zinc plating.
- **Chromic acid exposure** — used in chromium plating; causes skin ulcers, lung cancer.
- **Acid/alkali burns** — sulfuric, hydrochloric, nitric acids; sodium hydroxide.

**Preventive measures:** PPE (gloves, aprons, goggles), fume hoods, neutralization baths, antidotes (e.g., hydroxylamine for cyanide), MSDS availability.

**2. Electrical Hazards (1.5 marks):**
- DC supply up to 12 V, but current may be **1000s of amperes** → severe shock and burns.
- **Preventive measures:** Insulated bus bars, ELCBs, proper earthing, lockout-tagout (LOTO) procedures, dry hands, rubber-soled boots.

**3. Hydrogen Explosion Hazard (1.5 marks):**
- H₂ evolves at cathode; LEL = **4%** in air.
- **Preventive measures:** Adequate ventilation, explosion-proof fittings, gas detectors, no naked flames, automatic fire suppression.

**4. Environmental Hazards (1 mark):**
- Heavy metal effluent (Cr⁶⁺, Ni²⁺, Cd²⁺, Pb²⁺) in rinse water.
- **Preventive measures:** Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP), pH adjustment, precipitation, ion exchange, sludge disposal as per Hazardous Waste Rules.

**5. Ergonomic & Mechanical Hazards (1 mark):**
- Lifting heavy anodes, slippery floors, repetitive motion.
- **Preventive measures:** Mechanical hoists, anti-skid flooring, job rotation, training.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Faraday's Second Law Statement (2 marks):**
> *"When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes connected in series, the masses of the substances liberated at the electrodes are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights."*

$$m \propto E \quad \text{(equivalent weight)}$$

**Mathematical form:**
$$\dfrac{m_1}{E_1} = \dfrac{m_2}{E_2} = \text{constant} = \dfrac{Q}{F}$$

**Numerical Solution (5 marks):**

**Given:**
- $I = 4\ \text{A}$
- $t = 30\ \text{min} = 30 \times 60 = 1800\ \text{s}$
- $M = 63.5\ \text{g/mol}$
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}500\ \text{C/mol}$
- $\eta_c = 100\% = 1.0$

**Step 1: Calculate charge passed.**
$$Q = I \cdot t = 4 \times 1800 = 7200\ \text{C}$$

**Step 2: Apply Faraday's First Law.**
$$m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$
$$m = \dfrac{4 \times 1800 \times 63.5}{2 \times 96{,}500}$$

**Step 3: Numerator.**
$$4 \times 1800 = 7200$$
$$7200 \times 63.5 = 457{,}200$$

**Step 4: Denominator.**
$$2 \times 96{,}500 = 193{,}000$$

**Step 5: Final mass.**
$$m = \dfrac{457{,}200}{193{,}000} \approx 2.369\ \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 2.37\ \text{grams of copper}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — using $A$ in cm² with $J$ in A/dm² (lose 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting current efficiency** — examiners often give $\eta_c < 100\%$ to test awareness.
> 3. **Wrong valency** — chromium is $n=3$ in Cr³⁺ baths but $n=6$ in chromic acid (H₂CrO₄). Read the question carefully!
> 4. **Time unit confusion** — Faraday's $t$ is in **seconds**, not minutes.
> 5. **Writing anode reaction incorrectly** — at *inert* anode, oxygen evolves: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$ — don't blindly write metal oxidation.
> 6. **Missing safety angle** — In a safety paper, merely stating the *process* is incomplete; always link to **hazards + PPE + statutory norms**.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for last-minute KTU exam prep:**

- ✅ Electroplating = **electrochemical deposition** of metal on a conductive substrate using DC.
- ✅ **Cathode = workpiece (reduction); Anode = plating metal (oxidation, soluble type).**
- ✅ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — *the king formula.*
- ✅ **Faraday's Second Law:** $m \propto$ equivalent weight for the same charge.
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant $F$ = 96,485 C/mol** (use 96,500 for easier calculation).
- ✅ **Current efficiency** $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Thickness formula:** $T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$.
- ✅ **Current density** $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ (A/dm²) — most critical process parameter.
- ✅ **Pre-plating sequence:** Degrease → Rinse → Pickle → Rinse → Activate → Plate → Rinse → Dry.
- ✅ **Hydrogen evolution** at cathode is the **#1 explosion hazard** (LEL 4%).
- ✅ **Cyanide baths** demand strict pH control — acid + cyanide → **HCN gas (lethal)**.
- ✅ **Chromium plating** uses chromic acid → **carcinogen** (Group 1, IARC).
- ✅ **PPE mandatory:** Chemical splash goggles, acid-resistant gloves, rubber apron, closed-toe rubber boots, fume hoods.
- ✅ **Effluent must be treated** — Cr⁶⁺ reduced to Cr³⁺, then precipitated as hydroxide sludge.
- ✅ **Statutory norms:** Indian Factories Act 1948 (Section 41–45 for hazardous processes), OSHA 29 CFR 1910, EPA norms for effluent discharge.
- ✅ **Throwing Power** measures the ability to plate recessed areas — high in cyanide baths, low in acid baths.
- ✅ **Common platings & their metals:** Decorative Cr, Hard Cr, Zn (galvanizing), Ni, Cu, Ag, Au, Cd, Sn, Rh.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** In any 14-mark safety question, structure your answer as **Process → Hazards → Engineering Controls → PPE → Statutory Compliance** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Shops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Shops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Paint Shops

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Paint Shop** is a designated, engineered industrial enclosure or work area specifically designed and equipped for the **application of paints, varnishes, lacquers, enamels, primers, and other surface coating materials** onto manufactured components, sub-assemblies, or finished products. From an occupational safety perspective (KTU 2024 Scheme – PESFT864), a paint shop is classified as a **High-Hazard Process Zone** because it routinely combines **Class I (flammable vapor) atmospheres, electrostatic charging phenomena, toxic chemical exposure, and confined-space ventilation dynamics** within a single operating environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT864 – Module 4):**
> *A paint shop is a specialized industrial facility where surface coating operations (spraying, dipping, brushing, electrostatic deposition, powder coating, and curing) are carried out under controlled ventilation, fire-safety, and exposure-control conditions to mitigate fire, explosion, and occupational health hazards.*

The governing safety framework in India and internationally includes:
- **NFPA 33** – Standard for Spray Application Using Flammable or Combustible Materials
- **NFPA 34** – Standard for Dipping, Coating, and Printing Processes
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.94** – Ventilation
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.107** – Spray-finishing operations
- **IS 8217 / IS 9685** – Indian Standards for paint application safety
- **Factories Act 1948, Section 36 & 38** – Precautions against dangerous fumes

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a paint shop as a **"hospital operating theatre for industrial coatings."** Just as a surgical theatre is engineered to be sterile, smoke-free, with laminar airflow, controlled humidity, and zero ignition sources around flammable anesthetics — a paint shop is similarly engineered to be **vapor-controlled, spark-free, and toxic-substance-managed** around flammable solvents and isocyanates. Every breath of air, every spark of static, and every misplaced metal tool is engineered out of the environment.

**Real-World Analogy:** Picture a kitchen stove burner. The moment you spray perfume (volatile organic compounds – VOCs) near a lit burner, the vapor ignites instantly. Now scale this up: an industrial paint shop atomizes liters of paint into a fine mist (essentially maximizing the surface area of flammable liquid exposed to air). This mist, if it contacts any ignition source — a static spark, a hot exhaust, a switch contact — produces a **deflagration or vapor cloud explosion**. The paint shop's safety systems are essentially built to **control the vapor, control the mist, and control every possible spark**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard parameters govern paint shop design and are emphasized in KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Point of Common Paints | **−4 to 27** | °C | NFPA 33 |
| Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of paint vapors | **1.0 – 1.5** | % by volume | NFPA 68 |
| Minimum Spray Booth Face Velocity | **0.5** | m/s (100 ft/min) | NFPA 33 |
| Recommended Booth Face Velocity | **0.5 – 0.7** | m/s | ACGIH |
| Static Electricity Discharge Limit | **< 25** | mJ | NFPA 77 |
| Air Changes per Hour (ACH) – General Booth | **15 – 20** | ACH | ACGIH |
| Occupational Exposure Limit – Isocyanates (TWA) | **0.005** | ppm | OSHA PEL |
| Permissible Exposure Limit – Toluene (TWA) | **200** | ppm | OSHA PEL |
| Spray Booth Filtration Efficiency | **≥ 95** | % | NFPA 33 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor for KTU Exam:** Whenever a numerical problem appears on paint shops, the **defining triad** is: (1) Face Velocity ≥ 0.5 m/s, (2) LEL below 25% threshold, and (3) Static charge < 25 mJ. Memorize this triad.

## 1.4 Classification of Paint Shops

Paint shops are classified by KTU 2024 scheme according to the **degree of enclosure** and **application method**:

1. **Open-Face Spray Booth** – Operator works on one side; partial enclosure
2. **Enclosed Spray Booth** – Fully enclosed cabin with mechanical exhaust
3. **Cross-Draft Booth** – Air moves horizontally across the workpiece
4. **Down-Draft Booth** – Air moves vertically downward through floor grilles
5. **Electrostatic Spray Booth** – Uses high voltage (60–100 kV) to charge paint particles
6. **Powder Coating Booth** – Uses dry powder, recoverable, lower fire risk
7. **Dip Tank / Flow Coating Area** – Submersion-based application

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hazard Inventory in Paint Shops

A paint shop is a **multi-hazard zone**. The KTU 2024 scheme (Module 4) requires students to systematically identify, evaluate, and control these hazards.

### 2.1.1 Fire and Explosion Hazards (Primary Hazard)

Paint spray operations atomize coatings into droplets of **10–50 micrometers**. This fine mist:
- Drastically increases the **surface-area-to-volume ratio** of the liquid
- Brings the solvent into a state where vapor-air mixture is **within the flammable range (between LEL and UEL)**
- A **spark energy of just 0.2 mJ** (less than a human body's static discharge) can ignite the cloud

**Mechanism of Explosion:**
1. Atomization creates vapor-air mixture
2. Vapor concentration rises above LEL in booth atmosphere
3. Ignition source (static, hot surface, electrical arc) appears
4. Flame propagates through the mist
5. Pressure rise in enclosed booth = **deflagration** (subsonic) or **detonation** (supsonic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Paint mists are classified as **"hybrid mixtures"** because they contain both vapor-phase and aerosol-phase flammable material. This is why paint shop fires propagate faster than pure vapor fires.

### 2.1.2 Chemical and Health Hazards

| Chemical Class | Examples | Health Effect | TLV (ppm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aromatic Hydrocarbons | Toluene, Xylene | CNS depression, liver damage | 50–200 |
| Isocyanates | HDI, MDI, TDI | Occupational asthma, sensitization | 0.005 |
| Heavy Metals | Lead, Chromium, Cadmium | Neurotoxicity, carcinogenicity | < 0.05 |
| Epoxy Resins | Bisphenol-A | Dermatitis, sensitization | — |
| Ketones | MEK, Acetone | Mucous membrane irritation | 200–750 |

**Routes of Entry:**
- **Inhalation** (Primary, ~85% of exposure)
- **Skin absorption** (Secondary)
- **Ingestion** (Poor hygiene)
- **Eye contact** (Splash/spray)

### 2.1.3 Physical and Mechanical Hazards

- **Slip hazards** from spilled paint/solvents
- **Falls from height** when painting large objects (vehicles, aircraft)
- **Ergonomic strain** from repetitive spraying motions
- **Noise** from exhaust fans and air movers (often 85–95 dB)
- **Confined space entry** inside large booths

## 2.2 Engineering Control Hierarchy

The KTU 2024 scheme follows the **hierarchy of controls** (most effective to least effective):

1. **Elimination** → Replace solvent-based with water-based/powder coatings
2. **Substitution** → Use high-solid coatings, UV-cured coatings
3. **Engineering Controls** → Spray booths, LEV, explosion-proof equipment
4. **Administrative Controls** → SOPs, training, work-rest cycles
5. **PPE** → Respirators, gloves, coveralls (last line of defense)

## 2.3 Spray Booth Ventilation Design (Core Theory)

The most critical engineering control in a paint shop is **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)**. KTU examinations frequently test the **face velocity** and **exhaust volumetric flow** calculations.

### 2.3.1 Key Design Parameters

**Face Velocity (Vf):** The velocity of air entering the open face of the spray booth.

$$V_f = \frac{Q}{A_f}$$

Where:
- $V_f$ = Face velocity (m/s)
- $Q$ = Exhaust volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $A_f$ = Cross-sectional area of booth face (m²)

**Code Requirement:** $V_f \geq 0.5$ m/s (NFPA 33) to ensure contaminant capture and prevent vapor escape.

### 2.3.2 Capture Velocity Concept

Capture velocity is the air velocity required at the source of contaminant generation to overcome the **induction effect** of a contaminant's escape velocity.

$$V_c = \frac{V_{esc} \cdot (10 \cdot X)}{A_{source}}$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = Capture velocity at the point of generation
- $V_{esc}$ = Escape velocity of the contaminant
- $X$ = Distance from source to hood (m)
- $A_{source}$ = Area of the source

For solvent spraying, the recommended **capture velocity is 0.5 to 1.0 m/s**.

### 2.3.3 Air Changes per Hour (ACH)

$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{booth}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Exhaust flow rate (m³/s)
- $V_{booth}$ = Booth volume (m³)

**Standard:** 15–20 ACH for general spray booths; 60–100 ACH for curing ovens.

### 2.3.4 Explosion Pressure Relief Area

For a paint spray booth, the **required relief area** is calculated using the **NFPA 68 cubical law** (applicable for low-LST dusts and hybrid mists):

$$A_v = C \cdot \frac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{0.5}}$$

Where:
- $A_v$ = Required vent area (m²)
- $C$ = Material constant (for paints/mists, typically C = 0.4 to 0.6)
- $V$ = Enclosure volume (m³)
- $P_{red}$ = Reduced explosion pressure (typically 0.1–0.2 bar gauge)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for paint shop safety calculations. Every formula is examinable under Module 4 of PESFT864.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_f}$ | Face velocity of spray booth | m/s | NFPA 33 |
| 2 | $Q = V_f \cdot A_f$ | Required exhaust flow | m³/s | NFPA 33 |
| 3 | $ACH = \dfrac{3600 \cdot Q}{V_{booth}}$ | Air changes per hour | ACH | ACGIH |
| 4 | $P_{red} = P_{max} \cdot K_{st}$ | Reduced explosion pressure | bar | NFPA 68 |
| 5 | $A_v = C \cdot \dfrac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{0.5}}$ | Explosion vent area | m² | NFPA 68 |
| 6 | $L_{EL,25\%} = 0.25 \cdot LEL$ | 25% LEL alarm threshold | % vol | NFPA 69 |
| 7 | $I = \dfrac{C}{T}$ | Exposure index = Conc./TLV | dimensionless | ACGIH |
| 8 | $V_c = k \cdot \dfrac{V_{esc}}{X}$ | Capture velocity at hood | m/s | ACGIH Vent Manual |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{V_f}{V_{c,req}}$ | Capture effectiveness ratio | dimensionless | Engineering judgment |
| 10 | $L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level | dB | ISO 3744 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Modern car-body paint shops use **downdraft booths** with 0.5 m/s face velocity to ensure uniform coating and zero orange-peel defects while maintaining OSHA compliance.
- **Aerospace Industry:** High-solid polyurethane paints require **explosion-proof booth lighting, intrinsically safe wiring, and isocyanate-monitored breathing-air systems**.
- **Furniture Industry:** Powder coating booths operate at **lower fire risk** but require **dust collectors rated for Class II combustible dust**.
- **Shipyards:** Large paint operations near sea use **forced ventilation + respiratory protection** to manage both solvent exposure and worker fall hazards.
- **Electronics:** Conformal coating uses **UV-cured, solvent-free** materials — a classic example of **hazard substitution** in the hierarchy of controls.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spray Booth Exhaust Flow Rate

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**
A paint spray booth has an open face of dimensions **3 m wide × 2.5 m high**. The minimum face velocity required by NFPA 33 for adequate contaminant capture is **0.5 m/s**. Calculate:
(a) The minimum exhaust volumetric flow rate.
(b) The number of air changes per hour if the booth internal volume is 50 m³.

**Given:**
- Width of booth face: $W = 3$ m
- Height of booth face: $H = 2.5$ m
- Required face velocity: $V_f = 0.5$ m/s
- Booth internal volume: $V_{booth} = 50$ m³

### Solution:

**Part (a): Volumetric Flow Rate**

Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the booth face.

$$A_f = W \times H = 3 \times 2.5$$

$$\boxed{A_f = 7.5 \text{ m}^2}$$

Step 2: Apply the face velocity formula to find the volumetric flow rate.

$$V_f = \frac{Q}{A_f} \implies Q = V_f \cdot A_f$$

Substitute the values:

$$Q = 0.5 \, \text{m/s} \times 7.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{Q = 3.75 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

Step 3: Convert to standard units often used in industry (m³/hr) for the procurement of fans.

$$Q_{hr} = Q \times 3600 = 3.75 \times 3600$$

$$\boxed{Q_{hr} = 13{,}500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}$$

**Part (b): Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**

Step 4: Apply the ACH formula.

$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{booth}}$$

Substitute the values:

$$ACH = \frac{3.75 \times 3600}{50} = \frac{13{,}500}{50}$$

$$\boxed{ACH = 270 \text{ air changes per hour}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 270 ACH result is high — typical for a small-volume booth with high vapor generation. ACGIH typically recommends 15–20 ACH for solvent-based booths. This high value indicates the booth is **either undersized or over-ventilated**, which has energy cost implications. A real-world engineer would either reduce fan speed (using VFD) or enlarge the booth.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Explosion Vent Area for a Paint Booth

**Problem Statement:**
A paint spray booth has an internal volume of **64 m³**. The material constant for the paint mist is **C = 0.5**, and the maximum reduced explosion pressure is **P_red = 0.2 bar (g)**. Calculate the required explosion vent area per NFPA 68.

**Given:**
- $V = 64$ m³
- $C = 0.5$
- $P_{red} = 0.2$ bar (gauge)

### Solution:

Step 1: Recall the NFPA 68 vent area equation (low-LST hybrid mixture formula).

$$A_v = C \cdot \frac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{0.5}}$$

Step 2: Calculate the volume term $V^{2/3}$.

$$V^{2/3} = 64^{2/3} = (4^3)^{2/3} = 4^2$$

$$\boxed{V^{2/3} = 16}$$

Step 3: Calculate the pressure term $P_{red}^{0.5}$.

$$P_{red}^{0.5} = (0.2)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{red}^{0.5} \approx 0.4472 \, \text{bar}^{0.5}}$$

Step 4: Substitute into the main equation.

$$A_v = 0.5 \times \frac{16}{0.4472}$$

$$A_v = 0.5 \times 35.78$$

$$\boxed{A_v \approx 17.89 \, \text{m}^2}$$

Step 5: Practical interpretation.

> [!NOTE]
> A 17.89 m² vent area is enormous. For a 64 m³ booth, this implies the booth has insufficient venting and would likely require **explosion suppression (NFPA 69)** rather than venting alone. Alternatively, the booth should be re-engineered with **smaller enclosures** (e.g., multiple small booths of 8 m³ each) to reduce vent area to manageable size.

**Verification by Alternative Method (Cubical Law):**

The NFPA 68 cubical law approximate formula for hybrid mists:

$$A_v \approx 0.0024 \cdot V^{0.75}$$

For $V = 64$ m³:

$$A_v \approx 0.0024 \times 64^{0.75} = 0.0024 \times 24.0 \approx 0.058 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> Note the discrepancy — the two formulas use different units and reference pressures. In KTU exams, **always state which formula and reference pressure you are using** to avoid valuation penalty.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard-Control Mapping

The following KTU 2024-style analytical matrix maps every identified hazard in a paint shop to its corresponding control measure, PPE, and regulatory citation. This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam topic.

| S.No. | Hazard | Source / Cause | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE Required | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vapor Cloud Explosion** | Solvent atomization | LEV, explosion vents | LEL monitoring, hot-work permits | FR clothing | NFPA 33, 68 |
| 2 | **Static Spark Ignition** | Operator clothing, fluid flow | Grounding, bonding | Conductive footwear, humidification | Static-dissipative gloves | NFPA 77 |
| 3 | **Isocyanate Asthma** | Polyurethane spray | Spray booth, air-line respirator | Medical surveillance, training | Supplied-air respirator | OSHA 1910.1052 |
| 4 | **Lead Poisoning** | Lead-based primers | Substitution (zinc-rich epoxy) | Hygiene, washing facilities | Chemical-resistant gloves | OSHA 1910.1025 |
| 5 | **Skin Dermatitis** | Solvent contact | Local exhaust, automation | Gloves, barrier cream | Nitrile gloves, coveralls | OSHA 1910.132 |
| 6 | **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss** | Exhaust fans, conveyors | Acoustic enclosure | Hearing conservation program | Ear plugs/muffs | OSHA 1910.95 |
| 7 | **Slip, Trip, Fall** | Spilled paint/thinner | Non-slip flooring, drip trays | Housekeeping SOPs | Safety footwear | OSHA 1910.22 |
| 8 | **Fire (Curing Oven)** | Solvent-laden oven | Explosion relief doors | LEL cutoff at 25% LEL | Heat-resistant gloves | NFPA 86 |
| 9 | **Confined Space Entry** | Booth maintenance | Gas testing, ventilation | Permit-to-work, attendant | SCBA, harness | OSHA 1910.146 |
| 10 | **Eye Injury** | Splash, aerosol | Eye-wash stations, side shields | Eyewash inspection | Chemical goggles | ANSI Z87.1 |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Static Electricity Control Logic

Static electricity is the **single most cited ignition source** in paint shop fire investigations. The KTU 2024 scheme tests the following logical sequence:

Step 1: **Identify charging mechanisms:**
- Fluid flow through pipes (triboelectric charging)
- High-speed atomization (charge separation)
- Human movement across non-conductive floors (body voltage)

Step 2: **Quantify hazard:**
- A human can accumulate **body voltage of 10–25 kV** walking on a dry floor
- Discharge energy: $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$, where C ≈ 100–200 pF (human body capacitance)
- $E = 0.5 \times 150 \times 10^{-12} \times (25{,}000)^2 \approx 47$ mJ
- **Minimum ignition energy of paint mist ≈ 0.2 mJ**
- Therefore, even a tiny fraction of human body discharge exceeds MIE by **>200 times**.

Step 3: **Apply controls:**
- Ground all metal containers (resistance ≤ 1 megohm)
- Bond containers to receiving vessel before transfer
- Use **conductive or static-dissipative footwear** (resistance ≤ 10⁸ Ω)
- Maintain relative humidity > 60% to dissipate surface charges
- Use **static-dissipative paint hoses**

Step 4: **Verify:**
- Daily ground continuity checks (clamp resistance)
- Periodic body voltage measurement
- Equipment certification

$$\boxed{E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 \geq MIE_{paint} \Rightarrow \text{UNSAFE}}$$

---

## 3.5 Detailed Worked Example: 25% LEL Threshold Calculation

**Problem:**
A spray booth atmosphere contains **800 ppm of toluene vapor**. The LEL of toluene is **1.2% (12,000 ppm)**. The OSHA PEL is 200 ppm. Determine:
(a) The % LEL of the atmosphere
(b) Whether operations should be shut down (using 25% LEL rule)
(c) Whether health exposure is within limits

### Solution:

**Part (a): Percent LEL calculation**

Step 1: Express concentration as a fraction of LEL.

$$\% LEL = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{LEL}} \times 100$$

Step 2: Substitute the values.

$$\% LEL = \frac{800}{12{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% LEL = 6.67\%}$$

**Part (b): Compliance with 25% LEL rule (NFPA 69)**

Step 3: Compare with the 25% LEL alarm threshold.

$$\text{Measured: } 6.67\% \, LEL \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{Limit: } 25\% \, LEL$$

$$\boxed{6.67\% < 25\% \Rightarrow \text{OPERATIONS MAY CONTINUE}}$$

**Part (c): Health exposure check**

Step 4: Compare with OSHA PEL.

$$\text{Measured: } 800 \, \text{ppm} \quad \text{vs} \quad \text{PEL: } 200 \, \text{ppm}$$

$$\boxed{800 \, \text{ppm} > 200 \, \text{ppm} \Rightarrow \text{HEALTH EXPOSURE EXCEEDED}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exam:** A booth may be **fire-safe (< 25% LEL)** but simultaneously **health-hazardous (> PEL)**. These are two different compliance regimes. Many students lose marks by only checking fire safety, ignoring occupational health. Always evaluate BOTH criteria in KTU paint shop questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Define the term "Paint Shop" as applied in industrial safety. List any four major hazards associated with paint shops.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A paint shop is a specially designed and engineered industrial facility where surface coating operations such as spraying, brushing, dipping, and curing of paints, varnishes, and other coating materials are carried out under controlled ventilation, fire-safe, and occupational health conditions to mitigate fire, explosion, and toxic-exposure hazards.

**Four Major Hazards (1.5 marks, any four):**
1. **Fire and explosion** due to flammable solvent vapors
2. **Toxic exposure** to isocyanates, VOCs, and heavy metals
3. **Static electricity discharge** igniting the vapor-air mixture
4. **Slip and fall hazards** from spilled paint and thinners
5. **Ergonomic strain** from repetitive motion and overhead spraying
6. **Noise-induced hearing loss** from exhaust fans and pneumatic equipment

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**Q: What is meant by the "25% LEL rule" in paint shop safety? Why is it used instead of the LEL itself?**

**Model Answer:**

**Meaning (1.5 marks):**
The 25% LEL rule is a safety threshold specified in **NFPA 69** for flammable atmospheres. It states that **process operations must be automatically shut down or alarmed when the concentration of flammable vapor reaches 25% of the Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)**. For example, if the LEL of toluene is 1.2%, the alarm/shutdown threshold is 0.3% by volume.

**Why 25% and not 100% (1.5 marks):**
The 25% LEL threshold is used because:
1. It provides a **safety margin** to account for sensor inaccuracy, dead time, and transient vapor pockets
2. It allows time for **operator intervention and emergency response** before the atmosphere reaches flammable range
3. Industrial atmospheres are rarely uniform; **local concentrations** can exceed the average reading
4. NFPA research has shown that **delayed ignition near the LEL can produce overpressure damage** even below 100% LEL due to turbulence and inhomogeneity

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Q: A paint spray booth has an open face of dimensions 4 m (width) × 3 m (height). The required minimum face velocity is 0.5 m/s. The booth internal volume is 90 m³.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the volumetric exhaust flow rate required and the number of air changes per hour. State whether the booth design meets NFPA 33 standards.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various fire and explosion hazards in a paint shop. Explain the engineering and administrative control measures used to mitigate these hazards, with reference to NFPA standards.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) – Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Width: $W = 4$ m
- Height: $H = 3$ m
- Required face velocity: $V_f = 0.5$ m/s
- Booth volume: $V_{booth} = 90$ m³

**Step 1: Calculate the face area.**

$$A_f = W \times H = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the required exhaust flow rate.**

$$Q = V_f \times A_f = 0.5 \times 12 = 6.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/hr:

$$Q_{hr} = 6.0 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3: Calculate ACH.**

$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{booth}} = \frac{6.0 \times 3600}{90} = \frac{21{,}600}{90} = 240 \, ACH$$

**Step 4: Check NFPA 33 compliance.**

Since $V_f = 0.5$ m/s meets the **NFPA 33 minimum**, the booth design is compliant. The 240 ACH is **above the ACGIH recommended 15–20 ACH** — this is over-engineered, indicating an energy cost concern. Recommendation: install a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) to modulate fan speed during non-operational periods.

#### Part (b) – Descriptive Solution

**Fire and Explosion Hazards in a Paint Shop (3 marks):**

1. **Flammable Vapor Clouds** – Atomized solvent mist forms explosive vapor-air mixture between LEL and UEL.
