$$\sum L_i U_i = -1.200 + j(0.360 - 0.720) = -1.20 - j\,0.36 \quad \text{kg·m}^2$$

#### STEP 4 — Set Up the Two Complex Equations

Let $U_L = m_L r_L \, e^{j\theta_L}$ (plane $L$, $L_L = -0.10$ m) and $U_R = m_R r_R \, e^{j\theta_R}$ (plane $R$, $L_R = +0.70$ m).

**Force equation:**

$$U_L + U_R = -\sum U_i = +2.0 - j\,0.6 \quad \cdots(1)$$

**Couple equation:**

$$(-0.10)\,U_L + (0.70)\,U_R = -\sum L_i U_i = +1.20 + j\,0.36 \quad \cdots(2)$$

#### STEP 5 — Solve Simultaneously

From equation (1): $\;U_L = (2.0 - j\,0.6) - U_R$

Substitute into equation (2):

$$\begin{aligned}
-0.10 \big[(2.0 - j\,0.6) - U_R\big] + 0.70\,U_R &= 1.20 + j\,0.36 \\
-0.20 + j\,0.06 + 0.10\,U_R + 0.70\,U_R &= 1.20 + j\,0.36 \\
0.80\,U_R &= 1.20 + 0.20 \;+\; j(0.36 - 0.06) \\
0.80\,U_R &= 1.40 + j\,0.30 \\
U_R &= \dfrac{1.40 + j\,0.30}{0.80} = 1.75 + j\,0.375
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to polar:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert U_R \vert &= \sqrt{1.75^2 + 0.375^2} = \sqrt{3.0625 + 0.1406} = \sqrt{3.2031} \approx 1.7898 \ \text{kg·m} \\
\theta_R &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.375}{1.75}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.2143) \approx 12.09^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

Now substitute back to find $U_L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_L &= (2.0 - j\,0.6) - (1.75 + j\,0.375) \\
U_L &= (2.0 - 1.75) \;+\; j(-0.6 - 0.375) \\
U_L &= 0.25 - j\,0.975
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to polar:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert U_L \vert &= \sqrt{0.25^2 + 0.975^2} = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.9506} = \sqrt{1.0131} \approx 1.0065 \ \text{kg·m} \\
\theta_L &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-0.975}{0.25}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-3.90) \approx -75.61^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

#### STEP 6 — Compute Actual Correction Masses at $r = 0.15$ m

$$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} m_R &= \frac{\vert U_R \vert}{r_R} = \frac{1.7898}{0.15} \approx 11.93 \ \text{kg at } \theta_R = 12.09^\circ \text{ (in plane R)} \\[6pt] m_L &= \frac{\vert U_L \vert}{r_L} = \frac{1.0065}{0.15} \approx 6.71 \ \text{kg at } \theta_L = -75.61^\circ \text{ (in plane L)} \end{aligned} }$$

#### STEP 7 — Verification

Check both equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_L + U_R &= (0.25 + 1.75) + j(-0.975 + 0.375) = 2.0 - j\,0.6 \quad ✓ \\
-0.10\,U_L + 0.70\,U_R &= -0.10(0.25 - j\,0.975) + 0.70(1.75 + j\,0.375) \\
&= (-0.025 + j\,0.0975) + (1.225 + j\,0.2625) \\
&= 1.20 + j\,0.36 \quad ✓
\end{aligned}$$

The shaft is now **completely dynamically balanced**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow of the Dynamic Balancing Procedure

```mermaid

    s3["Step 3: Compute Cartesian components of U_i"] --> s4

    s5["Step 5: Compute L_i x U_i for every mass"] --> s6
    s6["Step 6: Sum the couples: SUM_LU real and imag"] --> s7
    s7["Step 7: Choose two correction planes L_A and L_B"] --> s8
    s8["Step 8: Solve the 2x2 complex equation system"] --> s9
    s9["Step 9: Convert U_L and U_R to polar form: magnitude and angle"] --> s10
    s10["Step 10: Divide by correction radius to get m_L and m_R"] --> s11
    s11["Step 11: Apply ISO 1940-1 quality grade check"] --> endA
    endA([END: Balanced Rotor])

    s8 -.->|"alternative"| s8b["Graphical Force-Couple Polygon Method"] -.-> s9
    s8 -.->|"alternative"| s8c["Matrix / Influence Coefficient Method"] -.-> s9
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define dynamic balancing. State the essential difference between static balancing and dynamic balancing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dynamic balancing is the process of ensuring that the **resultant centrifugal force** and the **resultant centrifugal couple** acting on a rotating shaft are both **zero**, so that the shaft does not experience any vibration or bearing reaction during rotation.

A body is **statically balanced** if its centre of gravity lies on the axis of rotation — this requires only a **single-plane correction**. However, a statically balanced body can still generate a **couple** between unbalanced masses located in **different axial planes**, causing vibration during rotation. **Dynamic balancing** eliminates this couple as well, requiring correction in **at least two planes**. Thus static balance is necessary but **not sufficient** for dynamic balance in multi-plane rotors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State the two mathematical conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied for a rotor to be completely dynamically balanced.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Let a rotor have $n$ unbalanced masses $m_i$ at radii $r_i$ and axial positions $L_i$ from a reference plane. The two conditions are:

1. **Resultant Centrifugal Force must be zero (Static condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \vec{r}_i = 0 \quad \text{(vector form)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

2. **Resultant Centrifugal Couple about the reference plane must be zero (Dynamic condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \vec{r}_i \, L_i = 0 \quad \text{(vector form)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

All four scalar equations must hold simultaneously for complete dynamic balance.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A rigid shaft carries **four masses** $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$ in four different planes. The data is given below:

| Mass | $m$ (kg) | $r$ (m) | $\theta$ (deg) | Plane location from $A$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 10 | 0.10 | 0 | 0 |
| $B$ | 15 | 0.12 | 90 | 0.20 |
| $C$ | 20 | 0.15 | 180 | 0.40 |
| $D$ | 12 | 0.10 | 270 | 0.60 |

Find the magnitude and angular position of balance masses to be placed in planes at $-0.10$ m (plane $L$) and $+0.70$ m (plane $R$) at a radius of 0.15 m. Verify your result.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **graphical force-couple polygon method** for the dynamic balancing of a four-mass rotor. State two industrial situations where this method is preferred over the analytical method.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks** *(See Section 3.3 worked example for the complete step-by-step solution.)*

| Quantity | Value | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Step 1–2: Force polygon SUM U | $-2.0 + j\,0.6$ kg·m | 1 |
| Step 3: Couple polygon SUM L_i U_i | $-1.20 - j\,0.36$ kg·m² | 1 |
| Step 4: Two complex equations set up correctly | as in section 3.3 | 1 |
| Step 5: Solving the system (U_L and U_R in Cartesian) | $U_L = 0.25 - j\,0.975$, $U_R = 1.75 + j\,0.375$ | 2 |
| Step 6: Polar conversion and final masses | $m_L \approx 6.71$ kg at $-75.61^\circ$, $m_R \approx 11.93$ kg at $+12.09^\circ$ | 1 |
| Step 7: Verification check | Both equations satisfied | 1 |

**Final Answer:**
* **Plane L ($-0.10$ m):** $m_L = 6.71$ kg at $\theta_L = -75.61^\circ$ (or equivalently $284.39^\circ$)
* **Plane R ($+0.70$ m):** $m_R = 11.93$ kg at $\theta_R = +12.09^\circ$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Graphical Force–Couple Polygon Method:**

1. **Draw the rotor to scale** showing all unbalanced masses and proposed correction planes.
2. **Draw the Force Polygon:**
   * Draw vectors $m_A r_A$, $m_B r_B$, $m_C r_C$, $m_D r_D$ tip-to-tail at their respective angles $\theta$.
   * The closing vector of the polygon represents $-\sum U_i$.
   * From the same starting point, draw the unknown $U_L$ and $U_R$ such that their tip-to-tail sum equals the closing vector. This is achieved by drawing two **trial lines** from the two ends of the closing vector and finding their intersection.
3. **Draw the Couple Polygon (about reference plane):**
   * Multiply each force vector by its axial distance $L_i$ (effectively stretching each force vector) to obtain moment vectors.
   * Close this polygon similarly; the closing vector equals $-\sum L_i U_i$.
   * Draw lines from the two ends, scaled by $L_L$ and $L_R$, to obtain the magnitudes and angles of $U_L$ and $U_R$ in this couple polygon.
4. The intersection points in both polygons give the **mass-radius products** $m_L r_L$ and $m_R r_R$ and their **angular positions**.

**Two industrial situations preferring graphical method:**
* **Field balancing** of large rotors (turbine, marine propeller shaft) where quick on-site sketches are needed before instrument-assisted fine balancing.
* **Educational/training laboratories** where students develop physical intuition about unbalance forces and couples.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **two scalar conditions** for the complete dynamic balance of a multi-plane rotor. State clearly the physical meaning of each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A turbine rotor has a measured **residual unbalance** of $U = 240$ g·mm at the correction radius. The rotor mass is 18 kg. Using **ISO 1940-1**, determine the **balance quality grade** achieved and the corrective action required. *(Take $e_{per} \cdot \omega = 6.3$ mm/s as the limit.)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Consider $n$ masses $m_i$ at radii $r_i$, angles $\theta_i$, axial positions $L_i$ from a reference plane $O$. Each generates a centrifugal force $\vec{F}_i = m_i \omega^2 \vec{r}_i$.

**Force balance (Static condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \vec{r}_i = 0$$

In component form:

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Physical meaning:** The **vector sum of all centrifugal forces** acting on the shaft must vanish. If satisfied, the shaft centre of pressure on the bearings does not move during rotation.

**Couple balance (Dynamic condition):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, \vec{r}_i \, L_i = 0$$

In component form:

$$\sum m_i r_i L_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i L_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Physical meaning:** The **resultant moment of all centrifugal forces** about any chosen reference plane must vanish. If satisfied, the shaft has no rocking tendency — the bearing reactions remain constant.

For both to hold **simultaneously**, the rotor is said to be **completely dynamically balanced** — also termed *"running smooth"* in industry.

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-body diagram and force expression | 1.5 |
| 2 | Static condition (force) derived in vector and component form | 2 |
| 3 | Couple condition derived in vector and component form | 2 |
| 4 | Physical meaning clearly stated | 1.5 |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
* Measured residual unbalance $U = 240$ g·mm at the correction radius
* Rotor mass $m = 18$ kg
* Permissible specific unbalance: $e_{per} \cdot \omega = 6.3$ mm/s (corresponds to ISO Grade **G 6.3**)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert residual unbalance to specific unbalance:**

$$e_{per} \cdot \omega = \frac{U \cdot \omega}{m}$$

Assuming the rotor runs at $N = 3000$ rpm:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 100\pi \approx 314.16 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific unbalance:**

$$e_{per} \cdot \omega = \frac{U \cdot \omega}{m} = \frac{(240 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg·m}) \times 314.16}{18\,\text{kg}} = \frac{75.398}{18} \approx 4.19 \ \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ISO 1940-1 limit (G 6.3 = 6.3 mm/s):**

$$4.19 \ \text{mm/s} \;<\; 6.3 \ \text{mm/s}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The rotor satisfies the ISO 1940-1 **G 6.3** balance quality grade (suitable for general machinery, pump impellers, machine-tool drives).

**Step 4 — Tighter grade check (G 2.5 = 2.5 mm/s):**

Since $4.19 > 2.5$, the rotor **does NOT meet G 2.5** (which would be required for gas-turbine rotors and precision grinding spindles).

**Step 5 — Corrective Action Required (1.5 marks):**

To meet **G 2.5**, the permissible residual unbalance must be reduced to:

$$U_{per} = \frac{e_{per} \cdot \omega \cdot m}{\omega} = \frac{2.5 \times 18}{314.16} \times 1000 \approx 143.2 \ \text{g·mm}$$

**Required correction:** Remove $240 - 143.2 = 96.8$ g·mm of unbalance by:
* Adding a **correction mass** at the appropriate angle, OR
* **Material removal** (drilling / grinding) at the heavy spot, OR
* Re-balance on a **balancing machine** to within G 2.5.

| Step | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convert RPM to rad/s | 1 |
| 2 | Compute specific unbalance | 2 |
| 3 | Compare with ISO 1940 limit and identify grade | 1.5 |
| 4 | Recompute for tighter grade and required correction mass | 1.5 |
| 5 | Corrective action listed | 1 |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Dynamic Balancing problems:**
> 1. **Sign of axial distance $L_i$** — students often use unsigned distances. The sign matters because it sets the direction of the couple. If the reference plane is chosen at one end, half the $L_i$ will be negative.
> 2. **Confusing angle convention** — angles are measured consistently (e.g., all anticlockwise from the $X$-axis). Mixing CW and CCW is a 2-mark deduction in KTU papers.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** in numerical problems involving actual forces — but recall that for *balance computation* the $\omega^2$ cancels out and only mass-radius products are needed.
> 4. **Not verifying the answer** — the closing of both force and couple polygons must be checked. Loss of 2 marks if omitted.
> 5. **Mixing units** — kg·m vs g·mm. Always convert to SI first, then convert back for the final answer.
> 6. **Ignoring ISO 1940-1** in safety-related numericals — KTU 2024 specifically asks for the grade achieved (G 6.3, G 2.5, etc.) — losing 1 to 2 marks here is common.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Dynamic balance** = Zero resultant force AND zero resultant couple.
- [x] **Static balance** = Zero resultant force only (achieved by a single-plane correction).
- [x] **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ — scales with the **square** of speed.
- [x] For analytical balance, **only mass-radius products $m \cdot r$** are needed — speed cancels out.
- [x] A rotor with masses in **one plane** is balanced by a **single-plane correction**.
- [x] A rotor with masses in **two or more planes** requires **two (or more) correction planes**.
- [x] **Two complex equations** govern the two-plane problem: force balance + couple balance.
- [x] Choose **bracing** correction planes — one on each side of all unbalance masses — for stable numerical solutions.
- [x] **ISO 1940-1** specifies permissible residual unbalance via the grade $G$ (in mm/s).
- [x] Common grades: **G 4000** (crankshafts of large slow marine diesels) → **G 6.3** (pumps, industrial fans) → **G 2.5** (turbines, machine-tool drives) → **G 0.4** (grinder spindles, gyro rotors).
- [x] **Balancing machine types**: soft-bearing (low-speed, sensitive) and hard-bearing (high-speed, production).
- [x] **Field balancing** is performed on-site using portable instruments when shop balancing is impractical.
- [x] **Always verify** both force and couple closure after computing correction masses.
- [x] **Safety implication**: A 70 % rule-of-thumb — most rotating-machinery premature bearing failures trace back to unbalance.
- [x] **Sign convention**: Adopt $+X$ datum and **anticlockwise positive** for all angular measurements — be consistent throughout.
- [x] The **mass-radius product** $m \cdot r$ is a vector quantity — both magnitude (kg·m) and direction ($\theta$) are essential.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydro Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydro Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Hydro Testing

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Hydro Testing** (also known as **Hydrostatic Testing** or **Hydrostatic Pressure Testing**) is a non-destructive quality assurance procedure in which a pressure-retaining component — such as a pressure vessel, boiler, pipeline, storage tank, heat exchanger, or fire-extinguishing system — is filled with a nearly **incompressible fluid** (most commonly **water**) and pressurized to a predetermined **test pressure** greater than its normal operating pressure. The purpose is to verify **structural integrity**, **leak-tightness**, **weld quality**, and **material soundness** before the equipment is commissioned into service or returned to service after repair or modification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** As per ASME Section V, Article 10 and IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels), hydrostatic testing is a mandatory qualification test for all new and modified pressure equipment, performed prior to commissioning and at scheduled intervals thereafter.

The governing relationship used universally in pressure-vessel design verification is the **Hoop Stress (Circumferential Stress) Equation**, derived from thin-walled pressure-vessel theory:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_h$ = Hoop (circumferential) stress in MPa or psi
- $P$ = Internal gauge pressure in MPa or psi
- $D$ = Internal diameter of the vessel in mm or inches
- $t$ = Wall thickness in mm or inches

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Balloon Stress Test"

Imagine inflating a long, cylindrical balloon with air. If you pump it to a very high pressure, it will stretch and might burst at its weakest seam. Now imagine the same balloon, but **filled with water** instead of air, and pressurized. Because **water is incompressible**, it stores almost no elastic energy. If the balloon bursts, the water simply leaks out — there is no explosive release of stored energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why Water" Principle:** Water has a compressibility of approximately **0.0005 / 100 MPa** (essentially zero). The energy stored in compressed water is roughly **1/100th to 1/200th** of the energy stored in compressed air at the same pressure. This makes hydro testing **vastly safer** than pneumatic (air/gas) testing for verifying the same pressure level.

This is the core engineering rationale for hydro testing: **achieve the same stress verification with dramatically lower stored catastrophic energy**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Hydro Testing

| Metric | Value / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Density of water (testing medium) | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Compressibility of water | **~0.45 × 10⁻⁹** | Pa⁻¹ |
| Acceleration due to gravity (g) | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Standard test pressure multiplier (ASME) | **1.3 × MAWP** | dimensionless |
| Standard test pressure multiplier (Piping) | **1.5 × design** | dimensionless |
| Hold period (typical, ASME) | **10 – 30** | minutes |
| Hydro vs Pneumatic stored-energy ratio | **~1:100 to 1:200** | ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** If a numerical problem provides a test pressure without specifying the multiplier, students must **always state the governing code** (ASME / IS / API) before applying any factor. Marks are commonly lost for assuming the wrong multiplier.

## 1.4 Classification of Hydro Testing

Hydro Testing is broadly classified into four engineering categories:

1. **Strength Test** — Verifies that the equipment can withstand pressures **above** its design pressure without yielding or rupturing.
2. **Leak Test / Tightness Test** — Performed at the **normal operating pressure** to detect minute leaks through gaskets, joints, and weld seams.
3. **Initial Hydro Test** — Conducted on **newly manufactured** equipment at the manufacturer's shop or site before commissioning.
4. **Periodic / Re-qualification Hydro Test** — Conducted at scheduled intervals (typically every **4 to 8 years**, depending on service and code) on in-service equipment to verify continued fitness for service.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Rationale — Why Hydro Test?

The driving principle behind hydro testing is the verification of two distinct engineering limits:

> **[A] Yield Strength Verification** — Confirm that under the elevated test pressure, the induced **hoop stress** in the vessel wall does not exceed the **allowable stress** of the material (typically a fraction of the Yield Strength $S_y$).

> **[B] Leak-Tightness Verification** — Confirm that all welded joints, flanged connections, threaded joints, and gasket surfaces remain **leak-free** under sustained test pressure for the prescribed hold period.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Hydro Test Pressure Selection

The selection of test pressure is governed by a hierarchical logic rooted in **code compliance**:

- **Step 1:** Determine the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** of the equipment at its **design temperature**.
- **Step 2:** Multiply MAWP by a **code-defined factor** (typically 1.3 for vessels, 1.5 for piping) to compute the **Hydrostatic Test Pressure ($P_t$)**.
- **Step 3:** Verify that the test pressure does **not** cause the calculated hoop stress to exceed the material's allowable stress at test temperature (usually **90 % of yield** for carbon steel per ASME).
- **Step 4:** If the calculated $P_t$ would over-stress the component at room temperature, **the test pressure is reduced** to a level that keeps stress within limits — this is called the "**limited test pressure**".

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Governing Code / Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_t = 1.3 \times \text{MAWP}$ | Test pressure for pressure vessels | ASME Section VIII Div. 1 |
| 2 | $P_t = 1.5 \times \text{Design Pressure}$ | Test pressure for piping systems | ASME B31.1 / B31.3 |
| 3 | $P_t = 1.25 \times \text{MAWP} \times \frac{S_{yt}}{S_{yd}}$ | Adjusted test pressure for high-temp service | ASME VIII-1, UG-99(c) |
| 4 | $\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder | Universal — Derived |
| 5 | $\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | Universal — Derived |
| 6 | $\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$ | Hoop-to-longitudinal stress ratio | Universal — Derived |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} = 0.9 \cdot S_y$ | Max allowable stress during test | ASME VIII-1, UG-99(b) |
| 8 | $t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P}$ | Required wall thickness (cylinder) | ASME VIII-1, UG-27 |
| 9 | $E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{P^2 \cdot V}{2 \cdot \beta}$ | Stored elastic energy in fluid | Used in hazard classification |
| 10 | $V_{\text{water}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L$ | Internal volume of cylindrical vessel | General geometric |
| 11 | $H_{\text{static}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Static head pressure of water column | Hydrostatic pressure formula |
| 12 | $P_t^{\text{low}} = 0.9 \cdot S_y \cdot \frac{2t}{D}$ | Limited test pressure (when 1.3×MAWP over-stresses) | Derived from (1)+(4)+(7) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In all table rows above, the absolute-value / divisibility symbol is rendered using plain math (`P × MAWP`, not `P | MAWP`). In a written exam, prefer the explicit multiplication sign `×` or the word "times" to avoid ambiguity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydro testing is **not** an academic exercise — it is a **life-safety gate** in the following industries:

- **Oil & Gas:** Verification of cross-country pipelines, well-head christmas trees, and LNG storage spheres.
- **Power Generation:** Boiler tubes, feedwater heaters, and high-pressure steam headers.
- **Chemical & Petrochemical:** Reactors, distillation columns, and heat-exchanger shells.
- **Fire Protection:** Sprinkler risers, hydrant networks, and fire-water storage tanks.
- **Nuclear:** Reactor coolant pressure boundaries and spent-fuel pool liners.
- **Aerospace:** Hydraulic accumulators and high-pressure gas bottles (after manufacture).

## 2.5 Why Not Pneumatic Testing Alone?

Pneumatic testing uses **gas** (air or nitrogen) as the pressurizing medium. While it is useful for equipment that cannot be dried easily or is sensitive to water contamination, it carries a **far higher hazard** because:

- Gas is **compressible** — it stores **enormous energy** at high pressure.
- A pneumatic failure releases that energy as a **shock wave, fragmentation, and fire** (if the gas is flammable).
- Pneumatic testing is permitted by ASME only at **lower test pressures (1.1 × MAWP for vessels, 1.2 × design for piping)** and requires more rigorous safety controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU-Favorite):** *If hydrostatic testing is physically possible, it shall be preferred over pneumatic testing.* — paraphrased from ASME Section V, Article 10.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

This is the foundational equation for ALL hydro-test stress calculations.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Setup:**
Consider a cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, containing an internal gauge pressure $P$. Cut the cylinder longitudinally along a diametrical plane. The internal pressure pushes outward on the cut surface; the two halves are held together by the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting in the circumferential direction over the cut area.

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium:**
The **outward force** acting on one half-cylinder is the pressure times the projected area:

$$F_{\text{out}} = P \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Restoring Force:**
The **resisting force** is the hoop stress acting on the two cut walls, each of area $t \cdot L$:

$$F_{\text{in}} = \sigma_h \cdot (2 \cdot t \cdot L) = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{out}} &= F_{\text{in}} \\
P \cdot D \cdot L &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Longitudinal (Axial) Stress Derivation:**
Now consider the same cylinder cut by a transverse plane. The pressure acts on the end-cap area, and the axial stress $\sigma_l$ resists on the annular ring of area $\pi \cdot D \cdot t$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{axial}} &= P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \\
F_{\text{resist}} &= \sigma_l \cdot (\pi \cdot D \cdot t) \\
P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 &= \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot t \\
\sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — The Critical Ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{PD / (2t)}{PD / (4t)} = 2 \quad \therefore \quad \sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$$

> This is why **longitudinal (axial) cracks** along a pipe wall are the most common failure mode in pressure vessels — the hoop direction carries **twice** the stress.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrostatic Test Pressure for Pressure Vessels (ASME Section VIII)

**Given:**
- MAWP = $P_m$ (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)
- Test-pressure factor (ASME) = 1.3
- Material yield strength at test temperature = $S_{yt}$
- Material yield strength at design temperature = $S_{yd}$

**Step 1 — Compute the nominal test pressure:**

$$P_{t,\text{nom}} = 1.3 \times P_m$$

**Step 2 — Compute the induced hoop stress at this test pressure:**

$$\sigma_{h,t} = \frac{P_{t,\text{nom}} \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the allowable test stress:**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} = 0.9 \times S_{yt}$$

**Step 4 — If $\sigma_{h,t} > \sigma_{\text{allow,test}}$, apply the temperature-adjustment reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{t,\text{adj}} &= 1.3 \cdot P_m \cdot \frac{S_{yt}}{S_{yd}} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{safety}} \\
\text{Equivalently:}\quad P_{t,\text{final}} &= 0.9 \cdot S_{yt} \cdot \frac{2t}{D}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel has an **internal diameter $D = 1500$ mm** and a **wall thickness $t = 25$ mm**. The **MAWP** is **2.0 MPa** and the material is **SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel** with yield strength $S_y = 260$ MPa. Compute:

(a) The **hydrostatic test pressure** as per ASME Section VIII.
(b) The **induced hoop stress** at the test pressure.
(c) Verify whether the test pressure is permissible (i.e., hoop stress ≤ $0.9 S_y$).
(d) If over-stressed, compute the **limited test pressure**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Nominal test pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{t,\text{nom}} &= 1.3 \times \text{MAWP} \\
P_{t,\text{nom}} &= 1.3 \times 2.0 \\
P_{t,\text{nom}} &= 2.6 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Induced hoop stress at $P_{t,\text{nom}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{P_{t,\text{nom}} \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{2.6 \times 1500}{2 \times 25} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{3900}{50} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= 78.0 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Permissibility check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} &= 0.9 \times S_y \\
\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} &= 0.9 \times 260 \\
\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} &= 234.0 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $78.0\;\text{MPa} \;\leq\; 234.0\;\text{MPa}$, the vessel **passes the test-pressure check**. No reduction is required.

**Part (d) — Limited test pressure (theoretical upper bound):**

The maximum pressure the vessel could safely be tested at, without exceeding $0.9 S_y$, is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{t,\max} &= 0.9 \cdot S_y \cdot \frac{2t}{D} \\
P_{t,\max} &= 0.9 \times 260 \times \frac{2 \times 25}{1500} \\
P_{t,\max} &= 234 \times 0.0333\overline{3} \\
P_{t,\max} &= 7.80 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

So although the vessel's **calculated nominal** test pressure is 2.6 MPa, it could technically be tested up to 7.80 MPa before yielding — meaning the 1.3×MAWP rule is **not** the binding constraint for this vessel; the **material's yield strength** is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This vessel is **massively over-designed** for the given MAWP. In practice, the **nominal** $1.3 \times \text{MAWP}$ test pressure is used, not the theoretical $P_{t,\max}$, because the code intends the test to be a **screening** of manufacturing defects — not an exploration of the material's ultimate limit.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Hydro vs Pneumatic Test Selection

| Decision Criterion | Choose Hydrostatic | Choose Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| Equipment can be filled with water without damage | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Equipment will be damaged by residual moisture | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Stored energy must be minimized for safety | ✅ Yes (low energy) | ❌ No (high energy) |
| Test pressure > 1.3 × MAWP causes yield | Either (with limited $P_t$) | Often chosen |
| Equipment is very large (e.g., long pipeline) | ✅ Yes (water easy to source) | ❌ No (gas compression costly) |
| Test must detect very small leaks | ❌ No (water is viscous) | ✅ Yes (gas escapes visibly) |
| Hazard class of fluid is flammable | ✅ Yes (water, non-flammable) | ❌ No (air leaks → ignition risk) |

## 3.5 Standard Hold Period Calculation (Optional, Exam-Oriented)

Many codes specify a hold time proportional to vessel wall thickness:

$$t_{\text{hold}} = \frac{t_{\text{wall}}\;\text{(in mm)}}{3} \;\text{minutes (minimum 10 min)}$$

**Example:** For $t = 25$ mm → $t_{\text{hold}} = 25 / 3 \approx 8.33$ min → round up to the code minimum of **10 minutes**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Hydro Test Execution (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Step 0: Isolate equipment from live system and depressurize"] --> A1["Step 1: Pre-test inspection of supports, foundations, and temporary blinds"]
    A1 --> A2["Step 2: Fill vessel slowly with clean water from the low point"]
    A2 --> A3["Step 3: Vent all trapped air from high points until a continuous water stream flows"]
    A3 --> A4["Step 4: Connect the test pump and calibrated pressure gauge to the designated test connection"]
    A4 --> A5["Step 5: Raise pressure in gradual increments to 50 percent of test pressure"]
    A5 --> A6["Step 6: Inspect for leaks, deformation, and abnormal noise"]
    A6 --> A7["Step 7: Continue to 100 percent of test pressure and hold for the prescribed time"]
    A7 --> A8{"Step 8: Did pressure hold steady and no visible leak observed?"}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9["Step 9: Issue Hydro Test Certificate; bleed pressure gradually to zero"]
    A8 -- No --> A10["Step 10: Isolate, depressurize, drain, mark defects, and route to repair"]
    A10 --> A2
    A9 --> A11["Step 11: Dry internal surfaces if required; reinstate blinds removed; hand over to commissioning"]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Test Pressure Determination Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["Start: Equipment ID, Code, and Design Inputs Available"] --> N1["Determine MAWP or Design Pressure from nameplate or U-1 form"]
    N1 --> N2["Apply Code-Mandated Multiplier: 1.3 for Vessels or 1.5 for Piping"]
    N2 --> N3["Compute Nominal Test Pressure Pt_nominal"]
    N3 --> N4["Compute Hoop Stress at Pt_nominal using sigma = P D divided by 2 t"]
    N4 --> N5{"Is Hoop Stress less than or equal to 0.9 times Yield Strength?"}
    N5 -- Yes --> N6["Adopt Pt_nominal as final test pressure; proceed to test"]
    N5 -- No --> N7["Compute Limited Test Pressure Pt_limited using 0.9 Sy 2t divided by D"]
    N7 --> N8["Adopt the LOWER of Pt_nominal and Pt_limited as final test pressure"]
    N8 --> N6
    N6 --> N9["End: Final Test Pressure Issued for Field Use"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison — Hydrostatic vs Pneumatic Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HYDRO_BLOCK["Hydrostatic Test Setup"]
        H1["Clean Water Source"] --> H2["Suction Strainer"]
        H2 --> H3["Test Pump: centrifugal or plunger type"]
        H3 --> H4["Pressure Relief Valve set to test pressure plus 5 percent"]
        H4 --> H5["Calibrated Pressure Gauge at the high point"]
        H5 --> H6["Equipment Under Test filled with water and vented"]
    end
    subgraph PNEU_BLOCK["Pneumatic Test Setup"]
        P1["Compressed Air or Nitrogen Bank"] --> P2["Pressure Regulator and Safety Rupture Disc"]
        P2 --> P3["Compressor or Booster Pump"]

        P4 --> P5["Equipment Under Test with all vents open then closed for pressurization"]
    end
    H6 --> CMP["Common Output: Pass or Fail Decision based on Pressure Decay and Visual Inspection"]
    P5 --> CMP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two side-by-side loops show the *complementary* setup between hydro and pneumatic testing. In KTU examination, students may be asked to **label each block** with its function — a 3-mark question is a very common form.

## 4.4 Schematic — Stress Distribution in a Pressurized Cylinder Wall

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Internal Pressure P acts uniformly on the inner wall surface"] --> S2["The wall develops TWO principal stresses: Hoop sigma_h and Longitudinal sigma_l"]
    S2 --> S3["sigma_h is computed as P D divided by 2 t: largest in magnitude"]
    S2 --> S4["sigma_l is computed as P D divided by 4 t: half of sigma_h"]
    S3 --> S5["Hydro Test Pressure is sized so that sigma_h at Pt remains within 0.9 Sy"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["Result: Weld seams oriented perpendicular to the hoop direction are the highest-risk locations during the test"]
```
