# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define hydrostatic testing. List any four engineering applications where it is mandatory. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

Hydrostatic testing is a non-destructive pressure test in which a pressure-retaining component is filled with a nearly incompressible liquid (usually water) and pressurized to a level above its normal operating pressure to verify its strength and leak-tightness.

**Four mandatory applications:**

1. Newly manufactured pressure vessels before commissioning (per ASME Section VIII).
2. Cross-country oil and gas pipelines before they are put into hydrocarbon service.
3. Fire-protection sprinkler and hydrant systems before they are charged.
4. Post-repair and periodic re-qualification of boilers, heat exchangers, and storage tanks.

*(Full marks: 1 for definition + 4 × 0.5 for valid applications.)*

### Q2. State two reasons why water is preferred over air as a pressurizing medium in hydrostatic testing. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Safety due to low stored energy:** Water is essentially incompressible, so the elastic energy stored in it at a given pressure is approximately **1/100th** of the energy stored in compressed air. If the component fails, the energy release is non-explosive.
2. **Non-flammable and non-toxic:** Water does not support combustion and does not present an inhalation or asphyxiation hazard, which simplifies area classification and PPE requirements during the test.

*(Full marks: 1.5 per valid reason.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel subjected to internal pressure. Show that the hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **1.2 m** and a wall thickness of **20 mm**. Its MAWP is **1.5 MPa** and the material yield strength is **250 MPa**. Compute (i) the ASME hydrostatic test pressure, (ii) the induced hoop stress at the test pressure, and (iii) verify whether the test pressure is permissible. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Consider a thin-walled cylinder of internal diameter $D$, length $L$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal gauge pressure $P$.

**Step 2 — Hoop Stress Derivation:**
Cut the cylinder longitudinally through a diametrical plane. The internal pressure pushes outward on the projected area $D \cdot L$ with force $F = P \cdot D \cdot L$. This is resisted by hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting over the two cut walls, each of area $t \cdot L$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{pressure}} &= F_{\text{stress}} \\
P \cdot D \cdot L &= 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \\
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \quad \text{...(i)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Stress Derivation:**
Now cut the cylinder transversely. The pressure acts on the end-cap area $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ and is resisted by axial stress $\sigma_l$ on the wall annulus $\pi \cdot D \cdot t$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 &= \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot t \\
\sigma_l &= \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} \quad \text{...(ii)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — The Ratio:**

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{PD/(2t)}{PD/(4t)} = 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l \;\;\blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 1.2\;\text{m} = 1200\;\text{mm}$, $t = 20\;\text{mm}$, MAWP $= 1.5\;\text{MPa}$, $S_y = 250\;\text{MPa}$.

**Part (i) — Hydrostatic Test Pressure (ASME Section VIII):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= 1.3 \times \text{MAWP} \\
P_t &= 1.3 \times 1.5 \\
P_t &= 1.95\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii) — Induced Hoop Stress at Test Pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{P_t \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{1.95 \times 1200}{2 \times 20} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= \frac{2340}{40} \\
\sigma_{h,t} &= 58.5\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iii) — Permissibility Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{allow,test}} &= 0.9 \times S_y = 0.9 \times 250 = 225\;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $58.5\;\text{MPa} < 225\;\text{MPa}$, the test pressure is **safe and permissible**. The vessel passes.

> **Allocation:** Part (a) = 7 marks (1 for setup + 2 for hoop + 2 for longitudinal + 2 for ratio). Part (b) = 7 marks (1.5 + 2.5 + 3 distributed across the three sub-parts).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with a neat schematic the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting a hydrostatic test on a newly fabricated pressure vessel. List **five safety precautions** to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Compare hydrostatic testing and pneumatic testing in terms of **energy stored, safety, equipment suitability, leak detection sensitivity, and code-mandated test pressure multiplier**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Procedure and Safety

**Procedure Steps (each 1 mark):**

1. **Isolation & Blinding:** Disconnect the vessel from the live piping system using spectacle blinds or slip blinds.
2. **Pre-test Inspection:** Examine supports, anchor bolts, nozzles, and reinforcing pads for visible defects.
3. **Filling & Venting:** Fill the vessel from the lowest point with clean water. Open all high-point vents until a continuous water stream is observed, ensuring complete air removal.
4. **Pressurisation:** Connect a calibrated test pump and pressure gauge. Raise pressure in steps of 10–15 % of test pressure up to 50 %, inspect, then continue to 100 % of test pressure.
5. **Hold & Inspect:** Hold at test pressure for the code-specified hold time (typically 10–30 min). Inspect all welds, flanges, and joints for leakage, sweating, or deformation.
6. **Depressurise & Document:** Bleed pressure slowly. Drain the vessel, dry if required, and issue the **Hydro Test Certificate** signed by the competent authority.

**Five Safety Precautions (each 0.4 mark):**

1. Barricade the test zone with a minimum **10 m exclusion radius**.
2. Use **calibrated pressure gauges** with valid calibration certificates.
3. Set a **pressure relief valve** at 105 % of test pressure on the test pump.
4. Restrict access to **qualified personnel only** (wearing hard hats, safety boots, and eye protection).
5. **Never** perform hydro testing on a vessel with a known defect — repair first, then retest.
6. Ensure **continuous communication** between pump operator and inspector.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Hydrostatic Test | Pneumatic Test |
|---|---|---|
| Energy stored in medium | Very low (water is incompressible) | Very high (gas is compressible) |
| Safety in case of failure | Low hazard; leak only | High hazard; shock wave, fragmentation, possible fire |
| Test-pressure multiplier (vessels) | **1.3 × MAWP** | **1.1 × MAWP** |
| Leak-detection sensitivity | Moderate (water viscosity masks micro-leaks) | Very high (gas escaping is easily heard/seen) |
| Equipment suitability | Vessels, piping, tanks — any system that can be wetted | Equipment sensitive to water, e.g., electrical enclosures, certain instruments |
| Test medium cost | Low (water) | Moderate to high (compressed air / N₂) |
| Code preference (ASME) | **Preferred** if feasible | Allowed only when hydro is not practical |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hydro Testing Problems:**
>
> 1. **Using 1.5 instead of 1.3** (or vice versa) as the test pressure multiplier without stating the applicable code. *Always identify ASME Section VIII (vessels, 1.3) vs ASME B31 (piping, 1.5).*
> 2. **Confusing MAWP with Design Pressure** — they are not the same; MAWP is the **maximum permitted** working pressure at design temperature, derived from the **lowest** of all component design limits.
> 3. **Forgetting the static-head correction** — if the test gauge is below the high point of the vessel, the pressure at the top is reduced by $\rho g h$. For a 10 m vertical column of water, this is about **0.098 MPa** (≈ 1 bar).
> 4. **Not stating units** in numerical answers — the loss is typically 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Confusing $S_y$ (yield) with $S_u$ (ultimate)** — the test-pressure allowable is based on **yield**, not ultimate.
> 6. **Skipping the limited-test-pressure check** when $1.3 \times \text{MAWP}$ is the binding constraint. Examiners award a **dedicated 2-mark sub-question** for this — don't skip it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam):**
>
> - **Definition:** Hydro testing = filling a pressure component with **water** and pressurising to **verify strength and leak-tightness**.
> - **Governing Codes:** ASME Section VIII (Vessels, 1.3×MAWP), ASME B31 (Piping, 1.5×DP), IS 2825 (Indian), API 570 (In-service inspection).
> - **Hoop Stress Formula:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ — **most important equation** of this module.
> - **Longitudinal Stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ — always **half** of hoop stress.
> - **Test-Pressure Allowable Stress:** $0.9 \times S_y$ at test temperature.
> - **Limited Test Pressure:** $P_{t,\text{lim}} = 0.9 \cdot S_y \cdot \dfrac{2t}{D}$ — used when nominal $1.3 \times$MAWP would over-stress.
> - **Why Water?** Incompressible → **low stored energy** → **safer failure mode**.
> - **Standard Hold Time:** typically 10–30 minutes; visual inspection during hold.
> - **Pressure Gauge:** Must be calibrated, located at the high point of the vessel.
> - **Static-Head Correction:** $P_{\text{top}} = P_{\text{gauge}} - \rho g h$ for gauges mounted below the top.
> - **Hydro vs Pneumatic:** Hydro is the **default preferred** method; pneumatic is used only when water would damage the equipment.
> - **Venting is critical:** Trapped air is the **#1 cause of false failures** and explosive rupture during hydro testing.
> - **Documentation:** A **Hydro Test Certificate** (pressure, hold time, inspector signature, gauge calibration reference) is a legal record and must be filed.
> - **Hazard Awareness:** Although hydro is safer than pneumatic, a **10 m exclusion zone**, **rigorous barricading**, and **PPE** are still mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Technical Definition

In the context of industrial process safety, a **valve** is defined as a mechanical device installed in a piping system to **start, stop, regulate, isolate, or divert the flow of process fluids** (liquids, gases, vapors, slurries, or two-phase mixtures) by means of an external or automatic actuating force. Per **ASME B16.34** and **API 6D** standards, valves are the **primary active safety elements** in any hydrocarbon, chemical, or steam-bearing system, because they provide the controlled boundary between the pressurized inventory and the external environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In *Safety in Engineering Industry*, valves are studied not merely as flow-control devices, but as **Loss-of-Containment (LOC) prevention assets**. The syllabus places special emphasis on **Safety Relief Valves (SRVs)**, **Emergency Shutdown Valves (ESDV)**, **Block Valves**, and **Check Valves**, because these directly govern the **Bow-Tie risk barriers** (prevention + mitigation) for overpressure, backflow, and runaway reaction scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Police of the Pipe"

Imagine a busy multi-lane highway intersection: the traffic signal does not destroy the road or the cars; it simply **permits, restricts, or halts the flow** according to a pre-defined safety logic. A valve is the **traffic police officer of an industrial pipeline** — when the system is normal, the valve is *open* (green light); when a hazardous deviation (overpressure, leak, fire) is detected, the valve *closes or opens* to isolate the danger, dump inventory safely, or starve the fire of fuel. A **Safety Relief Valve (SRV)** is the *ambulance on standby* — it does nothing during normal operation, but the instant upstream pressure crosses a set threshold, it opens automatically to vent fluid and protect vessels from catastrophic rupture.

## 1.3 The "Why" of Valves in Process Safety — Five Safety Functions

| # | Safety Function | Consequence Prevented | Example Valve Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Isolation** | Release of inventory to atmosphere during maintenance | Gate, Ball, Plug |
| 2 | **Overpressure Protection** | Vessel/pipe rupture | Safety Relief Valve (SRV) |
| 3 | **Reverse-Flow Prevention** | Backflow into safe header, contamination | Check (Non-Return) Valve |
| 4 | **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** | Escalation of leak/fire to major accident | ESD Ball Valve |
| 5 | **Controlled Venting** | Toxic/flammable gas accumulation | Pressure/Vacuum Relief Valve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics** for valves in safety service:
> - **Cv (US)** or **Kv (Metric)** — flow coefficient, dimensionless capacity index.
> - **Seat Tightness Class** — per **API 598** (Class I to Class VI).
> - **Set Pressure (Pset)** — inlet pressure at which a relief valve opens.
> - **Blowdown** — difference between set pressure and reseat pressure (typically **< 5% of Pset** for modern valves).
> - **Leakage Rate (NACE / FCI 70-2)** — Class I through Class VI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Valves by Motion, Actuation, and Safety Role

Valves may be classified on three independent axes, and the KTU examiner often frames questions around this **multi-axis classification**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Motion of the Closure Element
- **Linear Motion Valves** — closure member moves along a straight line (Gate, Globe, Diaphragm, Pinch).
- **Rotary Motion Valves** — closure member rotates (Ball, Butterfly, Plug).
- **Quarter-Turn vs Multi-Turn** — Rotary valves are *quarter-turn* (90°), offering very **fast closure** — a key reason they are preferred in **ESD** service.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Actuation Method
- **Manual** — handwheel, lever, gear.
- **Automatic / Self-Actuated** — operates purely on process energy (Check valves, SRVs, Pressure Reducing Valves).
- **Actuated (Powered)** — pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, solenoid, or electro-hydraulic.

### 2.1.3 Classification by Safety Function
- **Isolation (DBB/DIB-1, DIB-2)** — Double Block & Bleed configurations.
- **Relief / Pressure Safety** — spring-loaded, balanced-bellows, rupture-disc + SRV combinations.
- **Non-Return** — swing, lift, dual-plate wafer, axial-flow check.

## 2.2 Operating Principle of the Most Safety-Critical Valve: The Spring-Loaded Safety Relief Valve (SRV)

The **Spring-Loaded Pressure Relief Valve** is the *heart* of Module 4's safety content. Its operating cycle is the most likely derivation-style question on the KTU 2024 ESE paper.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Cycle
1. **Normal Condition (P < Pset):** The process pressure acts on the disc area **A**. The downward force (P × A + disc weight) is *exceeded* by the **upward spring force** (Fs = k × x). The disc stays seated — system is leak-tight to **API 527** standard.
2. **Set Pressure Reached (P = Pset):** Upward hydraulic force **equals** spring preload. Disc begins to lift. The point where the disc first lifts is called **"Simmer"** (audible hiss) or **"Blowdown Begin"**.
3. **Opening (P = Pset + Overpressure):** Valve lifts to full stroke; fluid discharges at **rated capacity**. The valve must achieve full lift at **Pset + 3%** for steam/gas, **Pset + 10%** for liquid (per **API 520**).
4. **Blowdown (P decreasing):** As upstream pressure drops, the spring force re-seats the disc at a pressure called **Reseat Pressure (Pr)**. The difference `Pset − Pr` is the **blowdown**.
5. **Leak Tight (P < Pr):** Disc is fully seated; leakage rate must be ≤ Class II of **API 527** (bubbles/min).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations for Valve Sizing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for the most frequently tested valve calculations. The examiner expects students to know which equation to apply for **liquid, gas/vapor, and two-phase** services. The constants, conversion factors, and correction factors below are **non-negotiable** for full marks.

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units / Nomenclature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flow Coefficient — Liquid** | $$C_v = Q \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$ | $Q$ = GPM, $\Delta P$ = psi, $SG$ = specific gravity |
| 2 | **Pressure Drop from Cv (Liquid)** | $$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{C_v}\right)^2 \cdot SG$$ | psi |
| 3 | **Gas/Vapor Capacity Equation** | $$C_v = \dfrac{Q \cdot \sqrt{SG \cdot T}}{(830 \cdot P_1)} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{Z}}$$ | $Q$ = SCFH, $T$ = °R, $P_1$ = psia, $Z$ = compressibility |
| 4 | **Orifice Discharge Coefficient Form** | $$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ | SI units — $A$ in m², $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 5 | **API 520 Sizing — Vapor/Gas** | $$A = \dfrac{W \cdot \sqrt{T \cdot Z \cdot M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_b \cdot K_1 \cdot P_1}$$ | $A$ = in², $W$ = lb/hr, $M$ = MW |
| 6 | **API 520 Sizing — Liquid** | $$A = \dfrac{Q \cdot \sqrt{G}}{C_v \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_b \cdot K_1}$$ | $Q$ = GPM, $G$ = SG at flowing T |
| 7 | **Spring Force Equilibrium** | $$F_s = P \cdot A_d + W_d$$ | $A_d$ = disc area, $W_d$ = disc weight |
| 8 | **Blowdown Percentage** | $$\% \, BD = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100$$ | Typical: 2–5% |
| 9 | **Time to Close (Quarter-Turn)** | $$t_{close} = \dfrac{L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_v}$$ | $L$ = line length, used in **surge analysis** for ESD |
| 10 | **Valve Reynolds Number** | $$N_{Re} = \dfrac{D \cdot v \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \dfrac{124 \cdot Q \cdot SG}{D \cdot \mu}$$ | Determines laminar vs choked flow regime |

### 2.3.1 Correction Factors — Mandatory Vocabulary for Full Marks
- **Kd** — coefficient of discharge (typical **0.975** for R orifice; **0.953** for T).
- **Kc** — combination correction factor (backpressure effect for bellows valves).
- **Kb** — viscosity correction factor (**Reynolds-based**, applicable when $\mu > 100$ cP).
- **K1, K2, K3** — overpressure and superheat corrections for fire-case relief.
- **C** — gas constant depending on specific-heat ratio $\gamma$ (e.g., **356** for $\gamma = 1.4$ at standard conditions).

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

These equations are not academic — they are used daily in the **design of pressure safety systems for hydrocracker reactors, ethylene crackers, LPG bullets, ammonia storage, and steam boiler drums**. The **API 520 / 521** framework is the de-facto global standard mandated by **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM)** and **Kerala Factories Rules** for any facility handling fluids above **150 psig** or storing more than the **threshold quantity (TQ)** of a hazardous chemical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Liquid Flow Coefficient ($C_v$) from First Principles

The flow coefficient $C_v$ is defined as the **number of US gallons per minute of water at 60 °F** that will pass through a valve with a **1 psi pressure drop**. We derive this from Bernoulli's equation with an empirical discharge coefficient.

### Step 1: Apply the steady-state mechanical energy balance across the valve

The isothermal, incompressible Bernoulli equation between upstream (1) and vena-contracta (2) points is:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 + \Delta P_{loss}$$

### Step 2: Neglect the upstream velocity head (large reservoir assumption)

Because the upstream pipe cross-section is much larger than the valve orifice, $v_1 \approx 0$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 + \Delta P_{loss}$$

### Step 3: Introduce the discharge coefficient $C_d$ to lump the irreversible losses

By definition, the **effective** velocity accounting for vena-contracta and friction is:

$$v_{eff} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to volumetric flow rate

Multiplying both sides by orifice area $A$:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute water properties at 60 °F and express in GPM / psi

For water, $SG = 1$, $\rho = 62.4 \text{ lb/ft}^3$, and converting the entire expression so that $Q$ emerges in **GPM** and $\Delta P$ in **psi**, all unit-conversion constants collapse to the number **1** — *by definition*. This is precisely the construction of $C_v$:

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$

### Step 6: Re-arrange to compute pressure drop from a known $C_v$

Inverting Step 5 to find $\Delta P$ when flow and $C_v$ are known:

$$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{C_v}\right)^2 \cdot SG$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Step 6 is what an engineer *actually* uses on the shop floor — a manufacturer publishes a valve's $C_v$ at full opening, and the process engineer wants to know the pressure loss the valve imposes at a given duty flow $Q$.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Spring Force Equation for a Conventional SRV

A **conventional (non-balanced)** safety relief valve has the disc exposed to **backpressure on the bonnet side** as well. The mechanical force balance on the disc at the instant of lift-off is:

$$F_{spring} = (P_{set} \cdot A_d) + (P_{bp} \cdot A_b) + W_d - F_{friction}$$

Where:
- $A_d$ — disc area at the seat (effective discharge area, in²).
- $A_b$ — bonnet area exposed to backpressure.
- $P_{bp}$ — superimposed backpressure (psig).
- $W_d$ — weight of the disc and movable parts (lb).
- $F_{friction}$ — friction in the stem guide.

At **set pressure**, friction is overcome and the disc lifts, so:

$$F_{spring} = P_{set} \cdot A_d + P_{bp} \cdot A_b + W_d$$

### Why this derivation is critical for KTU
It demonstrates **why backpressure destabilizes a conventional SRV** — an increase in $P_{bp}$ directly *increases* the required spring force, hence raises the *true* set pressure. This is the **fundamental justification** for using a **balanced-bellows SRV** in services with variable backpressure (e.g., manifolded relief headers in refineries).

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — A KTU 2024-Style Calculation

**Problem:** A 4-inch Schedule 40 gate valve is installed in a water line at 25 °C. The valve's manufacturer-rated $C_v$ at 100% opening is **480**. If the duty flow is **600 GPM**, calculate the **pressure drop** across the valve. Take $SG_{water} = 0.997$.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation
The correct formula is the *inverted* $C_v$ relation:

$$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{C_v}\right)^2 \cdot SG$$

### Step 2: Substitute the numerical values

$$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{600 \, \text{GPM}}{480}\right)^2 \cdot 0.997$$

### Step 3: Compute the ratio

$$\left(\dfrac{600}{480}\right)^2 = (1.25)^2 = 1.5625$$

### Step 4: Multiply by specific gravity

$$\Delta P = 1.5625 \times 0.997 = 1.5578 \, \text{psi}$$

### Step 5: State the result with two significant figures

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.56 \, \text{psi}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Valve for Safety Service

| Service / Hazard | Recommended Valve | Why It Is the Safe Choice | Failure Mode if Mis-Selected |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual isolation of hydrocarbon line** | Full-bore ball valve (DBB) | Tight shut-off, **bubble-tight (Class VI)**, fast quarter-turn | Gate valve can leak past seat during maintenance → LOC |
| **Overpressure on a steam boiler** | Spring-loaded SRV (ASME-stamped) | Automatic, fail-*open*, certified per **ASME PTC 25** | A check valve cannot relieve pressure → vessel rupture |
| **Pump discharge to prevent backflow** | Swing check or axial-flow non-return | Closes on flow reversal, prevents water-hammer reflux | No check → pump reversal, motor burn-out, flange leak |
| **LPG bullet relief (fire case)** | Balanced-bellows SRV + rupture disc | Variable backpressure tolerance, instant full lift | Conventional SRV chatters or fails to open at design Pset |
| **Toxic gas cylinder venting** | Diaphragm valve with **PTFE liner** | Zero stem leakage to atmosphere | Globe valve → fugitive emissions → chronic exposure |
| **Fire-water main isolation** | Lugged butterfly valve | Quick-close, low pressure drop, bidirectional dead-end service | Ball valve may be oversized & cost-prohibitive for 24"+ lines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The matrix above is a *cheat-code* for KTU 2024 module questions. When asked "which valve would you recommend for X hazardous service?", justify the choice by linking (a) the **specific hazard**, (b) the **valve's design strength**, and (c) the **applicable standard** (API/ASME/NACE).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Industrial Valves for Safety Service

The following Mermaid diagram presents the taxonomy of valves most relevant to **Process Safety Management (PSM)** as studied in this module.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[INDUSTRIAL VALVES] --> catB[Motion-Based Classification]
    rootA --> catC[Actuation-Based Classification]
    rootA --> catD[Safety-Role Classification]
    catB --> linE[LINEAR MOTION VALVES]
    catB --> rotF[ROTARY MOTION VALVES]
    catC --> manG[MANUAL VALVES]
    catC --> actH[POWER ACTUATED VALVES]
    catC --> selfI[SELF ACTUATED VALVES]
    catD --> isoJ[ISOLATION VALVES]
    catD --> relK[RELIEF AND SAFETY VALVES]
    catD --> nrvL[NON RETURN CHECK VALVES]
    catD --> esdM[EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN VALVES]
    linE --> e1[Gate Valve]
    linE --> e2[Globe Valve]
    linE --> e3[Diaphragm Valve]
    rotF --> f1[Ball Valve]
    rotF --> f2[Butterfly Valve]
    rotF --> f3[Plug Valve]
    manG --> g1[Handwheel Operated]
    manG --> g2[Gear Operated]
    actH --> h1[Pneumatic Actuator]
    actH --> h2[Hydraulic Actuator]
    actH --> h3[Electric Actuator]
    selfI --> i1[Check Valve]
    selfI --> i2[Safety Relief Valve]
    selfI --> i3[Pressure Reducing Valve]
    isoJ --> j1[Double Block and Bleed]
    relK --> k1[Spring Loaded SRV]
    relK --> k2[Balanced Bellows SRV]
    relK --> k3[Rupture Disc]
    nrvL --> l1[Swing Check]
    nrvL --> l2[Lift Check]
    nrvL --> l3[Dual Plate Wafer Check]
    esdM --> m1[ESD Ball Valve]
    esdM --> m2[ESD Butterfly Valve]
```

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Safety Instrumented Valve Loop (SIS)

The schematic below illustrates how a **Safety Relief Valve (SRV)**, **Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESDV)**, and **Check Valve** integrate as the three principal barriers in a typical **Bow-Tie** overpressure scenario for a pressurized vessel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Process Inlet Header] --> isolB[Manual Isolation Ball Valve DBB]
    isolB --> ctrlC[Flow Control Valve FCV]
    ctrlC --> vesselD[Pressurized Process Vessel]
    vesselD --> invE[Inlet Check Valve NRV]
    invE --> psigF[Pressure Safety Valve PSV SRV]
    psigF --> ventG[Atmospheric Vent Safe Location]
    psigF -.Backup.-> discH[Bursting Disc Combo]
    vesselD --> presI[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    presI --> logicJ[SIS Logic Solver SIL 2 or SIL 3]
    logicJ --> esdK[Emergency Shutdown Valve ESDV]
    esdK -.Closes on.-> ctrlC
    logicJ --> blkL[Block Valve Remote]
    blkL -.Closes on.-> isolB
    manualM[Operator Field] -.Manual Override.-> logicJ
    fdetN[Fire Detector Heat] --> logicJ
    gdetO[Gas Detector Toxic] --> logicJ
```

### 4.2.1 Reading the Block Architecture
- The **left-to-right** flow represents the *prevention* barriers of the Bow-Tie.
- The **dotted (`.->`)** lines represent the *mitigation* path activated **only** when the pressure transmitter detects deviation, **OR** when the fire / gas detector trips the SIS.
- The **SIS Logic Solver** is the brain — it must be **SIL-rated** per **IEC 61511**, and it commands both the ESDV and remote block valve simultaneously to bring the system to a safe state within the **Safety Integrity Time (SIT)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 ESE** pattern: Part A (3 marks, no choice) and Part B (14 marks, internal choice with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Model answers are *valuation-key standard* — concise, structured, and standards-tagged.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a "gate valve" and a "globe valve" in terms of construction, flow characteristic, and typical safety-service application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Attribute | Gate Valve | Globe Valve |
|---|---|---|
| **Closure member** | Wedge / gate that moves perpendicular to flow | Disc / plug that moves parallel to flow, against a seat |
| **Flow direction** | Bidirectional, **straight-through**, low pressure drop | Unidirectional, **Z-shaped** path, high pressure drop |
| **Flow characteristic** | On/off only; **NOT** for throttling (seat erosion) | Excellent **linear throttling** characteristic |
| **Safety application** | **Full-bore isolation** (DBB) in hydrocarbon lines | **Flow regulation** in steam, cooling water, instrument air |

*(1 mark each point — examiner will award 3 if all four rows are crisp and standards are mentioned.)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "set pressure", "blowdown", and "overpressure" as applicable to a spring-loaded safety relief valve, with reference to API 520.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Set Pressure ($P_{set}$):** The inlet gauge pressure at which the SRV begins to open (i.e., disc lift-off). It is the **primary setpoint** of the safety device. *(1 mark)*
- **Blowdown:** The difference between $P_{set}$ and the **reseat pressure** ($P_{reseat}$), usually expressed as a percentage of $P_{set}$. Per **API 520**, blowdown is typically **≤ 5% for modern valves** to minimize product loss while avoiding chattering. *(1 mark)*
- **Overpressure:** The pressure rise *above* $P_{set}$ during discharge. Per **API 520**, it is limited to **3% for compressible fluids** and **10% for liquids** at the valve's rated capacity. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4 Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **spring-loaded safety relief valve (SRV)**. Highlight the role of the disc, spring, nozzle, and bonnet.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (4 marks)**
- **Body & Nozzle:** The valve body is bolted to the pressurized vessel/nozzle. The **nozzle** is the pressure-bearing throat against which the disc seats. The effective flow area is calculated at the nozzle throat using **API 520** orifice designations (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L).
- **Disc & Holder:** The disc is held against the nozzle by the **spring** (helical compression type, calibrated by shim adjustment). The disc holder guides the disc perpendicular to the seat.
- **Spring & Bonnet:** The spring is housed inside the **bonnet**, which is open to atmosphere (conventional type) or sealed by a **bellows** (balanced type). The bonnet also encloses the **adjusting screw** and **lift lever**.
- **Lifting Lever (Optional):** A manual lever to test the valve's free operation without over-pressurizing the system.

**2. Working (3 marks)**
- **Normal state:** Upward hydraulic force $P \cdot A_d$ < downward spring force $F_s$ → disc seated, **leak-tight** per **API 527**.
- **At $P = P_{set}$:** Force balance is reached; disc lifts; first audible discharge = **"simmer"**.
- **At $P = P_{set}$ + Overpressure:** Full lift achieved; rated capacity discharged.
- **Reseat:** As upstream pressure falls, spring re-seats disc at $P_{reseat} = P_{set} - $ blowdown.

> **Diagram cue for examiner:** Even a hand-drawn sketch showing the labelled cross-section (body, nozzle, disc, spring, bonnet, spindle, adjusting screw, lifting lever) earns full marks. A Mermaid/textual schematic is acceptable in answer scripts.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A hydrocarbon storage bullet has a design pressure of 4.5 bar (g) and is protected by a single spring-loaded SRV. Calculate the minimum required effective discharge area $A$ for a fire case involving saturated LPG vapour. Use the API 520 vapour sizing equation with the following data:**
- **W** (required mass flow) = 12,000 kg/hr
- **T** (vapour temperature) = 320 K
- **M** (molecular weight of LPG) = 50
- **Z** (compressibility) = 0.9
- **$k$** (specific heat ratio) = 1.15  ⟹  **C = 340**
- **$K_d$** = 0.975, **$K_c$** = 1.0, **$K_b$** = 1.0, **$K_1$** = 1.0
- **$P_1$** (set pressure + allowable overpressure) = 4.5 × 1.1 = 4.95 bar (g) = **5.95 bar (a)** ≈ **86.2 psia** (1 bar = 14.504 psi)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the API 520 sizing equation:**

$$A = \frac{W \sqrt{T \cdot Z \cdot M}}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_b \cdot K_1 \cdot P_1}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $P_1$ to psia:**

$$P_1 = 4.95 \, \text{bar (g)} + 1.01325 \, \text{bar (a)} = 5.96325 \, \text{bar (a)} \approx 86.49 \, \text{psia}$$

**Step 3 — Convert temperature to Rankine (°R):**

$$T = 320 \, \text{K} \times 1.8 = 576 \, °R$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$A = \frac{12{,}000 \cdot \sqrt{576 \cdot 0.9 \cdot 50}}{340 \cdot 0.975 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 86.49}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\sqrt{576 \times 0.9 \times 50} = \sqrt{25{,}920} = 161.0$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 12{,}000 \times 161.0 = 1{,}932{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Compute the denominator:**

$$340 \times 0.975 \times 86.49 = 340 \times 84.33 = 28{,}672$$

**Step 7 — Final value of A:**

$$A = \frac{1{,}932{,}000}{28{,}672} \approx 67.4 \, \text{in}^2$$

**Step 8 — Round up and select the API orifice:**

The nearest standard API 520 orifice designation with area $\geq 67.4$ in² is the **"L" orifice (78.85 in²)** or larger. *(1 mark awarded for orifice selection.)*

$$\boxed{A_{design} \approx 67.4 \, \text{in}^2, \text{ choose an L-orifice or larger.}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4 Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with examples the five major safety functions performed by valves in a process industry. How does a "Double Block and Bleed" (DBB) configuration achieve positive isolation?**

**Model Solution:**

**The Five Safety Functions (5 × 1 mark):**
1. **Isolation** — manual or remote block valve to separate equipment from live inventory during maintenance (e.g., gate valve upstream of a heat exchanger).
2. **Overpressure Protection** — SRVs and rupture discs prevent vessel rupture when upstream pressure exceeds design limits (e.g., PSV on a steam boiler).
3. **Reverse-Flow Prevention** — check valves (swing, lift, dual-plate) prevent backflow that could damage pumps, contaminate headers, or cause water hammer.
4. **Emergency Shutdown** — ESDVs are SIL-rated ball or butterfly valves that close within seconds on a process trip, isolating the hazardous section.
5. **Controlled Venting / Purging** — pressure/vacuum relief valves on atmospheric storage tanks manage breathing losses and prevent tank implosion.

**Double Block and Bleed (DBB) — Positive Isolation (2 marks):**
A **DBB** uses **two independent block valves in series** with a **bleed valve between them**.
- Both blocks are **closed**.
- The **bleed is opened** to verify isolation: if the bleed shows *zero* pressure, the upstream block is proven leak-tight.
- Per **API 6D** and **OSHA 1910.119**, DBB is the **gold standard** for *positive mechanical isolation* before personnel enter a line — it provides **two independent barriers** plus a **verifiable test port**, which is the engineering equivalent of a "belt and suspenders" approach to safety.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A centrifugal pump handling seawater at 30 °C delivers 1500 GPM through a 6-inch Schedule 40 steel line. A swing-check valve is installed on the discharge to prevent backflow. If the valve is rated at $C_v = 720$, calculate the pressure drop across the valve. Take $SG_{seawater} = 1.025$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{Q}{C_v}\right)^2 \cdot SG$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\Delta P = \left(\dfrac{1500}{720}\right)^2 \cdot 1.025$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\left(\dfrac{1500}{720}\right)^2 = (2.0833)^2 = 4.340$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by specific gravity:**

$$\Delta P = 4.340 \times 1.025 = 4.448 \, \text{psi}$$

**Step 5 — Round and report:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 4.45 \, \text{psi}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Comment (1 mark for interpretation):**
A swing-check valve exhibiting **< 5 psi** pressure drop at full flow is within normal design limits for a 6" line; if $\Delta P$ exceeded **10 psi**, it would indicate that the disc is partially stuck open (a common *failure mode*) and the valve should be inspected for hinge-pin wear and seat erosion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing API 520 vapour and liquid equations.** A common error is using the liquid equation $A = Q\sqrt{G}/(C_v K_d K_c K_b K_1)$ for a gas service. Always check whether $W$ is in **lb/hr (gas)** or $Q$ is in **GPM (liquid)** *first*.
> 2. **Unit mismatches in $C_v$ calculation.** $Q$ must be in GPM, $\Delta P$ in psi, and $T$ in **Rankine (°R)** — not Celsius or Kelvin. One missed unit conversion costs a full 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting backpressure in SRV force balance.** A "conventional" SRV set-pressure is *not* independent of backpressure. Examiners love to test this; mention **balanced-bellows** as the correct solution.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why" in isolation questions.** A bald statement like "we use a gate valve" gets 1 mark. Linking it to *flow characteristic, leakage class, and standard* (API 598 Class VI) fetches the full 7.
> 5. **No sketch on SRV construction questions.** Even a labelled block diagram is *mandatory* per the rubric — examiners are instructed to deduct up to 2 marks if the sketch is missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this checklist in the final 24 hours before the KTU ESE. Each bullet is a **guaranteed potential question trigger**.

- 🔹 A **valve** is the primary *active* safety device in any pressure-containing system — the first line of defense in the **Bow-Tie prevention** barrier.
- 🔹 Valves are classified along three axes: **Motion** (linear vs rotary), **Actuation** (manual / powered / self-actuated), and **Safety Role** (isolation / relief / NRV / ESD).
- 🔹 The **$C_v$** of a valve is the GPM of water at 60 °F passing through with 1 psi drop; for a given fluid, $\Delta P = (Q/C_v)^2 \cdot SG$.
- 🔹 The **API 520** vapour sizing equation uses **$W$ (lb/hr)**, $T$ in **°R**, $M$, $Z$, and the four correction factors $K_d, K_c, K_b, K_1$.
- 🔹 The **API 520** liquid sizing equation uses **$Q$ (GPM)** and the **$C_v$** approach.
- 🔹 **Set pressure**, **Blowdown** (≤ 5%), and **Overpressure** (3% gas, 10% liquid) are the three defining parameters of an SRV.
- 🔹 **Conventional SRV** is *backpressure-sensitive*; **Balanced-bellows SRV** is the correct choice for variable backpressure service (e.g., manifolded headers).
- 🔹 A **Double Block and Bleed (DBB)** configuration is the gold standard for **positive mechanical isolation** before maintenance.
- 🔹 **Gate valves** are for on/off service only; **Globe valves** are for throttling; **Ball and butterfly** valves are quarter-turn and dominate ESD service.
- 🔹 **Check valves** must be installed on pump discharge lines to prevent reverse rotation and water-hammer damage.
- 🔹 The **SIS logic solver** must be **SIL-rated** per **IEC 61511**; ESDV closure time must be within the **Safety Integrity Time (SIT)**.
- 🔹 The four cardinal standards to remember: **API 520/521** (relief sizing), **API 527** (SRV seat tightness), **API 598** (valve inspection & testing), **ASME B16.34** (valve design).
- 🔹 Always link your answer to the **standard** + **the specific hazard prevented** — this is what distinguishes a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler Drums and Headers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Boiler Drums and Headers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Boiler Drums — Formal KTU Definition A **boiler drum** is a large, cylindrical, high-pressure pressure vessel that forms the principal water-steam separation and circulation control unit in a **water-tube boiler**. It is fabricated from heavy-gauge carbon or alloy steel plates, rolled and welded longitudinally with circumferential dished (torispherical or ellipsoidal) end closures. Boiler drums are classified functionally into: 1. **Steam Drum (Upper Drum / Water-Steam Drum):** The top-mounted vessel that receives the boiler-water-tube riser mixture, separates saturated steam from water, and returns the unvaporized water via downcomers. 2. **Mud Drum (Lower Drum / Soot Drum):** The bottom-mounted vessel that collects sediment, sludge, and heavy impurities, and feeds water to the convective heating surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT864 — Module 4):** Boiler drums and headers are categorized as **Class I Pressure Vessels** under the Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR) and must satisfy the design, fabrication, inspection, and hydrostatic test rules of **ASME Section I** and **IBR 1950**. ## 1.2 Boiler Headers — Formal KTU Definition A **header** is a smaller-diameter (typically **150 mm to 600 mm**), thick-walled, cylindrical pressure vessel with welded end closures used to **collect steam from multiple boiler tubes or distribute feedwater to multiple boiler tubes**. Headers operate at the same pressure as the steam drum and are critical stress-risers due to numerous tube-hole penetrations. The three functional header classes are: - **Steam Header:** Collects saturated/superheated steam from banks of generating tubes. - **Water Header (Downcomer Header):** Distributes feedwater to the lower headers of the generating tubes. - **Combustion / Soot Header (in some designs):** Collects water from the soot blowers. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building Plumbing" View Think of a high-rise apartment building: - The **Steam Drum** is like the **overhead water tank** on the roof. It receives a chaotic mixture (boiling water + steam from risers), lets the steam rise out (to turbines), and returns the heavy water downward (via downcomers). - The **Mud Drum** is like the **basement sump pit**. It catches the heavy sludge and grit, protecting the rest of the system. - The **Headers** are like the **floor-level distribution manifolds** — vertical pipes with multiple horizontal take-offs (the boiler tubes are the lateral pipes feeding each apartment/radiator). > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for safety:** A failure of any of these components releases an enormous inventory of high-temperature, high-pressure steam — a **Boiler Explosion** event — which is one of the most catastrophic industrial accidents in engineering history. Hence KTU places this topic under **Safety in Engineering Industry**. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Materials | Component | Typical Parameter | Standard Value (Industrial Practice) | |---|---|---| | Steam Drum Diameter | Outer Diameter | **0.9 m – 1.8 m** | | Steam Drum Length | Longitudinal Length | **8 m – 25 m** | | Shell Thickness (Drum) | Plate Thickness | **50 mm – 175 mm** | | Header Outer Diameter | Diameter Range | **150 mm – 600 mm** | | Header Wall Thickness | Plate Thickness | **25 mm – 90 mm** | | Material — Drums | Carbon / C-Mo / Cr-Mo Steel | **SA-302 Gr.B, SA-387 Gr.22** | | Material — Headers | Alloy Steel | **SA-335 P11, P22, P91** | | Design Pressure (Drums) | Operating Pressure | **10 MPa – 25 MPa (sub-critical)** | | Hydrotest Pressure | 1.5 × Design | **Per IBR / ASME Sec. I** | | Design Temperature | Metal Skin | **300 °C – 545 °C** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory fabrication standard:** All boiler drums and headers in India must comply with the **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)**, 1950, enforced by the **Central Boilers Board (CBB)** under the Boiler Operation Act. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Functions of the Steam Drum — The 5 Critical Roles 1. **Steam–Water Separation:** Cyclone separators, steam scrubbers, and dryer chevrons in the top internals remove entrained water droplets from the steam (target moisture: **< 0.1 %** for turbine admission). 2. **Cyclic Surge Buffer:** Accommodates rapid load swings (e.g., a 500 MW unit may swing 50 MW/min). The large water inventory dampens pressure transients. 3. **Chemical Treatment Reservoir:** Continuous blowdown and chemical dosing (phosphate, hydrazine) maintain **pH 8.5 – 9.5** and oxygen < **0.007 mg/L**. 4. **Solid Particle Settling:** Heavy particulates (iron oxide scale, hardness salts) settle and are removed via **continuous and bottom blowdown**. 5. **Downcomer Inlet Header:** Receives recirculated water from downcomers and distributes it back into the riser circuits. ## 2.2 Functions of the Mud Drum 1. **Sludge Collection:** Receives heavy sediments from downcomers and the lower water-wall headers. 2. **Inspection Access:** Manholes and handholes permit internal cleaning and NDT inspection. 3. **Lower Header for Water Walls:** Often serves as the common lower water-wall header in single-drum, bent-tube ("D"-type) boilers. 4. **Blowdown Outlet:** Periodic bottom blowdown discharges concentrated sludge. ## 2.3 Functions of the Headers 1. **Flow Distribution:** Ensures uniform feedwater distribution into the tube bank (parallel flow paths of equal hydraulic resistance). 2. **Flow Collection:** Gathers steam/water from multiple tubes into a single high-pressure stream. 3. **Structural Anchorage:** Mechanically supports the tube banks against thermal expansion. 4. **Inspection / Cleaning Access:** Handholes allow internal descaling and eddy-current testing of tube stubs. ## 2.4 Stress Analysis — The "Why" of Wall Thickness A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel experiences two principal membrane stresses: - **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress** $\sigma_h$ — tends to split the cylinder longitudinally. - **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress** $\sigma_l$ — tends to pull the cylinder apart at the ends. Because $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$, the **hoop direction controls the minimum required wall thickness**. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following equations are high-yield for KTU ESE and Module Tests. Memorize the formula, the boundary condition, and the units.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Limit | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hoop stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p D_i}{2 t}$ | Thin wall: $D_i / t \ge 20$ | MPa | | 2 | Longitudinal stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p D_i}{4 t}$ | Thin wall assumption | MPa | | 3 | Min. shell thickness (ASME I, UG-27) | $t = \dfrac{p R}{S E - 0.6 p}$ | $t \ge t_{corr} + 0$ | mm | | 4 | Equivalent form (using $D_o$) | $t = \dfrac{p D_o}{2 S E + 1.2 p}$ | — | mm | | 5 | Hydrotest stress check | $\sigma_{ht} = \dfrac{p_{ht} D_i}{2 t}$ | $\sigma_{ht} \le 0.9 \, S_y$ | MPa | | 6 | Allowable stress (carbon steel) | $S$ | From ASME II Part D, Table 1A | MPa | | 7 | Joint efficiency | $E$ | Spot-examined: 0.85; Full: 1.00 | — | | 8 | Corrosion allowance | $c$ | Typically **1.6 mm – 3.2 mm** | mm | | 9 | Number of downcomers | $n_d = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{circ}}{\rho_w \, v_d \, A_d}$ | $v_d \le 3.0$ m/s | — | | 10 | Drum residence time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{liq}}{\dot{m}_{circ}}$ | $\tau \ge 60$ s preferred | s | | 11 | Tangent line (TEMA) stress | $\sigma_{TL} = \dfrac{p D_i}{4 t \cos\theta}$ | For saddle support | MPa | | 12 | Thermal expansion (free) | $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ in mm/(mm·°C) | mm | > [!NOTE] > **Symbols legend:** $p$ = internal design pressure (MPa), $D_i$ = inside diameter (mm), $D_o$ = outside diameter (mm), $R$ = inside radius (mm), $t$ = required thickness (mm), $S$ = allowable stress (MPa), $E$ = joint efficiency, $c$ = corrosion allowance, $p_{ht}$ = hydrotest pressure, $S_y$ = yield strength, $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Generation:** Every thermal power station from 60 MW to 1100 MW ultra-supercritical uses boiler drums as the heart of the Rankine cycle. Modern supercritical boilers (>22.1 MPa) replace the drum with a **once-through "Benson" or "Sulzer"** design — but sub-critical and many industrial boilers retain the drum. - **Process Industry:** Refinery and petrochemical heaters, ethylene crackers, and pulp-and-paper recovery boilers all use drums and headers. - **Failure Statistics:** Per the **National Board of Boiler and Pressure Vessel Inspectors (US)**, tube failures in headers account for **~45 %** of all boiler incidents, while drum failures account for **~3 %** (drums are very reliable due to conservative code design). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder ### Step 1: Free-Body Cut Imagine slicing the cylindrical drum longitudinally along its axis and isolating half of the cylinder. The internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected area $D_i \times L$, where $D_i$ is the inner diameter and $L$ is the axial length considered. The balancing force is provided by the two longitudinal wall sections, each of area $t \times L$. Thus the total resisting area is $2 t L$. ### Step 2: Force Equilibrium The pressure force pushing the half-cylinder apart: $$F_p = p \, D_i \, L$$ The resisting force from wall tension (hoop stress $\sigma_h$): $$F_r = 2 \, \sigma_h \, t \, L$$ ### Step 3: Equate the Two For static equilibrium, $F_p = F_r$: $$p \, D_i \, L = 2 \, \sigma_h \, t \, L$$ ### Step 4: Cancel the Common Length $L$ cancels from both sides: $$p \, D_i = 2 \, \sigma_h \, t$$ ### Step 5: Solve for Hoop Stress $$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{p \, D_i}{2 \, t}}$$ This is the famous **Barlow formula** (1833, revised by ASME). The result is valid only for thin-walled vessels where: $$\frac{D_i}{t} \ge 20$$ For modern boiler drums with $D_i/t \approx 10$, the **ASME Code** corrects the formula with a $-0.6p$ term to account for the radial stress contribution. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder ### Step 1: Transverse Free-Body Cut Now slice the cylinder with a transverse (radial) plane. The internal pressure pushes on the end-cap area $\frac{\pi}{4} D_i^{\,2}$. ### Step 2: Force from Pressure The axial force due to pressure on the cap: $$F_p = p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^{\,2}$$ ### Step 3: Resisting Force from Wall The cylindrical wall carries the load over its full circumferential area $\pi D_i t$: $$F_r = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \, D_i \, t$$ ### Step 4: Equate $$p \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_i^{\,2} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \, D_i \, t$$ ### Step 5: Solve for $\sigma_l$ $$p \cdot \frac{D_i}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot t$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{p \, D_i}{4 \, t}}$$ ### Step 6: Key Ratio Dividing the two stress results: $$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p D_i / (2t)}{p D_i / (4t)} = 2$$ Hence, the hoop stress is exactly **twice** the longitudinal stress — this is why cylindrical pressure vessels always split **longitudinally** in over-pressure failures (the famous "split drum" failure mode). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Required Shell Thickness (ASME Section I, UG-27) ### Step 1: Start from the Thin-Wall Hoop Stress Equation For thick vessels, ASME modifies the formula by including the radial stress term, giving the basis: $$\sigma_h = \frac{p \, R}{t - 0.4 \, p \cdot R / S}$$ (An implicit equation; we linearize it for design.) ### Step 2: Set the Hoop Stress Equal to Allowable $S \cdot E$ For design, the maximum allowable hoop stress equals the allowable stress multiplied by the joint efficiency $E$: $$\frac{p \, R}{t - 0.4 \, p \, R / S} = S \cdot E$$ ### Step 3: Multiply Both Sides $$p \, R = S \cdot E \cdot t - 0.4 \, p \, R \, E$$ Wait — re-deriving from the rigorous form (Lamé's equation for thick cylinders, linearized): $$\frac{p \, R}{t} = S \cdot E - 0.6 \, p$$ This gives the design thickness. ### Step 4: Solve for $t$ Rearrange: $$p \, R = S \, E \, t - 0.6 \, p \, t$$ $$p \, R = t \, (S \, E - 0.6 \, p)$$ $$t = \frac{p \, R}{S \, E - 0.6 \, p}$$ ### Step 5: Add Corrosion Allowance and Round Up $$\boxed{t_{design} = \frac{p \, R}{S \, E - 0.6 \, p} + c}$$ > [!NOTE] > In terms of inside diameter $D_i$: since $R = D_i / 2$, the alternative form is: > $$t = \frac{p \, D_i}{2 \, S \, E - 1.2 \, p} + c$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A boiler steam drum has an inside diameter $D_i = 1500$ mm, design pressure $p = 12$ MPa, allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa (at 350 °C for SA-302 Gr.B), full radiographic joint efficiency $E = 1.0$, corrosion allowance $c = 3$ mm. Calculate the minimum required shell thickness. ### Solution **Step 1:** Compute the inside radius. $$R = \frac{D_i}{2} = \frac{1500}{2} = 750 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the ASME formula. $$t = \frac{p \, R}{S \, E - 0.6 \, p} + c$$ **Step 3:** Plug in numerical values. $$t = \frac{12 \times 750}{137 \times 1.0 - 0.6 \times 12} + 3$$ $$t = \frac{9000}{137 - 7.2} + 3$$ $$t = \frac{9000}{129.8} + 3$$ $$t = 69.34 + 3$$ **Step 4:** Round up to the next standard plate thickness (typically 5 mm increments). $$\boxed{t_{design} = 75 \text{ mm}}$$ **Step 5:** Verification — Hoop stress check. $$\sigma_h = \frac{p \, D_i}{2 \, t} = \frac{12 \times 1500}{2 \times 75} = \frac{18000}{150} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$ Since $120 \text{ MPa} < 137 \text{ MPa} = S$, the design is **safe**. ✓ **Step 6:** Hydrotest pressure check (1.5 × design per IBR). $$p_{ht} = 1.5 \times 12 = 18 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_{ht} = \frac{p_{ht} \, D_i}{2 \, t} = \frac{18 \times 1500}{2 \times 75} = 180 \text{ MPa}$$ Compare with the hydrotest limit $0.9 \, S_y$. For SA-302 Gr.B at room temperature, $S_y \approx 345$ MPa, so $0.9 \times 345 = 310.5$ MPa. Since $180 < 310.5$, the **hydrotest is safe**. ✓ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drums vs. Headers | Parameter | Steam Drum | Mud Drum | Steam Header | Water Header | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Location** | Top of boiler | Bottom of boiler | Adjacent to superheater | Below riser tubes | | **Diameter** | 0.9 – 1.8 m | 0.6 – 1.2 m | 150 – 600 mm | 150 – 500 mm | | **Wall thickness** | 50 – 175 mm | 40 – 100 mm | 25 – 90 mm | 20 – 75 mm | | **Internal fluid** | Steam + water | Water + sludge | Steam (saturated/superheated) | Liquid water | | **Hole penetrations** | Few (downcomers) | Few (downcomers) | **Many** (tube stubs) | **Many** (tube stubs) | | **Dominant failure mode** | Overpressure split | Corrosion, cracking | Tube-stub cracking | Tube-stub cracking | | **Inspection focus** | UT, MPI, hydrotest | Internal sludge, UT | Tube-stub weld MPI | Tube-stub weld MPI | | **Code** | IBR + ASME I | IBR + ASME I | ASME I (PG-58) | ASME I | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Two-Drum Boiler ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FeedwaterSystem["FEEDWATER SUPPLY TRAIN"] condPump["Condensate Pump"] --> deaerator["Deaerator (O2 removal)"] deaerator --> ecoBypass["Economiser Inlet Header"] end subgraph BoilerBank["BOILER TUBE BANK - WATER WALL"] ecoBypass --> lowerHeader1["Lower Water-Wall Header"] lowerHeader1 --> riser1["Riser Tubes - Water Wall"] riser1 --> upperHeader1["Upper Water-Wall Header"] end subgraph SteamDrumBlock["STEAM DRUM - UPPER"] upperHeader1 --> steamDrum steamDrum["STEAM DRUM
Steam-Water Separation
Cyclic Buffer
Chemical Reservoir"] steamDrum --> cyclone1["Primary Cyclone Separator"] cyclone1 --> cyclone2["Secondary Cyclone Separator"] cyclone2 --> scrubber["Steam Scrubber & Dryer"] scrubber --> steamOutlet["Saturated Steam to Superheater"] end subgraph RecirculationLoop["RECIRCULATION LOOP - DOWNCOMERS"] steamDrum --> downcomer1["Downcomer No.1 - Unheated Pipe"] steamDrum --> downcomer2["Downcomer No.2 - Unheated Pipe"] steamDrum --> downcomer3["Downcomer No.3 - Unheated Pipe"] downcomer1 --> mudDrum downcomer2 --> mudDrum downcomer3 --> mudDrum end subgraph MudDrumBlock["MUD DRUM - LOWER"] mudDrum["MUD DRUM
Sludge Collection
Blowdown Outlet
Lower Header for Walls"] mudDrum --> blowdown["Bottom Blowdown to Drain"] mudDrum --> lowerHeader1 end subgraph SafetyChain["SAFETY INSTRUMENTATION"] steamDrum --> lv1["Drum Level Transmitter"] steamDrum --> psv1["Pressure Safety Valve Set 110 Percent"] steamDrum --> ft1["Feedwater Flow Sensor"] lv1 --> tripSystem["Boiler Trip Logic - BMS"] psv1 --> tripSystem tripSystem --> tripValve["Main Steam Trip Valve"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Follow the path from `deaerator` → `ecoBypass` → `lowerHeader1` → `riser1` → `upperHeader1` → `steamDrum`. The steam drum separates steam (going right to the superheater) and returns water down the `downcomers` to the `mudDrum`. The mud drum completes the loop back to the risers. The `SafetyChain` branch shows the **Boiler Management System (BMS)** — critical for KTU safety studies. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Header Tube-Stub Penetration Zone ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HeaderShell["HEADER SHELL - CYLINDRICAL"] shell["Cylindrical Shell
Material SA-335 P22
Thickness t"] handhole["Handhole Cover
Bolted for Inspection"] end subgraph PenetrationZone["TUBE-STUB PENETRATION ZONE"] tube1["Tube Stub No.1
Rolled and Welded"] tube2["Tube Stub No.2
Rolled and Welded"] tube3["Tube Stub No.3
Rolled and Welded"] tube4["Tube Stub No.4
Rolled and Welded"] tube5["Tube Stub No.5
Rolled and Welded"] ligament["Ligament Width
CRITICAL - Must Satisfy
ASME PG-58 Rules"] end subgraph StressConcentrators["STRESS CONCENTRATION POINTS"] ligament --> crackInit["Creep Crack Initiation Site"] crackInit --> creepFatigue["Creep-Fatigue Interaction"] creepFatigue --> rupture["Header Rupture Event"] end shell --> tube1 shell --> tube2 shell --> tube3 shell --> tube4 shell --> tube5 shell --> ligament handhole --> shell ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the ligament matters:** The minimum **ligament width** between adjacent tube holes governs the longitudinal ligament efficiency. If the pitch is too small, the ligament becomes the weakest path and **header rupture** can occur — a documented failure mode in vintage power stations. ## 4.3 Schematic Listing — Drum Internals | # | Internal Component | Function | Location in Drum | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Primary Cyclone Separators | Bulk steam-water separation | Top hemisphere | | 2 | Secondary Cyclone Separators | Polishing stage | Top hemisphere | | 3 | Corrugated Scrubber Plates | Drop-size reduction | Above water level | | 4 | Dryer Chevron Bank | Final moisture removal | Top dome | | 5 | Chemical Feed Pipe | Phosphate / Hydrazine dosing | Top | | 6 | Continuous Blowdown Pipe | TDS control | At working water level | | 7 | Surge Line | Equalizes circulation | Center of drum | | 8 | Manways (Front & Rear) | Human access for inspection | Both ends | | 9 | Internal Downcomer Pipes | Feed unheated downcomers | Lower section | | 10 | Sludge Pocket | Concentrated blowdown suction | Bottom |

topics.title: Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pressure Vessel** is a closed container, typically cylindrical or spherical in geometry, designed to hold gases, liquids, vapors, or a combination of these fluids at pressures substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT864 – Safety in Engineering Industry), a pressure vessel is formally defined as any container having an internal or external gauge pressure exceeding **0.5 kgf/cm² (≈ 49 kPa)** under operating conditions, irrespective of its size, and includes components such as shells, heads, nozzles, flanges, and supporting structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT864 – Safety in Engineering Industry):**
> A pressure vessel is a leak-tight pressure containment structure designed to safely withstand the combined effects of internal/external pressure, temperature, corrosion, and other environmental loadings without exceeding allowable stress limits prescribed by national/international design codes.

## 1.2 Classification of Pressure Vessels

Pressure vessels are classified based on multiple engineering criteria:

### A) Based on Wall Thickness Ratio

| Type | t/d Ratio | Theory Applied |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin-Walled Vessels** | t/d ≤ 1/20 (or 1/10 in conservative practice) | Membrane theory (neglects radial stress) |
| **Thick-Walled Vessels** | t/d > 1/20 | Lame's theory (radial stress included) |

### B) Based on Geometry

1. **Cylindrical Vessels** — most common; used for storage of liquids and gases (e.g., LPG bullets, air receivers).
2. **Spherical Vessels** — used for high-pressure storage of gases (e.g., Horton spheres for ammonia).
3. **Conical Vessels** — used as transition pieces in process plants.
4. **Toroidal Vessels** — used in nuclear and aerospace applications.
5. **Hemispherical / Ellipsoidal / Torispherical Heads** — used as end closures.

### C) Based on Service Conditions

- **Steam Boilers** (IBR governed)
- **Storage Vessels** (atmospheric → moderate pressure)
- **Process Vessels** (heat exchangers, reactors)
- **Liquefied Gas Vessels** (cryogenic – LPG, LNG)
- **Nuclear Vessels** (ASME Sec III)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Note:** The course PESFT864 focuses on **thin-walled pressure vessels** because membrane theory is sufficient for the majority of industrial safety design analysis at the undergraduate level.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine blowing up a balloon 🎈. The rubber skin of the balloon stretches in two directions simultaneously:
- **Around the circumference** (this stretch is large — the balloon bulges sideways).
- **Along the length** (this stretch is smaller — the balloon becomes longer).

The rubber film acts like the **wall of a pressure vessel**. Now imagine the same experiment with a pressurized garden hose — the hose tries to split open lengthwise and also tries to "pull apart" at the ends. These two natural tendencies are what engineers call **hoop stress** and **longitudinal stress**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Safety Connection:**
> Every catastrophic pressure vessel failure (Bhopal 1984, Flixborough 1974, Texas City 2005) fundamentally involved one or both of these two stresses exceeding the material's ultimate tensile strength. Understanding them is the cornerstone of safety engineering.

## 1.4 Standard Design Codes & Safety Constants

The following codes govern pressure vessel design worldwide — KTU expects students to know at least one Indian and one international code:

| Standard | Governing Body | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 2825** | Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) | Unfired pressure vessels (India) |
| **ASME Sec VIII Div 1** | American Society of Mechanical Engineers | General industrial vessels (USA/Global) |
| **PD 5500** | British Standards Institution | UK & Commonwealth countries |
| **IBR 1950** | Indian Boiler Regulations | Steam boilers (India) |

**Standard Safety Constants (to memorize):**

- **Factor of Safety (FoS) for Carbon Steel:** 4.0 (ASME), 4.5 (IS 2825)
- **Joint Efficiency (η):** 0.7 (single riveted), 0.85 (double riveted), 1.0 (welded with full radiography)
- **Design Pressure:** 1.05 × Maximum Working Pressure (ASME)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Thin-Walled Theory

The membrane theory (applicable when **t/d ≤ 1/20**) is based on these key postulates:

1. The vessel material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
2. **Plane sections remain plane** after deformation (no bending).
3. **Radial stress σ_r is negligible** compared to membrane stresses (σ_r << σ_h, σ_l).
4. **Stress distribution is uniform** across the wall thickness.
5. The vessel is loaded by a **uniform internal pressure p** only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ignore radial stress?** Because for thin walls, σ_r varies from -p at the inner surface to 0 at the outer surface, with a mean value much smaller than the membrane stresses.

## 2.2 Stresses in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

When a cylindrical vessel is subjected to internal pressure, **two principal membrane stresses** are developed:

### A) Hoop Stress (σ_h) — Circumferential Stress

The hoop stress acts in a direction **tangent to the circumference**. It is the dominant stress and is responsible for the longitudinal splitting failure mode observed in pressure vessels.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t}$$

### B) Longitudinal Stress (σ_l) — Axial Stress

The longitudinal stress acts **along the axis of the cylinder** and resists the "pull-apart" force at the closed ends.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$$

### C) Radial Stress (σ_r)

The radial stress varies across the wall thickness from **−p (inner surface)** to **0 (outer surface)**. Under thin-wall assumption, σ_r is taken as zero.

$$\sigma_r = 0 \quad \text{(at mid-wall thickness)}$$

## 2.3 Stresses in a Thin-Walled Spherical Pressure Vessel

In a spherical vessel, the geometry is symmetric in all directions, so the two membrane stresses are equal:

$$\sigma_h = \sigma_l = \sigma_s = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** For the same diameter, pressure, and wall thickness, a **spherical vessel carries only HALF the hoop stress** of a cylindrical vessel. This is why spherical pressure vessels (e.g., LPG Horton spheres) are preferred for high-pressure gas storage.

## 2.4 Principal Stresses & Stress Ordering

For cylindrical vessels, the stress ordering (using the convention that tensile stresses are positive) is:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_h \quad (\text{Major principal stress})$$
$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_l \quad (\text{Intermediate principal stress})$$
$$\sigma_3 = 0 \quad (\text{Minor principal stress — radial ignored})$$

For spherical vessels:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_s \quad \text{(Biaxial equal stresses)}$$
$$\sigma_3 = 0$$

## 2.5 Maximum Shear Stress (For Safety Analysis)

For cylindrical vessels, the maximum shear stress occurs on a plane at **45°** to the axis:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - 0}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{2} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$$

For spherical vessels:

$$\tau_{max} = 0 \quad \text{(under pure internal pressure)}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula needed to solve KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on Pressure Vessels. Memorize these — they constitute ~60% of the marks in any pressure vessel problem.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in cylindrical vessel | MPa or N/mm² |
| 2 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ | Longitudinal stress in cylindrical vessel | MPa or N/mm² |
| 3 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ | Membrane stress in spherical vessel | MPa or N/mm² |
| 4 | $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ | Stress ratio for cylindrical vessels | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ | Maximum shear stress in cylinder | MPa or N/mm² |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow}}$ | Required thickness with joint efficiency | mm |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \eta \sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$ | Thickness per IS 2825 (cylindrical shell) | mm |
| 8 | $p_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_u \cdot t}{d}$ | Burst pressure (no FoS) | MPa |
| 9 | $p_{allow} = \dfrac{p_{burst}}{FoS}$ | Allowable working pressure | MPa |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{h} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(2 - \mu)$ | Hoop strain | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\varepsilon_{l} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$ | Longitudinal strain | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(5 - 4\mu)$ | Volumetric strain (cylinder) | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(3 - 2\mu)$ | Volumetric strain (sphere) | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $T_{perm} = \dfrac{\sigma_h \cdot t}{p}$ | Required wall thickness (transposed) | mm |

> **Symbols used:** $p$ = internal pressure, $d$ = internal diameter, $t$ = wall thickness, $\eta$ = joint efficiency, $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress, $E$ = Young's modulus, $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are used in:
- **LPG/Ammonia bullet tanks** (calibration of safety valves)
- **Compressed Air Receivers** (ASME Sec VIII design)
- **Steam Boilers** (IBR design verification)
- **Pressure Pipelines** (IS 2825 pipeline codes)
- **Aircraft fuselages and rocket motor casings** (aerospace safety)
- **Nuclear Reactor Pressure Vessels** (ASME Sec III)

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress (σ_h) in a Cylindrical Vessel

### Step 1 — Setup and Free Body Diagram

Consider a cylindrical pressure vessel of:
- Internal diameter = $d$
- Wall thickness = $t$
- Length = $L$
- Internal pressure = $p$

Cut the cylinder along its longitudinal axis and consider **half of the cylinder** as a free body. This exposes the two longitudinal edges of the cut.

### Step 2 — Identify Resisting Force

The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts uniformly over the cross-section of the wall. At each cut edge, the resisting area is:

$$A_{resist} = t \cdot L$$

Since there are **two cut edges**, total resisting area = $2 \cdot t \cdot L$.

Therefore, the resisting force:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Identify Driving Force

The internal pressure $p$ acts on the **projected area** of the half-cylinder on the diametral plane. The projected area is:

$$A_{proj} = d \cdot L$$

Therefore, the driving force:

$$F_{drive} = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

### Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium

For static equilibrium: $\sum F_x = 0$, which gives:

$$F_{resist} = F_{drive}$$

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L = p \cdot d \cdot L$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

Cancel $L$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t = p \cdot d$$

Solving for $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \cdot t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The hoop stress is **directly proportional** to pressure and diameter, and **inversely proportional** to wall thickness. Doubling the diameter doubles the stress; doubling the thickness halves the stress.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Stress (σ_l) in a Cylindrical Vessel

### Step 1 — Setup and Free Body Diagram

Consider the same cylindrical vessel. Now make a **transverse cut** perpendicular to the axis at any section. The free body consists of the **end cap and the contents** on one side of the cut.

### Step 2 — Identify Driving Force

The internal pressure $p$ acts on the entire cross-sectional area of the cylinder (the circular end):

$$A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

Therefore, the driving force trying to push the cap off:

$$F_{drive} = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Resisting Force

The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts over the ring of cylindrical wall material at the cut section. The ring area is:

$$A_{ring} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

Therefore, the resisting force:

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

### Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium

$$\sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

Cancel $\pi \cdot d$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_l \cdot t = p \cdot \dfrac{d}{4}$$

Solving for $\sigma_l$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** $\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$. This means that for an internally pressurized thin cylinder, the **circumferential stress is twice the longitudinal stress**. This is why pressure vessels always fail along a longitudinal split first, never in a circular pattern.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Membrane Stress in a Spherical Vessel

### Step 1 — Setup

Consider a spherical pressure vessel of diameter $d$ and thickness $t$. Because of symmetry, cut the sphere into two equal hemispheres along any great circle.

### Step 2 — Identify Driving Force

Internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected circular area of the great circle:

$$A_{proj} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

$$F_{drive} = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Identify Resisting Force

The membrane stress $\sigma_s$ acts uniformly on the ring of material along the cut. The ring circumference is $\pi \cdot d$ and the thickness is $t$:

$$A_{ring} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

$$F_{resist} = \sigma_s \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t$$

### Step 4 — Equilibrium

$$\sigma_s \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot t = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

$$\sigma_s = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{2 \cdot t} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \cdot t}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Strain Analysis (Hoop Strain)

For a thin-walled cylindrical vessel under internal pressure $p$, using **Generalized Hooke's Law** in 2D:

$$\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot (\sigma_h - \mu \cdot \sigma_l)$$

Substituting $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$ and $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$:

$$\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{1}{E} \cdot \left( \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t} - \mu \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t} \right)$$

$$\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t \cdot E} \cdot (2 - \mu)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(2 - \mu)}$$

Similarly, longitudinal strain:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)}$$

And volumetric strain (cylinder):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_l + \varepsilon_r \approx \varepsilon_h + \varepsilon_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(5 - 4\mu)}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Wall Thickness Required (IS 2825 Method)

For a cylindrical shell under internal pressure, IS 2825 specifies:

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$$

**Derivation Rationale:**

Starting from hoop stress equation:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t} \leq \dfrac{\eta \cdot \sigma_{allow}}{1.2}$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t \geq \dfrac{1.2 \cdot p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow}} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow}/1.2}$$

After rearranging:

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$$

The **1.2 factor** accounts for corrosion allowance and minor fabrication imperfections (10–20% safety margin).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Mapping

| Failure Mode | Governing Stress | Physical Description | KTU Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal Split** | $\sigma_h$ exceeds $\sigma_u$ | Vessel ruptures along a generator line | Most common failure mode (75%) |
| **End Cap Blow-off** | $\sigma_l$ exceeds $\sigma_u$ | Caps detach at the girth weld | Rare (FoS prevents it) |
| **Buckling** | Compressive hoop (external pressure) | Vessel wall wrinkles inward | Sub-atmospheric vessels |
| **Creep Rupture** | Sustained high temperature | Grain boundary separation | Boilers operating > 400°C |
| **Fatigue Cracking** | Cyclic pressure | Crack propagation at welds | Pressure cycling in compressors |
| **Stress Corrosion Cracking** | Combined $\sigma_h$ + corrosive medium | Branched cracking | LPG, ammonia service |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example (Built into derivation context)

**Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel has diameter 1.5 m, wall thickness 12 mm, and internal pressure 2 MPa. Calculate:
(a) Hoop stress
(b) Longitudinal stress
(c) Maximum shear stress

**Solution using derived formulas:**

Given: $d = 1500$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $p = 2$ MPa.

**(a) Hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t} = \dfrac{2 \times 1500}{2 \times 12} = \dfrac{3000}{24} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

**(b) Longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t} = \dfrac{2 \times 1500}{4 \times 12} = \dfrac{3000}{48} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(c) Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{2} = \dfrac{125}{2} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ ✓ (125 = 2 × 62.5) — confirms derivation correctness.
