# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q:** Define a pressure vessel. State the conditions under which a pressure vessel is classified as "thin-walled" under KTU design standards.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A pressure vessel is a closed leak-tight container designed to hold fluids (gases, liquids, vapors) at pressures substantially different from the ambient pressure.

Classification into thin-walled category requires the following:
1. **Geometric Criterion:** The wall thickness to diameter ratio must satisfy $t/d \leq 1/20$ (or 1/10 in conservative practice).
2. **Material Criterion:** The material must be homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
3. **Loading Criterion:** Loading must be predominantly internal pressure with negligible external loading.
4. **Membrane Assumption:** The radial stress must be negligible compared to membrane stresses (i.e., $\sigma_r \ll \sigma_h, \sigma_l$).

When these conditions are met, the simple membrane formulas $\sigma_h = pd/2t$ and $\sigma_l = pd/4t$ are valid.

*(Marks distribution: Definition = 1 mark, 4 conditions with explanation = 2 marks)*

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q:** State the formula for hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel. Why is hoop stress greater than longitudinal stress?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Hoop stress (circumferential):** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$

**Longitudinal stress (axial):** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$

**Reasons why hoop stress is greater:**

1. **Force distribution:** In the hoop direction, the resisting area per unit length is only $2t$ (two walls of thickness $t$ each), while in the longitudinal direction, the resisting area per unit length is $\pi d t$ (the full circumference).
2. **Projected area effect:** The pressure acts on a smaller projected area in the hoop direction ($d \cdot L$) than on the cap area in the longitudinal direction ($\pi d^2/4$).
3. **Geometric outcome:** Combining these two effects gives $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$.

This is why pressure vessels, when they fail, rupture **along a longitudinal seam** rather than at the circumferential girth.

*(Marks distribution: 2 formulas = 1 mark each, reasoning = 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Alternative 1]**

**Q (a)** A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 600 mm and a wall thickness of 10 mm. It is subjected to an internal gauge pressure of 3 MPa. Calculate:
   (i) Hoop stress in the cylinder
   (ii) Longitudinal stress in the cylinder
   (iii) Maximum shear stress in the wall

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Internal diameter, $d = 600$ mm
- Wall thickness, $t = 10$ mm
- Internal pressure, $p = 3$ MPa

**Step 1 — Check thin-wall assumption:**

$$\dfrac{t}{d} = \dfrac{10}{600} = \dfrac{1}{60} \leq \dfrac{1}{20} \quad \checkmark$$

The thin-wall assumption is valid. Membrane theory applies.

**Step 2 — Compute hoop stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t} = \dfrac{3 \times 600}{2 \times 10} = \dfrac{1800}{20}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 90 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute longitudinal stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t} = \dfrac{3 \times 600}{4 \times 10} = \dfrac{1800}{40}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_l = 45 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \dfrac{90 - 45}{2} = \dfrac{45}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 22.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

*(Marks: Step 1 = 1, Hoop = 2, Longitudinal = 2, Shear = 2)*

---

**Q (b)** A spherical pressure vessel of internal diameter 1.2 m is required to store gas at a working pressure of 2.5 MPa. The allowable tensile stress for the material is 130 MPa, and the joint efficiency is 85%. Calculate the required wall thickness using the IS 2825 formula and verify safety using a factor of safety of 4.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Internal diameter, $d = 1200$ mm
- Internal pressure, $p = 2.5$ MPa
- Allowable stress, $\sigma_{allow} = 130$ MPa
- Joint efficiency, $\eta = 0.85$
- FoS = 4

**Step 1 — IS 2825 thickness formula for spherical shell:**

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 \eta \sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$t = \dfrac{2.5 \times 1200}{4 \times 0.85 \times 130 - 1.2 \times 2.5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$4 \times 0.85 \times 130 = 442$$
$$1.2 \times 2.5 = 3$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 442 - 3 = 439$$

**Step 4 — Compute thickness:**

$$t = \dfrac{3000}{439} = 6.834$$

$$\boxed{t_{design} = 7 \text{ mm (rounded up to standard size)}}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using Factor of Safety:**

Ultimate stress: $\sigma_u = 4 \times 130 = 520$ MPa (since FoS is based on allowable stress)

Actual hoop stress in sphere at design thickness:

$$\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 t_{design}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 1200}{4 \times 7} = \dfrac{3000}{28} = 107.14 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_{actual} = 107.14 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 130 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Design is SAFE}$$

**Burst pressure check:**

$$p_{burst} = \dfrac{4 \sigma_u t_{design}}{d} = \dfrac{4 \times 520 \times 7}{1200} = 12.13 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{Actual FoS} = \dfrac{p_{burst}}{p_{working}} = \dfrac{12.13}{2.5} = 4.85 > 4 \quad \checkmark$$

*(Marks: Formula = 1, Computation = 2, Final answer = 1, Safety verification = 3)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative 2 — Internal Choice]**

**Q (a)** Compare cylindrical and spherical pressure vessels. State the membrane stress equations for both and explain why spherical vessels are preferred for high-pressure gas storage. What is the volumetric strain in a cylindrical vessel of diameter 800 mm, thickness 8 mm, internal pressure 4 MPa? Take $E = 200$ GPa and $\mu = 0.3$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Cylindrical Vessel | Spherical Vessel |
|---|---|---|
| Membrane stress | $\sigma_h = pd/2t$, $\sigma_l = pd/4t$ | $\sigma_s = pd/4t$ (both directions) |
| Stress ratio | $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ | $\sigma_h = \sigma_l$ |
| Max shear stress | $pd/4t$ | Zero (under pure internal pressure) |
| Fabrication complexity | Easy to manufacture | Complex (requires skilled welders) |
| Material required | More (for same pressure) | Less (only half of cylinder) |
| Cost | Lower initial | Higher initial, but material-efficient |
| Application | General storage, process | High-pressure gas (LPG, ammonia) |

**Why spherical vessels are preferred for high-pressure gas storage:**
1. For the same $p$, $d$, and $t$, the stress in a spherical vessel is **half** of the hoop stress in a cylindrical vessel.
2. **No stress concentration** at junctions (in pure sphere).
3. **Minimum surface area for given volume** → lower heat loss for cryogenic service.
4. **Isotropic stress state** → no preferred failure direction.

**Volumetric Strain Calculation:**

Given: $d = 800$ mm, $t = 8$ mm, $p = 4$ MPa, $E = 200 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\mu = 0.3$.

Formula for volumetric strain in a cylinder:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4tE}(5 - 4\mu)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $(5 - 4\mu)$:**

$$5 - 4 \times 0.3 = 5 - 1.2 = 3.8$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{4 \times 800}{4 \times 8 \times 200000} \times 3.8$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{3200}{6400000} \times 3.8 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.8$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = 1.9 \times 10^{-3} = 0.19\%}$$

*(Marks: Comparison table = 2, Justification = 2, Volumetric strain derivation = 1, Final answer = 2)*

---

**Q (b)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel is fabricated by fusion welding and is to operate at an internal pressure of 5 MPa. The vessel has an internal diameter of 1000 mm. The material has an ultimate tensile strength of 450 MPa and yield strength of 250 MPa. Using a factor of safety of 4 on ultimate strength and assuming a joint efficiency of 0.85, determine:
   (i) The required wall thickness
   (ii) The bursting pressure
   (iii) Comment on whether thin-wall theory is valid

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $p = 5$ MPa, $d = 1000$ mm, $\sigma_u = 450$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, FoS = 4, $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Allowable stress:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\text{FoS}} = \dfrac{450}{4} = 112.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Required wall thickness (using IS 2825):**

$$t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 \eta \sigma_{allow} - 1.2 p}$$

$$t = \dfrac{5 \times 1000}{2 \times 0.85 \times 112.5 - 1.2 \times 5}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$2 \times 0.85 \times 112.5 = 191.25$$
$$1.2 \times 5 = 6$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 191.25 - 6 = 185.25$$

**Step 4 — Thickness:**

$$t = \dfrac{5000}{185.25} = 26.99 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{t_{design} \approx 27 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Bursting pressure:**

$$p_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_u t}{d} = \dfrac{2 \times 450 \times 27}{1000} = 24.3 \text{ MPa}$$

*(Without safety factor and joint efficiency)*

**Step 6 — Validity of thin-wall theory:**

$$\dfrac{t}{d} = \dfrac{27}{1000} = 0.027 = \dfrac{1}{37}$$

Since $\dfrac{1}{37} \leq \dfrac{1}{20}$ ✓, the thin-wall assumption is **valid**, and the membrane theory can be safely applied.

**Comment:** The vessel design is acceptable, though the wall is fairly thick. For vessels with $t/d > 1/20$, Lame's thick-walled theory would be needed to capture the radial stress distribution accurately.

*(Marks: Allowable stress = 1, Thickness formula + computation = 3, Burst pressure = 1.5, Thin-wall check = 1.5)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often mix units — e.g., using diameter in meters while thickness is in mm, or pressure in bar instead of MPa. **Always convert all quantities to consistent SI units (N, mm, MPa) before substitution.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.2 factor in IS 2825 (–2 marks):** The IS 2825 formula is $t = pd/(2\eta\sigma_{allow} - 1.2p)$, NOT $t = pd/(2\eta\sigma_{allow})$. The 1.2p term in the denominator represents the corrosion allowance and must be subtracted.
> 
> 3. **Incorrect Joint Efficiency (–1 mark):** When the problem specifies "fusion welded" and "radiographed," $\eta = 1.0$. When "spot radiographed," $\eta = 0.85$. When "not radiographed," $\eta = 0.70$. Read the problem carefully!
> 
> 4. **Skipping Thin-Wall Validation (–1 mark):** KTU requires explicit verification that $t/d \leq 1/20$ BEFORE applying membrane formulas. If this check is missing, marks are deducted.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Hoop and Longitudinal Stress (–2 marks):** A very common error is writing $\sigma_h = pd/4t$ and $\sigma_l = pd/2t$. The hoop stress has the smaller denominator (2t), giving the larger value. Memorize the mnemonic: **"Hoop = Twice Longitudinal"**.
> 
> 6. **Not stating assumptions (–1 mark):** Membrane theory requires homogeneous, isotropic material; plane sections remain plane; radial stress negligible. These should be stated at the start of any derivation.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🎯 High-Density Revision Checklist

#### ✅ Definition & Classification
- [ ] Pressure vessel = leak-tight container for fluids at pressures different from ambient
- [ ] Thin-walled criterion: $t/d \leq 1/20$ (membrane theory)
- [ ] Thick-walled criterion: $t/d > 1/20$ (Lame's theory)
- [ ] Geometric types: Cylindrical, Spherical, Conical, Toroidal

#### ✅ Core Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [ ] $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2t}$ (cylinder hoop)
- [ ] $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ (cylinder longitudinal)
- [ ] $\sigma_s = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ (sphere membrane)
- [ ] $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ (cylinder stress ratio)
- [ ] $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4t}$ (cylinder max shear)
- [ ] $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2\eta\sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$ (IS 2825 cylinder)
- [ ] $t = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4\eta\sigma_{allow} - 1.2p}$ (IS 2825 sphere)

#### ✅ Strain Equations
- [ ] $\varepsilon_h = \dfrac{pd}{4tE}(2 - \mu)$
- [ ] $\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{pd}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$
- [ ] $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{pd}{4tE}(5 - 4\mu)$ (cylinder)
- [ ] $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{pd}{4tE}(3 - 2\mu)$ (sphere)

#### ✅ Engineering Safety Knowledge
- [ ] Always verify thin-wall assumption before applying formulas
- [ ] Hoop stress governs vessel failure (longitudinal split)
- [ ] Spherical vessels carry only half the hoop stress of cylinders
- [ ] FoS typically 4 (ASME) or 4.5 (IS 2825) for carbon steel
- [ ] Design pressure = 1.05 × Maximum working pressure
- [ ] Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.5 × design pressure
- [ ] Joint efficiency depends on welding + radiography quality

#### ✅ KTU Standard Codes
- [ ] **IS 2825** (India – unfired vessels)
- [ ] **ASME Sec VIII Div 1** (International)
- [ ] **IBR 1950** (India – boilers)
- [ ] **PD 5500** (UK)

#### ✅ Common Failure Modes
- [ ] Longitudinal rupture (governed by σ_h)
- [ ] End cap blow-off (governed by σ_l)
- [ ] Buckling (external pressure)
- [ ] Creep (high temperature)
- [ ] Fatigue (cyclic pressure)
- [ ] Stress corrosion cracking

#### ✅ 3-Mark Quick-Fire Points
- [ ] Why hoop = 2 × longitudinal? (Resisting area difference)
- [ ] Why spherical is material-efficient? (Half stress for same t)
- [ ] Why ignore radial stress? (Small compared to membrane stresses)
- [ ] When does thin-wall fail? (t/d > 1/20, use Lame's theory)
- [ ] What governs longitudinal split? (σ_h vs σ_u)

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, you are expected to:
> 1. State all given data clearly (1 mark)
> 2. Verify thin-wall assumption with explicit $t/d$ calculation (1 mark)
> 3. State the relevant formula before substitution (1 mark)
> 4. Show full step-by-step numerical substitution (3–4 marks)
> 5. Provide final answer with correct units (1 mark)
> 6. Discuss engineering significance, safety check, or failure mode (3–4 marks)
> 7. Comment on validity and assumptions (1–2 marks)
> 
> **Practice 5–7 numerical problems** from the KTU pattern above before the exam. The most common question type asks for hoop stress + longitudinal stress + thickness design all in one problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Leak Test
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Leak Test

# Module 4 — Air Leak Test

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Air Leak Test** is a quantitative and qualitative Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method used to identify, locate, and measure the rate of leakage of gas (typically compressed air) from a pressurized component, vessel, piping system, or enclosure. The test works on the principle of **pressurizing the test specimen with air** to a predetermined test pressure and then monitoring either the **decay of internal pressure** with respect to time or **observing the formation of bubbles** when the pressurized part is immersed in water, or detecting escaping air using a soap solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT864, Module 4):**
> *Air Leak Test* is a pneumatic pressure test conducted to verify the integrity of a sealed system. The test specimen is pressurized with clean, dry, oil-free compressed air (or an air–water mixture) to a specified pressure, isolated from the supply, and the pressure drop is measured over a fixed interval. A measurable pressure loss is interpreted as the presence of a leak path, the location of which can be traced by soap-solution bubbling, sonic detection, or submersion methods.

### 1.2 Classification within NDT Family

Air Leak Test belongs to the family of **Leak Testing (LT)** methods codified under **ASME Section V, Article 10** and **ASME Section V, Article 11**. The relevant classification chart is:

$$\text{Leak Testing} \;\supset\; \begin{cases} \text{Air Under Water (Bubble Test)} \\ \text{Pressure Change (Decay) Test} \\ \text{Sniffer Probe Test} \\ \text{Bubble Emission (Soap Solution) Test} \\ \text{Mass Flow / Volumetric Test} \end{cases}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pin-Hole Balloon

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Pin-Hole Balloon Analogy"**
>
> Imagine you blow up a balloon and pinch the nozzle tight. If the balloon slowly shrinks, you can be sure there is a tiny hole. Now, place this same balloon in a bucket of water — you will see a thin stream of bubbles escaping from the pin-hole. **That is exactly how an Air Under Water Leak Test works on industrial pressure vessels and pipelines.** The "balloon" is your pressure vessel, the "air inside" is the compressed gas, the "bucket of water" is the immersion tank, and the "bubble stream" is the leak path made visible.

### 1.4 Fundamental Physical Principles

The test is governed by four fundamental physics laws that the student must internalize:

| # | Law / Principle | Role in Air Leak Test |
|---|-----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Boyle's Law** ($P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ at constant T) | Determines pressure drop for a fixed-volume leak |
| 2 | **Ideal Gas Law** ($PV = nRT$) | Predicts pressure-temperature coupled variations |
| 3 | **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation** | Predicts laminar leak rate through a known orifice geometry |
| 4 | **Adiabatic / Isothermal Expansion** | Determines whether leak flow is sonic, sub-sonic, or molecular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Pressure for KTU Problems:**
> The universally accepted standard atmospheric pressure is **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \; \text{bar}$**.
> The conventional test pressure for low-pressure vessels is **1.1 × Design Pressure**, and for high-pressure components may go up to **1.5 × Design Pressure** (per ASME BPVC Section VIII).

### 1.5 Why Air (and Not Water)?

Air is the preferred test medium over water for the following safety-relevant reasons:

1. **Low Viscosity:** Viscosity of air $\mu_{air} \approx 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·s}$ versus water $\mu_{water} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·s}$ — so air can escape through **far smaller defects** that water cannot penetrate. Sensitivity is therefore higher.
2. **Low Compressibility Effect at Low Pressures:** Allows simple Boyle's Law interpretation.
3. **Dry Medium:** Prevents corrosion of internal surfaces during the test.
4. **No Hydrostatic Head Complications:** Pressure is essentially uniform throughout the test piece.
5. **Safety on Impact:** If a pressurized air vessel ruptures, stored energy is roughly **1/1000th** of an equivalent hydrostatically-filled vessel, drastically reducing blast radius — critical in the *Safety in Engineering Industry* context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Side-Note (KTU High-Yield):**
> Compressed air at **$7\;\text{bar}$** has approximately the same rupture energy as an equivalent mass of TNT only when the volume is enormous. Nevertheless, **never** subject a vessel to a pneumatic test pressure exceeding the *MAWP* (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) marked on its nameplate.

### 1.6 Typical Sensitivity Range

The detectability of leaks is quantified by the **Standard Leak Rate** $L$ in units of $\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ or $\text{atm·cc/s}$:

$$L_{standard} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t}$$

| Method | Typical Lower Detection Limit |
|--------|------------------------------|
| Soap-Solution Bubble | $10^{-3}\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Air Under Water | $10^{-4}\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Pressure Decay | $10^{-2}\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Helium Mass Spectrometer | $10^{-9}\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Ultrasonic Sniffer | $10^{-4}\;\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical Air Leak Test Procedures

#### A. Air-Under-Water (Bubble Immersion) Test

**Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic):**

1. The component is fully filled with compressed air at the test pressure.
2. The component is then **submerged in a water tank** of sufficient size.
3. An observer scans the submerged surface for bubble formation.
4. The **location, size, and frequency** of bubbles indicate the leak site and qualitative leak rate.

**Why It Works:** Air escaping from a defect creates a pressure differential across the orifice; the expanding gas forms visible bubbles in the surrounding water. The size of the bubble stream correlates with leak rate.

**Engineering Utility:** Used for **non-porous metallic components** like valve castings, pump bodies, small pressure vessels, and welded fabrications during QC inspection.

#### B. Pressure Decay (Pneumatic Pressure Drop) Test

**Procedure Logic:**

1. Pressurize the test specimen to $P_{test}$ using clean dry air.
2. **Isolate** the specimen from the supply (close inlet valve, disconnect hose).
3. Allow the system to **stabilize** (equalize temperature, settle diaphragms).
4. Record the pressure $P_1$ at time $t_1$ (after stabilization).
5. Wait for a specific dwell time $T$ (typically 1–30 minutes).
6. Record the pressure $P_2$ at time $t_2 = t_1 + T$.
7. Compute the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.
8. Compare $\Delta P$ against the maximum allowable drop $\Delta P_{allow}$ defined by the governing code or client specification.

**Why It Works:** If the test piece is leak-tight, the trapped mass of air remains constant; hence, $P$ and $T$ related by the gas law should not change. Any observed drop directly implies mass loss through leaks.

**Engineering Utility:** Used for **sealed assemblies** (gas-tight enclosures, refrigerator evaporators, automotive fuel rails, hydraulic accumulators).

#### C. Soap-Solution (Bubble Emission) Test

**Procedure Logic:**

1. Pressurize the part to $P_{test}$.
2. Apply a foaming soap solution (commercial "Snoop" liquid or equivalent) over all joints, welds, and suspect regions.
3. Observe bubble formation.

**Why It Works:** Surface tension of the soap film is broken by escaping gas, creating a visible bubble that grows until buoyancy lifts it off.

**Engineering Utility:** Used when the part is too large to submerge (pipelines, pressure vessels on site, large heat exchangers).

### 2.2 The Pressure Decay Equation — The Heart of the Method

For a **rigid** (non-expanding) test specimen of internal volume $V$ held at approximately constant temperature, the **leak rate** in terms of pressure decay is:

$$L = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V}{\Delta t}$$

where:
- $L$ = leak rate, $[\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}]$ (or $[\text{bar·L/s}]$)
- $P_1$ = initial stabilized pressure $[\text{Pa}]$
- $P_2$ = final pressure after dwell $[\text{Pa}]$
- $V$ = internal volume of the test piece $[\text{m}^3]$
- $\Delta t$ = dwell time $[\text{s}]$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Air Leak Test (Module 4)**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2) \cdot V}{\Delta t}$ | Leak rate from pressure decay | $\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{allow} = \dfrac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{V}$ | Maximum allowable pressure drop | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (Boyle, $T$ = const) | Pressure–volume relation for fixed mass | $\text{Pa, m}^3$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Combined gas law correction for temperature drift | $\text{Pa, m}^3, \text{K}$ |
| 5 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Conversion gauge ↔ absolute | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | $L = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{air}}}$ | Orifice leak model (sub-sonic) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $L = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R T} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | Choked (sonic) orifice flow | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{leak} = \rho_{air} L$ | Mass leak rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 9 | $Q_{leak} = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{128 \mu L_{ch}} \Delta P$ | Hagen–Poiseuille leak (laminar capillary) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $E_{stored} = P \cdot V \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_{atm}}\right)$ | Pneumatic stored energy (rupture hazard) | $\text{J}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Reminder (Table-Safe):**
> All absolute-value notations $\vert x \vert$ in equations are written as `\vert x\vert` to preserve the markdown table. Substitution must use **absolute** pressure for gas-law calculations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component Tested | Reason for Air Leak Test |
|----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Oil & Gas | Cross-country pipelines, wellhead X-mas trees | Prevent hydrocarbon release |
| Power Plants | Boiler tubes, condenser shells | Vacuum & pressure integrity |
| Automotive | Air-conditioner evaporators, fuel tanks | Emission control (SHED test) |
| Aerospace | Cabin-pressurization bulkheads | Catastrophic-failure prevention |
| Pharmaceutical | Sterile isolators, glove boxes | Containment of active compounds |
| Cryogenics | LN2/LHe storage vessels | Minimize boil-off losses |
| Process Plants | Heat-exchanger channel heads | Avoid cross-contamination of streams |

### 2.5 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why pressurize with air, not water, on a leak test?** Air is more sensitive due to low viscosity → escapes through smaller defects.
- **Why stabilize temperature before recording $P_1$?** Compression heating raises the air temperature; cooling reduces pressure artificially, mimicking a leak. KTU examiners *will* test this!
- **Why use dry, oil-free air?** Moisture corrodes internals; oil contaminates processes and can give false bubble indications.
- **Why calculate stored energy $E = PV \ln(P/P_{atm})$?** To assess the explosion hazard if the vessel fails during test — directly relevant to *Safety in Engineering Industry*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pressure Decay to Leak Rate

We start from the definition of **leak rate** as the volume of gas (at the prevailing upstream conditions) escaping per unit time. For a fixed-volume test specimen:

$$L = \frac{\Delta(PV)}{\Delta t}$$

Because the test specimen is rigid, $V$ is constant. Therefore, $V$ comes out of the difference:

$$L = V \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t}$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & L = \frac{1}{\Delta t}\left(P_1 V - P_2 V\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & L = \frac{V}{\Delta t}\left(P_1 - P_2\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \Delta P = P_1 - P_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; L = \frac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **primary KTU equation** for the Pressure Decay Test. The student must commit it to memory.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Allowable Pressure Drop from Specified Leak Rate

In industrial specifications, the customer often quotes a *maximum allowable leak rate* $L_{max}$ (because the component will be used with expensive or hazardous gas in service). We must derive the corresponding **pressure drop that the testing instrument must be able to resolve**.

Rearranging the previous result:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{V}$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & L_{max} = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) V}{\Delta t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & L_{max} \cdot \Delta t = (P_1 - P_2) V \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & P_1 - P_2 = \frac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{V} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \boxed{\Delta P_{allow} = \frac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{V}} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:**
> For a *given* $L_{max}$, the test becomes **more sensitive** (i.e., a smaller $\Delta P$ becomes significant) when:
> - $V$ is **large** (larger vessels have more stored mass to lose),
> - $\Delta t$ is **long** (longer dwell amplifies any small leak).
> Hence, KTU problems frequently state "5-minute dwell" or "30-minute dwell" — these are not arbitrary; they are chosen to make $\Delta P_{allow}$ fall in the readable range of the pressure gauge.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature-Corrected Pressure Decay

A common KTU trick question is: *"The pressure dropped, but the temperature also fell by 3 K during the test. Is there really a leak?"*

Using the combined gas law for the fixed mass of trapped air:

$$\frac{P_1 V}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V}{T_2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$$

Solving for $P_2$:

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \Delta P_{temp} = P_1 - P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_1} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test the Reading:** If the *observed* $\Delta P$ exceeds the *temperature-corrected* $\Delta P_{temp}$, the difference is due to an actual leak. This is a frequent KTU part-(a) and part-(b) sub-question.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Orifice Leak Model (Sub-Sonic Case)

For a small defect modelled as a thin-plate orifice of area $A$ with discharge coefficient $C_d$:

$$L = C_d A \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{air}}}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation from Bernoulli:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \frac{1}{2}\rho u^2 = \Delta P \quad\Rightarrow\quad u = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \dot{V} = u \cdot A = A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & L = C_d \cdot \dot{V} = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{air}}} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Range:** This equation is valid when the upstream-to-downstream pressure ratio $P_2/P_1 > 0.528$ (i.e., critical ratio for air, $\gamma = 1.4$). For higher ratios, the flow becomes **sonic (choked)** and Equation 7 from the formula sheet must be used.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement (modelled on KTU 2022 University Exam):**
A stainless-steel accumulator of internal volume $V = 8.0 \;\text{L}$ is pressurised with dry air to a gauge pressure of $P_{1,g} = 6.0\;\text{bar}$ at temperature $T_1 = 300\;\text{K}$. After a 10-minute dwell, the pressure is found to be $P_{2,g} = 5.92\;\text{bar}$ and the temperature has dropped to $T_2 = 296\;\text{K}$. Atmospheric pressure is $1.013\;\text{bar}$.

**Find:**
(a) The absolute leak rate $L$ in $\text{bar·L/s}$.
(b) The temperature-corrected leak rate, and determine whether the observed drop is due to a real leak or to cooling alone.

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert to absolute pressures:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_{1,g} + P_{atm} = 6.00 + 1.013 = 7.013 \;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
P_2 &= P_{2,g} + P_{atm} = 5.92 + 1.013 = 6.933 \;\text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Apply the simple pressure-decay equation:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= P_1 - P_2 = 7.013 - 6.933 = 0.080\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
\Delta t &= 10\;\text{min} = 600\;\text{s} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{8.0\;\text{L} \times 0.080\;\text{bar}}{600\;\text{s}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{L &= 1.067 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{bar·L/s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute the temperature-only pressure change:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2,expected} &= P_1 \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 7.013 \times \frac{296}{300} = 6.919\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{temp} &= 7.013 - 6.919 = 0.094\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
L_{temp} &= \frac{8.0 \times 0.094}{600} = 1.253 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{bar·L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Interpret:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{observed} &= 0.080\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{cooling-only} &= 0.094\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{leak} &= \Delta P_{observed} - \Delta P_{cooling-only} = -0.014\;\text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the **observed drop is *less* than what cooling alone would produce**, the component is **leak-tight**; the apparent pressure fall is fully explained by the temperature drop.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagrams use only **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `nodeA`, `tank1`) and **plain uppercase labels** inside double-quoted node text — no bold, no italics, no HTML tables — to satisfy the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Pressure Decay Test Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[COMPRESSED AIR SUPPLY 7 BAR] -->|REGULATOR| nodeB[FILTER DRIER AND OIL REMOVER]
    nodeB -->|FLEX HOSE| nodeC[INLET VALVE V1]
    nodeC -->|CHARGING LINE| nodeD[TEST SPECIMEN VESSEL]
    nodeD -->|INTERNAL VOLUME V| nodeE[PRESSURE TRANSDUCER PT]
    nodeE -->|4 TO 20 MILLIAMP SIGNAL| nodeF[DATA LOGGER OR DIGITAL MANOMETER]
    nodeD -->|THERMOCOUPLE TC| nodeG[TEMPERATURE INDICATOR TI]
    nodeG -->|SIGNAL| nodeF
    nodeF -->|COMPUTE DELTA P OVER DELTA T| nodeH[LEAK RATE L CALCULATION MODULE]
    nodeH -->|COMPARE WITH L MAX SPEC| nodeI{LEAK OR NO LEAK DECISION}
    nodeI -->|L LESS THAN L MAX| nodeJ[PASS RELEASE COMPONENT]
    nodeI -->|L GREATER THAN L MAX| nodeK[FAIL MARK DEFECT LOCATION REPAIR]
    nodeK -->|SOAP SOLUTION OR ULTRASONIC SNIFF| nodeL[PINPOINT LEAK SITE]
    nodeL -->|REWELD OR REPLUG| nodeM[RE TEST CYCLE]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Air-Under-Water Bubble Test Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AIR COMPRESSOR 0 TO 10 BAR] -->|PRESSURE REGULATOR PR| nodeB[INLET MANIFOLD]
    nodeB -->|QUICK CONNECT| nodeC[TEST COMPONENT FILLED WITH AIR]
    nodeC -->|HOIST OR CRANE| nodeD[IMMERSION WATER TANK]
    nodeD -->|SUBMERGE COMPLETELY| nodeE[OBSERVATION WINDOW REGION]
    nodeE -->|VISUAL INSPECTION| nodeF[OPERATOR IDENTIFIES BUBBLE PLUME]
    nodeF -->|MARK WITH CHALK OR WAX PENCIL| nodeG[LEAK LOCATION COORDINATES]
    nodeG -->|RAISE COMPONENT| nodeH[DRYING STATION]
    nodeH -->|REPAIR OR REWORK| nodeI[RE TEST OR SCRAP]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow of the Test Decision Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[START] --> prepA[PREPARE SPECIMEN CLEAN DRY SEAL ALL PORTS]
    prepA --> pressA[PRESSURISE TO P TEST USING REGULATED AIR]
    pressA --> stabA[STABILISE 5 MIN FOR TEMPERATURE EQUILIBRIUM]
    stabA --> recA[RECORD P 1 T 1]
    recA --> dwellA[DWELL FOR DELTA T USUALLY 10 TO 30 MIN]
    dwellA --> recB[RECORD P 2 T 2]
    recB --> compA[COMPUTE OBSERVED DELTA P]
    compA --> compB[COMPUTE TEMPERATURE CORRECTION]
    compB --> compC[COMPUTE TRUE LEAK INDUCED DELTA P]
    compC --> judgeA{DELTA P LEAK LESS THAN DELTA P ALLOW}
    judgeA -->|YES| passA[PASS DOCUMENT CERTIFICATE]
    judgeA -->|NO| failA[FAIL LOCALISE WITH SOAP OR ULTRASONIC]
    failA --> repairA[REPAIR AND RECYCLE TO PRESSURISE]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram of Test Method Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    selA[WHAT IS THE TEST OBJECTIVE] --> selB{IS QUANTITATIVE LEAK RATE NEEDED}
    selB -->|YES| selC[PRESSURE DECAY OR MASS FLOW]
    selB -->|NO| selD[IS OBJECT SMALL ENOUGH TO IMMERSE]
    selD -->|YES| selE[AIR UNDER WATER BUBBLE TEST]
    selD -->|NO| selF[SOAP SOLUTION OR ULTRASONIC SNIFF]
    selC --> selG[RECORD DELTA P OVER DELTA T]
    selE --> selH[VISUALLY LOCATE BUBBLE PLUME]
    selF --> selI[APPLY SOAP AND TRACE BUBBLES]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define "Air Leak Test" and list any two advantages of using air as the test medium over water.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 for definition, 1 + 1 for two advantages):**

*Definition:* Air Leak Test is a non-destructive pneumatic test in which a sealed component is pressurised with compressed air and held for a specified dwell time; the resulting pressure drop (or bubble formation when immersed) is used to detect the presence, location, and rate of leakage.

*Advantages:*
1. **Low viscosity** of air ($1.81 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·s}$) allows detection of much smaller defects than water would reveal.
2. **Low stored rupture energy** in compressed air vessels reduces the blast hazard in case of test failure — a critical safety advantage.

*(Acceptable alternative: dry medium, uniform pressure, no hydrostatic head, simpler instrumentation.)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**State the formula relating pressure drop to leak rate in a Pressure Decay Test, and explain why a temperature-correction step is essential.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental relation is:

$$L = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) V}{\Delta t} = \frac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t}$$

A **temperature-correction step is essential** because the air inside the test piece heats up during compression and cools down during the dwell. Even a leak-tight vessel will exhibit a pressure drop of:

$$\Delta P_{temp} = P_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_1}$$

If this is not corrected, a perfectly sound vessel can be falsely rejected (Type-I error) or, worse, a leaking vessel can be wrongly accepted (Type-II error) — both of which are safety-significant outcomes in the *Safety in Engineering Industry* context.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulation:**
> Each Part B question carries 14 marks. There will be an **internal choice** — the student must answer either *Question A* OR *Question B* in full. Each part-question is sub-divided into (a) carrying **7 marks** and (b) carrying **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Series-II]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Air-Under-Water (Bubble Immersion) Leak Test** procedure. List four safety precautions that must be observed while conducting this test in an industrial workshop.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A pressure vessel of internal volume $V = 12.0\;\text{L}$ is charged with air to an absolute pressure of $P_1 = 8.5\;\text{bar}$ at $T_1 = 305\;\text{K}$. After a 15-minute dwell, the absolute pressure drops to $P_2 = 8.42\;\text{bar}$ and temperature falls to $T_2 = 300\;\text{K}$. Calculate:
(i) The observed leak rate.
(ii) The pressure change that would have occurred due to cooling alone.
(iii) The actual leak rate attributable to a real defect. State your conclusion.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Procedure (4 marks) + Safety Precautions (3 marks):**

*Procedure:*
1. Thoroughly clean and dry the test specimen; seal all openings except the charging port.
2. Connect the charging port to a regulated compressed-air supply through a calibrated pressure gauge and safety relief valve.
3. Slowly pressurise the vessel in **increments of 0.5 bar**, holding at each step to allow stress equalisation, until the specified test pressure $P_{test}$ is reached.
4. Close the inlet valve, disconnect the supply, and allow **temperature stabilisation** (5–10 minutes).
5. Submerge the component completely in a **water-filled inspection tank**, ensuring no air pockets are trapped on the surface.
6. Sweep the entire surface visually (or use a strobe light for better visibility) and mark the location of any bubble plume.
7. **Raise the component, dry, repair, and re-test** if leaks are observed.
8. **Release the pressure slowly** through a vent valve, not by sudden opening.

*Safety Precautions:*
1. **Pressure relief valve** must be installed and set at *not more than* the test pressure + 10 %.
2. The test must be conducted inside a **blast enclosure or behind a safety shield** rated for the stored energy.
3. The operator must wear **PPE**: full-face shield, hearing protection, gloves, and safety shoes.
4. **Bonding and grounding** of the air-line to prevent static charge accumulation; only **oil-free, dry, certified breathing-quality air** is to be used.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

*Step 1 — Convert dwell to seconds:*

$$\Delta t = 15\;\text{min} = 900\;\text{s}$$

*Step 2 — Observed leak rate:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{obs} &= 8.500 - 8.420 = 0.080\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
L_{obs} &= \frac{12.0 \times 0.080}{900} = 1.067 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{bar·L/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Cooling-only pressure change:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2,cooling} &= 8.500 \times \frac{300}{305} = 8.361\;\text{bar} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{temp} &= 8.500 - 8.361 = 0.139\;\text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — True (defect-induced) pressure change:*

$$\Delta P_{leak} = 0.080 - 0.139 = -0.059\;\text{bar}$$

*Step 5 — Conclusion:*

Since the **observed pressure drop is *smaller* than the drop explained by cooling alone**, the component is **leak-tight**. The slight "deficit" of $-0.059\;\text{bar}$ indicates that the vessel may have actually *warmed* slightly during the test (e.g., due to elastic relaxation of the shell) — this is acceptable per ASME Section V. **Pass.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Series-II Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Pressure Decay Leak Test** method. Derive the relationship between leak rate $L$, pressure drop $\Delta P$, volume $V$, and dwell time $\Delta t$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pneumatic system must have a maximum allowable leak rate of $L_{max} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{bar·L/s}$. The system volume is $V = 25\;\text{L}$. The test gauge has a resolution of $0.005\;\text{bar}$. Determine the **minimum dwell time** required so that the smallest resolvable pressure drop is at least equal to the allowable drop.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram (3 marks) + Theory + Derivation (4 marks):**

*Block Diagram (described verbally for the answer book — the student should redraw the Mermaid block from §4.2):*
"Compressed air supply → Filter-drier → Regulator → Inlet valve → Test specimen (with pressure transducer and thermocouple) → Data logger → Computation of $\Delta P$ → Compare with $\Delta P_{allow}$ → Pass/Fail."

*Derivation:*

For a fixed mass of gas trapped in a rigid volume $V$, the leak rate is defined as the rate at which the $P \cdot V$ product changes:

$$L = \frac{\Delta (PV)}{\Delta t}$$

Because $V$ = constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= V \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow\quad \Delta P &= \frac{L \cdot \Delta t}{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the governing equation of the Pressure Decay Test.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

*Step 1 — Identify the key relation:*

$$\Delta P_{allow} = \frac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t}{V}$$

*Step 2 — Determine the required pressure drop:*
The gauge resolution is $0.005\;\text{bar}$, so the **smallest resolvable** pressure drop is $\Delta P_{min} = 0.005\;\text{bar}$. For a valid test, this must equal or exceed the allowable drop:

$$\Delta P_{min} = \frac{L_{max} \cdot \Delta t_{min}}{V}$$

*Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta t_{min}$:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{min} &= \frac{\Delta P_{min} \cdot V}{L_{max}} \\[4pt]
\Delta t_{min} &= \frac{0.005\;\text{bar} \times 25\;\text{L}}{5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{bar·L/s}} \\[4pt]
\Delta t_{min} &= \frac{0.125}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 250\;\text{s} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\Delta t_{min} &\approx 4.17\;\text{minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Practical Recommendation:*
To give a **safety factor of 2**, the test dwell time should be **at least 10 minutes** in practice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing Gauge and Absolute Pressures (–2 marks typical):** Always convert to absolute before applying Boyle's Law. KTU examiners are strict on this.
> 2. **Forgetting Temperature Correction (–2 to –3 marks):** On any decay-test problem, you **must** check whether the temperature also changed. If yes, the combined gas law must be invoked.
> 3. **Reporting Leak Rate in Wrong Units (–1 mark):** Be consistent. Stick to $\text{bar·L/s}$ or $\text{Pa·m}^3/\text{s}$. Do not mix $\text{kPa·m}^3/\text{h}$ with $\text{bar·cc/s}$ in the same solution.
> 4. **Confusing Air-Under-Water with Hydrostatic Test (–2 marks):** Air-Under-Water uses *air* inside the component; Hydrostatic Test uses *water* filling. The bubble emission is from the *inside* of the test piece, not the outside.
> 5. **Omitting PPE in Safety Questions (–1 mark):** A Safety-in-Engineering paper *requires* explicit mention of PPE and blast protection — generic statements are insufficient.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Leak Test (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Air Leak Test is a **pneumatic NDT** method for detecting, locating, and quantifying leaks.
- **Three primary variants:** (i) Air-Under-Water, (ii) Pressure Decay, (iii) Soap-Solution (Bubble Emission).
- **Master Equation:** $L = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{\Delta t}$ — units **must** be consistent.
- **Always use absolute pressure** in gas-law calculations; $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- **Temperature correction** is mandatory: $\Delta P_{temp} = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_1}$.
- **Stored Energy Hazard:** $E = P V \ln(P/P_{atm})$ — relevant to industrial safety.
- **Sensitivity Ranking (best → worst):** Helium MS > Air-Under-Water > Ultrasonic Sniffer > Soap > Pressure Decay > Hydrostatic.
- **Test Medium:** Always use **clean, dry, oil-free air** to prevent corrosion and false indications.
- **Test Pressure:** Typically **1.1 × to 1.5 × Design Pressure**, never exceeding MAWP.
- **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{atm} = 101.325\;\text{kPa} = 1.01325\;\text{bar}$.
- **Codes & Standards:** ASME BPVC Section V Article 10 & 11; ASTM E515; EN 1779.
- **Safety Precautions:** PPE, blast shields, pressure relief valves, bonding/grounding, certified air.
- **Sign Convention:** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 > 0$ for a true leak.
- **Unit Conversions to Memorise:** $1\;\text{bar} = 10^5\;\text{Pa} = 100\;\text{kPa}$.
- **Leak Rate Acceptance Threshold:** Quoted in part spec — must compare $L_{actual}$ with $L_{max}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steam Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steam Testing** is a category of pre-commissioning, commissioning, and integrity verification procedures in which **saturated or superheated steam** is used as the pressurising and/or thermal medium to validate the mechanical strength, leak-tightness, and cleanliness of pressure-containing equipment such as **boilers, pressure vessels, process piping, heat exchangers, turbines, and headers** before they are placed into hazardous service.

In the framework of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PESFT864 – Safety in Engineering Industry**), steam testing is positioned under **Module 4** as a critical *occupational safety and process safety* activity that combines principles of:
- **Pressure Safety** (controlled pressurisation of plant),
- **Thermal Safety** (management of high-temperature media),
- **Mechanical Integrity** (verification of welded joints, flanges, gaskets),
- **Loss Prevention** (mitigation of fire, explosion, and burn injuries during commissioning).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT864 – Module 4):**
> *"Steam testing is a controlled engineering procedure in which steam is introduced into a system at prescribed pressure, temperature, and duration to verify integrity, remove debris, condition refractory, and demonstrate fitness-for-service of pressure equipment, while ensuring personnel and asset safety through permit-to-work, isolation, and protective systems."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **brand-new pressure cooker** before it is shipped from the factory. The manufacturer cannot just fill it with dal and assume it is safe. They must:
1. Seal it completely,
2. Pressurise it with **steam or air** to a pressure **higher** than its working pressure,
3. Hold that pressure for a defined time,
4. Inspect for leaks, deformation, or rupture.

That process *is* steam testing — only scaled up to industrial dimensions where the cooker becomes a **10-tonne reactor** and the steam is supplied by a **40 TPH boiler**. The goal is the same: **prove the vessel can hold pressure safely, with zero compromise to life and property.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle — "Test Beyond Service":**
> Steam tests (especially hydrostatic and pneumatic) are conducted at **1.5 × design pressure** (or as per ASME/IS codes) so that any latent defects in welds, forgings, or castings fail *during the test* — when the system is empty of hazardous process fluids and personnel exposure is controlled.

## 1.3 Classification of Steam Testing Activities

Steam testing, in the KTU 2024 syllabus, is broadly classified into **four functional categories**:

| # | Category | Primary Purpose | Typical Medium State |
|---|----------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Steam Pressure / Leak Test** | Verify mechanical integrity and leak-tightness | Saturated steam |
| 2 | **Steam Blow (Steam Flushing)** | Remove mill scale, rust, weld spatter, debris from new piping | Superheated, high-velocity steam |
| 3 | **Steam Purging / Sterilisation** | Displace air, hydrocarbons, moisture from process lines | Saturated / dry steam |
| 4 | **Steam Soak / Warm-Up** | Thermally condition equipment, dry refractory, equalise stresses | Saturated steam at low pressure |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, remember the **3 D's of Steam Testing**:
> - **D**isplace air (purging)
> - **D**isplace debris (blow/flush)
> - **D**emonstrate integrity (pressure test)

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard test metrics are repeatedly used in steam testing:

- **Specific heat of superheated steam (Cp):** **2.1 kJ/kg·K** (approximate)
- **Latent heat of vaporisation (hfg) at 1 atm:** **2,257 kJ/kg**
- **Steam specific volume at 1 atm, 100 °C:** **1.673 m³/kg**
- **Critical point of water/steam:** **22.064 MPa, 374.15 °C**
- **Standard test pressure multiplier:** **1.5 × design pressure** (hydrostatic) or **1.25 × design pressure** (pneumatic/steam)
- **Standard test hold time:** **30 minutes minimum** for leak tests
- **Acceptable leak rate (typical):** **0.1 % of test pressure drop per hour** at constant temperature
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Basis of Steam Testing

Steam exists in three principal states that dictate its testing behaviour:

1. **Saturated Liquid** — water at its boiling point, contains zero vapour.
2. **Saturated Steam (Wet Steam)** — a two-phase mixture with dryness fraction **x** (0 ≤ x ≤ 1).
3. **Superheated Steam** — steam at temperature above saturation, behaves as a near-ideal gas.

For a given pressure, the saturation temperature is **fixed**. This is the cornerstone of steam testing — by controlling pressure, we control temperature, and vice versa.

**Specific Enthalpy of Wet Steam:**
$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = enthalpy of saturated liquid (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporisation (kJ/kg)
- $x$ = dryness fraction (dimensionless, 0 to 1)

**Specific Volume of Wet Steam:**
$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For most steam testing calculations, the **saturated steam tables** (also called steam tables) are the authoritative source. Memorise the key values at **1 bar, 5 bar, 10 bar, 20 bar, and 50 bar** for board exams.

### 2.1.2 Why Steam? — Engineering Logic

Steam is preferred over compressed air for many integrity tests because of:

- **Self-indicating leaks** — steam condenses visibly, unlike invisible compressed air leaks.
- **Fire safety** — steam suppresses combustion in flammable atmospheres (used as inerting medium in refineries).
- **Thermal realism** — steam testing exposes the equipment to the *actual thermal expansion stresses* it will face in service.
- **Moisture removal** — wet steam purges residual water from pipelines, preventing water hammer.
- **Cost** — steam is readily available in any operating plant with a boiler.

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use compressed air for leak testing of equipment that previously contained flammable/ hydrocarbon service** without gas-freeing and inerting. Residual hydrocarbons + hot surfaces + air = **catastrophic explosion**. Use **steam** or **nitrogen** instead.

## 2.2 Types of Steam Testing — Detailed Operation

### 2.2.1 Steam Pressure (Leak) Test

**Procedure Logic:**
1. Isolate the equipment — close all connecting block valves, install **spectacle blinds** or **spades** for positive isolation.
2. Install calibrated **pressure gauges** at the highest and lowest points.
3. Connect a **steam supply header** through a **pressure-reducing valve (PRV)** and a **safety relief valve** set to **110 % of test pressure**.
4. Slowly admit steam, venting air through high-point vents.
5. Raise pressure in **increments of 10 %** of test pressure, holding each step for **10–15 minutes** for thermal equilibration.
6. Hold final test pressure for **30 minutes minimum** (ASME Section I) or as per code.
7. Inspect all joints, welds, flanges, and valve packings for leaks.
8. Reduce pressure gradually, vent condensate, allow natural cooling — **never open a hot, pressurised vessel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Reference:** Per **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section I (PG-99)** and **Indian Standard IS 2825**, the hydrostatic test pressure is **1.5 × Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**. For **steam** (pneumatic-equivalent) tests, a lower multiplier of **1.25 × MAWP** is used because the stored energy in compressed gas is far higher than in liquid.

### 2.2.2 Steam Blow (Steam Flushing)

**Purpose:** To remove mill scale, rust, weld slag, sand, construction debris, and corrosion products from new piping systems before they enter hydrocarbon service.

**Operational Sequence:**

| Step | Activity | Parameter |
|------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | Pre-blow inspection | All supports, hangers, anchors verified |
| 2 | Install temporary blow-off stack | Discharge to safe atmospheric location |
| 3 | Warm-up the line | Slow steam admission, **< 2 °C/min** thermal gradient |
| 4 | Pressurise to **1/3 design** | Hold 15 min for thermal soak |
| 5 | Pressurise to **2/3 design** | Hold 15 min |
| 6 | Pressurise to **full design** | Hold 30 min |
| 7 | Perform **blow** — vent through stack | Target velocity **100–150 m/s** |
| 8 | Repeat blows until **Target Plate Test** passes | Target plate: ≤ **0.3 mm dent** for clean steam |

> [!TIP]
> **Target Plate Test:** A polished carbon-steel or stainless plate is exposed to the blow stream for a calibrated duration. The depth of impact dents is measured; **≤ 0.3 mm** indicates acceptable cleanliness. This is a **favourite KTU exam question** under Module 4.

### 2.2.3 Steam Purging

**Purpose:** Displace air, hydrocarbon vapours, or moisture from process equipment and lines.

**Method:**
- Introduce steam at **low pressure (1–3 bar)** at the lowest point.
- Vent at the highest point until **condensate temperature reaches 100 °C** and vent steam is **dry and odour-free**.
- Maintain a **positive pressure** to prevent air ingress.

**Rule of Thumb (3-Volume Rule):**
Pass a minimum of **3 system volumes** of steam to achieve complete air displacement.

$$N_{volumes} = \frac{V_{steam\_in}}{V_{system}}$$

### 2.2.4 Steam Soak / Warm-Up

**Purpose:** Thermally condition thick-walled vessels, dry refractory linings, and pre-heat turbines to prevent thermal shock.

**Constraint:** The **thermal gradient** (ΔT across wall thickness) must not exceed **2 °C/min** for carbon steel to avoid brittle fracture.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times MAWP$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | bar / MPa | Pressure vessel testing |
| 2 | $P_{test} = 1.25 \times MAWP$ | Pneumatic / steam test pressure | bar / MPa | Pneumatic equivalence |
| 3 | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | Enthalpy of wet steam | kJ/kg | Energy calculations |
| 4 | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Dryness fraction | dimensionless | Steam quality |
| 5 | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | Specific volume of wet steam | m³/kg | Mass-to-volume conversion |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{(h_2 - h_1)}$ | Steam mass flow from heat duty | kg/s | Boiler sizing |
| 7 | $Q = A \cdot V \cdot \rho$ | Volumetric blow rate | kg/s | Steam blow sizing |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop | Pa | Pipe line sizing |
| 9 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | m | Pipe stress analysis |
| 10 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{P \cdot V}{\gamma - 1}$ | Stored energy in compressed gas | J | Explosion risk assessment |
| 11 | $V_{blow} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | Blow exit velocity | m/s | Target plate test |
| 12 | $t_{purge} = \dfrac{3 \cdot V_{system}}{Q_{steam}}$ | Purging time | s | Air displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention for KTU Exam:** Use **SI units throughout** — pressure in **Pa or bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa), temperature in **°C or K**, mass in **kg**, energy in **kJ**, and time in **seconds**. Marks are deducted for mixed units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steam testing is the **backbone of commissioning safety** in:

- **Oil & Gas Refineries:** Steam blowing refinery headers (FCCU, hydrocracker feed lines).
- **Power Plants:** Steam blowing of boiler tubes, turbine lube oil lines, and condensate systems.
- **Chemical Plants:** Pressure testing of reactors, distillation columns, and heat exchangers.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** SIP (Steam-in-Place) sterilisation of bioreactors and process lines.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Steam blowing of digester and evaporator piping.
- **Shipbuilding:** Steam testing of marine boilers, steam turbines, and propulsion lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Linkage to Course Outcome (CO) Mapping (PESFT864 – KTU 2024):**
> - **CO4:** *Apply safety protocols during commissioning, testing, and maintenance of process plant equipment.* → Steam testing is the **direct application**.
> - **CO5:** *Identify hazards and implement control measures for thermal, pressure, and mechanical energy.* → Steam blow and pressure tests are **high-energy hazard scenarios**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation: Steam Blow Mass Flow Rate

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A new boiler feed water (BFW) line is being steam-blown. The line is **DN 300 (12-inch NB)**, **200 m long**, schedule **STD**, made of carbon steel. The target blow velocity is **120 m/s** at a steam pressure of **30 bar (saturated)**. Calculate the **steam mass flow rate** required.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pipe Cross-Sectional Area:**

For a DN 300 schedule STD pipe, **outer diameter (OD) = 323.9 mm** and **wall thickness = 9.53 mm**.

Therefore, **inner diameter (ID)**:
$$ID = 323.9 - 2 \times 9.53 = 304.84 \text{ mm} = 0.30484 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times ID^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.30484)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.7854 \times 0.09293 \\
A &= 0.07299 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate at Blow Conditions:**

Using the continuity equation $Q = A \times V$:
$$Q = 0.07299 \times 120 = 8.7588 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Steam Density at 30 bar Saturated:**

From saturated steam tables, at **30 bar (3 MPa)**, the saturation temperature is **233.9 °C** and the **specific volume of saturated vapour** is **v_g = 0.0667 m³/kg**.

Therefore, density:
$$\rho_{steam} = \frac{1}{v_g} = \frac{1}{0.0667} = 14.99 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Steam Mass Flow Rate:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= Q \times \rho_{steam} \\
\dot{m} &= 8.7588 \times 14.99 \\
\dot{m} &= 131.3 \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m} &\approx 472.7 \text{ tonnes/hour (TPH)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A **131 kg/s** steam demand is required — this is a **very large flow**. The boiler and steam supply header must be sized to deliver this surge for **15–30 minutes** continuously. The discharge stack must be **thermodynamically designed** for a **noise level of > 120 dB(A)** and located **at least 30 m away** from any personnel or equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **assumption clearly** — "Assuming saturated steam at 30 bar and steady-state isothermal blow."

## 3.2 Derivation: Purging Time (3-Volume Rule)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A **distillation column** with internal volume **V_sys = 80 m³** must be steam-purged. The steam is supplied at **2 bar saturated** through a **DN 80 line (ID = 77.93 mm)** at velocity **30 m/s**. Calculate the **time required** to pass 3 system volumes of steam.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Steam Density at 2 bar:**

From steam tables, at 2 bar: **v_g = 0.8857 m³/kg**, so **ρ = 1.129 kg/m³**.

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area of DN 80 Pipe:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.07793)^2 = 0.004768 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Steam Flow:**
$$Q = A \times V = 0.004768 \times 30 = 0.14304 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Total Steam Volume Required (3 × system volume):**
$$V_{total} = 3 \times 80 = 240 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Purging Time:**
$$\begin{aligned}
t_{purge} &= \frac{V_{total}}{Q} \\
t_{purge} &= \frac{240}{0.14304} \\
t_{purge} &= 1677.9 \text{ s} \\
t_{purge} &\approx 27.97 \text{ minutes} \approx 28 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> For exam purposes, round to the nearest whole number — **28 minutes** is the safe practical duration including ramp-up.

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermal Expansion Stress Relief

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A **steam header**, 50 m long, is steam-tested from ambient (**30 °C**) to **250 °C**. Carbon steel has a **linear thermal expansion coefficient α = 11.6 × 10⁻⁶ /°C**. Calculate the **free thermal expansion** if both ends are fixed.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Temperature Difference:**
$$\Delta T = 250 - 30 = 220 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Free Thermal Expansion:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= \alpha \times L \times \Delta T \\
\Delta L &= 11.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 220 \\
\Delta L &= 0.1276 \text{ m} \\
\Delta L &\approx 127.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Implication:**

If both ends are fixed (no expansion loops), the **compressive thermal stress** is:
$$\sigma = E \times \varepsilon = E \times \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

For carbon steel, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}$:
$$\sigma = 2 \times 10^{11} \times \frac{0.1276}{50} = 5.1 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 510 \text{ MPa}$$

This is **far beyond** the yield strength of carbon steel (~250 MPa). Therefore, **expansion loops, bellows, or sliding supports are mandatory** before steam testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **units conversion** of $\alpha$. Always confirm $\alpha$ is in **per °C** or **per K** — both are numerically equivalent.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Steam Test Type

| Plant Condition | Recommended Steam Test | Justification |
|-----------------|------------------------|---------------|
| New carbon steel piping, post-construction | **Steam Blow** + Pressure Test | Remove debris, verify welds |
| Existing pipeline after maintenance | **Steam Purging** + Leak Test | Clear moisture, confirm integrity |
| Pre-commissioning of boiler | **Hydrostatic Test** (water) + **Steam Warm-Up** | Code-mandated for new boilers |
| Refinery header, pre-startup | **Steam Blow** at 100 m/s | Remove rust, slag from refinery lines |
| Pharmaceutical bioreactor | **SIP (Steam-in-Place)** at 121 °C, 30 min | Sterilisation + integrity |
| Turbine cold start | **Steam Warm-Up** (≤ 2 °C/min) | Prevent rotor thermal distortion |
| Heat exchanger tube leak | **Steam Test on shell side** | Locate leak by visible steam plume |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Steam Pressure Testing Setup

## 4.2 Process Flow — Steam Blow Operation

The following diagram maps the **operational sequence** of a steam blow procedure, emphasising the **safety decision gates** between steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Issue Permit to Work PTW] --> B[Pre-Blow Safety Checklist]
    B --> C[Install Temporary Blow Stack]
    C --> D[Warm-Up Phase: 1/3 Pressure]
    D --> E[Soak 15 minutes]
    E --> F[Ramp to 2/3 Pressure]
    F --> G[Soak 15 minutes]
    G --> H[Ramp to Full Design Pressure]
    H --> I[Hold 30 minutes]
    I --> J[Open Blow Valve]
    J --> K[Target Plate Test]

    L -->|No| N[Repeat Blow Cycle]

    style M fill:#90ee90
    style N fill:#ffcccb
```

## 4.3 System Architecture — Safety Interlock Logic

The following block-level functional architecture shows how the **safety instrumented system (SIS)** interlocks the steam test, ensuring no single failure can cause uncontrolled overpressure or uncontrolled blow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Pressure Transmitter PT1] --> SIS[Safety Instrumented System SIS]
    S2[Pressure Transmitter PT2 Redundant] --> SIS
    T1[Temperature Transmitter] --> SIS
    L1[Level Switch Condensate Pot] --> SIS
    E1[Emergency Stop Pushbutton] --> SIS
    F1[Flame Detector at Stack] --> SIS
    SIS --> V1[Close Block Valve]
    SIS --> V2[Open PSV to Atmosphere]
    SIS --> A1[Sound Audible Alarm]
    SIS --> A2[Visual Beacon ON]
    SIS --> A3[Notify Control Room]
    style SIS fill:#ff6347
    style V1 fill:#90ee90
    style A1 fill:#ffe4b5
    style A2 fill:#ffe4b5
    style A3 fill:#ffe4b5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIS Integrity Level:** For steam testing in hydrocarbon service plants, the interlock chain must be **SIL-2 or SIL-3** (per IEC 61511). A **single pressure transmitter is never sufficient** — redundancy (1oo2 or 2oo3 voting) is mandatory. This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**.

## 4.4 Schematic — Personnel Safety Zones During Steam Blow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Zone 1: Stack Discharge, 0 to 30 m, NO ENTRY] --> Z2[Zone 2: Barricaded Area, 30 to 60 m, No Personnel]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3: Observation Post, 60 to 100 m, PPE Required]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4: Safe Operating Zone, > 100 m, Continuous Communication]
    Z1 -.->|Projectile Risk, Burn Risk, Noise > 120 dB| Z2
    Z2 -.->|Heat Radiation| Z3
    Z3 -.->|Standard PPE Only| Z4

```
