# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PESFT864]

**Q: Define steam testing. List any four types of steam testing commonly used during plant commissioning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Steam Testing** is a controlled engineering safety procedure in which steam is used as the test medium to verify the **mechanical integrity, leak-tightness, and cleanliness** of pressure vessels, boilers, and process piping systems before they enter hazardous service.

**Four Types of Steam Testing:**

1. **Steam Pressure / Leak Test** — verifies leak-tightness at 1.25 × MAWP.
2. **Steam Blow / Flushing** — removes mill scale and debris from new piping.
3. **Steam Purging** — displaces air and hydrocarbons from process equipment.
4. **Steam Soak / Warm-Up** — thermally conditions equipment to prevent thermal shock.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning **"MAWP"** (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) in a 3-mark question instantly signals syllabus-aware knowledge. KTU examiners reward correct terminology.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PESFT864]

**Q: What is the "Target Plate Test" in steam blowing? Mention the acceptance criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Target Plate Test** is a quality-control method used to determine whether the steam blow of a new pipeline has achieved the required level of cleanliness.

**Procedure:**
A polished **carbon-steel or stainless-steel target plate** is placed in the discharge path of the blow stream for a calibrated duration (typically 1–2 minutes). The plate is then removed and inspected for impact dents caused by entrained debris.

**Acceptance Criterion:**
The maximum dent depth on the target plate must be **≤ 0.3 mm** (often quoted as **≤ 0.005 inches**) for the blow to be declared satisfactory. If the dent exceeds this value, the blow cycle is **repeated** until the criterion is met.

> [!NOTE]
> A supplementary test — the **white cloth test** (passing a clean white cloth through the vent stream and checking for discolouration) — is sometimes used for low-pressure service.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions of Module 4 carry **14 marks** with a **choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure for steam pressure testing of a pressure vessel**. State the safety precautions to be observed. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A new boiler feed pump discharge line, **DN 200, NB 8-inch, schedule 40, 150 m long**, is to be steam-blown. Calculate the **steam mass flow rate (kg/s)** required for an effective blow at **velocity 100 m/s** and **steam pressure 20 bar (saturated)**. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Procedure & Safety Precautions** **\[7 Marks\]**

**Step 1 — Preparation and Isolation** (1 mark)
- Obtain **Permit to Work (PTW)** for steam testing.
- Issue **Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO)** on all energy sources.
- Isolate the vessel using **spectacle blinds** or **spades** for positive isolation.
- Verify all upstream/downstream block valves are **double-block-and-bleed**.

**Step 2 — Instrument and Safety Installation** (1 mark)
- Install **two calibrated pressure gauges** (high and low point).
- Install a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** set to **110 % of test pressure**.
- Install a **thermometer** at the vessel shell.
- Verify **drain valve** and **vent valve** are operational.

**Step 3 — Pressurisation** (2 marks)
- Open vent valve; admit steam slowly.
- Vent until **dry, odour-free steam** appears (purging complete).
- Close vent; raise pressure in **increments of 10 %** of test pressure.
- Hold each step for **10–15 minutes** for **thermal equilibration**.

**Step 4 — Hold and Inspect** (1 mark)
- Hold at **1.25 × MAWP** for **30 minutes minimum** (pneumatic/steam test).
- Inspect all **flanges, welds, valve packings, threaded joints** for leaks.
- Use **mirror + flashlight** or **soap solution** for leak detection.

**Step 5 — Depressurise and Isolate** (1 mark)
- Reduce pressure gradually; vent condensate.
- Allow natural cooling to ambient — **do not force-cool hot vessels**.
- Apply **LOTO** on the test supply connection.

**Step 6 — Safety Precautions** (1 mark)
- Personnel must wear **PPE**: hard hat, heat-resistant gloves, face shield, flame-resistant coveralls, safety boots.
- Maintain **safe exclusion zone** around the vessel.
- Keep **fire extinguisher** and **first aid kit** on standby.
- Continuous **communication** between operator, supervisor, and safety officer.
- A **standby fire watch** must be present if hydrocarbons are in adjacent equipment.

**Part (b) — Numerical: Steam Blow Mass Flow Rate** **\[7 Marks\]**

**Given:**
- Pipe: DN 200, schedule 40 → **OD = 219.1 mm**, wall thickness = 8.18 mm
- **ID** = 219.1 − 2(8.18) = 202.74 mm = **0.20274 m**
- Length = 150 m (irrelevant for mass flow but given for context)
- Velocity V = 100 m/s
- Steam pressure = 20 bar (saturated)
- From steam tables at 20 bar: **v_g = 0.0996 m³/kg**, so **ρ = 1/v_g = 10.04 kg/m³**

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area** (2 marks)
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.20274)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.7854 \times 0.04110 \\
A &= 0.03228 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate** (2 marks)
$$Q = A \times V = 0.03228 \times 100 = 3.228 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate** (2 marks)
$$\dot{m} = Q \times \rho = 3.228 \times 10.04$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= 32.41 \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m} &= 32.41 \times 3600 / 1000 \text{ TPH} \\
\dot{m} &\approx 116.7 \text{ TPH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Conclusion** (1 mark)
A steam demand of **~32.4 kg/s (117 TPH)** is required. The boiler must be sized to deliver this continuously for the **blow duration** (typically 15–30 min per cycle). Multiple blow cycles may be required until the **Target Plate Test** is satisfied.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Alternative]

**(a)** What are the **safety hazards and control measures** during steam blowing of a high-pressure pipeline? Discuss the role of **PPE, permit-to-work, and isolation**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A **distillation column** of internal volume **V = 60 m³** is to be **steam-purged** to remove air before commissioning. Steam is supplied through a **DN 50 (ID = 52.5 mm) line** at **velocity 25 m/s** and **pressure 3 bar (saturated)**. Calculate the **time required** to pass 3 system volumes of steam. **\[7 Marks\]**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Safety Hazards and Control Measures** **\[7 Marks\]**

**Major Hazards During Steam Blow:** (2 marks)

1. **Burn Injuries** — high-temperature steam (up to 350 °C) and high-velocity condensate.
2. **Noise Pollution** — discharge stacks generate **> 120 dB(A)** noise.
3. **Projectile Risk** — improperly secured stack, supports, or temporary piping can rupture.
4. **Thermal Stress** — uneven heating can cause pipe supports, anchors, or flanges to fail.
5. **Water Hammer** — sudden condensation can cause destructive pressure surges.

**Control Measures:** (3 marks)

| Hazard | Control Measure |
|--------|-----------------|
| Burns | **PPE**: heat-resistant gloves, face shield, flame-retardant coveralls, hard hat |
| Noise | **Ear protection** (muffs + plugs), safe exclusion zone > 60 m |
| Projectile | **Pre-blow inspection** of stack, supports, anchors; barricading |
| Thermal stress | **Slow warm-up** (≤ 2 °C/min); thermal monitoring |
| Water hammer | **Pre-warmed lines**; gradual pressure ramp-up; condensate drains open |

**Role of Permit-to-Work (PTW):** (1 mark)
The PTW is the **legal authorisation** document. It defines scope, hazards, controls, validity period, signatories, and isolation boundaries. **No steam blow is permitted without a valid, signed PTW.**

**Role of Isolation (LOTO):** (1 mark)
All energy sources (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, process) must be **locked out and tagged** to prevent inadvertent energisation. **Double-block-and-bleed** isolation is the gold standard for steam systems.

**Part (b) — Numerical: Purging Time** **\[7 Marks\]**

**Given:**
- System volume: V_sys = 60 m³
- Steam line: DN 50, ID = 52.5 mm = 0.0525 m
- Steam velocity: V = 25 m/s
- Steam pressure: 3 bar saturated

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area of Steam Line** (2 marks)
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0525)^2 = 0.7854 \times 0.002756 = 0.002165 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Steam Density at 3 bar** (1 mark)
From steam tables at 3 bar: **v_g = 0.6058 m³/kg**, so **ρ = 1.651 kg/m³**

**Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate** (2 marks)
$$Q = A \times V = 0.002165 \times 25 = 0.05413 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Total Steam Volume for 3× Purge** (1 mark)
$$V_{total} = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Purging Time** (1 mark)
$$t_{purge} = \frac{V_{total}}{Q} = \frac{180}{0.05413} = 3325 \text{ s} \approx 55.4 \text{ min}$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **55 minutes** of steam supply is required to achieve complete air displacement. Practically, an additional **15–20 % safety margin** is added for ramp-up, giving a total of **~65 minutes**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Steam Testing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dryness fraction (x)** in enthalpy calculations — for wet steam, always use $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$, not $h_g$ alone. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Mixing units** (e.g., pressure in bar and area in mm²) without conversion. Standardise to SI **before** substituting. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Skipping the assumption statement** in numerical problems — examiners expect a line such as *"Assuming saturated steam, steady-state, isothermal blow, and negligible heat loss."* **(Lose 0.5–1 mark)**
> 4. **Confusing hydrostatic (1.5 × MAWP, water) with steam (1.25 × MAWP, gas)** test pressures. This is a **favourite trap** question. **(Lose 2–3 marks)**
> 5. **Omitting the Target Plate Test** acceptance value of **0.3 mm** — examiners deduct for vagueness. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 6. **Ignoring the 3-Volume Rule** in purging calculations — this is a KTU staple formula. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 7. **No mention of PPE or PTW** in safety questions — module-level CO4 maps directly to permit systems. **(Lose 2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Steam Testing (PESFT864 / Module 4)**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- Steam testing uses **steam as the test medium** for **integrity, leak, blow, and purge** operations.
- Four types: **Pressure Test, Blow, Purge, Soak** — remember with mnemonic **PBS-P** (Pressure-Blow-Soak-Purge).
- Test pressure = **1.5 × MAWP (hydrostatic)** or **1.25 × MAWP (pneumatic/steam)**.
- Hold time = **30 minutes minimum** for leak tests.

### 📌 Key Formulas (must memorise)
- $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$
- $\dot{m} = A \cdot V \cdot \rho$
- $t_{purge} = 3 \cdot V_{sys} / Q$
- $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$

### 📌 Critical Safety Numbers
- Target Plate acceptance: **≤ 0.3 mm** dent.
- Purging criterion: **3 system volumes** of steam.
- Warm-up rate: **≤ 2 °C/min** for carbon steel.
- PSV set pressure: **110 % of test pressure**.
- Noise at blow stack: **> 120 dB(A)** — ear protection mandatory.
- Exclusion zone: **minimum 30 m** from blow stack.

### 📌 Code References (for "name-drop" marks)
- **ASME Section I** (boilers), **Section VIII** (pressure vessels).
- **IS 2825** (Indian Standard for unfired pressure vessels).
- **IEC 61511** (Safety Instrumented Systems).
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.169** (Piping system testing).

### 📌 PPE Checklist (must list all in safety questions)
1. Hard hat (head protection)
2. Heat-resistant gloves (hand protection)
3. Face shield + safety goggles (face/eye)
4. Flame-retardant coveralls (body)
5. Safety boots (foot)
6. Ear muffs + plugs (hearing)

### 📌 Permit-to-Work Checklist
- PTW signed by **Area Authority + Safety Officer + Execution Engineer**.
- Validity: typically **8–12 hours**, renewable.
- Define **isolation boundaries**, **PPE**, **emergency contacts**.

### 📌 SIS Interlock Rule
- Steam test interlocks must be **SIL-2 minimum** (per IEC 61511).
- **Redundant transmitters** (1oo2 or 2oo3 voting) — never single.

### 📌 Examiner's Pet Questions
- "Differentiate between **steam blow** and **steam purging**."
- "Explain the **Target Plate Test** with acceptance criteria."
- "A DN 200 line is to be steam-blown at 100 m/s. Calculate steam mass flow rate."
- "Discuss **safety precautions** during steam testing of a pressure vessel."
- "What is the **3-Volume Rule** in steam purging?"
- "State the **PPE** required for steam blow operations."

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:** Steam Testing is a **high-scoring, application-oriented topic**. Master the **four types, four formulas, four PPEs, and four codes** — and you will comfortably secure **7–14 marks** in any Module 4 question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety in Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Industrial Radiography — Formal KTU Definition

**Industrial Radiography (NDT Radiographic Testing)** is a non-destructive testing (NDT) technique that uses penetrating **ionising radiation** — either **X-rays** (generated artificially by an X-ray tube) or **gamma rays** (emitted by radioactive isotopes) — to inspect the internal structure and integrity of materials, welds, castings, and components. The radiation passes through the test object and records differential attenuation on a detector (film, digital panel, or computed radiography plate), producing an image of internal defects such as **cracks, voids, porosity, inclusions, and lack of fusion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Safety in Radiography** is a mandatory engineering discipline within Module 4 because ionising radiation is *invisible, odourless, and cannot be detected by human senses*. A single overexposure can cause severe biological damage, deterministic effects (radiation burns, cataracts, sterility), or stochastic effects (cancer, genetic mutations). Therefore, regulatory control, engineering controls, administrative controls, and personal protective practices are statutory requirements.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sun and the Shadow"

Imagine holding a piece of broken glass in front of a strong flashlight in a dark room.

- The **light rays** are like **ionising radiation** (X-rays/gamma rays).
- The **solid parts of the glass** block more light, while the **cracks and bubbles** allow more light to pass.
- The **shadow cast on the wall** is the radiographic image — darker where radiation passed through (thin/defective zones) and lighter where radiation was blocked (sound/dense zones).

The "safety" aspect comes in because, unlike a flashlight, **ionising radiation damages living cells the same way it exposes film**. Standing too close to the source is like staring at the sun — invisible harm that accumulates silently.

## 1.3 Why Industrial Radiography is Hazardous

| Hazard Aspect | Description |
|---|---|
| **Ionising nature** | Radiation has sufficient energy (>5 keV) to remove tightly bound electrons from atoms, breaking chemical bonds in DNA. |
| **Penetration power** | Gamma rays from Co-60 can penetrate several centimetres of **steel**, meaning ordinary walls do NOT shield the radiographer. |
| **No immediate sensation** | The human body has no pain receptors that respond to ionising radiation — exposure gives zero warning. |
| **Cumulative damage** | Biological effects (especially cancer) are cumulative over a worker's lifetime, tracked via **personal dosimetry**. |
| **Source accountability** | Lost or stolen radioactive sources (e.g., Ir-192) become orphan sources and severe public hazards. |

> [!NOTE]  
> The internationally accepted governing body is the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP)**, and in India, the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** enforces the *Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules* and the *Radiation Surveillance Procedures for Safe Handling of Radioactive Sources*. The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with AERB safety standards.

## 1.4 Classification of Radiation Used in Radiography

| Type | Source | Energy Range | Penetration | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **X-rays** | X-ray tube (artificial) | 50 kV – 400 kV (up to **20 MeV** for linear accelerators) | Low to medium (depends on kV) | Thin welds, light alloys, electronics |
| **Gamma rays — Ir-192** | Iridium-192 isotope | ~0.3 – 0.6 MeV (avg ~**0.4 MeV**) | Medium (steel up to ~70 mm) | Most common in field radiography |
| **Gamma rays — Co-60** | Cobalt-60 isotope | 1.17 and 1.33 MeV (avg ~**1.25 MeV**) | High (steel up to ~200 mm) | Thick welds, heavy castings |
| **Gamma rays — Cs-137** | Caesium-137 isotope | 0.662 MeV | Moderate | Less common, used in some gauges |

## 1.5 Units of Radiation — A KTU Must-Know

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Memorise these units — they appear in every KTU examination.

| Quantity | SI Unit | Legacy Unit | Conversion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Exposure (X)** | Coulomb per kilogram (**C/kg**) | Roentgen (**R**) | $1 \, R = 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \, C/kg$ |
| **Absorbed Dose (D)** | Gray (**Gy**) | rad | $1 \, Gy = 100 \, rad$ |
| **Equivalent Dose (H)** | Sievert (**Sv**) | rem | $1 \, Sv = 100 \, rem$ |
| **Activity (A)** | Becquerel (**Bq**) | Curie (**Ci**) | $1 \, Ci = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, Bq$ |

The relationship between **Equivalent Dose** and **Absorbed Dose** is:

$$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$

where $w_R$ is the **radiation weighting factor** (X-rays, gamma, beta = 1; alpha = 20; neutrons = 5–20 depending on energy).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Radiation Protection

The KTU syllabus and AERB guidelines are built upon **three engineering controls** that, when applied together, reduce personnel dose to negligible levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Mnemonic — "TDS": Time, Distance, Shielding.** Every radiography safety question on these three factors is worth 3–7 marks.

### Pillar 1: TIME — Minimise Exposure Duration

Dose is **directly proportional** to the time spent near the source. The governing equation is:

$$D = \dot{D} \cdot t$$

where $\dot{D}$ is the **dose rate** (Sv/h) and $t$ is the exposure duration (h).

**Practical rule:** Plan the work before the source is exposed. Never linger in the radiation field. Use **remote handling tools (crab, source projector)** to keep hands out of the beam.

### Pillar 2: DISTANCE — The Inverse Square Law

This is the **most powerful natural protection**. Doubling the distance quarters the dose. The equation is:

$$\dot{D}(d) = \dot{D}(d_0) \cdot \left( \frac{d_0}{d} \right)^{\!2}$$

- $d_0$ = reference distance from source (often 1 m)
- $d$ = actual distance
- $\dot{D}(d_0)$ = dose rate at reference distance

**Why it works:** Radiation spreads out spherically. The surface area of a sphere grows as $4\pi d^2$, so intensity (energy per unit area) falls as $1/d^2$.

### Pillar 3: SHIELDING — Material Attenuation

When time and distance cannot be reduced, we interpose a dense material. The fundamental attenuation law is:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

- $I_0$ = incident intensity
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), depends on material and photon energy
- $x$ = shield thickness (cm)

A more practical engineering metric is the **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** — the thickness of a material that reduces intensity by 50 %.

$$x_{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

The **Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)** reduces intensity to 10 %:

$$x_{TVL} = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}$$

| Radiation Energy | Recommended Shield Material | Typical HVL |
|---|---|---|
| 100 keV X-rays | **Lead** | ~0.3 mm |
| 400 keV X-rays | **Lead** | ~3.6 mm |
| Ir-192 (0.4 MeV) | **Lead** | ~6 mm |
| Co-60 (1.25 MeV) | **Lead** or **Depleted Uranium** | ~12 mm lead |

## 2.2 The ALARA Principle

> [!NOTE]  
> **ALARA = As Low As Reasonably Achievable.** This is the *philosophical foundation* of all radiation protection. It obligates every employer and worker to reduce doses below the regulatory limits by *all reasonable means*, even when doses are already below the limit.

Engineering application of ALARA:
1. Substitute lower-energy sources (Ir-192 in preference to Co-60 where possible).
2. Use **automatic exposure** devices instead of manual cranking.
3. Use **collimators** to restrict beam size.
4. Use **source position indicators** to verify safe retraction.
5. Apply **interlocks** on exposure rooms.

## 2.3 Annual Dose Limits (AERB / ICRP)

| Category | Effective Dose Limit | Lens of Eye | Skin, Hands, Feet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Occupational worker (adult)** | **20 mSv/year** (averaged over 5 years, with no single year > 50 mSv) | 150 mSv/year | 500 mSv/year |
| **Trainee / student (< 18 yrs)** | 6 mSv/year | 50 mSv/year | 150 mSv/year |
| **Member of public** | 1 mSv/year | 15 mSv/year | 50 mSv/year |
| **Embryo/Foetus** | 1 mSv (once pregnancy declared) | — | — |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{D}(d) = \dot{D}(d_0) \cdot (d_0/d)^2$ | Inverse square law | Distance problems |
| 2 | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay | Source activity over time |
| 3 | $\lambda = \ln 2 \,/\, t_{1/2}$ | Decay constant from half-life | Half-life conversions |
| 4 | $t_{1/2} = 0.693 \,/\, \lambda$ | Half-life from decay constant | Half-life conversions |
| 5 | $t_{eff} = (t_{1/2} \cdot t_{biol})/(t_{1/2} + t_{biol})$ | Effective half-life (in body) | Internal dosimetry |
| 6 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Linear attenuation | Shielding thickness |
| 7 | $x_{HVL} = 0.693 \,/\, \mu$ | Half-value layer | Shielding selection |
| 8 | $N = N_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Attenuation of particles/photons | Photon transmission |
| 9 | $D = \dot{D} \cdot t$ | Dose from dose rate × time | Time-distance trade-off |
| 10 | $H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ | Equivalent dose | Dose equivalency |
| 11 | $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ | Effective dose (whole body) | Whole-body risk |
| 12 | $R = 2.58 \times 10^{-4} \, C/kg$ | 1 Roentgen conversion | Unit conversion |

## 2.5 Radiation Zoning and Controlled Areas

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> AERB requires every radiography site to be demarcated into zones based on expected dose rates.

| Zone | Dose Rate Range | Access Control | Typical Marking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Controlled Area** (High) | > **100 µSv/h** (10 mR/h) | Restricted to authorised workers with TLD; locked barriers | Red ropes, radiation trefoil sign, "RADIOACTIVE" placard |
| **Supervised Area** (Medium) | 1 – 100 µSv/h (0.1 – 10 mR/h) | Monitored access; supervision | Yellow tape, warning signs |
| **Unrestricted / Public Area** (Low) | < 1 µSv/h (< 0.1 mR/h) | Open | No special marking |

## 2.6 Personal Dosimetry — The Four Instruments

| Instrument | Type | Principle | Reading Range | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Film Badge** | Passive | Photographic film darkening | 0.1 mSv – 10 Sv | Cumulative monthly dose |
| **TLD Badge** (Thermo-Luminescent Dosimeter) | Passive | Crystal stores energy; releases light on heating | 0.01 mSv – 10 Sv | Cumulative, more accurate than film |
| **Pocket Dosimeter (Direct Reading)** | Active | Quartz-fibre electroscope | 0 – 200 mR | Real-time task dose |
| **Survey Meter (GM tube / Ion chamber)** | Active | Counts ionisation events | 0.1 µSv/h – 1 Sv/h | Area monitoring, source leakage |
| **Electronic Personal Dosimeter (EPD)** | Active | Solid-state Si-diode | 1 µSv – 10 Sv | Real-time with alarms |

## 2.7 Sources and Storage of Radioactive Isotopes

- **Ir-192** is supplied as a **sealed source** encapsulated in stainless steel (typically classified as **Special Form Radioactive Material** per IAEA regulations).
- Sources must be stored in a **Type B(U) container** (e.g., the **540° gamma ray projector / "pig"**).
- Storage rooms must be **locked, access-controlled, and labelled** with the radiation trefoil.
- **Leak testing** of sealed sources is performed every 6 months (or annually) per AERB guidelines.
- **Source inventory records** must be maintained permanently.

## 2.8 Emergency Procedures

Common radiography emergencies:
1. **Source stuck in the exposed position** — evacuate the area, increase distance, notify RSO (Radiation Safety Officer), attempt retrieval only with the **emergency retrieval tool (long-handled pliers)**.
2. **Source lost / missing** — activate emergency plan, search the last-known collimated path, notify AERB within 24 hours.
3. **Accidental exposure of personnel** — perform immediate bioassay (whole-body counting), calculate dose, provide medical care, and document the event.
4. **Source container damaged** — isolate the area, use **reverse-cut sheet lead** to contain the source.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety in radiography is vital in:
- **Oil & gas pipeline welds** (cross-country, in-plant).
- **Aerospace component inspection** (turbine blades, fuselage welds).
- **Nuclear power plant construction** (reactor vessel, primary loop).
- **Shipbuilding and offshore platforms**.
- **Casting and forging inspection** in steel plants.

> [!WARNING]  
> In India, the **Bhopal-style** Goiana-2 (2011) incident in Delhi, where a radiography source was illegally sold as scrap metal, caused one death and several injuries. This shows that **end-of-life source management** is a non-negotiable safety requirement.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

### Statement
The intensity of radiation at a point varies inversely as the square of the distance from a point source.

### Derivation

Consider a point source emitting radiation isotropically (uniformly in all directions) with total power $P$ (energy per second).

Step 1 — The radiation crosses a **sphere of radius $d$** centred on the source. The surface area of this sphere is:

$$A_{sphere} = 4 \pi d^2$$

Step 2 — By the principle of energy conservation, the total power $P$ passing through the sphere equals the power emitted by the source. Therefore, the intensity (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$I(d) = \frac{P}{A_{sphere}} = \frac{P}{4 \pi d^2}$$

Step 3 — Take the ratio of intensities at two distances $d_0$ and $d$:

$$\frac{I(d)}{I(d_0)} = \frac{P / (4 \pi d^2)}{P / (4 \pi d_0^2)} = \frac{d_0^2}{d^2}$$

Step 4 — Rearranging yields the standard **Inverse Square Law**:

$$\boxed{\,I(d) = I(d_0) \cdot \left( \frac{d_0}{d} \right)^{\!2}\,}$$

This equation is valid for **point sources only** (i.e., when $d \gg$ source dimensions). In practical field radiography, Ir-192 pellets are small (≈ 3 mm) and distances are in metres, so the point-source approximation is excellent.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Radioactive Decay Law

### Statement
The activity of a radioactive source decreases exponentially with time.

### Derivation

Step 1 — The rate of radioactive decay is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ present. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (s⁻¹), positive by convention; the negative sign indicates decreasing $N$.

Step 2 — This is a first-order linear differential equation. Separate the variables:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial state $(N_0, t=0)$ to current state $(N, t)$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN'}{N'} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt'$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals. The left side gives $\ln N - \ln N_0 = \ln(N/N_0)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Step 6 — Since activity $A = \lambda N$ (and $A_0 = \lambda N_0$), the same proportionality holds for activity:

$$\boxed{\,A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\,}$$

Step 7 — In terms of **half-life** $t_{1/2}$ (the time for $A$ to fall to $A_0/2$):

$$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-\lambda t_{1/2} = \ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{t_{1/2}}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Linear Attenuation Law

### Statement
A narrow beam of mono-energetic photons is attenuated exponentially as it passes through matter.

### Derivation

Step 1 — Consider a thin slice of shielding material of thickness $dx$. The probability of a photon interacting within $dx$ is proportional to $dx$:

$$dI = -\mu I \, dx$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (cm⁻¹), which depends on the material density and atomic number.

Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $x$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI'}{I'} = -\mu \int_0^x dx'$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\mu x$$

Step 4 — Exponentiate to obtain the **Lambert-Beer Law** for radiation:

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}\,}$$

Step 5 — For a shielding material, it is more practical to express the required thickness in **half-value layers (HVL)**. By definition, after one HVL the intensity is halved:

$$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu \cdot x_{HVL}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu \cdot x_{HVL}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu \cdot x_{HVL} = \ln 2$$

$$\boxed{\,x_{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}\,}$$

For multiple HVLs, the transmission factor after $n$ HVLs is $(1/2)^n$:

$$I = I_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\!n}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** A radiographer stands at **2 m** from an Ir-192 source. The dose rate at **1 m** is **80 mGy/h**. Calculate:

(a) The dose rate at 2 m.
(b) The dose received if he stays at 2 m for 30 minutes.
(c) How long can he stay at 2 m before reaching the AERB occupational limit of 20 mSv/year (assume 1 Gy ≈ 1 Sv for gamma rays)?

### Solution

**Part (a):** Use the inverse square law with $d_0 = 1$ m and $d = 2$ m:

$$\dot{D}(2) = \dot{D}(1) \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{\!2} = 80 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = 20 \, mGy/h$$

**Part (b):** Convert 30 minutes to hours: $t = 0.5$ h.

$$D = \dot{D} \cdot t = 20 \, mGy/h \cdot 0.5 \, h = 10 \, mGy \equiv 10 \, mSv$$

**Part (c):** Time to reach 20 mSv at 20 mGy/h (which equals 20 mSv/h for gamma):

$$t = \frac{D_{limit}}{\dot{D}} = \frac{20 \, mSv}{20 \, mSv/h} = 1 \, h$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Although 1 hour seems significant, this is the *time-budget for the entire year* at that location. The radiographer must rely on **distance, shielding, and rotation of workers** to stay ALARA. In real practice, the dose rate is reduced by **collimation** and **shielding** so that 1 m dose rates are typically far lower (often 0.1–1 mGy/h).

## 3.5 Worked Example — Source Activity Decay

**Problem:** An Ir-192 source has an initial activity of **100 Ci** on 1 January 2024. The half-life of Ir-192 is **73.83 days**. Calculate the activity on 1 July 2024 (after 182 days).

### Solution

Step 1 — Calculate the decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{73.83} = 9.39 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$$

Step 2 — Apply the decay equation:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = 100 \cdot e^{-(9.39 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 182}$$

Step 3 — Compute the exponent:

$$-9.39 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 182 = -1.709$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-1.709} = 0.181$$

Step 5 — Multiply by initial activity:

$$A(182 \, d) = 100 \cdot 0.181 = 18.1 \, Ci$$

**Engineering interpretation:** After ~6 months, the source has decayed to roughly **18 %** of its original activity. This is why radiography sources are replaced periodically — the dose rate falls below the usable threshold, and the cost per exposure rises.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Industrial Radiography Safety Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Radiography Job Commenced]) --> planA[Job Planning and Risk Assessment]
    planA --> perA[Personal Dosimetry: TLD and EPD Worn]
    perA --> zoneA[Demarcate Controlled Area > 100 µSv per hr]
    zoneA --> signA[Post Radiation Trefoil Warning Signs]
    signA --> workA[Worker Uses Source Projector Crab]
    workA --> expA[Source Exposed via Remote Handling]
    expA --> monA[Continuous Area Monitoring with Survey Meter]
    monA --> checkA{Dose Rate Within Limit?}
    checkA -- Yes --> recA[Record Readings in Logbook]
    checkA -- No --> evacA[Evacuate Personnel Immediately]
    recA --> retA[Retrieve Source to Shielded Container]
    evacA --> notifyA[Notify Radiation Safety Officer RSO]
    retA --> doseA[Read Personal Dosimeter at End of Shift]
    doseA --> repA[Document Dose Records AERB Compliance]
    notifyA --> rsoA[RSO Investigates and Re plans Job]
    rsoA --> planA
    repA --> stopA([Radiography Job Closed])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Three Pillars of Radiation Protection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceNode[Radioactive Source or X Ray Tube] --> timeNode[TIME: Minimise Duration]
    sourceNode --> distNode[DISTANCE: Inverse Square Law]
    sourceNode --> shieldNode[SHIELDING: Lead Concrete Depleted Uranium]
    timeNode --> doseNode[Reduced Worker Dose]
    distNode --> doseNode
    shieldNode --> doseNode
    doseNode --> alaraNode[ALARA Achieved]
    alaraNode --> recordNode[Documented in Safety Records]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the ALARA principle. List the three cardinal principles of external radiation protection.**

**Model Answer:**

**ALARA** stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Achievable"**. It is the foundational philosophy of radiation protection, endorsed by the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP)** and adopted by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** of India. ALARA requires that all radiation exposures to workers and the public be kept *as low as is reasonably achievable*, taking into account economic and social factors, *below* the prescribed dose limits — not merely *at* the limit.

The three cardinal principles of external radiation protection are:

1. **TIME** — Minimise the duration of exposure.
2. **DISTANCE** — Maximise the distance from the radiation source (Inverse Square Law).
3. **SHIELDING** — Interpose appropriate absorbing material (lead, concrete, depleted uranium) between the source and the worker.

> [!NOTE]  
> Examiner's tip: Always mention *both* the philosophy and the *practical engineering triad* for full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Controlled Area and a Supervised Area in industrial radiography. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Controlled Area | Supervised Area |
|---|---|---|
| **Dose rate range** | **> 100 µSv/h** (> 10 mR/h) | **1 – 100 µSv/h** (0.1 – 10 mR/h) |
| **Access** | Restricted to authorised personnel only with personal dosimeters | Monitored access; supervision by RSO |
| **Marking** | Red ropes, radiation trefoil sign, "DANGER — RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL" | Yellow tape, "CAUTION — RADIATION" signs |
| **Example** | The immediate area within a **2 m radius** of an exposed Ir-192 source during field radiography | The area between 2 m and 5 m around an exposed source |
| **Monitoring** | Continuous survey-meter check; interlock required | Periodic monitoring |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice A]`

**(a)** Explain the **biological effects of ionising radiation** on the human body, distinguishing between **deterministic** and **stochastic** effects. Give two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radiographer works with a **Co-60 source**. The dose rate at **1 m** from the source is **150 mGy/h**. Calculate:
- (i) the dose rate at **3 m**,
- (ii) the dose received in **45 minutes** at 3 m,
- (iii) the number of **half-value layers of lead (HVL = 12 mm)** required to reduce the dose rate at 1 m to the AERB public limit of **1 µSv/h**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Biological Effects (7 marks):**

**Deterministic effects** (also called *tissue reactions*) have a **threshold dose** below which they do not occur, and their **severity increases with dose**. They are caused by the killing or malfunction of a large population of cells.

*Examples:*
1. **Radiation dermatitis / skin burns** — occur at doses > 2–3 Gy to the skin.
2. **Cataract formation** in the lens of the eye — threshold ~ 0.5 Gy single exposure.
3. **Acute radiation syndrome (ARS)** — whole-body dose > 1 Gy, manifesting as nausea, hair loss, and bone-marrow failure.
4. **Sterility** — temporary at ~ 0.3 Gy; permanent at > 3 Gy.

**Stochastic effects** have **no threshold dose**. Probability of occurrence increases with dose, but severity is independent of dose. They arise from DNA damage in a single cell.

*Examples:*
1. **Radiation-induced carcinogenesis** (e.g., leukaemia, thyroid cancer) — latency 5–20 years.
2. **Genetic mutations** in offspring of exposed individuals.

> [!NOTE]  
> Key point for marks: deterministic effects have thresholds and are severity-related, whereas stochastic effects have no threshold and are probability-related.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (b)(i):** Inverse square law:

$$\dot{D}(3) = \dot{D}(1) \cdot \left( \frac{1}{3} \right)^{\!2} = 150 \cdot \frac{1}{9} = 16.67 \, mGy/h$$

**Part (b)(ii):** Convert 45 minutes to 0.75 h:

$$D = \dot{D} \cdot t = 16.67 \, mGy/h \cdot 0.75 \, h = 12.5 \, mGy \equiv 12.5 \, mSv$$

(using $w_R = 1$ for gamma rays)

**Part (b)(iii):** We need to reduce $\dot{D}_0 = 150 \, mGy/h = 1.5 \times 10^{5} \, mGy/h$ to $\dot{D}_{target} = 1 \, \mu Sv/h = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, mGy/h$ (treating mGy ≈ mSv for gamma).

The transmission ratio required:

$$\frac{\dot{D}_{target}}{\dot{D}_0} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.5 \times 10^{5}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-9}$$

Using $n$ HVLs:

$$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\!n} = 6.67 \times 10^{-9}$$

Take logarithms:

$$n \cdot \ln 2 = -\ln(6.67 \times 10^{-9}) = 18.83$$

$$n = \frac{18.83}{0.693} = 27.2$$

Therefore **28 half-value layers** of lead are required, corresponding to a thickness of:

$$x = 28 \cdot 12 \, mm = 336 \, mm \approx 33.6 \, cm \, \text{of lead}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This illustrates why it is impractical to *fully* shield a high-activity Co-60 source with lead alone — the radiographer's primary protection comes from **distance and limited exposure time**, with shielding reserved for the source container and exposure room walls.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Choice B]`

**(a)** Describe the construction, working principle, and limitations of a **Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (TLD)**. Compare it with a **Film Badge** in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **Ir-192 source** has an initial activity of **50 Ci** on the date of calibration. Calculate:
- (i) the activity after **90 days** (half-life of Ir-192 = **73.83 days**),
- (ii) the activity after **6 months** (~ 183 days),
- (iii) the percentage of original activity remaining. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — TLD vs Film Badge (7 marks):**

**Construction of TLD:**
A TLD consists of a small crystal (most commonly **Lithium Fluoride doped with Magnesium and Titanium, LiF:Mg,Ti** — commercially known as TLD-100) sealed in a plastic holder, often with a set of filters (plastic, aluminium, copper, lead) to differentiate between beta, gamma, and X-ray exposures.

**Working principle:**
When the crystal is exposed to ionising radiation, electrons in the crystal lattice are excited and trapped in metastable energy states. The energy stored is *proportional to the absorbed dose*. Upon heating in a TLD reader (~ 300 °C), the trapped electrons return to the ground state, emitting **visible light (thermoluminescence)**. The intensity of emitted light is measured by a photomultiplier tube and converted to dose.

**Limitations of TLD:**
1. Reading is destructive — once heated, the dose information is erased.
2. Cannot be re-read except by annealing (heat treatment) and re-exposure, which is rarely done.
3. Energy dependence — requires calibration at specific photon energies.
4. Fading — some signal loss over time at high temperature/humidity.

**Tabular comparison:**

| Feature | TLD Badge | Film Badge |
|---|---|---|
| **Detector material** | LiF:Mg,Ti crystal | Photographic emulsion on film |
| **Sensitivity range** | 0.01 mSv – 10 Sv | 0.1 mSv – 10 Sv |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (5–10 %) | Lower (15–20 %) |
| **Re-readability** | No (destructive) | Yes (archive film) |
| **Fading** | Some at high humidity | Minimal in sealed pack |
| **Energy dependence** | Less | More |
| **Cost per badge** | Slightly higher | Lower |
| **Typical use** | Long-term cumulative | Long-term cumulative |

---

**Part (b) — Ir-192 Decay (7 marks):**

**Part (b)(i):** Decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{73.83} = 9.388 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}^{-1}$$

Activity after 90 days:

$$A(90) = 50 \cdot e^{-(9.388 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 90} = 50 \cdot e^{-0.8449}$$

$$e^{-0.8449} = 0.4296$$

$$A(90) = 50 \cdot 0.4296 = 21.48 \, Ci$$

**Part (b)(ii):** Activity after 183 days:

$$A(183) = 50 \cdot e^{-(9.388 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 183} = 50 \cdot e^{-1.718}$$

$$e^{-1.718} = 0.1793$$

$$A(183) = 50 \cdot 0.1793 = 8.97 \, Ci$$

**Part (b)(iii):** Percentage of original activity remaining after 6 months:

$$\%\text{ remaining} = \frac{A(183)}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{8.97}{50} \times 100 = 17.94\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This corresponds closely to ~ 2.47 half-lives, giving $(1/2)^{2.47} \approx 0.180$, validating the calculation.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Mixing up units:** Students frequently convert mGy and mSv incorrectly. For X-rays and gamma rays, the radiation weighting factor $w_R = 1$, so numerically $D$ (in Gy) = $H$ (in Sv) in equivalent magnitude. However, for **alpha particles**, $w_R = 20$, and the conversion changes drastically.  
> 2. **Forgetting to convert minutes to hours** in time × dose-rate calculations. Always check unit consistency.  
> 3. **Half-life of Ir-192 confusion:** Use **73.83 days** (NOT 73 years — that is C-14). Co-60 has a half-life of **5.27 years**.  
> 4. **Inverse square law vs HVL:** Inverse square applies to *distance* (no material in between); HVL applies to *shielding thickness* (with material). Do not confuse the two.  
> 5. **In the KTU answer sheet, never write "TLD" or "AERB" without expansion** in the first occurrence — full form first, abbreviation in brackets thereafter.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Safety in Radiography**

- ☐ **Industrial radiography** uses **ionising radiation** (X-rays, gamma from Ir-192 / Co-60 / Cs-137) to inspect welds and components.
- ☐ **SI units:** Becquerel (activity), Gray (absorbed dose), Sievert (equivalent dose). Legacy: Curie, rad, rem.
- ☐ **Three pillars of protection: TIME, DISTANCE, SHIELDING** — full marks guaranteed if you list these.
- ☐ **Inverse Square Law:** $\dot{D} \propto 1/d^2$ — distance is the most powerful control.
- ☐ **Attenuation Law:** $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ ; **HVL** = $0.693 / \mu$.
- ☐ **Decay Law:** $A = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ ; $\lambda = 0.693 / t_{1/2}$.
- ☐ **ALARA** is the philosophy; **AERB** is the Indian regulator; **ICRP** is the international body.
- ☐ **Dose limits (AERB):** Worker = **20 mSv/yr** (5-yr avg), Public = **1 mSv/yr**, Foetus = **1 mSv**.
- ☐ **Controlled Area:** > 100 µSv/h with restricted access; **Supervised Area:** 1–100 µSv/h.
- ☐ **TLD** (LiF:Mg,Ti crystal) is the standard personal dosimeter; **Film Badge** is the older passive alternative; **EPD** is real-time electronic.
- ☐ **Survey Meter** (GM tube or ion chamber) is used for *area* monitoring; **Pocket Dosimeter** is used for *real-time personal* dose.
- ☐ **Sealed sources** must be **leak tested every 6 months**, stored in **Type B(U) containers**, and **inventoried permanently**.
- ☐ **Deterministic effects** have a *threshold* and severity scales with dose; **Stochastic effects** have *no threshold* and probability scales with dose.
- ☐ **Ir-192 half-life = 73.83 days**; **Co-60 half-life = 5.27 years**.
- ☐ **Radiation trefoil** sign (magenta or black on yellow background) marks all radiation zones.
- ☐ **Emergency tool** for source recovery = long-handled pliers / source retrieval rod; only authorised RSO should perform retrieval.
- ☐ **Record keeping** of dose, source movement, and leak tests is **legally mandatory** under AERB.

> End of Module 4 Notes — Safety in Radiography.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personal Monitoring Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Personal Monitoring Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personal Monitoring Devices (PMDs)** are portable, wearable, or body-mounted instruments used to continuously measure, record, and evaluate an individual worker's exposure to occupational hazards (physical, chemical, biological, or ergonomic) over a defined reference period, typically a full work shift (8 hours).

As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT864 – Safety in Engineering Industry, Module 4), Personal Monitoring Devices are classified as **personal protective equipment (PPE) adjuncts** that provide a *quantitative dose-measurement interface* between the worker and the hazardous environment, enabling Occupational Health & Safety (OHS) professionals to compare measured exposures against **Threshold Limit Values (TLV)** and **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)**.

### Formal Classification of Personal Monitors

| Sl. No. | Hazard Domain | Typical PMD Type | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Acoustic Noise** | Noise Dosimeter (Type 2 / Type 3) | IS 9779 / IEC 61672 |
| 2 | **Airborne Dust / Aerosol** | Personal Dust Monitor (PDM), Cyclone Sampler | IS 5182 / BIS |
| 3 | **Toxic Gases / Vapors** | Single/Multi-Gas Detector (LEL, H₂S, CO, O₂) | IS 9436 / OSHA |
| 4 | **Ionizing Radiation** | TLD Badge, Film Badge, Pocket Dosimeter | AERB / ICRP |
| 5 | **Heat Stress** | WBGT Sensor with body-attached logger | ISO 7243 |
| 6 | **Hand-Arm / Whole-Body Vibration** | Vibration Dosimeter (Triaxial accelerometer) | ISO 5349 / ISO 2631 |
| 7 | **Non-Ionizing Radiation** | RF/EMF personal monitor | ICNIRP guidelines |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Health Black-Box" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a pilot's *Flight Data Recorder* (the famous "black box"). A pilot cannot see turbulence inside a cloud, but the FDR records every parameter (altitude, speed, G-forces) for post-flight analysis. Similarly, a worker walking inside a noisy foundry, a chemical plant, or a radiology wing **cannot perceive** his cumulative exposure to invisible hazards like ionizing radiation, hydrogen sulfide, or 90 dB(A) noise. The Personal Monitoring Device is that worker's **"occupational black box"** — silently worn on the lapel, collar, or belt, recording the dose that the worker's body actually receives, regardless of where he moves inside the plant.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Reference Exchange Rate for Noise:** **3 dB** (doubling of sound energy → 3 dB increase; this is the basis of the **Dose %** calculation).
- **Criterion Level (8-hr TWA):** **90 dB(A)** for OSHA / IS standards; **85 dB(A)** for NIOSH recommended exposure.
- **Exchange Rate Threshold Limit Value (TLV):** **5 dB** as per ACGIH (older convention).
- **Standard WBGT Reference:** **30 °C** (for unacclimatized workers).
- **Ionizing Radiation Annual Dose Limit (Worker):** **20 mSv/year** averaged over 5 years (ICRP).
- **Air Sampling Flow Rate (Personal):** typically **1.0 to 4.0 L/min** (NIOSH method).

## 1.4 Purpose and Hierarchy of Controls Context

> [!NOTE]
> Personal Monitoring Devices occupy the **detection and measurement layer** of the *Hierarchy of Controls*. They do NOT eliminate the hazard — they **quantify** it so that administrative controls (rotation, training) and PPE (earplugs, respirators) can be effectively justified and deployed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Personal Monitor (Block Logic)

Every PMD, regardless of hazard type, follows the same fundamental five-stage signal-processing pipeline:

1. **Sensor / Transducer Stage** — Converts the physical/chemical stimulus (sound pressure, gas concentration, β/γ radiation, acceleration) into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Amplification, filtering (e.g., A-weighting for noise), and impedance matching.
3. **Data Acquisition & Integration Stage** — Samples the signal (e.g., every second) and integrates it over the exposure duration to produce a **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** or **Cumulative Dose**.
4. **Storage / Display Stage** — Logs data in non-volatile memory; displays TWA, peak, STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit), or Dose %.
5. **Communication / Retrieval Stage** — Data downloaded via USB, Bluetooth, or IR-dock to a PC for post-shift analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In KTU board examinations, students are frequently asked to *"explain the working of a noise dosimeter"* or *"describe a personal dust sampler."* Always structure your answer around these **5 stages** — sensor → conditioner → integrator → display → retrieval. This framework scores full marks.

## 2.2 Noise Dosimeter – Detailed Operation

A **Noise Dosimeter** is the most frequently tested PMD in KTU papers. It is worn on the worker's shoulder with a microphone clipped near the ear (within the hearing zone).

- It samples the **A-weighted sound pressure level (SPL)** in **dB(A)** at a fixed interval (typically every second).
- It computes the percentage dose relative to the **Criterion Level** and **Time-Intensity Trading Relationship (Exchange Rate)**.

### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet – Personal Monitoring Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the formulas verbatim, including units.

| Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sound Pressure Level** | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | $p$ = RMS sound pressure (Pa), $p_0$ = $20 \;\mu Pa$ (reference) | Valid for plane/free progressive wave |
| **Sound Intensity Level** | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I$ = intensity (W/m²), $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | Doubling of $I$ → +3 dB |
| **Noise Dose (%)** | $\text{Dose} = \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{C_n}{T_n} \times 100$ | $C_i$ = time spent at SPL $L_i$; $T_i$ = reference duration allowed at $L_i$ | Dose = 100 % → exposure at Criterion Level |
| **Allowed Time at SPL $L$** | $T = \dfrac{T_0}{2^{(L - L_c)/q}}$ | $T_0$ = 8 h; $L_c$ = criterion level; $q$ = exchange rate (3 or 5 dB) | $q = 3 \Rightarrow$ OSHA; $q = 5 \Rightarrow$ ACGIH |
| **TWA from Dose** | $\text{TWA} = L_c + q \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right)$ | $D$ = dose (%); result in dB(A) | TWA capped at criterion level |
| **WBGT Index** | $\text{WBGT}_{\text{outdoor}} = 0.7\,T_{nw} + 0.2\,T_g + 0.1\,T_{db}$ | $T_{nw}$ = natural wet bulb, $T_g$ = globe, $T_{db}$ = dry bulb (°C) | Outdoor with solar load |
| **WBGT Index (Indoor)** | $\text{WBGT}_{\text{indoor}} = 0.7\,T_{nw} + 0.3\,T_g$ | All temperatures in °C | No solar load |
| **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** | $\text{VDV} = \left[\displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} a_w^{4}(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$ | $a_w$ = frequency-weighted RMS acceleration (m/s²); $T$ = duration (s) | Used for vibration exposure |
| **Radiation Dose Equivalent** | $H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$ | $D_{T,R}$ = absorbed dose (Gy); $w_R$ = radiation weighting factor | $H_T$ in Sieverts (Sv) |
| **Personal Air Sampling Volume** | $V = Q \times t$ | $Q$ = flow rate (L/min); $t$ = sampling time (min) | Used in dust/gas sampling |

### 2.3 Why Personal Monitoring Is Engineering-Critical

- **Spatial Variability Correction:** Area monitors (fixed) measure at one point; PMDs capture the *true* exposure as the worker moves between hot and cold zones.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** IS 8522, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95, Factories Act 1948 (India, Section 13) mandate quantitative exposure assessment.
- **Epidemiological Evidence:** Personal dosimetry data forms the *quantitative basis* for compensation claims in occupational diseases (e.g., NIHL – Noise-Induced Hearing Loss, asbestosis, byssinosis).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Allowed Exposure Time from OSHA Exchange Rate (q = 3 dB)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical 7-Mark Question):** A worker is exposed to a constant noise level of **96 dB(A)**. Calculate the maximum permissible exposure time per shift as per OSHA (Criterion Level = 90 dB(A), Exchange Rate q = 3 dB, Reference Duration T₀ = 8 h).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The OSHA standard uses the **5 dB doubling rule** historically, but the *modern OSHA Hearing Conservation Amendment* uses a **3 dB exchange rate** for dose calculation. The allowed time formula is:

$$T = \dfrac{T_0}{2^{(L - L_c)/q}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$T_0 = 8 \text{ h}, \quad L = 96 \text{ dB(A)}, \quad L_c = 90 \text{ dB(A)}, \quad q = 3 \text{ dB}$$

$$T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(96 - 90)/3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$\dfrac{96 - 90}{3} = \dfrac{6}{3} = 2$$

$$T = \dfrac{8}{2^{2}} = \dfrac{8}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{T = 2 \text{ hours per 8-hour shift}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (engineer's commentary).**

At 96 dB(A), the worker's permissible exposure is reduced to **25 %** of the shift. The remaining 6 hours must be spent in zones where $L < 80$ dB(A), or administrative rotation must be enforced.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Noise Dose and TWA Calculation (Multi-Level Exposure)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A worker spends **2 hours at 95 dB(A)**, **3 hours at 100 dB(A)**, and **3 hours at 85 dB(A)**. Compute (a) the cumulative Noise Dose %, and (b) the 8-hour TWA. Use OSHA criterion: $L_c = 90$ dB(A), $q = 5$ dB, $T_0 = 8$ h.

### Step (a) — Noise Dose Calculation

**Step 1 — Use the reference-duration table (OSHA standard).**

For an exchange rate $q = 5$ dB, the allowed duration $T_i$ at a given SPL $L_i$ is:

$$T_i = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $T_i$ for each exposure level.**

For $L_1 = 95$ dB(A):

$$T_1 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{1}} = 4 \text{ h}$$

For $L_2 = 100$ dB(A):

$$T_2 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{2}} = 2 \text{ h}$$

For $L_3 = 85$ dB(A):

$$T_3 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(85-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{-1}} = 8 \times 2 = 16 \text{ h}$$

> The value at 85 dB(A) exceeds the shift duration, which means partial dose contribution.

**Step 3 — Apply the Dose formula.**

$$\text{Dose} = \left[ \dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2} + \dfrac{C_3}{T_3} \right] \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Dose} = \left[ \dfrac{2}{4} + \dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{3}{16} \right] \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Numerically simplify each term.**

$$\dfrac{2}{4} = 0.5000$$

$$\dfrac{3}{2} = 1.5000$$

$$\dfrac{3}{16} = 0.1875$$

$$\text{Dose} = \left[ 0.5000 + 1.5000 + 0.1875 \right] \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dose} = 218.75\%}$$

**Step 5 — Examiner-style verdict.** A dose exceeding **100 %** indicates the worker has been *over-exposed*; engineering controls and PPE compliance must be urgently reviewed.

### Step (b) — Time-Weighted Average (TWA)

**Step 1 — Use the TWA formula.**

$$\text{TWA} = L_c + q \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{100}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 5 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{218.75}{100}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithmic term.**

$$\dfrac{218.75}{100} = 2.1875$$

$$\log_{10}(2.1875) \approx 0.3399$$

**Step 4 — Final computation.**

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 5 \times 0.3399$$

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 1.6995$$

$$\boxed{\text{TWA} \approx 91.70 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The worker's TWA of **91.70 dB(A)** is above the OSHA **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) of 90 dB(A)**. Therefore the worker is **non-compliant** with the action level, triggering mandatory enrollment in a Hearing Conservation Program (HCP).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Selection Guide for Personal Monitors

| Hazard | Sensor Principle | Output Metric | Selection Criterion for KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Noise | Electret condenser microphone | dB(A), Dose %, TWA | Type 2 / IEC 61672-1 compliance |
| Dust (Respirable) | Gravimetric filter + cyclone | mg/m³ | Cyclone 4 = 4 µm cut-point (NIOSH) |
| Gas (Toxic) | Electrochemical cell | ppm | 4-gas: O₂, LEL, CO, H₂S |
| Radiation (γ) | TLD (LiF:Mg,Ti) crystal | mSv | AERB approved |
| Vibration | Piezoelectric triaxial accelerometer | m/s² rms, VDV | ISO 8041 (human response) |
| Heat | Thermistor + globe thermometer | °C WBGT | ISO 7243 threshold values |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Generic Personal Monitoring Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HZ[Hazard Source e.g. Noise, Gas, Radiation] --> SN[Sensor / Transducer]
    SN --> SC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    SC --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    ADC --> CPU[Microprocessor with Real Time Clock]
    CPU --> MEM[Non Volatile Memory Storage]
    CPU --> DSP[Display LCD Module]
    CPU --> ALM[Audible and Visual Alarm Buzzer LED]
    MEM --> PC[PC Interface USB or Bluetooth Dock]
    PC --> SW[Safety Management Software Dose Report]
    SN -. power .-> BAT[Rechargeable Lithium Battery 3.6 V]
    BAT -. regulated supply .-> SC
    BAT -. regulated supply .-> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This block schematic represents the *universal architecture* common to **all** personal monitoring devices (noise dosimeters, gas monitors, radiation dosimeters, vibration dosimeters). The **dashed lines** represent the *power distribution rails* (battery → regulator → subsystems), while the **solid arrows** represent the *signal flow* from hazard pickup to data retrieval.

## 4.2 Process Flow – Personal Noise Dosimetry Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Noise Hazard Zone] --> B[Conduct Initial Area Survey]
    B --> C{Survey Result Above 85 dBA}
    C -- Yes --> D[Issue Personal Noise Dosimeter to Worker]
    C -- No --> E[Document and Archive Survey Results]
    D --> F[Worker Wears Dosimeter for Full Shift]
    F --> G[Download Data to PC via Dock]
    G --> H[Software Computes TWA and Dose Percent]
    H --> DOSE{Dose Above 100 Percent}
    DOSE -- Yes --> I[Implement Engineering Controls and Reassign Worker]
    DOSE -- No --> J[Continue Periodic Monitoring Annually]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map – Personal Dust Sampler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR[Ambient Air in Breathing Zone] --> CY[Cyclone Separator 4 micrometer cut]
    CY --> FIL[Pre Weighed PVC Filter Cassette 5 micrometer pore]
    FIL --> PUMP[Constant Flow Pump 1.7 to 2.0 L per minute]
    PUMP --> ROT[Rotameter with Calibration Orifice]
    ROT --> EXH[Clean Exhaust Air]
    PUMP -. powered by .-> BATT2[NIMH Rechargeable Battery]
    FIL -. sent to lab .-> LAB[Gravimetric Lab Analysis]
    LAB --> CONC[Concentration in milligram per cubic meter]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a **Noise Dosimeter**. List any **four essential components** of a personal noise dosimeter.

**Model Answer:**
A noise dosimeter is a portable, body-worn instrument that measures and integrates the **A-weighted sound pressure level** over a work shift to determine a worker's cumulative noise exposure expressed as a percentage of the permissible dose.

The four essential components are:
1. **Microphone** (omnidirectional, free-field) — usually clipped near the ear.
2. **A-weighting filter network** — to model human hearing response.
3. **Integrator / Microprocessor** — computes TWA and Dose % in real time.
4. **Display + Memory module** — shows SPL and stores time-history for download.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is the significance of **WBGT index** in personal heat-stress monitoring? State the indoor WBGT formula.

**Model Answer:**
The **Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT)** index is a composite metric used to evaluate heat stress on the human body by combining the effects of air temperature, humidity, radiant heat, and air movement. It is the globally accepted standard (ISO 7243) for personal heat-stress assessment in industries like steel, foundry, and glass manufacturing.

**Indoor WBGT formula:**

$$\text{WBGT}_{\text{indoor}} = 0.7\,T_{nw} + 0.3\,T_g$$

where $T_{nw}$ = natural wet-bulb temperature (°C) and $T_g$ = globe thermometer temperature (°C).

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Each Part B question typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is between two full questions (Q11 OR Q12). Model both alternatives below.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** *(a)* Explain in detail the **working principle and components of a personal noise dosimeter** with a neat block diagram. *(b)* A worker is exposed to **100 dB(A) for 3 hours**, followed by **88 dB(A) for 5 hours**. Determine the (i) cumulative **Noise Dose** and (ii) **TWA** as per OSHA standard (Criterion = 90 dB(A), Exchange rate $q = 5$ dB).

#### Model Solution – Part (a) — Working of Noise Dosimeter

The personal noise dosimeter is a small, lightweight (typically < 100 g) instrument clipped to the worker's clothing with a microphone mounted near the ear (in the acoustic free field).

**1. Microphone (Electret / Condenser type):** Converts the acoustic pressure fluctuations into a proportional electrical voltage signal.

**2. Preamplifier and A-weighting filter:** The weak microphone signal is amplified. The A-weighting filter applies a frequency response that mimics the human ear's sensitivity (peaks around 1–4 kHz, attenuates very low and very high frequencies).

**3. Squaring and Integration circuit:** The instantaneous squared pressure is averaged (RMS) over short time intervals (typically 1 second). This produces the **slow-response SPL**.

**4. Microprocessor:** Computes the dose using the criterion-level and exchange-rate algorithm. Internally maintains a real-time clock and shift-timer.

**5. Display:** Shows current SPL, maximum SPL, run-time, and Dose %.

**6. Memory and Interface:** Stores 1-second SPL data for the entire shift; downloads to a computer via USB / IR / Bluetooth dock.

**7. Battery:** Typically a 9 V alkaline or rechargeable Li-ion cell providing 50+ hours of operation.

#### Model Solution – Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $C_1 = 3$ h at $L_1 = 100$ dB(A); $C_2 = 5$ h at $L_2 = 88$ dB(A); $L_c = 90$ dB(A); $q = 5$ dB; $T_0 = 8$ h.

**Step 1 — Compute reference allowed durations $T_i$.**

For $L_1 = 100$ dB(A):

$$T_1 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(100 - 90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{2}} = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ h}$$

For $L_2 = 88$ dB(A):

$$T_2 = \dfrac{8}{2^{(88 - 90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{-0.4}} = 8 \times 2^{0.4}$$

$$2^{0.4} \approx 1.3195 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_2 \approx 10.556 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Noise Dose.**

$$\text{Dose} = \left[\dfrac{C_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{T_2}\right] \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Dose} = \left[\dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{5}{10.556}\right] \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Dose} = \left[1.5000 + 0.4737\right] \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Dose} \approx 197.37\%}$$

**Step 3 — Compute TWA.**

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 5\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{197.37}{100}\right)$$

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 5\,\log_{10}(1.9737)$$

$$\log_{10}(1.9737) \approx 0.2953$$

$$\text{TWA} = 90 + 5 \times 0.2953$$

$$\boxed{\text{TWA} \approx 91.48 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**Step 4 — Examiner's verdict.** The dose is nearly **double** the permissible exposure, indicating serious non-compliance. Recommendations: (i) engineering noise control at source; (ii) mandatory use of earplugs + earmuffs (dual protection); (iii) reduction of shift hours at the 100 dB(A) zone.

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper – Internal Choice Alternative]** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a personal dust sampler (cyclone-based)** used for respirable dust monitoring. List any **four IS standards** relevant to air sampling. *(b)* A personal sampler operates at **2.0 L/min** for a full shift of **6 hours**. The pre-weight of the filter is **42.358 mg** and the post-weight is **43.015 mg**. Determine the **time-weighted average concentration** in mg/m³ and comment on the result against the ACGIH TLV of **3 mg/m³** for respirable dust.

#### Model Solution – Part (a) — Personal Dust Sampler

A **personal dust sampler** is a battery-powered, body-worn device used to collect **respirable dust** from the worker's breathing zone for gravimetric (weight-based) analysis.

**Block diagram (in words for textual answer):**

`Air Inlet → 10 mm Dorr-Oliver / GK 4.0 Cyclone → PVC Filter (5 µm pore) in cassette → Critical orifice → Constant-flow pump → Rotameter → Exhaust → Battery`

**Working:**

1. A **small sampling head (cyclone)** is clipped near the worker's collar in the breathing zone.
2. The cyclone uses centrifugal force to separate **non-respirable particles (> 4 µm)** which fall into a grit pot, while respirable particles (< 4 µm) pass through.
3. Respirable dust is collected on a **pre-weighed PVC filter** held in a cassette.
4. A **constant-flow pump** draws air through the cyclone at a calibrated rate (typically 1.7 – 2.0 L/min for NIOSH method 0600).
5. After the shift, the filter is **re-weighed** in a controlled lab; the weight difference gives the dust mass.

**Four relevant Indian Standards (IS):**

| Standard | Title |
|---|---|
| **IS 5182 (Part XIV)** | Methods for measurement of air pollution – Particulate matter |
| **IS 3670** | Glossary of terms relating to air sampling |
| **IS 8522** | Guide for personal dust monitoring |
| **IS 1795** | Specifications for personal air sampling cyclones |

#### Model Solution – Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Mass of dust collected.**

$$\Delta m = m_{final} - m_{initial} = 43.015 - 42.358 = 0.657 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of air sampled.**

$$V = Q \times t$$

Convert units: $Q = 2.0$ L/min = $2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/min; $t = 6$ h = 360 min.

$$V = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 360 = 0.720 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute TWA concentration.**

$$C = \dfrac{\Delta m}{V} = \dfrac{0.657 \text{ mg}}{0.720 \text{ m}^3}$$

$$\boxed{C = 0.9125 \text{ mg/m}^3 \approx 0.91 \text{ mg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ACGIH TLV.**

$$\dfrac{C}{\text{TLV}} = \dfrac{0.9125}{3.0} \approx 0.304 \;\; (30.4\%)$$

Since the measured concentration is **30.4 %** of the TLV, the exposure is **well within acceptable limits**, but since respirable dust is a **cumulative toxin** (pneumoconiosis risk), periodic monitoring should continue.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert L/min → m³/min. Always multiply by **$10^{-3}$**.
> 2. **Confusing Criterion Level (90 dB) with Action Level (85 dB)** in noise problems.
> 3. **Using wrong exchange rate** (3 vs 5 dB) without checking the standard cited in the question.
> 4. **Missing the WBGT components:** Students often forget the **0.3 weighting for globe temperature** or omit the natural wet-bulb component.
> 5. **Not labeling the diagram** — KTU examiners award **1 to 2 marks** specifically for labeled block diagrams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Personal Monitoring Devices**

- ✅ A **Personal Monitoring Device (PMD)** is a wearable instrument that quantifies a worker's exposure to a specific occupational hazard over time.
- ✅ The **Criterion Level** for noise is **90 dB(A)** for OSHA and IS standards; the **Action Level** (where HCP becomes mandatory) is **85 dB(A)**.
- ✅ The **OSHA Exchange Rate = 3 dB** (energy-doubling); the **ACGIH Exchange Rate = 5 dB** (historical, intensity-doubling).
- ✅ The **Noise Dose formula**: $\text{Dose} = \sum (C_i / T_i) \times 100\%$, and **TWA** = $L_c + q \log_{10}(D/100)$.
- ✅ **WBGT (Indoor)** = $0.7\,T_{nw} + 0.3\,T_g$ — no solar load term.
- ✅ **WBGT (Outdoor)** = $0.7\,T_{nw} + 0.2\,T_g + 0.1\,T_{db}$ — solar load included.
- ✅ Personal dust samplers operate at **1.7 – 2.0 L/min** using a **4 µm cyclone cut-point** (NIOSH / BIS).
- ✅ TLD badges use **LiF:Mg,Ti crystals**; annual dose limit for occupational worker = **20 mSv** (averaged over 5 years, per ICRP).
- ✅ Vibration monitoring: **Hand-Arm** uses ISO 5349; **Whole-Body** uses ISO 2631; both rely on **frequency-weighted RMS acceleration**.
- ✅ **Personal monitors ≠ Area monitors** — PMDs follow the worker and capture true exposure variability.
- ✅ The **5-block signal chain** of every PMD: Sensor → Conditioner → Integrator → Memory/Display → Retrieval Interface.
- ✅ Common KTU-exam synergy topics: **Noise + WBGT + Dust** are often paired in a single 14-mark question.
- ✅ Always **interpret the result** (compliance / non-compliance) — KTU examiners award marks for engineering commentary, not just numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT864 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radiation Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiation Hazards** refer to the potential adverse health effects on living organisms (including human workers) and the environment caused by exposure to ionizing and non-ionizing electromagnetic emissions or particulate emissions emitted from radioactive materials, nuclear reactors, X-ray equipment, accelerators, and a wide spectrum of industrial, medical, and natural sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT864):**
> *"Radiation hazards encompass the identification, measurement, control, and mitigation of energy emissions (in the form of particles or waves) that possess sufficient energy to ionize atoms of matter or cause significant thermal/non-thermal biological damage, thereby posing occupational and public health risks in engineering industries such as nuclear power, radiology, welding, semiconductor fabrication, telecommunications, and aerospace."*

### Classification of Radiation

| Class | Examples | Energy Range | Penetration |
|-------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Ionizing** | α, β, γ, X-rays, Neutrons | > 10 eV | High (γ, neutrons) to Low (α) |
| **Non-Ionizing** | UV, Visible, IR, Microwave, RF, ELF | < 10 eV | Surface / Thermal |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Sunlight-Beach Metaphor**
> Imagine standing on a beach at noon. The sunlight you feel is a form of radiation (electromagnetic waves). A short sunbath gives you a healthy tan (controlled exposure), but staying under the scorching sun for hours causes sunburn, heatstroke, and may lead to skin cancer (uncontrolled exposure = hazard). Similarly, X-ray machines used by doctors are 'safe' because the exposure is brief, controlled, and shielded. However, a worker standing daily near an unshielded radioactive source in a plant accumulates damage silently, just as repeated sunburn silently accumulates skin damage. **The "dose" is the cumulative beach time, and the "hazard" is the biological damage from the sun's invisible energy penetrating your skin.**

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **1 Becquerel (Bq)** = **1 disintegration per second (dps)** — SI unit of radioactivity.
> - **1 Curie (Ci)** = **3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** — Older unit, still used in USA.
> - **1 Gray (Gy)** = **1 Joule/kg** of absorbing material — SI unit of absorbed dose.
> - **1 Sievert (Sv)** = Absorbed dose (Gy) × **Quality Factor (Q)** — SI unit of equivalent/effective dose.
> - **1 Roentgen (R)** = **2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg** in air — Unit of exposure.
> - **Annual public dose limit (ICRP)** = **1 mSv/yr** (averaged over 5 years).
> - **Occupational worker limit (ICRP)** = **20 mSv/yr** (averaged over 5 years, not exceeding 50 mSv in any single year).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Ionizing Radiation — Operational Logic

### A. Alpha Particles (α)
- **Nature:** Helium-4 nuclei (2 protons + 2 neutrons), charge **+2e**, mass ~ 4 amu.
- **Mechanism:** Strip electrons from atoms (high ionization density).
- **Penetration:** Stopped by **a sheet of paper or outer skin (dead layer)**.
- **Internal Hazard:** **EXTREMELY DANGEROUS if inhaled/ingested** (e.g., Plutonium-239 dust).

### B. Beta Particles (β⁻ / β⁺)
- **Nature:** High-speed electrons or positrons, charge **∓1e**, negligible mass.
- **Mechanism:** Moderate ionization, deflected by electric/magnetic fields.
- **Penetration:** Stopped by **a few mm of aluminum or plastic**.
- **Shielding:** Acrylic, plastic, or aluminum — **NEVER use bare hands** (beta burns).

### C. Gamma Rays (γ) and X-rays
- **Nature:** High-frequency electromagnetic radiation, no charge, no mass.
- **Mechanism:** Penetrate deep into matter; produce secondary electrons via Compton, photoelectric, and pair-production interactions.
- **Penetration:** Requires **dense shielding — lead (Pb), depleted uranium, or thick concrete**.
- **Industrial Source:** Cs-137, Co-60, Ir-192 (radiography).

### D. Neutron Radiation
- **Nature:** Neutral particles from nuclear fission/activation.
- **Mechanism:** Interact via elastic scattering with hydrogen nuclei; produce secondary gamma rays.
- **Penetration:** Extremely high; **shielded by hydrogen-rich materials** (water, polyethylene, paraffin).

## 2.2 Non-Ionizing Radiation — Operational Logic

- **Ultraviolet (UV):** Causes photokeratitis (welder's flash), skin erythema, cataracts.
- **Infrared (IR):** Thermal burns, lens opacities in glass/metal workers.
- **Microwave / RF:** Thermal heating of tissues (SAR — Specific Absorption Rate); pacemaker interference.
- **Laser Radiation:** Class 1 (safe) to Class 4 (eye/skin burn, fire hazard) — used in cutting, surgery, alignment.
- **ELF / VLF:** 50/60 Hz power-line fields — IARC classifies as **Group 2B (possible carcinogen)**.

## 2.3 Biological Effects of Radiation

| Effect Type | Dose Threshold | Manifestation |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Deterministic** (severity ↑ with dose) | > 1 Sv (acute) | Nausea, hair loss, burns, sterility, death |
| **Stochastic** (probability ↑ with dose) | No threshold (probabilistic) | Cancer, leukemia, genetic mutations |
| **Teratogenic** (fetal exposure) | > 0.1 Sv | Congenital malformations, mental retardation |
| **Acute Radiation Syndrome (ARS)** | > 1–2 Sv whole-body | Hematopoietic, GI, neurovascular syndromes |

## 2.4 The Three Pillars of Radiation Protection (Time-Distance-Shielding)

1. **MINIMIZE TIME:** Dose is directly proportional to exposure time. **D ∝ t**.
2. **MAXIMIZE DISTANCE:** Inverse Square Law — doubling distance reduces dose to **¼**.
3. **OPTIMIZE SHIELDING:** Use appropriate material/thickness between source and worker.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS — Memorize for ESE (End Semester Examination)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Activity Decay | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | A = activity (Bq), λ = decay constant (s⁻¹) | Exponential decay |
| 2 | Half-Life | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | $t_{1/2}$ in seconds | λ = ln2 / t₁/₂ |
| 3 | Mean Life | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{t_{1/2}}{0.693}$ | τ = mean life (s) | 1.44 × t₁/₂ |
| 4 | Inverse Square Law | $I = \dfrac{I_0 \cdot d_0^2}{d^2}$ | I = intensity, d = distance (m) | Applies to point source |
| 5 | Absorbed Dose | $D = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | D in Gy, E in J, m in kg | E = energy deposited |
| 6 | Equivalent Dose | $H_T = D \cdot w_R$ | $H_T$ in Sv, $w_R$ = radiation weighting factor | $w_R$ (γ=1, β=1, n=5–20, α=20) |
| 7 | Effective Dose | $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ | E in Sv, $w_T$ = tissue weighting factor | Whole body = 1.0 |
| 8 | Exposure Rate Constant (Γ) | $\dot{X} = \dfrac{\Gamma \cdot A}{d^2}$ | $\dot{X}$ in R/hr, A in Ci, d in m | Specific to nuclide |
| 9 | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | $I = I_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{x / HVL}$ | x = shield thickness | Tenth-Value Layer (TVL) = 3.32 × HVL |
| 10 | Linear Exponential Attenuation | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | μ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹) | μ = ln2 / HVL |
| 11 | MPD (Annual Limit) | $MPD = 5(N-18)$ rem | N = age in years | NRC occupational formula |
| 12 | Count Rate (Dead Time) | $n = \dfrac{m}{1 - m\tau}$ | n = true rate, m = observed, τ = dead time | GM counter correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For γ-radiography shielding problems, use **HVL method** when asked for "number of HVLs to reduce dose by factor N". For point-source geometry, always apply the **Inverse Square Law** *before* accounting for shielding.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Power Plants (NPP):** Containment shielding, ALARA planning, emergency core cooling.
- **Industrial Radiography:** Pipeline weld inspection (Ir-192, Co-60); requires exclusion zones and collimators.
- **Medical Physics:** CT, LINAC, brachytherapy, nuclear medicine (Tc-99m, I-131).
- **Aerospace:** Cosmic radiation dose for flight crews; solar particle event shielding.
- **Semiconductor / NDT:** X-ray fluorescence, electron-beam lithography, ion implantation.
- **Welding & Cutting:** UV/IR exposure, plasma arc radiation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Radioactive Decay Law

We begin with the empirical observation that the **rate of decay** of a radioactive nuclide is **directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei (N) present** at that instant.

**Step 1:** Express the rate of decay (which is the negative of dN/dt because N decreases with time) as proportional to N.

$$
-\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \lambda \cdot N
